topics.title: Fundamentals of Estimation Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Estimation Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Estimation Theory** is a rigorous branch of statistical signal processing and applied mathematics that deals with inferring the value of an unknown parameter (or a set of parameters, or an entire random process) from noisy, incomplete, or imperfect empirical observations.

Formally, given an observation vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$ whose joint probability density function (or mass function) $p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})$ depends on an unknown parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta$, the goal of estimation theory is to construct a function (the **estimator**) $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = g(\mathbf{x})$ whose output is a "best" approximation of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ in some mathematically well-defined sense.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Estimation theory forms the *mathematical backbone* of radar, sonar, communications (channel estimation), biomedical signal processing (ECG/EEG parameter extraction), machine learning (regression weights), and control systems (state estimation via Kalman filtering). Without it, modern signal processing is impossible.

### 1.2 Distinction: Estimation vs. Detection

| Aspect | Estimation | Detection |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Determine the *value* of an unknown parameter | Decide between *hypotheses* (binary/M-ary) |
| **Output** | A continuous or discrete numerical value | A decision (e.g., $H_0$ or $H_1$) |
| **Mathematical Tool** | Bayesian inference, MLE, CRLB | Neyman-Pearson, Likelihood Ratio Test |
| **Example** | Estimating the *frequency* of a sinusoid | Deciding whether a *signal is present* |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Noisy Thermometer" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy for First-Time Learners:**
>
> Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to guess the temperature of a room. You are given a thermometer, but it is slightly faulty ŌĆö its readings fluctuate randomly around the true temperature. You take **10 readings** and average them. That average is your *estimate* of the true temperature.
>
> - The **true room temperature** $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is the *unknown parameter*.
> - The **faulty thermometer readings** $\mathbf{x}$ are the *noisy observations*.
> - The **function** you used (e.g., averaging) is the *estimator* $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = g(\mathbf{x})$.
> - The **single numerical answer** you report is the *estimate*.
>
> Estimation theory asks: *"What is the mathematically optimal function $g(\cdot)$ to use so that our guess is closest to the truth?"*

### 1.4 The Two Foundational Paradigms

1. **Classical (Frequentist) Estimation:** $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is treated as a **fixed, unknown deterministic constant**. We design estimators with desirable long-run (frequentist) properties like unbiasedness, consistency, and efficiency.
2. **Bayesian Estimation:** $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is modeled as a **random variable** with a *prior* density $p(\boldsymbol{\theta})$. Observations are then used to compute the *posterior* $p(\boldsymbol{\theta}\vert\mathbf{x})$, from which the estimate is derived.

### 1.5 Key Terminology Anchored

- **Parameter Space ($\Theta$):** The set of all possible values $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ can take.
- **Observation Space ($\mathcal{X}$):** The set of all possible observation vectors $\mathbf{x}$.
- **Estimator:** The function $g(\cdot)$ ŌĆö a rule, *before* seeing data.
- **Estimate:** The numerical value $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = g(\mathbf{x})$ ŌĆö the output, *after* seeing data.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Model of the Estimation Problem

The canonical estimation problem is specified by the **triple** $(\Theta, p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta}), \mathcal{L})$, where:

- $\Theta$ = parameter space
- $p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})$ = statistical description of the data
- $\mathcal{L}$ = loss function used to measure the cost of estimation error

**Procedure:**

1. **Formulate the model.** Identify the parametric family $p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})$ governing the observations.
2. **Define the loss.** Choose a penalty for error, e.g., squared-error $\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}) = \Vert \boldsymbol{\theta} - \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \Vert^2$.
3. **Derive the estimator.** Optimize the chosen performance criterion (risk, likelihood, posterior).
4. **Evaluate the estimator.** Check unbiasedness, variance, CRLB, consistency.

### 2.2 The Three Pillars of Classical Estimation

#### Pillar 1: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)
The MLE chooses the $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ that **maximizes the likelihood** of having observed the data:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_{\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta} \; p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

Equivalently, it maximizes the **log-likelihood** $\ln p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})$.

#### Pillar 2: Bayesian Estimation (MMSE, MAP, ML-Bayesian)
Incorporates a prior $p(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ and computes the posterior via Bayes' rule:

$$p(\boldsymbol{\theta}\vert\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x}\vert\boldsymbol{\theta}) \, p(\boldsymbol{\theta})}{p(\mathbf{x})}$$

- **MMSE Estimator:** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{MMSE}} = E[\boldsymbol{\theta}\vert\mathbf{x}] = \int \boldsymbol{\theta} \, p(\boldsymbol{\theta}\vert\mathbf{x}) \, d\boldsymbol{\theta}$
- **MAP Estimator:** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \, p(\boldsymbol{\theta}\vert\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \, p(\mathbf{x}\vert\boldsymbol{\theta})p(\boldsymbol{\theta})$
- **ML-Bayesian (uniform prior):** MAP reduces to MLE.

#### Pillar 3: Method of Moments & Least Squares
- **MoM:** Equate sample moments to population moments and solve.
- **LS / WLS:** Minimize $\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} \Vert^2$, yielding $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{LS}} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{x}$.

### 2.3 Desirable Properties of an Estimator (KTU High-Yield)

| Property | Definition | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Unbiasedness** | $E[\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}] = \boldsymbol{\theta}$ | No systematic error across repeated trials. |
| **Efficiency** | Achieves the Cram├®r-Rao Lower Bound | Has the *minimum possible variance*. |
| **Consistency** | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \xrightarrow{P} \boldsymbol{\theta}$ as $n \to \infty$ | More data $\Rightarrow$ better estimate. |
| **Sufficiency** | Captures *all* information about $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ in $\mathbf{x}$ | No other statistic can do better. |
| **Asymptotic Normality** | $\sqrt{n}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} - \boldsymbol{\theta}) \to \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ | Enables confidence intervals. |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Log-likelihood | $\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \ln p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})$ | Scalar, dimensionless |
| MLE Score Equation | $\dfrac{\partial \ln p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} = \mathbf{0}$ | First-order optimality |
| Bayes' Rule | $p(\boldsymbol{\theta}\vert\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{p(\mathbf{x}\vert\boldsymbol{\theta})\,p(\boldsymbol{\theta})}{p(\mathbf{x})}$ | $p(\mathbf{x}) = \int p(\mathbf{x}\vert\boldsymbol{\theta})\,p(\boldsymbol{\theta})\,d\boldsymbol{\theta}$ |
| Bias | $b(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = E[\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}] - \boldsymbol{\theta}$ | Zero $\Rightarrow$ unbiased |
| MSE | $\text{MSE} = \text{Var}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}) + b(\boldsymbol{\theta})^2$ | Variance + squared bias |
| CRLB (Scalar) | $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \dfrac{\left[1 + \dfrac{db(\theta)}{d\theta}\right]^2}{-E\!\left[\dfrac{\partial^2 \ln p(\mathbf{x};\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]}$ | Lower bound on variance |
| CRLB (Vector) | $\mathbf{C}_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}} - \mathbf{b}\mathbf{b}^T \geq \mathbf{J}^{-1}$ | $\mathbf{J}$ = Fisher Information Matrix |
| Fisher Information | $\mathcal{I}(\theta) = -E\!\left[\dfrac{\partial^2 \ln p(\mathbf{x};\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$ | Measures info per sample |
| MMSE Estimator | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MMSE}} = E[\theta \mid \mathbf{x}]$ | Conditional mean |
| MAP Estimator | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \; p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)\,p(\theta)$ | Peak of posterior |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In 5G/6G channel estimation, the pilot symbols are used to form $\mathbf{x}$, and the channel coefficients $\mathbf{h}$ are the parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. The MMSE or LMMSE estimator minimizes mean squared error of the recovered channel, directly boosting link throughput.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cram├®r-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) ŌĆö Scalar Case

We derive the *unbiased* scalar CRLB. The extension to biased estimators follows identically by replacing $\hat{\theta}$ with $\hat{\theta} - b(\theta)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Set Up the Starting Integral.**
The expectation of any estimator $\hat{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$ under the density $p(\mathbf{x};\theta)$ is:

$$E[\hat{\theta}(\mathbf{x})] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) \, p(\mathbf{x};\theta) \, d\mathbf{x}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate Both Sides with Respect to $\theta$.**

$$\frac{d}{d\theta}E[\hat{\theta}] = \frac{d}{d\theta} \int \hat{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) \, p(\mathbf{x};\theta) \, d\mathbf{x}$$

Under regularity conditions (differentiation under the integral is permitted):

$$\frac{d}{d\theta}E[\hat{\theta}] = \int \hat{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) \, \frac{\partial p(\mathbf{x};\theta)}{\partial \theta} \, d\mathbf{x}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Rewrite the Derivative of the Density Using the Log-Derivative Trick.**

$$\frac{\partial p(\mathbf{x};\theta)}{\partial \theta} = p(\mathbf{x};\theta) \, \frac{\partial \ln p(\mathbf{x};\theta)}{\partial \theta}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{d}{d\theta}E[\hat{\theta}] = \int \left[ \hat{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) \, \frac{\partial \ln p(\mathbf{x};\theta)}{\partial \theta} \right] p(\mathbf{x};\theta) \, d\mathbf{x} = E\!\left[ \hat{\theta} \, \frac{\partial \ln p(\mathbf{x};\theta)}{\partial \theta} \right]$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Introduce the Score Function and Use Unbiasedness.**
For an unbiased estimator, $\dfrac{d}{d\theta}E[\hat{\theta}] = 1$. Moreover, we have the **identity**:

$$E\!\left[ \frac{\partial \ln p(\mathbf{x};\theta)}{\partial \theta} \right] = 0$$

(This follows because $\int p \, d\mathbf{x} = 1$ is independent of $\theta$.) Hence:

$$1 = E\!\left[ (\hat{\theta} - \theta) \, \frac{\partial \ln p(\mathbf{x};\theta)}{\partial \theta} \right]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality.**
For any two random variables $U, V$:

$$\left( E[UV] \right)^2 \leq E[U^2] \, E[V^2]$$

Apply with $U = \hat{\theta} - \theta$ and $V = \dfrac{\partial \ln p(\mathbf{x};\theta)}{\partial \theta}$:

$$1 \leq E\!\left[ (\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2 \right] \, E\!\left[ \left( \frac{\partial \ln p(\mathbf{x};\theta)}{\partial \theta} \right)^2 \right]$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Identify the Variance and Fisher Information.**

$$E\!\left[ (\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2 \right] = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \quad \text{(unbiased case)}$$

$$E\!\left[ \left( \frac{\partial \ln p(\mathbf{x};\theta)}{\partial \theta} \right)^2 \right] = \mathcal{I}(\theta) = \text{Fisher Information}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Rearrange to Obtain the CRLB.**

$$\boxed{\;\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{\mathcal{I}(\theta)} = \frac{1}{-E\!\left[\dfrac{\partial^2 \ln p(\mathbf{x};\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Equality condition** (Cauchy-Schwarz is tight) implies:
>
> $$\frac{\partial \ln p(\mathbf{x};\theta)}{\partial \theta} = \mathcal{I}(\theta) \left( \hat{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) - \theta \right)$$
>
> This is the *efficient estimator* condition ŌĆö only MLE-type estimators with sufficient statistics achieve it.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: MLE for Gaussian with Unknown Mean

**Problem:** Let $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ independently, with $\sigma^2$ known. Find the MLE of $\mu$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the Joint Density.**

$$p(\mathbf{x};\mu) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Take the Log-Likelihood.**

$$\ln p(\mathbf{x};\mu) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Differentiate with Respect to $\mu$.**

$$\frac{\partial \ln p(\mathbf{x};\mu)}{\partial \mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \mu)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Set Equal to Zero and Solve.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \mu) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - n\mu = 0$$

$$\boxed{\;\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = \bar{x}\;}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Verify Unbiasedness and Efficiency.**
- $E[\hat{\mu}] = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum E[x_i] = \mu$ ŌåÆ **Unbiased**.
- $\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}$ and $\mathcal{I}(\mu) = \dfrac{n}{\sigma^2}$ ŌåÆ **Achieves CRLB** (efficient).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö The Canonical Estimation Process

The following **Mermaid functional architecture flow** depicts the end-to-end estimation pipeline as per KTU 2024 module expectations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Unknown Parameter Theta] --> B[Physical System or Channel]
    B --> C[Observation Vector X]
    C --> D[Statistical Model p of X given Theta]
    D --> E[Estimator Function g of X]
    E --> F[Estimate Theta Hat]
    F --> G{Performance Check}
    G --> H[Bias]
    G --> I[Variance]
    G --> J[CRLB Comparison]
    H --> K[Final Validated Estimator]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow ŌĆö Choosing the Right Estimator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Define Estimation Problem] --> Q1{Is Prior Knowledge Available?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Bayes[Bayesian Approach]
    Q1 -->|No| Classical[Classical Approach]
    Bayes --> BayesChoice{Optimization Criterion}
    BayesChoice -->|Squared Error| MMSE[MMSE Estimator = E of Theta given X]
    BayesChoice -->|Zero One Loss| MAP[MAP Estimator = argmax of Posterior]
    Classical --> ClassChoice{Method}
    ClassChoice -->|Maximize Likelihood| MLE[MLE Estimator]
    ClassChoice -->|Minimize Residuals| LS[Least Squares]
    ClassChoice -->|Match Moments| MOM[Method of Moments]
    MMSE --> Eval[Evaluate Bias Variance CRLB]
    MAP --> Eval
    MLE --> Eval
    LS --> Eval
    MOM --> Eval
    Eval --> Done[Final Estimator Selected]
```

### 4.3 Information-Geometric View of Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Parameter Space Theta] -->|Mapping via p of X given Theta| M[Manifold of Distributions]
    M -->|Fisher Metric| G[Fisher Information Matrix J]
    G -->|CRLB Inverse| V[Minimum Achievable Covariance]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in KTU exams:** When asked to "draw the block diagram of an estimation system," always include the **parameter $\rightarrow$ channel/system $\rightarrow$ observation $\rightarrow$ estimator $\rightarrow$ estimate** chain. Examiners award full marks only when the *estimator block* is explicitly shown as a function $g(\mathbf{x})$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define estimation theory. Differentiate between estimation and detection with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Estimation theory is a statistical framework that deals with inferring the value of an unknown parameter $\theta$ from noisy observations $\mathbf{x}$, by constructing an estimator $\hat{\theta} = g(\mathbf{x})$.
- **Difference (1.5 marks):**
   - *Estimation* outputs a numerical value (e.g., estimating the frequency of a sinusoid).
   - *Detection* outputs a decision between hypotheses (e.g., deciding whether a particular frequency is present or absent in noise).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the terms: (i) Bias, (ii) Consistency, (iii) Efficiency of an estimator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks ŌĆö 1 mark each):**
1. **Bias:** $b(\theta) = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$. Estimator is unbiased if $b(\theta) = 0$.
2. **Consistency:** $\hat{\theta}_n \xrightarrow{P} \theta$ as $n \to \infty$, i.e., the estimator converges to the true value with more data.
3. **Efficiency:** An unbiased estimator is efficient if its variance equals the CRLB: $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) = \dfrac{1}{\mathcal{I}(\theta)}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the mathematical model of the parameter estimation problem with a neat block diagram. Discuss the roles of parameter space, observation space, and estimator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Cram├®r-Rao Lower Bound for an unbiased estimator. State the equality condition under which the bound is attained. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Mathematical Model of Parameter Estimation (7 marks):**

The estimation problem is defined by the triple $(\Theta, p(\mathbf{x};\theta), \mathcal{L})$:

1. **Parameter Space $\Theta$:** The set containing all admissible values of the unknown scalar/vector parameter $\theta$. (1 mark)
2. **Observation Space $\mathcal{X}$:** The space of all possible data vectors $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, \dots, x_n]^T$ drawn according to the conditional density $p(\mathbf{x};\theta)$. (1 mark)
3. **Estimator $\hat{\theta} = g(\mathbf{x})$:** A measurable function mapping $\mathcal{X} \to \Theta$. The estimator is a *rule*; the estimate is its *output*. (1 mark)
4. **Loss Function $\mathcal{L}(\theta, \hat{\theta})$:** Quantifies the penalty of estimating $\theta$ by $\hat{\theta}$. Common choice: $\mathcal{L} = (\theta - \hat{\theta})^2$. (1 mark)
5. **Block Diagram (as in SECTION 4.1):** Parameter $\rightarrow$ System/Channel $\rightarrow$ Observation $\rightarrow$ Estimator $g(\cdot)$ $\rightarrow$ Estimate $\rightarrow$ Loss Evaluation. (2 marks)
6. **Example:** Estimating DC level $A$ of $x_i = A + w_i$, $w_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$; here $\theta = A$, $p(\mathbf{x};A) = (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-n/2} \exp\!\left(-\sum (x_i - A)^2 / 2\sigma^2\right)$. (1 mark)

**(b) Derivation of CRLB (7 marks):**

Step-by-step derivation as in **SECTION 3.1** above. Key checkpoints:

- Begin with $E[\hat{\theta}] = \int \hat{\theta}(\mathbf{x})\,p(\mathbf{x};\theta)\,d\mathbf{x}$. (1 mark)
- Differentiate w.r.t. $\theta$, use $\partial p / \partial \theta = p \cdot \partial \ln p / \partial \theta$. (1 mark)
- Use the identity $E[\partial \ln p / \partial \theta] = 0$ and unbiasedness ($E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$). (1 mark)
- Obtain $1 = E[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)(\partial \ln p / \partial \theta)]$. (1 mark)
- Apply Cauchy-Schwarz inequality. (1 mark)
- Recognize Fisher Information $\mathcal{I}(\theta) = E[(\partial \ln p / \partial \theta)^2]$. (1 mark)
- Conclude $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq 1 / \mathcal{I}(\theta)$, with equality iff $\partial \ln p / \partial \theta = \mathcal{I}(\theta)(\hat{\theta} - \theta)$. (1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the various types of estimators ŌĆö MLE, MAP, MMSE, and LS ŌĆö highlighting their assumptions, derivations, and inter-relationships. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given $n$ i.i.d. observations from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma^2$ known, derive the MLE of $\mu$. Show that the estimator is unbiased and efficient. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Types of Estimators (7 marks):**

1. **MLE (Maximum Likelihood) (2 marks):**
   - $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_\theta \, p(\mathbf{x};\theta)$
   - No prior required; classical approach.
   - Invariant under reparameterization.

2. **MAP (Maximum A Posteriori) (2 marks):**
   - $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_\theta \, p(\mathbf{x}\vert\theta)\,p(\theta)$
   - Requires prior $p(\theta)$; reduces to MLE if prior is uniform.

3. **MMSE (Minimum Mean Squared Error) (2 marks):**
   - $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MMSE}} = E[\theta \vert \mathbf{x}]$
   - Optimal under squared-error loss; minimizes Bayes risk.

4. **LS (Least Squares) (1 mark):**
   - $\hat{\theta}_{\text{LS}} = \arg\min_\theta \, \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{H}\theta \Vert^2 = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{x}$
   - Deterministic; does not require distributional assumptions.

**Inter-relationship:** For Gaussian observations with uniform/flat prior, all three (MLE, MAP, MMSE) coincide with LS.

**(b) MLE for Gaussian Mean (7 marks):**

As derived in **SECTION 3.2**:
1. Write joint density $p(\mathbf{x};\mu) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$. (1 mark)
2. Take log: $\ln p = -\dfrac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \dfrac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum(x_i - \mu)^2$. (1 mark)
3. Differentiate w.r.t. $\mu$ and set to zero: $\sum(x_i - \mu) = 0$. (1 mark)
4. Solve: $\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum x_i$. (1 mark)
5. Unbiasedness: $E[\hat{\mu}] = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum E[x_i] = \mu$. (1 mark)
6. Variance: $\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}$. Fisher Information: $\mathcal{I}(\mu) = -\dfrac{\partial^2 \ln p}{\partial \mu^2} = \dfrac{n}{\sigma^2}$. (1 mark)
7. Since $\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}) = \dfrac{1}{\mathcal{I}(\mu)} = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}$, the MLE is **efficient** (attains CRLB). (1 mark)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing "estimator" with "estimate":** Estimator = function $g(\mathbf{x})$; estimate = numerical value. Examiners deduct 0.5ŌĆō1 mark for interchange.
> 2. **Missing the regularity conditions** when deriving CRLB (differentiability under the integral sign, support of $p(\mathbf{x};\theta)$ independent of $\theta$). Always state them explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting the Jacobian** when changing variables in MAP/MLE derivations. If you reparametrize, multiply by $\vert \partial \theta / \partial \phi \vert$.
> 4. **Omitting the equality condition** of CRLB. Examiners often give 1 mark just for stating that equality holds iff $\partial \ln p / \partial \theta = \mathcal{I}(\theta)(\hat{\theta} - \theta)$.
> 5. **In Bayesian problems:** Forgetting to normalize by $p(\mathbf{x})$ when computing posteriors.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Estimation theory** Ōēł inferring unknown parameters from noisy data using a function $\hat{\theta} = g(\mathbf{x})$.
- **Three estimator families:** Classical (MLE, MoM, LS), Bayesian (MMSE, MAP), and Linear (BLUE).
- **MLE** maximizes likelihood; **MAP** maximizes posterior; **MMSE** minimizes squared-error Bayes risk.
- **Unbiased:** $E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$; **Efficient:** attains CRLB; **Consistent:** converges as $n \to \infty$.
- **CRLB** provides a *lower bound* on estimator variance: $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq 1/\mathcal{I}(\theta)$.
- **Fisher Information** $\mathcal{I}(\theta) = -E[\partial^2 \ln p / \partial \theta^2]$ quantifies how much information $\mathbf{x}$ carries about $\theta$.
- **Equality in CRLB** Ōćö estimator is a *sufficient statistic* of the form $\partial \ln p / \partial \theta = \mathcal{I}(\theta)(\hat{\theta} - \theta)$.
- For **i.i.d. Gaussian observations**, MLE of mean = sample mean = MMSE = MAP (uniform prior) and is efficient.
- **Block diagram** of estimation must always include: Parameter $\to$ System $\to$ Observation $\to$ Estimator $\to$ Estimate.
- Always state **regularity conditions** before any CRLB derivation for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Mathematical Estimation Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Mathematical Estimation Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Estimation and Detection Theory** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, the **Mathematical Estimation Problem** is formally defined as the procedure of inferring the value of an unknown parameter (or a set of parameters) of a probabilistic system from a finite set of noisy, incomplete, or corrupted observations.

Mathematically, the problem is stated as follows:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Formal Statement:**
> Given a set of observations $\mathbf{x} = [x[0], x[1], \dots, x[N-1]]^T$ whose joint probability density function (or mass function) $p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})$ depends on an unknown parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \boldsymbol{\Theta}$, the estimation problem is to construct a function $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = g(\mathbf{x})$ ŌĆö called the **estimator** ŌĆö that maps the observation space to the parameter space in an optimal manner.

Where:
- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X}$ is the **observation vector** (data).
- $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \boldsymbol{\Theta}$ is the **parameter vector** (the unknown we want to estimate).
- $\boldsymbol{\Theta}$ is the **parameter space** (set of all admissible values).
- $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ is the **estimate** (the realized value of the estimator for a given dataset).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a doctor trying to determine the **exact body temperature** of a patient using a digital thermometer. The thermometer does not give you the true temperature directly ŌĆö it gives you a *noisy reading* (slightly off due to sensor error, placement, body heat conduction, etc.).

| Real-World Object | Estimation-Theory Equivalent |
|-------------------|------------------------------|
| Patient's true body temperature | Unknown parameter $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ |
| Thermometer reading | Observation $x[n]$ |
| Multiple readings taken over time | Observation vector $\mathbf{x}$ |
| The rule you use to "best guess" the temperature | Estimator $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = g(\mathbf{x})$ |
| The actual number you finally announce | Estimate $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ |
| Small error tolerance you allow | Performance metric (e.g., MSE) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Board Exam:** The estimation problem is essentially the inverse of a probability problem. In probability, we know $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ and predict $\mathbf{x}$; in estimation, we observe $\mathbf{x}$ and infer $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

For the estimation problem, the following **bolded quantities** form the standard framework:

- **Bias:** $\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$
- **Variance:** $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) = E[(\hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}])^2]$
- **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** $\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = E[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2]$
- **Cramer-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB):** The theoretical minimum achievable variance for any unbiased estimator.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breaking Down the Mathematical Estimation Problem

The estimation problem is not a single equation ŌĆö it is a structured pipeline. Below is the logical breakdown expected by KTU examiners:

### Step 1: Define the Probabilistic Model
Choose a parametric statistical model that describes how the observation $\mathbf{x}$ is generated.

$$
\mathbf{x} \sim p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})
$$

### Step 2: Identify the Parameter Space $\boldsymbol{\Theta}$
Specify whether $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is:
- **Scalar** ($\theta \in \mathbb{R}$) or **Vector** ($\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^p$)
- **Continuous** or **Discrete**
- **Known range** (e.g., $0 \leq \theta \leq 1$) or **Unbounded** (e.g., $\theta \in \mathbb{R}$)

### Step 3: Choose the Estimation Philosophy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 demands students distinguish between the **three main estimation paradigms**:

| Paradigm | Core Idea | Key Output |
|----------|-----------|------------|
| **Classical (Frequentist)** | $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is a fixed but unknown constant. Use only the data. | Point estimate $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ |
| **Bayesian** | $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is a random variable with a prior $p(\boldsymbol{\theta})$. Combine prior with likelihood. | Posterior $p(\boldsymbol{\theta}\vert\mathbf{x})$ |
| **Maximum Likelihood (ML)** | Find $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ that makes the observed data most probable. | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{ML} = \arg\max_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})$ |

### Step 4: Define the Optimality Criterion
Choose a cost/loss function $C(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}})$ to minimize.

### Step 5: Construct the Estimator
Apply the chosen criterion to derive the estimator formula.

### Step 6: Evaluate Performance
Compute **Bias**, **Variance**, **MSE**, and compare against bounds like the CRLB.

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (The "Why")

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Model:** Without a model, there is no mathematical handle on the data.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Parameter Space:** Determines whether you can use calculus, convex optimization, or combinatorial search.
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Philosophy:** Determines the entire toolkit (Frequentist ŌåÆ MVUE, CRLB; Bayesian ŌåÆ MMSE, MAP).
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Criterion:** Defines what "optimal" means for your specific problem.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Construction:** The actual mathematical derivation.
- **Step 6 ŌĆö Evaluation:** Tells you if your estimator is *good enough*.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table for the KTU ESE ŌĆö these formulas appear in nearly every Part B question.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | Observation Model | $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{h}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) + \mathbf{w}$ | $\mathbf{w}$ is noise vector |
| 2 | Likelihood Function | $L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})$ | Function of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ for fixed $\mathbf{x}$ |
| 3 | Log-Likelihood | $\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \ln p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})$ | Used to simplify products ŌåÆ sums |
| 4 | ML Estimate | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{ML} = \arg\max_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} L(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ | Global maximum over $\boldsymbol{\Theta}$ |
| 5 | Bias | $b(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}) = E[\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}] - \boldsymbol{\theta}$ | **Zero** for unbiased estimator |
| 6 | Variance | $\text{Var}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}) = E[(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} - E[\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}])^2]$ | Always $\geq 0$ |
| 7 | MSE | $\text{MSE} = \text{Var}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}) + b^2(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}})$ | Scalar for scalar $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ |
| 8 | Cramer-Rao Lower Bound | $\text{Var}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}) \geq \frac{1}{-E\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \theta^2}\right]}$ | Holds for unbiased estimators |
| 9 | Fisher Information | $I(\theta) = -E\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$ | Measures info about $\theta$ in $\mathbf{x}$ |
| 10 | MMSE Estimate (Bayesian) | $\hat{\theta}_{MMSE} = E[\theta\vert\mathbf{x}] = \int \theta \, p(\theta\vert\mathbf{x}) \, d\theta$ | Posterior mean |
| 11 | MAP Estimate (Bayesian) | $\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} p(\boldsymbol{\theta}\vert\mathbf{x})$ | Maximum of posterior |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Never use the vertical pipe `\vert` inside the table cells for absolute value notation** ŌĆö KTU board examiners use strict markdown parsers; use `\mid` or `\vert` to avoid table-breaking.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Radar & Sonar:** Estimating range, velocity, and angle of a target from reflected signals in noise.
- **Communications:** Channel estimation in OFDM, equalizer coefficient estimation.
- **GPS & Navigation:** Estimating position from noisy satellite time-of-arrival measurements.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** Estimating heart rate from ECG in the presence of muscle noise.
- **Control Systems:** Estimating the state of a dynamic system via Kalman filters (a recursive MMSE estimator).
- **Machine Learning:** Linear/polynomial regression is fundamentally an estimation problem (least squares = ML under Gaussian noise).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Derivation 1: MSE Decomposition (BiasŌĆōVariance Tradeoff)

The KTU ESE frequently asks students to *prove* that the MSE decomposes into variance plus squared bias. We present the full, exhaustive derivation.

**Statement to Prove:**

$$
\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = E\left[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2\right] = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) + \left[\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta})\right]^2
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the MSE definition and add/subtract the mean $E[\hat{\theta}]$ inside the square.

$$
\text{MSE} = E\left[\left(\hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}] + E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta\right)^2\right]
$$

**Step 2:** Let $A = \hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}]$ and $B = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$.

The expression becomes $E[(A + B)^2]$.

**Step 3:** Expand using $(A + B)^2 = A^2 + 2AB + B^2$.

$$
\text{MSE} = E[A^2] + 2E[AB] + E[B^2]
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify $E[AB]$. Since $B = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$ is a constant (for fixed $\theta$ and fixed expectation),

$$
E[AB] = B \cdot E[A] = (E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta) \cdot E[\hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}]]
$$

**Step 5:** Since $E[\hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}]] = 0$ by definition of expectation,

$$
E[AB] = 0
$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the remaining terms.

- $E[A^2] = E[(\hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}])^2] = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta})$
- $E[B^2] = (E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)^2 = [\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta})]^2$

**Step 7:** Combine the results.

$$
\boxed{\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) + \left[\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta})\right]^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion for KTU:** This decomposition shows that even an unbiased estimator ($b=0$) can have a *large* MSE if its variance is large, and a low-variance estimator can have large MSE if it is biased. The CRLB bounds the variance term; for biased estimators, a similar bound (Barankin bound) applies to MSE.

---

## 3.2 Canonical Derivation 2: ML Estimation for the DC Level in Gaussian Noise

This is the **flagship KTU 2024 Module 1 problem** ŌĆö the DC signal in AWGN model.

### Problem Statement

We observe $N$ samples:

$$
x[n] = A + w[n], \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1
$$

where $A$ is the unknown deterministic constant (DC level) and $w[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ are i.i.d. Gaussian noise samples. Find the ML estimate $\hat{A}_{ML}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Write the joint PDF of the observations.

Since the samples are i.i.d., the joint PDF is the product of individual PDFs.

$$
p(\mathbf{x};A) = \prod_{n=0}^{N-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x[n] - A)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Form the likelihood function.

$$
L(A) = p(\mathbf{x};A) = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\right)^N \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n] - A)^2\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Take the natural logarithm to get the log-likelihood.

$$
\ell(A) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n] - A)^2
$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate $\ell(A)$ with respect to $A$ and set to zero.

$$
\frac{d\ell(A)}{dA} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n] - A) = 0
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $A$.

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] - N A = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\hat{A}_{ML} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The ML estimate of the DC level in AWGN is the sample mean.** This is a KTU favorite and is also the **Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimator (MVUE)** in this case.

**Step 6 (Performance Evaluation):** Compute the mean and variance of $\hat{A}_{ML}$.

Mean:

$$
E[\hat{A}_{ML}] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}E[x[n]] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} A = A
$$

Thus $\hat{A}_{ML}$ is **unbiased**.

Variance:

$$
\text{Var}(\hat{A}_{ML}) = \frac{1}{N^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\text{Var}(x[n]) = \frac{1}{N^2} \cdot N\sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}
$$

MSE (since unbiased):

$$
\text{MSE}(\hat{A}_{ML}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** As $N \to \infty$, $\text{MSE} \to 0$, meaning we can recover $A$ exactly in the limit. This is the **Consistency** property of the ML estimator.

---

## 3.3 Canonical Derivation 3: Cramer-Rao Lower Bound for the DC Level

**Step 1:** Write the log-likelihood.

$$
\ell(A) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n] - A)^2
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the first derivative.

$$
\frac{\partial \ell(A)}{\partial A} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n] - A)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the second derivative.

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \ell(A)}{\partial A^2} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(-1) = -\frac{N}{\sigma^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the Fisher Information.

$$
I(A) = -E\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ell(A)}{\partial A^2}\right] = \frac{N}{\sigma^2}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the CRLB.

$$
\text{Var}(\hat{A}) \geq \frac{1}{I(A)} = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}
$$

**Step 6:** Compare with the variance of $\hat{A}_{ML}$.

We computed $\text{Var}(\hat{A}_{ML}) = \sigma^2 / N$, which **exactly equals the CRLB**. Therefore, the sample mean is an **efficient estimator** ŌĆö it achieves the theoretical lower bound.

$$
\boxed{\hat{A}_{ML} \text{ is MVUE for the DC level in AWGN.}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Estimator | Formula | Bias | Variance | Achieves CRLB? |
|-----------|---------|------|----------|---------------|
| Sample Mean (ML for DC) | $\hat{A} = \frac{1}{N}\sum x[n]$ | 0 | $\sigma^2/N$ | **Yes (Efficient)** |
| First Sample ($x[0]$) | $\hat{A} = x[0]$ | 0 | $\sigma^2$ | No (wastes data) |
| Median | $\hat{A} = \text{median}(\mathbf{x})$ | 0 | $\approx 1.57\sigma^2/N$ (Gaussian) | No (suboptimal) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Estimation Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram captures the complete functional flow of solving a Mathematical Estimation Problem ŌĆö from physical observation to the final estimate and its evaluation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real World Physical Phenomenon] --> B[Probabilistic Model p of x given theta]
    B --> C[Define Parameter Space Theta]
    C --> D{Choose Estimation Paradigm}
    D -- Classical --> E1[Maximum Likelihood ML]
    D -- Classical --> E2[Method of Moments MoM]
    D -- Classical --> E3[Best Linear Unbiased Estimator BLUE]
    D -- Bayesian --> E4[MMSE Posterior Mean]
    D -- Bayesian --> E5[MAP Maximum A Posteriori]
    D -- Bayesian --> E6[ML becomes MAP if prior is uniform]
    E1 --> F[Construct Estimator Function g of x]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    E6 --> F
    F --> G[Apply to Observed Data x]
    G --> H[Realized Estimate theta hat]
    H --> I[Performance Evaluation]
    I --> J1[Compute Bias]
    I --> J2[Compute Variance]
    I --> J3[Compute MSE]
    I --> J4[Compute CRLB]
    J1 --> K{Efficient Estimator?}
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    J4 --> K
    K -- Yes --> L[Optimal Solution]
    K -- No --> M[Iterate via Modified Estimator]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: The Inverse Probability Concept

This diagram illustrates the foundational duality between **probability** and **estimation**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Known: Parameter theta] -- Forward Problem --> P2[Predict Observation x using p of x given theta]
    P2 --> P3[Probability Theory Direction]
    E1[Observed: Data x] -- Inverse Problem --> E2[Infer Parameter theta using p of theta given x]
    E2 --> E3[Estimation Theory Direction]
    P3 -.Bayes Theorem.-> E3
    E3 -.Bayes Theorem.-> P3
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow for Estimator Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Have Observations x] --> Q1{Is Prior Knowledge of theta Available?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is Prior Distribution Known?}
    Q1 -- No --> CL[Go Classical Route]
    Q2 -- Yes --> B[Bayesian MMSE or MAP]
    Q2 -- Uniform --> ML[ML is Equivalent to MAP]
    CL --> Q3{Is the PDF Differentiable?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> ML2[Use Maximum Likelihood]
    Q3 -- No --> MM[Use Method of Moments]
    ML --> EV[Evaluate Bias Variance MSE]
    ML2 --> EV
    MM --> EV
    B --> EV
    EV --> CR[Compare to CRLB]
    CR --> END[Final Report]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs in the above diagrams are alphanumeric (e.g., `E1`, `Q1`, `CR`) with no reserved keyword conflicts. All labels use plain uppercase text without bold or italic markdown tags to ensure clean rendering.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`
**Define the term "estimator" in the context of the mathematical estimation problem. How is it different from an "estimate"?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) An **estimator** is a *function* or *rule* $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = g(\mathbf{x})$ that maps the observation space to the parameter space. It is a formula or algorithm.
- (1 Mark) An **estimate** is the *numerical value* obtained when the estimator is evaluated on a specific dataset. It is a single number (or vector).
- (1 Mark) Example: For DC level in AWGN, $\hat{A} = \frac{1}{N}\sum x[n]$ is the estimator; if $\mathbf{x} = [1.1, 0.9, 1.0, 0.95, 1.05]^T$, then $\hat{A} = 1.0$ is the estimate.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**State and briefly explain the Cramer-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) for an unbiased estimator.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) **Statement:** For any unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of a scalar parameter $\theta$, the variance is bounded below by $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{I(\theta)}$, where $I(\theta)$ is the Fisher Information.
- (1 Mark) **Fisher Information:** $I(\theta) = -E\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln p(\mathbf{x};\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$.
- (1 Mark) **Significance:** An estimator that achieves equality is called **efficient**. The CRLB provides a benchmark to assess how close an estimator is to the theoretical optimum.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Internal Choice: Choose **ONE** of the following two questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Formulate the mathematical estimation problem. Clearly define the observation model, parameter space, estimator, and estimate with proper notation. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** For the observation model $x[n] = A + w[n]$, $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$, where $w[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ are i.i.d., derive the ML estimate of $A$. Show that it is unbiased and find its variance. Compare with the CRLB. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Mathematical Formulation (7 Marks):**

The Mathematical Estimation Problem is formally stated as follows.

We are given an observation vector $\mathbf{x} = [x[0], x[1], \dots, x[N-1]]^T$ drawn from a probability distribution parameterized by an unknown vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. The goal is to construct a function that produces an "optimal" guess of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.

$$
\mathbf{x} \sim p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta}), \quad \boldsymbol{\theta} \in \boldsymbol{\Theta}
$$

**Components of the formulation:**

1. **Observation space** $\mathcal{X}$: The set of all possible data vectors.
2. **Parameter space** $\boldsymbol{\Theta}$: The set of all admissible values of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. For example, if $\theta$ is a power, $\boldsymbol{\Theta} = [0, \infty)$.
3. **Probabilistic model** $p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})$: The joint PDF (or PMF) of $\mathbf{x}$, which is known up to $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.
4. **Estimator** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = g(\mathbf{x})$: A measurable function from $\mathcal{X}$ to $\boldsymbol{\Theta}$.
5. **Estimate**: The realized value $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ for a particular observed dataset.

The choice of optimality criterion (e.g., MSE minimization, MAP, ML) completes the problem formulation.

**(b) ML Derivation for DC Level (7 Marks):**

Given $x[n] = A + w[n]$ with $w[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ i.i.d.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Likelihood:** The joint PDF is:

$$
L(A) = \prod_{n=0}^{N-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\exp\left(-\frac{(x[n]-A)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Log-likelihood:**

$$
\ell(A) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n]-A)^2
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Differentiate and equate to zero:**

$$
\frac{d\ell}{dA} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n]-A) = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\hat{A}_{ML} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Bias:**

$$
E[\hat{A}_{ML}] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}E[x[n]] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}A = A
$$

Since $E[\hat{A}_{ML}] = A$, the estimator is **unbiased** (1 Mark).

**Step 5 ŌĆö Variance:**

$$
\text{Var}(\hat{A}_{ML}) = \frac{1}{N^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\text{Var}(x[n]) = \frac{N\sigma^2}{N^2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö CRLB Calculation:**

$$
I(A) = -E\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ell}{\partial A^2}\right] = -E\left[-\frac{N}{\sigma^2}\right] = \frac{N}{\sigma^2}
$$

$$
\text{CRLB} = \frac{1}{I(A)} = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Comparison:** $\text{Var}(\hat{A}_{ML}) = \text{CRLB} = \sigma^2/N$. Hence $\hat{A}_{ML}$ is **efficient**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the bias-variance decomposition of the Mean Squared Error (MSE). Why is this decomposition fundamental in estimation theory? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A random variable $X$ has a uniform distribution over $[\theta, \theta+1]$, where $\theta$ is unknown. Given $N$ i.i.d. observations $x[0], x[1], \dots, x[N-1]$, derive the ML estimate of $\theta$. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Bias-Variance Decomposition (7 Marks):**

For a scalar estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of $\theta$, the MSE is defined as:

$$
\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = E\left[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2\right]
$$

**Derivation (5 sub-steps):**

We write:

$$
\text{MSE} = E\left[(\hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}] + E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta)^2\right]
$$

Letting $\mu = E[\hat{\theta}]$ and $b = \mu - \theta$ (bias):

$$
\text{MSE} = E[(\hat{\theta}-\mu)^2] + 2b \cdot E[\hat{\theta}-\mu] + b^2
$$

Since $E[\hat{\theta}-\mu] = 0$:

$$
\boxed{\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) + b^2(\hat{\theta})}
$$

**Importance in estimation theory (2 Marks):**
1. It provides a **composite metric** that captures both accuracy (bias) and precision (variance) in one scalar.
2. It allows the designer to **trade off** bias for variance (e.g., a slightly biased estimator with much lower variance may have lower MSE than an unbiased but high-variance estimator).
3. It serves as the basis for defining **optimal estimators** like the MMSE estimator in the Bayesian framework.

**(b) ML for Uniform $[\theta, \theta+1]$ (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the PDF of a single observation:**

$$
p(x;\theta) = \begin{cases} 1, & \theta \leq x \leq \theta+1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Likelihood for $N$ i.i.d. samples:**

$$
L(\theta) = \prod_{n=0}^{N-1} p(x[n];\theta) = 1 \quad \text{if } \theta \leq x[n] \leq \theta+1 \; \forall n
$$

Otherwise $L(\theta) = 0$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Reformulate the constraint:** For all observations to lie in $[\theta, \theta+1]$, we need:

$$
\theta \leq \min_n x[n] \quad \text{and} \quad \theta+1 \geq \max_n x[n]
$$

That is:

$$
\max_n x[n] - 1 \leq \theta \leq \min_n x[n]
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Maximize the likelihood:** Since $L(\theta) = 1$ over the entire feasible region and $0$ outside, the likelihood is *flat*. However, the most common interpretation is that any value in the interval is a valid ML estimate. If we impose uniqueness via the principle that $\theta$ should be the smallest feasible value (so the interval $[\theta, \theta+1]$ is "as far right" as possible):

$$
\boxed{\hat{\theta}_{ML} = \min_n x[n]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** Some texts define $\hat{\theta}_{ML} = \min_n x[n] - 1$ depending on parameterization. KTU accepts either consistent with the given PDF definition.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing estimator with estimate** ŌĆö A 1-mark penalty is typical. Always state: estimator = formula; estimate = number.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify the second derivative** for ML ŌĆö Examiners expect $\frac{d^2\ell}{d\theta^2} < 0$ at the optimum to confirm it is a maximum, not a minimum or saddle.
> 3. **Skipping the bias check** ŌĆö Even if you derive $\hat{\theta}_{ML}$, you must compute $E[\hat{\theta}_{ML}]$ and show it equals $\theta$ to claim unbiasedness.
> 4. **Mixing CRLB with variance** ŌĆö CRLB is a *lower bound*, not the actual variance of an arbitrary estimator. Always state "$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \text{CRLB}$".
> 5. **Wrong Fisher Information sign** ŌĆö The Fisher Information is $-E[\frac{\partial^2 \ln L}{\partial \theta^2}]$, not $+E[\cdot]$. Sign error = 0 marks for that part.
> 6. **Assuming prior in classical estimation** ŌĆö ML and MVUE are *frequentist*; do not introduce $p(\theta)$.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Estimation problem** = infer $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ from noisy $\mathbf{x}$.
- [x] **Estimator** = function $g(\mathbf{x})$; **Estimate** = numerical output.
- [x] **Parameter space** $\boldsymbol{\Theta}$ defines admissible $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.
- [x] **Three paradigms**: Frequentist (ML, MVUE), Bayesian (MMSE, MAP), and the bridge case where ML ŌēĪ MAP under uniform prior.
- [x] **MSE = Variance + Bias┬▓** ŌĆö the central performance identity.
- [x] **CRLB** = $\frac{1}{I(\theta)}$, where $I(\theta) = -E\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln p}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$.
- [x] **Efficient estimator** = unbiased AND achieves CRLB.
- [x] **DC Level in AWGN**: $\hat{A}_{ML} = \frac{1}{N}\sum x[n]$, unbiased, efficient, MSE $= \sigma^2/N$.
- [x] **Likelihood vs Probability**: $p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})$ as a function of $\mathbf{x}$ is a probability; as a function of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ (for fixed $\mathbf{x}$), it is the likelihood.
- [x] **Consistency**: $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_N \to \boldsymbol{\theta}$ as $N \to \infty$ ŌĆö desirable property of ML estimators under regularity.
- [x] **Engineering applications**: Radar, communications (channel estimation), GPS, biomedical signals, Kalman filtering, regression in ML.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimator (MVUE):**
> An estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of a deterministic but unknown parameter $\theta$ is called a **Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimator (MVUE)** if it satisfies two simultaneous conditions:
> 1. **Unbiasedness:** $E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta \quad \forall \theta \in \Theta$
> 2. **Minimum Variance:** $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \leq \text{Var}(\tilde{\theta})$ for any other unbiased estimator $\tilde{\theta}$ of $\theta$, over the entire parameter space $\Theta$.

In simpler words, among **all** unbiased estimators of $\theta$, the MVUE is the one whose variance is the smallest possible. It is the *best* unbiased estimator in the minimum mean square error (MMSE) sense when restricted to the class of unbiased estimators.

## 1.2 Related Core Terminology

| Term | Mathematical Formulation | Plain English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Estimator** | $\hat{\theta} = g(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n)$ | A rule that maps observed data to a guess of $\theta$. |
| **Estimate** | $\hat{\theta}(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ | The specific numerical value produced once data is observed. |
| **Bias** | $b(\hat{\theta}) = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$ | How far, on average, the estimator is from the true value. |
| **Unbiased Estimator** | $E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$ | An estimator whose long-run average equals the true parameter. |
| **Mean Square Error (MSE)** | $\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = E[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2]$ | Combined measure of variance + squared bias. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MVUE Matters in Engineering:**
> In radar, communications, and biomedical signal processing, we often have only **finite noisy samples** to estimate a continuous physical quantity (e.g., the amplitude of a received signal, the mean noise floor, or the SNR). The MVUE guarantees the *most accurate* estimate possible without any systematic error, which is critical for high-stakes applications like target tracking and medical diagnosis.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆö The "Best Marksman" Problem:**
> Imagine 10 archers (estimators) shooting at a target whose true bullseye position $\theta$ is unknown to all of them. The **bias** tells us whether the group of arrows is systematically clustered to the left or right of the bullseye. The **variance** tells us how tightly the arrows are packed together.
> - An **unbiased** archer's arrows are, on average, *centered* on the true bullseye (no left/right drift).
> - A **minimum variance** unbiased archer's arrows are, on average, centered **AND** packed into the tightest possible cluster.
> The MVUE is therefore the *most consistent* and *most accurate* archer who never introduces systematic error.
> In engineering, the "arrows" are repeated independent trials (samples $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$), and the "archer" is the mathematical formula $\hat{\theta} = g(X)$ that produces each guess.

## 1.4 Statistical Foundation ŌĆö The GaussŌĆōMarkov Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In classical (frequentist) estimation theory, the MVUE is the **asymptotic benchmark** against which all practical estimators (ML, MAP, BLUE, Bayesian MMSE) are judged. If an MVUE exists, no other unbiased estimator can outperform it ŌĆö period. This makes MVUE the *gold standard* in unbiased estimation theory.

The flowchart below (Section 4) summarizes the systematic engineering procedure used to derive the MVUE for a given estimation problem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The BiasŌĆōVariance Decomposition

The mean square error of any estimator can always be written as:

$$\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) + \big(b(\hat{\theta})\big)^2$$

where $b(\hat{\theta}) = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$ is the bias.

- For an **unbiased** estimator, $b(\hat{\theta}) = 0$, so $\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta})$.
- Thus, **minimizing MSE within the unbiased class is identical to minimizing the variance.**

## 2.2 The Cram├®rŌĆōRao Lower Bound (CRLB)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Theorem (Cram├®rŌĆōRao):**
> For any unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of a scalar parameter $\theta$, under the *regularity conditions* (differentiability under the integral sign, etc.):
> $$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{I(\theta)} = \frac{1}{-n\, E\!\left[\dfrac{\partial^2 \ln f(X;\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]}$$
> The quantity $I(\theta)$ is called the **Fisher Information**. Equality is attained if and only if:
> $$\frac{\partial \ln f(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta} = I(\theta)\big(\hat{\theta} - \theta\big)$$

## 2.3 The General Cram├®rŌĆōRao Inequality (Biased Case)

For **any** estimator (possibly biased):

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{\left(\dfrac{\partial E[\hat{\theta}]}{\partial \theta}\right)^2}{-n\,E\!\left[\dfrac{\partial^2 \ln f(X;\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]} = \frac{\left(1 + \dfrac{\partial b}{\partial \theta}\right)^2}{I(\theta)}$$

This is the **most general form** asked in KTU derivations.

## 2.4 Efficiency of an Estimator

The **efficiency** of an unbiased estimator is defined as:

$$e(\hat{\theta}) = \frac{\text{CRLB}}{\text{Var}(\hat{\theta})} = \frac{1}{I(\theta)\,\text{Var}(\hat{\theta})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> - $e(\hat{\theta}) \in (0, 1]$ for any unbiased estimator.
> - $e(\hat{\theta}) = 1 \implies$ the estimator **attains the CRLB** and is automatically the MVUE.
> - $e(\hat{\theta}) < 1 \implies$ the estimator is sub-optimal, although it may still be the MVUE if the CRLB is unattainable.

## 2.5 Sufficient Statistics ŌĆö A Prerequisite Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (FisherŌĆōNeyman Factorization):**
> A statistic $T(X)$ is **sufficient** for $\theta$ if and only if the joint pdf/pmf can be factored as:
> $$f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n; \theta) = g\big(T(x), \theta\big)\, h(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$$
> The intuition: $T(X)$ captures **all the information** about $\theta$ present in the data ŌĆö knowing $T$ alone is as good as knowing the entire sample.

## 2.6 Complete Sufficient Statistic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Completeness):**
> A statistic $T$ is **complete** with respect to $\theta$ if:
> $$E_\theta[g(T)] = 0 \quad \forall\,\theta \in \Theta \;\implies\; g(T) \stackrel{a.s.}{=} 0$$
> No non-trivial unbiased estimator of **zero** can be constructed from $T$. Completeness is a *rare and powerful* property; it is the key that unlocks the LehmannŌĆōScheff├® theorem.

## 2.7 The LehmannŌĆōScheff├® Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (LehmannŌĆōScheff├®):**
> If $T$ is a **complete sufficient statistic** for $\theta$, then any function $g(T)$ that is unbiased for $\theta$ is the **unique** MVUE of $\theta$.

This is the **central engine** of MVUE construction in KTU examinations.

## 2.8 The RaoŌĆōBlackwell Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (RaoŌĆōBlackwell):**
> If $\hat{\theta}$ is any unbiased estimator and $T$ is a sufficient statistic, then:
> $$\hat{\theta}^* = E[\hat{\theta} \mid T]$$
> is also unbiased for $\theta$, and:
> $$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}^*) \leq \text{Var}(\hat{\theta})$$
> with strict inequality unless $\hat{\theta}$ is already a function of $T$.

> RaoŌĆōBlackwell improves any estimator; LehmannŌĆōScheff├® terminates the process when the statistic is *complete* sufficient.

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **When to Use** |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bias of an estimator | $b(\hat{\theta}) = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$ | Verify unbiasedness |
| 2 | Unbiasedness condition | $E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$ | First MVUE check |
| 3 | Mean Square Error (decomposition) | $\text{MSE} = \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) + b^2$ | Compare estimators |
| 4 | Fisher Information (1 sample) | $I(\theta) = -E\!\left[\dfrac{\partial^2 \ln f}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$ | CRLB calculation |
| 5 | Fisher Information (n samples iid) | $I_n(\theta) = n\,I(\theta)$ | For sample size $n$ |
| 6 | Cram├®rŌĆōRao Lower Bound (unbiased) | $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \dfrac{1}{I_n(\theta)}$ | Absolute floor on variance |
| 7 | Cram├®rŌĆōRao Bound (general) | $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \dfrac{\left(\partial E[\hat{\theta}]/\partial \theta\right)^2}{I_n(\theta)}$ | Biased estimators |
| 8 | Efficiency of an estimator | $e = \dfrac{\text{CRLB}}{\text{Var}(\hat{\theta})}$ | Performance metric |
| 9 | Sufficient Statistic Factorization | $f(x;\theta) = g(T,\theta)\,h(x)$ | Identify sufficiency |
| 10 | RaoŌĆōBlackwell | $\text{Var}(E[\hat{\theta}\mid T]) \leq \text{Var}(\hat{\theta})$ | Variance reduction |
| 11 | LehmannŌĆōScheff├® | Complete Sufficient $\Rightarrow$ MVUE | MVUE uniqueness |
| 12 | Attainability condition | $\dfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \theta} = I(\theta)(\hat{\theta} - \theta)$ | Equality in CRLB |

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where MVUE is used in KTU-relevant industries:**
> - **Wireless Communications:** Estimation of channel gain $h$ from pilot symbols (used in 5G/6G receiver design).
> - **Radar & Sonar:** Estimation of target range and Doppler shift from reflected signals.
> - **Speech Processing:** Estimation of the spectral envelope of voiced sounds.
> - **Biomedical Imaging:** Estimation of blood velocity from Doppler ultrasound measurements.
> - **Machine Learning:** The Maximum Likelihood Estimator asymptotically becomes MVUE under regularity conditions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö The Cram├®rŌĆōRao Lower Bound (CRLB)

> **Goal:** Show that for any unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of $\theta$, $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \dfrac{1}{I(\theta)}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the unbiasedness condition.**

Because $\hat{\theta}$ is unbiased, $E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$, which gives:

$$E[\hat{\theta}] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{\theta}(x)\, f(x;\theta)\, dx = \theta$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate both sides with respect to $\theta$.**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{\theta}(x)\, \frac{\partial f(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\, dx = 1$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Rewrite the derivative using a logarithmic trick.**

Since $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial \theta} = f \cdot \dfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \theta}$, we get:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{\theta}(x)\, f(x;\theta)\, \frac{\partial \ln f(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\, dx = 1$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Express the right-hand side as an expectation.**

We know that $\int f(x;\theta)\, dx = 1$, so:

$$1 = \int f(x;\theta)\, dx = \int 1 \cdot f(x;\theta)\, dx = E[1]$$

Therefore:

$$E\!\left[\hat{\theta}\, \frac{\partial \ln f(X;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right] = E[1]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Subtract to form a covariance-like structure.**

$$E\!\left[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)\, \frac{\partial \ln f(X;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right] = 1$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply the CauchyŌĆōSchwarz inequality.**

For any two random variables $A$ and $B$, the CauchyŌĆōSchwarz inequality states:

$$\big(E[AB]\big)^2 \leq E[A^2]\, E[B^2]$$

Apply with $A = \hat{\theta} - \theta$ and $B = \dfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \theta}$:

$$\big(E[(\hat{\theta}-\theta) \cdot \tfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \theta}]\big)^2 \leq E[(\hat{\theta}-\theta)^2]\; E\!\left[\big(\tfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \theta}\big)^2\right]$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Substitute the known left-hand side.**

$$\big(1\big)^2 \leq \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \cdot E\!\left[\big(\tfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \theta}\big)^2\right]$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Use the identity for Fisher Information.**

Under regularity conditions:

$$I(\theta) = E\!\left[\big(\tfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \theta}\big)^2\right] = -E\!\left[\tfrac{\partial^2 \ln f}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Conclude the CRLB.**

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{I(\theta)}}$$

**Step 10 ŌĆö Equality condition.**

CauchyŌĆōSchwarz attains equality iff $A$ and $B$ are linearly related, i.e.:

$$\frac{\partial \ln f(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta} = I(\theta)\,\big(\hat{\theta} - \theta\big)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö CRLB for $n$ i.i.d. Samples

> **Goal:** Generalize the bound when $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ are i.i.d. from $f(x;\theta)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the joint log-likelihood.**

$$\ln L(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln f(x_i;\theta)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate twice.**

$$\frac{\partial^2 \ln L}{\partial \theta^2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\partial^2 \ln f(x_i;\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Take expectation and apply linearity.**

$$-E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln L}{\partial \theta^2}\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(-E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln f(x_i;\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]\right) = n\, I(\theta)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute into the CRLB formula.**

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{n\, I(\theta)}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Doubling the sample size $n$ does **not** halve the variance ŌĆö it *quarters* it. This $1/n$ scaling is a fundamental law of statistical estimation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö CRLB for the Biased Case

> **Goal:** Prove $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \dfrac{\left(1 + \partial b/\partial\theta\right)^2}{I_n(\theta)}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express the expectation of the estimator.**

Let $g(\theta) = E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta + b(\theta)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate $g(\theta)$ w.r.t. $\theta$.**

$$\frac{\partial g}{\partial \theta} = 1 + \frac{\partial b}{\partial \theta}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Repeat the CauchyŌĆōSchwarz argument of Derivation 1 with this $g(\theta)$.**

By identical logic, replacing $\theta$ with $g(\theta)$:

$$\big(E[(\hat{\theta} - g) \cdot \tfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \theta}]\big)^2 \leq \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \cdot I_n(\theta)$$

The left-hand side equals $\left(\dfrac{\partial g}{\partial \theta}\right)^2$ by Step 1 of Derivation 1.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute and solve.**

$$\left(1 + \frac{\partial b}{\partial \theta}\right)^2 \leq \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \cdot I_n(\theta)$$

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{\left(1 + \dfrac{\partial b}{\partial \theta}\right)^2}{I_n(\theta)}}$$

> **For unbiased estimators**, $b(\theta) = 0 \Rightarrow \partial b/\partial\theta = 0$, and the bound reduces to $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq 1/I_n(\theta)$. Ō£ō

---

## 3.4 Worked Example ŌĆö MVUE for the Mean of a Normal Distribution

**Problem:** Let $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)$ where $\sigma^2$ is known. Find the MVUE of $\theta$ and verify it attains the CRLB.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify a sufficient statistic.**

The joint pdf is:

$$f(\mathbf{x};\theta) = (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-n/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \theta)^2\right)$$

Expanding the square:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \theta)^2 = \sum x_i^2 - 2\theta \sum x_i + n\theta^2$$

The factorization theorem gives $T = \sum X_i$ (equivalently, $\bar{X}$) as a sufficient statistic.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Check completeness.**

For a normal family with known variance, the family of distributions of $T = \sum X_i$ is the **exponential family with natural parameter** $\theta/\sigma^2$, which is **complete** (a well-known KTU result).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Construct an unbiased estimator of $\theta$.**

Consider $\hat{\theta}_0 = X_1$. Then $E[X_1] = \theta$, so $\hat{\theta}_0$ is unbiased.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply RaoŌĆōBlackwell.**

$$\hat{\theta}^* = E[X_1 \mid T] = E[X_1 \mid \textstyle\sum X_i] = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i = \bar{X}$$

(The symmetry of i.i.d. samples makes the conditional expectation equal to the mean.)

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply LehmannŌĆōScheff├®.**

Since $T$ is **complete sufficient** and $\bar{X}$ is unbiased (as $E[\bar{X}] = \theta$), $\bar{X}$ is the **unique MVUE**.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute the CRLB.**

$$\ln f(x;\theta) = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{(x-\theta)^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 \ln f}{\partial \theta^2} = -\frac{1}{\sigma^2}$$

$$I(\theta) = -E[-\sigma^{-2}] = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}$$

$$\text{CRLB} = \frac{1}{n/\sigma^2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Variance of the MVUE.**

$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Conclusion.**

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \text{CRLB} \implies \text{Efficiency} = 1 \implies \bar{X} \text{ attains the CRLB and is the MVUE.}}$$

> **Engineering Takeaway:** The **sample mean** is mathematically the *best possible unbiased estimator* of a normal mean ŌĆö no other unbiased estimator can beat it, no matter how cleverly designed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö The MVUE Construction Pipeline

The diagram below shows the **complete engineering workflow** used to construct and verify the MVUE for any given estimation problem. This is the algorithm examiners expect you to follow during KTU valuation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Observed Samples X1, X2, ... Xn] --> B[Step 1: Write Joint pdf pmf f of X1..Xn given theta]
    B --> C[Step 2: Apply Fisher Neyman Factorization Theorem]
    C --> D{Is T a Sufficient Statistic?}
    D -- No --> E[Revisit joint pdf and try another statistic]
    E --> C
    D -- Yes --> F[Step 3: Check if T is COMPLETE]
    F --> G{Is T Complete Sufficient?}
    G -- No --> H[Look for Complete Sufficient Statistic via Minimal Sufficient approach]
    H --> F
    G -- Yes --> I[Step 4: Construct any unbiased estimator theta hat zero]
    I --> J[Step 5: Apply Rao Blackwell: theta star = E theta hat zero given T]
    J --> K[Step 6: Verify unbiasedness E theta star equals theta]
    K --> L[Step 7: Apply Lehmann Scheffe Theorem]
    L --> M[theta star is the UNIQUE MVUE of theta]
    M --> N[Step 8: Compute CRLB = 1 over n I theta]
    N --> O{Variance of MVUE equals CRLB?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Attains CRLB: Efficient Estimator. Efficiency = 1]
    O -- No --> Q[Sub optimal but still MVUE. Efficiency less than 1]
    P --> R[END: MVUE Verified]
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Hierarchy ŌĆö Estimator Quality Classes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[All Estimators of theta] --> B[Unbiased Estimators]
    A --> C[Biased Estimators]
    B --> D[Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimator MVUE]
    D --> E[Efficient Estimator: attains CRLB]
    B --> F[Non MVUE Unbiased Estimators]
    C --> G[Bayesian Estimators MMSE MAP]
    C --> H[Maximum Likelihood Estimator MLE]
    E --> I[Optimal Estimator: zero bias + min variance + attains CRLB]
    H -. asymptotic limit .-> E
    G -. related via risk function .-> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The MVUE sits in the **intersection** of the unbiased class and the minimum-variance class.
> - An **efficient estimator** is the strongest category ŌĆö it is unbiased, minimum variance, *and* achieves the CRLB.
> - The **MLE asymptotically** (as $n \to \infty$) joins the efficient class under regularity conditions (this is the BernsteinŌĆōvon Mises theorem).

## 4.3 CRLB Variance-Bound Visualization (Text Schematic)

For an exponential family with Fisher information $I(\theta) = 1/\sigma^2$ and sample size $n$:

| Sample Size $n$ | CRLB $\sigma^2/n$ | Engineering Interpretation |
|:---:|:---:|---|
| 1 | $\sigma^2$ | No averaging benefit. |
| 4 | $\sigma^2/4$ | Variance cut to one-quarter. |
| 16 | $\sigma^2/16$ | Variance cut to one-sixteenth. |
| 100 | $\sigma^2/100$ | Standard error reduced 10├Ś. |

> **Practical Takeaway for KTU Lab Courses:** In real DSP and communication labs, doubling the number of pilot symbols (samples) reduces the channel estimator's variance by a factor of **2**, not 2├Ś. This is why high-accuracy estimators (e.g., 5G channel estimation) use long training sequences.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**Define Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimator (MVUE). What are the two key properties an estimator must satisfy to qualify as an MVUE?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of a parameter $\theta$ is called a **Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimator (MVUE)** if it satisfies:
> 1. **Unbiasedness:** $E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$ for all values of $\theta \in \Theta$.
> 2. **Minimum Variance:** Among all unbiased estimators of $\theta$, $\hat{\theta}$ has the smallest variance, i.e., $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \leq \text{Var}(\tilde{\theta})$ for every other unbiased estimator $\tilde{\theta}$.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**
**State the Cram├®rŌĆōRao Lower Bound (CRLB) for an unbiased estimator of a scalar parameter $\theta$ from $n$ i.i.d. samples. Define the Fisher Information $I(\theta)$ appearing in the bound.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The CRLB states that for any unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of a scalar parameter $\theta$ based on $n$ i.i.d. samples:
> $$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{n\, I(\theta)}$$
> where the **Fisher Information** is:
> $$I(\theta) = -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln f(X;\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right] = E\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial \ln f(X;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right)^2\right]$$
> under the regularity condition that differentiation under the integral sign is permitted.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the Cram├®rŌĆōRao Lower Bound for an unbiased estimator of a scalar parameter $\theta$. State clearly the regularity conditions and the equality condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Let $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ be i.i.d. samples from an exponential distribution with pdf $f(x;\theta) = \dfrac{1}{\theta} e^{-x/\theta}$ for $x \geq 0$, $\theta > 0$. Find the MVUE of $\theta$ and verify whether it attains the CRLB. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö State the unbiasedness condition.**

$$E[\hat{\theta}] = \int \hat{\theta}(x)\, f(x;\theta)\, dx = \theta$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate w.r.t. $\theta$ (using regularity).**

$$\int \hat{\theta}(x)\, \frac{\partial f(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\, dx = 1$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Use the log-derivative trick.**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta} = f \cdot \frac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \theta}$$

Substituting:

$$\int \hat{\theta}(x)\, f(x;\theta)\, \frac{\partial \ln f(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\, dx = 1$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Add and subtract $\theta$.**

Since $\int f(x;\theta)\, dx = 1$:

$$\int (\hat{\theta} - \theta)\, f(x;\theta)\, \frac{\partial \ln f(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\, dx = 1$$

$$\implies E\!\left[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)\, \frac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \theta}\right] = 1$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply CauchyŌĆōSchwarz.**

$$\big(E[AB]\big)^2 \leq E[A^2]\, E[B^2]$$

With $A = \hat{\theta} - \theta$ and $B = \dfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \theta}$:

$$1^2 \leq E[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2]\; E\!\left[\big(\tfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \theta}\big)^2\right]$$

$$1 \leq \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \cdot I(\theta)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Conclude.**

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{I(\theta)}}$$

**Equality condition:** $\dfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \theta} = I(\theta)(\hat{\theta} - \theta)$ (linear relationship).

**Regularity conditions:**
- The pdf $f(x;\theta)$ is twice differentiable w.r.t. $\theta$.
- Differentiation under the integral sign is valid.
- The support of $f$ does not depend on $\theta$.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find a sufficient statistic.**

Joint pdf:

$$f(\mathbf{x};\theta) = \frac{1}{\theta^n} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{\theta}\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\right)$$

By the factorization theorem, $T = \sum X_i$ is sufficient for $\theta$. The exponential family form is $\exp\!\left(-\frac{T}{\theta} - n\ln\theta\right) \cdot h(\mathbf{x})$, confirming $T$ is a **complete sufficient statistic** (exponential family with natural parameter $-1/\theta$).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct an unbiased estimator of $\theta$.**

For a single sample $X_1 \sim \text{Exp}(1/\theta)$, we have $E[X_1] = \theta$. So $\hat{\theta}_0 = X_1$ is unbiased.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply RaoŌĆōBlackwell.**

$$\hat{\theta}^* = E[X_1 \mid T]$$

By symmetry of i.i.d. samples:

$$E[X_1 \mid T] = \frac{T}{n} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i = \bar{X}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verify unbiasedness.**

$$E[\bar{X}] = \frac{1}{n}\sum E[X_i] = \frac{1}{n} \cdot n\theta = \theta \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply LehmannŌĆōScheff├®.**

Since $T$ is complete sufficient and $\bar{X}$ is unbiased, $\bar{X}$ is the **unique MVUE** of $\theta$.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute the CRLB.**

$$\ln f(x;\theta) = -\ln\theta - \frac{x}{\theta}$$

$$\frac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \theta} = -\frac{1}{\theta} + \frac{x}{\theta^2}$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 \ln f}{\partial \theta^2} = \frac{1}{\theta^2} - \frac{2x}{\theta^3}$$

$$I(\theta) = -E\!\left[\frac{1}{\theta^2} - \frac{2X}{\theta^3}\right] = -\frac{1}{\theta^2} + \frac{2\theta}{\theta^3} = \frac{1}{\theta^2}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö CRLB for $n$ samples.**

$$\text{CRLB} = \frac{1}{n/\theta^2} = \frac{\theta^2}{n}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Variance of $\bar{X}$.**

$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{1}{n^2}\sum \text{Var}(X_i) = \frac{1}{n^2} \cdot n\theta^2 = \frac{\theta^2}{n}$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Conclude.**

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \text{CRLB} = \frac{\theta^2}{n} \implies \text{Efficiency} = 1}$$

The MVUE $\bar{X}$ is also an **efficient estimator** (attains the CRLB).

---

### Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** State and prove the **RaoŌĆōBlackwell theorem**. Explain how it helps in reducing the variance of an estimator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with both $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ unknown, identify the **complete sufficient statistic** for $(\mu, \sigma^2)$ using the factorization theorem. Hence find the MVUE of $\sigma^2$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Statement of RaoŌĆōBlackwell Theorem:**

> If $\hat{\theta}$ is an unbiased estimator of $\theta$ and $T$ is a sufficient statistic for $\theta$, then:
> $$\hat{\theta}^* = E[\hat{\theta} \mid T]$$
> is also unbiased for $\theta$, and $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}^*) \leq \text{Var}(\hat{\theta})$, with strict inequality unless $\hat{\theta}$ is already a function of $T$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Unbiasedness via law of total expectation.**

$$E[\hat{\theta}^*] = E\!\left[E[\hat{\theta} \mid T]\right] = E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Variance decomposition using the law of total variance.**

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) = E\!\left[\text{Var}(\hat{\theta} \mid T)\right] + \text{Var}\!\left(E[\hat{\theta} \mid T]\right) = E\!\left[\text{Var}(\hat{\theta} \mid T)\right] + \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}^*)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Conclude.**

Since $E[\text{Var}(\hat{\theta} \mid T)] \geq 0$:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}^*)$$

Equality holds iff $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta} \mid T) = 0$ a.s., i.e., $\hat{\theta}$ is a function of $T$.

> **Engineering Significance:** RaoŌĆōBlackwell guarantees that conditioning on a sufficient statistic can only **improve** (or leave unchanged) an estimator. It is the variance-reduction workhorse of statistical signal processing (e.g., used in Kalman filter derivations).

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the joint pdf.**

$$f(\mathbf{x};\mu,\sigma^2) = (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-n/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2\right)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Expand the quadratic.**

$$\sum (x_i - \mu)^2 = \sum x_i^2 - 2\mu\sum x_i + n\mu^2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Identify $T = (\sum X_i, \sum X_i^2)$ via factorization.**

The joint pdf becomes:

$$f = (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-n/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\sum x_i^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{\mu\sum x_i}{\sigma^2} - \frac{n\mu^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

So $T_1 = \sum X_i$ and $T_2 = \sum X_i^2$ together form the sufficient statistic. The pair $T = (T_1, T_2)$ is **complete sufficient** for $(\mu, \sigma^2)$ (exponential family with natural parameter vector).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Construct an unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$.**

The MLE of $\sigma^2$ is $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2$, but it is biased:

$$E[\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}}] = \frac{n-1}{n}\sigma^2$$

A simple unbiased estimator:

$$S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2 \quad \text{with} \quad E[S^2] = \sigma^2$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Express $S^2$ as a function of the complete sufficient statistic $T$.**
