$$S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\!\left(\sum X_i^2 - \frac{(\sum X_i)^2}{n}\right) = g(T_1, T_2)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply LehmannŌĆōScheff├®.**

Since $T = (T_1, T_2)$ is complete sufficient and $S^2$ is a function of $T$ that is unbiased for $\sigma^2$:

$$\boxed{S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2 \text{ is the unique MVUE of } \sigma^2}$$

> **Engineering Note:** This is precisely the **unbiased sample variance** used in every DSP lab, oscilloscope noise-floor analysis, and statistical quality control system.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the regularity condition** before applying CRLB ŌĆö partial credit loss of **1 mark** in (a) parts.
> 2. **Confusing $I(\theta)$ with $I_n(\theta)$** ŌĆö many students write $\text{Var} \geq 1/I(\theta)$ for $n$ samples instead of $1/(nI(\theta))$. This loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the unbiasedness verification** after RaoŌĆōBlackwell ŌĆö examiners **always** check this step; missing it costs **1ŌĆō2 marks**.
> 4. **Not stating the equality condition** of CRLB ŌĆö required for full marks in derivation questions.
> 5. **Mixing up completeness with sufficiency** ŌĆö they are *related but distinct* concepts. Sufficiency is needed for RaoŌĆōBlackwell; completeness is *additionally* required for LehmannŌĆōScheff├®. Confusing them is a **favourite KTU trap**.
> 6. **Writing "MVUE" without computing CRLB** ŌĆö in KTU, MVUE problems typically ask you to **also verify** whether the MVUE attains the CRLB. Omitting this is a **3-mark penalty**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 ŌĆö MVUE):**
> - [x] **Unbiased Estimator:** $E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$.
> - [x] **MVUE:** Unbiased estimator with **minimum variance** in its class.
> - [x] **Bias-Variance Decomposition:** $\text{MSE} = \text{Var} + b^2$.
> - [x] **Fisher Information (single sample):** $I(\theta) = -E[\partial^2 \ln f/\partial \theta^2]$.
> - [x] **Fisher Information ($n$ iid samples):** $I_n(\theta) = n\,I(\theta)$.
> - [x] **CRLB (unbiased):** $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq 1/I_n(\theta)$.
> - [x] **CRLB (general, possibly biased):** $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq (1 + \partial b/\partial\theta)^2 / I_n(\theta)$.
> - [x] **Equality Condition:** $\partial \ln f / \partial \theta = I(\theta)(\hat{\theta} - \theta)$.
> - [x] **Efficient Estimator:** One that **attains the CRLB** (efficiency = 1).
> - [x] **Sufficient Statistic** (Factorization): $f(\mathbf{x};\theta) = g(T,\theta)\,h(\mathbf{x})$.
> - [x] **Complete Statistic:** $E_\theta[g(T)] = 0 \ \forall\theta \implies g(T) = 0$ a.s.
> - [x] **RaoŌĆōBlackwell:** $\text{Var}(E[\hat{\theta} \mid T]) \leq \text{Var}(\hat{\theta})$.
> - [x] **LehmannŌĆōScheff├®:** Complete Sufficient + Unbiased $\implies$ **Unique MVUE**.
> - [x] **Standard Examples:**
>   * $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$, $\sigma^2$ known: MVUE of $\mu$ is $\bar{X}$; CRLB = $\sigma^2/n$.
>   * $X \sim \text{Exp}(1/\theta)$: MVUE of $\theta$ is $\bar{X}$; CRLB = $\theta^2/n$.
>   * $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$, both unknown: MVUE of $\sigma^2$ is $S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2$.
> - [x] **Asymptotic Fact:** MLE $\xrightarrow{n\to\infty}$ MVUE under regularity (BernsteinŌĆōvon Mises).
> - [x] **Pitfall:** CRLB may be **unattainable** even when MVUE exists ŌĆö in that case MVUE has efficiency $< 1$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Cramer-Rao Lower Bound
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Cramer-Rao Lower Bound

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cram├®rŌĆōRao Lower Bound (CRLB):** The Cram├®rŌĆōRao Lower Bound establishes a *theoretical lower limit* on the variance of any **unbiased estimator** of a deterministic (but unknown) parameter. In rigorous notation, for any unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of a scalar parameter $\theta$, the variance satisfies:
> $$\text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \;\geq\; \frac{1}{I(\theta)}$$
> where $I(\theta)$ is the **Fisher Information** about $\theta$ contained in the observation. No estimator can beat this bound; an estimator whose variance equals this bound is called **efficient**.

The CRLB is a *fundamental performance benchmark* in statistical signal processing, parameter estimation, radar/sonar detection, communication receiver design, and machine learning. It tells the engineer the best achievable estimation accuracy for a given observation model ŌĆö *before* designing any estimator.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine you are trying to estimate the weight of a sack of rice using only a noisy spring scale. No matter how careful your reading is, the spring's stiffness and the inherent jitter of the needle set a *physical floor* on precision. The CRLB plays exactly this role for an estimator: it is the **"noise floor" of estimation accuracy**. Even the most clever estimator cannot surpass it because the data itself carries only a finite amount of information about the unknown parameter.

A few intuitive cornerstones:

- **More data ŌćÆ Better bound.** As the number of observations $N$ grows, the Fisher Information typically grows linearly ($I(\theta) \propto N$), and hence the CRLB shrinks as $\frac{1}{N}$ ŌĆö more data brings estimates closer to the truth.
- **Efficiency rating.** The ratio $\frac{\text{CRLB}}{\text{var}(\hat{\theta})}$ is called the **efficiency** of an estimator. It lies in $[0,1]$; an *efficient* estimator has efficiency $= 1$.
- **Why "Lower Bound"?** Because variance is always $\geq 0$, the CRLB gives the *smallest* possible value the variance can take for an unbiased estimator.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Parameter | $\theta$ | The unknown deterministic quantity to be estimated |
| Estimator | $\hat{\theta}$ | A function of the data that approximates $\theta$ |
| Bias | $b(\theta)$ | $E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$ |
| Fisher Information | $I(\theta)$ | Expected curvature of the log-likelihood |
| Score Function | $s(x;\theta)$ | $\frac{\partial \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}$ |
| Efficiency | $e(\hat{\theta})$ | $\frac{\text{CRLB}}{\text{var}(\hat{\theta})}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Score Function

For a random observation $x$ drawn from a probability density function (PDF) $p(x;\theta)$ that depends on the parameter $\theta$, the **score function** is defined as the gradient of the log-likelihood with respect to $\theta$:

$$s(x;\theta) \;\triangleq\; \frac{\partial \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}$$

**Key statistical properties of the score (under regularity):**

$$E[s(x;\theta)] \;=\; 0$$

$$\text{var}[s(x;\theta)] \;=\; E\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right)^{\!2}\right]$$

> [!TIP]
> **Regularity Condition:** The CRLB theorem assumes that differentiation under the integral sign is permissible, i.e., $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\int p(x;\theta)\,dx = \int \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}p(x;\theta)\,dx = 0$. Most engineering PDFs satisfy this ŌĆö exceptions are rare.

---

## 2.2 Fisher Information

The Fisher Information $I(\theta)$ measures how much "information" the observation $x$ carries about the unknown parameter $\theta$. There are **two equivalent definitions**:

$$I(\theta) \;=\; E\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right)^{\!2}\right]$$

$$I(\theta) \;=\; -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^{2} \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta^{2}}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For $N$ independent, identically distributed (i.i.d.) observations** $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$, the Fisher Information adds up:
> $$I_N(\theta) \;=\; N \cdot I(\theta)$$
> This is the single most important fact for KTU problems ŌĆö it gives the **$\frac{1}{N}$ scaling** of the CRLB.

---

## 2.3 The Cram├®rŌĆōRao Inequality (Scalar Case)

**Theorem (Cram├®rŌĆōRao):** If $\hat{\theta}$ is any **unbiased** estimator of $\theta$ based on an observation $x$ with PDF $p(x;\theta)$, and if the regularity conditions hold, then:

$$\text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \;\geq\; \frac{1}{I(\theta)} \;=\; \text{CRLB}$$

**Equality holds (estimator is efficient) if and only if:**

$$\frac{\partial \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta} \;=\; I(\theta)\,\bigl(\hat{\theta} - \theta\bigr)$$

---

## 2.4 Multivariate / Vector Parameter Case

For estimating a $p \times 1$ parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta} = [\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_p]^T$, define the **Fisher Information Matrix (FIM)** $\mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ with entries:

$$[\mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta})]_{ij} \;=\; -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^{2} \ln p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \theta_i\,\partial \theta_j}\right]$$

The CRLB on the covariance matrix of any unbiased estimator $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ is:

$$\mathbf{C}_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}} \;\geq\; \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

The individual variance bounds are obtained from the diagonal entries:

$$\text{var}(\hat{\theta}_i) \;\geq\; \bigl[\mathbf{J}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\bigr]_{ii}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Table ŌĆö Essential Equations for KTU Board Exams**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | Score function | $s(x;\theta)=\frac{\partial \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}$ | First derivative of log-likelihood |
| 2 | Mean of score | $E[s(x;\theta)] = 0$ | Requires regularity |
| 3 | Fisher Info (form 1) | $I(\theta)=E\!\left[\bigl(\tfrac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}\bigr)^{2}\right]$ | Always valid under regularity |
| 4 | Fisher Info (form 2) | $I(\theta)=-E\!\left[\tfrac{\partial^{2} \ln p}{\partial \theta^{2}}\right]$ | Equivalent to form 1 |
| 5 | CRLB (scalar) | $\text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \tfrac{1}{I(\theta)}$ | Unbiased estimator |
| 6 | CRLB (N i.i.d.) | $\text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \tfrac{1}{N I(\theta)}$ | Independence $\Rightarrow$ info adds |
| 7 | CRLB (vector) | $\mathbf{C}_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}} \geq \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ | Matrix inequality |
| 8 | Efficiency | $e(\hat{\theta}) = \tfrac{\text{CRLB}}{\text{var}(\hat{\theta})}$ | $0 \leq e \leq 1$ |
| 9 | Efficient estimator (iff) | $\tfrac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}=I(\theta)(\hat{\theta}-\theta)$ | Achieves the bound |
| 10 | Bias-corrected bound | $\text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \tfrac{\left(1+\tfrac{d b}{d\theta}\right)^{2}}{I(\theta)}$ | General biased case |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Radar & Sonar:** Bounds the minimum achievable error in estimating target range, velocity, and angle from noisy returns.
- **Digital Communications:** Sets a fundamental floor on symbol-timing and carrier-phase estimation error (e.g., MLE vs. CRLB in phase estimation).
- **Spectrum Sensing:** Determines minimum SNR required to estimate a carrier frequency to a given tolerance.
- **Biomedical Imaging (MRI, Ultrasound):** Limits on parameter accuracy in tissue-property estimation.
- **Machine Learning:** The CRLB underpins the *Information Matrix Equality* used in active learning and optimal experimental design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cram├®rŌĆōRao Inequality (Scalar Parameter)

Let $x$ be a random observation with PDF $p(x;\theta)$, and let $\hat{\theta}(x)$ be any unbiased estimator of $\theta$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Unbiasedness as an integral identity:**

$$E[\hat{\theta}(x)] \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{\theta}(x)\, p(x;\theta)\, dx \;=\; \theta$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate both sides with respect to $\theta$:**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{\theta}(x)\, \frac{\partial p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\, dx \;=\; 1$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Rewrite the derivative of $p$ via log-derivative:**

Since $\frac{\partial p}{\partial \theta} = p \cdot \frac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}$, we have:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{\theta}(x)\, \frac{\partial \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\, p(x;\theta)\, dx \;=\; 1$$

That is,

$$E\!\left[\hat{\theta}(x) \cdot \frac{\partial \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right] \;=\; 1$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Subtract $\theta \cdot E\!\left[\tfrac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}\right] = \theta \cdot 0 = 0$:**

$$E\!\left[\bigl(\hat{\theta}(x) - \theta\bigr) \cdot \frac{\partial \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right] \;=\; 1$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the CauchyŌĆōSchwarz inequality:**

For any two random variables $A$ and $B$:

$$\bigl(E[AB]\bigr)^{2} \;\leq\; E[A^{2}]\,E[B^{2}]$$

Let $A = \hat{\theta}(x) - \theta$ and $B = \tfrac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}$. Then:

$$\bigl(E[AB]\bigr)^{2} \;\leq\; E\!\left[(\hat{\theta}-\theta)^{2}\right] \cdot E\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}\right)^{\!2}\right]$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Substitute the known values:**

$$(1)^{2} \;\leq\; \text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \cdot I(\theta)$$

(Recall: $E[(\hat{\theta}-\theta)^{2}] = E[\hat{\theta}^{2}] - 2\theta E[\hat{\theta}] + \theta^{2} = E[\hat{\theta}^{2}] - \theta^{2}$, which for an unbiased estimator equals $\text{var}(\hat{\theta})$.)

**Step 7 ŌĆö Final Result (Cram├®rŌĆōRao Bound):**

$$\boxed{\;\text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \;\geq\; \frac{1}{I(\theta)}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equality condition:** Equality holds in CauchyŌĆōSchwarz **iff** $A = k B$ for some constant $k$ (a.e.), which gives the efficient-estimator condition:
> $$\hat{\theta}(x) - \theta \;=\; k \cdot \frac{\partial \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}$$
> Taking expectations shows $k = 1/I(\theta)$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example ŌĆö Estimating the Mean of a Gaussian

**Problem statement.** Let $x \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^{2})$ with known variance $\sigma^{2}$. Find the CRLB for any unbiased estimator of $\theta$, and check whether the sample mean is efficient.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the PDF:**

$$p(x;\theta) \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^{2}}}\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\theta)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Log-likelihood:**

$$\ln p(x;\theta) \;=\; -\tfrac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^{2}) \;-\; \frac{(x-\theta)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö First derivative (score):**

$$\frac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta} \;=\; -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \cdot 2(\theta - x) \;=\; \frac{x-\theta}{\sigma^{2}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Second derivative:**

$$\frac{\partial^{2} \ln p}{\partial \theta^{2}} \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\frac{x-\theta}{\sigma^{2}}\right) \;=\; -\frac{1}{\sigma^{2}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Fisher Information:**

$$I(\theta) \;=\; -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^{2} \ln p}{\partial \theta^{2}}\right] \;=\; -E\!\left[-\frac{1}{\sigma^{2}}\right] \;=\; \frac{1}{\sigma^{2}}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö CRLB:**

$$\text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \;\geq\; \frac{1}{I(\theta)} \;=\; \sigma^{2}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö For $N$ i.i.d. observations:**

$$\text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \;\geq\; \frac{\sigma^{2}}{N}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Check the sample mean $\bar{x} = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$:**

$$E[\bar{x}] = \theta \quad (\text{unbiased})$$

$$\text{var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{N}$$

The sample mean achieves the bound exactly. Therefore, **$\bar{x}$ is an efficient estimator of the Gaussian mean**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example ŌĆö Estimating the Variance of a Zero-Mean Gaussian

**Problem.** Given $x_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \theta)$ i.i.d., $i = 1, \ldots, N$. Find the CRLB for an unbiased estimator of $\theta > 0$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö PDF and log-likelihood:**

$$p(x_i;\theta) \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\theta}}\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{x_i^{2}}{2\theta}\right)$$

$$\ln p(x_i;\theta) \;=\; -\tfrac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi\theta) \;-\; \frac{x_i^{2}}{2\theta}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö First derivative:**

$$\frac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta} \;=\; -\frac{1}{2\theta} \;+\; \frac{x_i^{2}}{2\theta^{2}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Second derivative:**

$$\frac{\partial^{2} \ln p}{\partial \theta^{2}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\theta^{2}} \;-\; \frac{x_i^{2}}{\theta^{3}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Fisher Information per observation:**

$$I(\theta) \;=\; -E\!\left[\frac{1}{2\theta^{2}} - \frac{x_i^{2}}{\theta^{3}}\right]$$

Since $E[x_i^{2}] = \theta$ (the variance):

$$I(\theta) \;=\; -\frac{1}{2\theta^{2}} \;+\; \frac{\theta}{\theta^{3}} \;=\; -\frac{1}{2\theta^{2}} \;+\; \frac{1}{\theta^{2}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\theta^{2}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö CRLB for $N$ observations:**

$$\text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \;\geq\; \frac{1}{N \cdot I(\theta)} \;=\; \frac{2\theta^{2}}{N}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compare with the sample variance $\hat{\theta} = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum x_i^{2}$:**

$$E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta \quad (\text{unbiased})$$

$$\text{var}(\hat{\theta}) = \frac{2\theta^{2}}{N}$$

The sample variance achieves the bound, hence it is **efficient**.

---

## 3.4 Summary Matrix of Standard Results

| Distribution | Parameter | Fisher Info $I(\theta)$ | CRLB | Efficient Estimator |
|--------------|-----------|-------------------------|------|---------------------|
| $\mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^{2})$, $\sigma^{2}$ known | mean $\theta$ | $\tfrac{1}{\sigma^{2}}$ | $\tfrac{\sigma^{2}}{N}$ | Sample mean $\bar{x}$ |
| $\mathcal{N}(0, \theta)$, mean known | variance $\theta$ | $\tfrac{1}{2\theta^{2}}$ | $\tfrac{2\theta^{2}}{N}$ | $\tfrac{1}{N}\sum x_i^{2}$ |
| Poisson$(\lambda)$ | rate $\lambda$ | $\tfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\tfrac{\lambda}{N}$ | Sample mean |
| Exponential$(\lambda)$ | rate $\lambda$ | $\tfrac{1}{\lambda^{2}}$ | $\tfrac{\lambda^{2}}{N}$ | $1/\bar{x}$ |
| Bernoulli$(p)$ | prob. $p$ | $\tfrac{1}{p(1-p)}$ | $\tfrac{p(1-p)}{N}$ | Sample mean |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Procedural Flowchart ŌĆö Computing the CRLB

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **step-by-step computational recipe** that the KTU examiner expects a student to follow when asked to "find the CRLB for parameter $\theta$ given observation model $p(x;\theta)$".

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Identify observation model p x theta"] --> B["Write the log likelihood Ln p x theta"]
    B --> C["Compute the score function: d Ln p by d theta"]
    C --> D{"Which Fisher Information form?"}
    D -- "Form 1: square then expectation" --> E1["Square the score, take expectation wrt p x theta"]
    D -- "Form 2: second derivative" --> E2["Take second derivative d2 Ln p by d theta2, then negative expectation"]
    E1 --> F["Obtain I theta"]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G{"Multiple i.i.d. observations?"}
    G -- "Yes, N samples" --> G1["Multiply Fisher Info by N: I_N = N I theta"]
    G -- "Single observation" --> H["Proceed directly"]
    G1 --> I["Compute CRLB = 1 over I_N"]
    H --> I
    I --> J{"Check unbiasedness of proposed estimator"}
    J -- "Yes" --> K["Verify if var of estimator equals CRLB"]
    J -- "No" --> L["Use biased CRLB formula or design MVUE"]
    K --> M{"Bound achieved?"}
    M -- "Yes" --> N["Estimator is efficient"]
    M -- "No" --> O["Estimator is not efficient: report efficiency ratio"]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö Performance Hierarchy of Estimators

This Mermaid block-schematic shows how estimators are ranked against the CRLB, the absolute floor of unbiased estimation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ideal["Theoretical Optimum"]
        A["CRLB: var theta hat equals 1 over I theta"]
    end
    subgraph tier1["Tier 1: Efficient Estimators"]
        B["Maximum Likelihood Estimator MLE when regular"]
        C["Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimator MVUE if unique"]
    end
    subgraph tier2["Tier 2: Asymptotically Efficient"]
        D["MLE for large N: var approaches CRLB as N to infinity"]
    end
    subgraph tier3["Tier 3: Suboptimal but Useful"]
        E["Method of Moments estimator"]
        F["Least Squares estimator"]
        G["Linear Bayesian estimators MMSE LMMSE"]
    end
    A --- B
    A --- C
    B --- D
    C --- D
    D --- E
    D --- F
    D --- G
```

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram ŌĆö Fisher Information as Curvature of Log-Likelihood

The following Mermaid plot maps the geometric interpretation: **Fisher Information is the expected curvature (sharpness) of the log-likelihood around the true parameter**. Sharper peaks ŌćÆ more information ŌćÆ tighter CRLB.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X["Parameter axis theta"] --> Y1["Log likelihood curve 1: flat peak, low I theta"]
    X --> Y2["Log likelihood curve 2: moderate curvature, medium I theta"]
    X --> Y3["Log likelihood curve 3: sharp peak, high I theta"]
    Y1 --> Z1["CRLB is large: poor estimation"]
    Y2 --> Z2["CRLB is moderate"]
    Y3 --> Z3["CRLB is small: excellent estimation"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exams:** *Sharper log-likelihood ŌćÆ more Fisher Information ŌćÆ smaller CRLB ŌćÆ better estimator.* Memorize this geometric intuition ŌĆö it often earns a quick conceptual mark in Part A questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** Define the Cram├®rŌĆōRao Lower Bound (CRLB) and state the Cram├®rŌĆōRao inequality for an unbiased estimator of a scalar parameter.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** The Cram├®rŌĆōRao Lower Bound is the *minimum possible variance* attainable by any unbiased estimator of a deterministic parameter, given a probabilistic observation model.
>
> **Statement (1 mark):** For any unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of scalar parameter $\theta$,
> $$\text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{I(\theta)}$$
> where $I(\theta) = -E\!\left[\tfrac{\partial^{2} \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta^{2}}\right]$ is the Fisher Information.
>
> **Interpretation (1 mark):** The bound is achieved when the score function is linearly related to $\hat{\theta} - \theta$; the corresponding estimator is termed *efficient*.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]** What is Fisher Information? Mention any two equivalent expressions for it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Fisher Information quantifies the amount of information that an observable random variable $x$ carries about an unknown parameter $\theta$ on which its PDF depends. It is a non-negative scalar function of $\theta$.

**Two expressions (2 marks):**

$$I(\theta) \;=\; E\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right)^{\!2}\right]$$

$$I(\theta) \;=\; -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^{2} \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta^{2}}\right]$$

The two forms are equivalent under the **regularity conditions** (interchange of differentiation and integration).

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]*

**(a)** Derive the Cram├®rŌĆōRao Lower Bound for an unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of a scalar parameter $\theta$ given a single observation $x$ with PDF $p(x;\theta$. State clearly the regularity condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider $N$ i.i.d. observations $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N$ drawn from $\mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^{2})$, where $\sigma^{2}$ is known. Find the CRLB for $\theta$ and show that the sample mean $\bar{x}$ is an efficient estimator. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to A(a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Unbiasedness identity (1 mark):**

$$E[\hat{\theta}] = \int \hat{\theta}(x)\,p(x;\theta)\,dx = \theta$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate both sides with respect to $\theta$ (1 mark):**

$$\int \hat{\theta}(x)\,\frac{\partial p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\,dx = 1$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Rewrite derivative of $p$ using log-derivative (1 mark):**

$$\int \hat{\theta}(x)\,\frac{\partial \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\,p(x;\theta)\,dx = 1$$

This is $E\!\left[\hat{\theta}\cdot \tfrac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}\right] = 1$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Subtract zero (regularity) (1 mark):** Since $E\!\left[\tfrac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}\right] = 0$:

$$E\!\left[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)\cdot \frac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}\right] = 1$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply CauchyŌĆōSchwarz (2 marks):** With $A = \hat{\theta}-\theta$ and $B = \tfrac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}$:

$$\bigl(E[AB]\bigr)^{2} \leq E[A^{2}]\,E[B^{2}]$$

$$1 \leq \text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \cdot I(\theta)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Result and regularity condition (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\;\text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{I(\theta)}\;}$$

**Regularity condition:** The order of differentiation and integration can be interchanged:
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\int p(x;\theta)\,dx = \int \frac{\partial p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\,dx$$

---

#### **Model Solution to A(b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Single-observation Fisher Information (2 marks):**

For $x \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^{2})$:

$$\ln p(x;\theta) = -\tfrac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^{2}) - \frac{(x-\theta)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}$$

$$\frac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta} = \frac{x-\theta}{\sigma^{2}}, \qquad \frac{\partial^{2} \ln p}{\partial \theta^{2}} = -\frac{1}{\sigma^{2}}$$

$$I(\theta) = -E\!\left[-\frac{1}{\sigma^{2}}\right] = \frac{1}{\sigma^{2}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Fisher Information for N i.i.d. observations (1 mark):**

$$I_N(\theta) = N \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma^{2}} = \frac{N}{\sigma^{2}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö CRLB (1 mark):**

$$\text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{I_N(\theta)} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{N}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Sample mean statistics (2 marks):**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i, \qquad E[\bar{x}] = \theta, \qquad \text{var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{N}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Conclusion (1 mark):** Since $\text{var}(\bar{x}) = \text{CRLB}$, the sample mean is an **efficient estimator** of the mean of a Gaussian with known variance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023 ŌĆö Alternative Module Choice]*

**(a)** Define Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) for a vector parameter $\boldsymbol{\theta} = [\theta_1, \theta_2]^{T}$. Write the Cram├®rŌĆōRao bound for the covariance matrix of any unbiased estimator and explain how the individual variance bounds are extracted. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Suppose $x_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$ i.i.d. for $i=1,\ldots,N$, where **both** $\mu$ and $\sigma^{2}$ are unknown. Find the FIM, its inverse, and the individual CRLBs for $\mu$ and $\sigma^{2}$. Comment on the structure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to B(a):**

**Definition (3 marks):** For vector parameter $\boldsymbol{\theta} = [\theta_1, \theta_2]^{T}$ with observation PDF $p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})$, the **Fisher Information Matrix** is a $2\times 2$ symmetric matrix:

$$[\mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta})]_{ij} = -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^{2} \ln p(\mathbf{x};\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \theta_i\, \partial \theta_j}\right]$$

Equivalent form:

$$[\mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta})]_{ij} = E\!\left[\frac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta_i}\cdot\frac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta_j}\right]$$

**Matrix CRLB (2 marks):** For any unbiased estimator $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$:

$$\mathbf{C}_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}} \geq \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

(positive-semidefinite sense). The diagonal of $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$ contains individual variance bounds:

$$\text{var}(\hat{\theta}_i) \geq [\mathbf{J}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\theta})]_{ii}$$

**Extraction (2 marks):** To obtain $\text{var}(\hat{\theta}_i)$, we must first compute $\mathbf{J}$, then explicitly invert it, and read off the $i$-th diagonal entry. Off-diagonal entries of $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$ also bound the cross-covariances between estimators.

---

#### **Model Solution to B(b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Log-likelihood (1 mark):**

$$\ln p(\mathbf{x};\mu,\sigma^{2}) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^{2}) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i-\mu)^{2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö First derivatives (1 mark):**

$$\frac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i-\mu)$$

$$\frac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \sigma^{2}} = -\frac{N}{2\sigma^{2}} + \frac{1}{2\sigma^{4}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i-\mu)^{2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Second derivatives (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\partial^{2} \ln p}{\partial \mu^{2}} = -\frac{N}{\sigma^{2}}$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2} \ln p}{\partial (\sigma^{2})^{2}} = \frac{N}{2\sigma^{4}} - \frac{1}{\sigma^{6}}\sum(x_i-\mu)^{2}$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2} \ln p}{\partial \mu\,\partial \sigma^{2}} = -\frac{1}{\sigma^{4}}\sum(x_i-\mu)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Take negative expectations (1 mark):** Use $E[x_i] = \mu$, $\text{var}(x_i) = \sigma^{2}$, $E[(x_i-\mu)^{2}] = \sigma^{2}$:

$$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{N}{\sigma^{2}} & 0 \\[4pt] 0 & \tfrac{N}{2\sigma^{4}} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Inverse and bounds (2 marks):**

$$\mathbf{J}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{\sigma^{2}}{N} & 0 \\[4pt] 0 & \tfrac{2\sigma^{4}}{N} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Individual CRLBs:**

$$\text{var}(\hat{\mu}) \geq \frac{\sigma^{2}}{N}, \qquad \text{var}(\widehat{\sigma^{2}}) \geq \frac{2\sigma^{4}}{N}$$

**Comment on structure (1 mark):** The FIM is **diagonal** ŌĆö $\mu$ and $\sigma^{2}$ are *orthogonal parameters* in the Fisher sense. Estimating one does not degrade the bound on the other; the MLEs $\hat{\mu} = \bar{x}$ and $\widehat{\sigma^{2}} = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^{2}$ are asymptotically efficient.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callouts

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Valuation ŌĆö Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Regularity Condition (ŌĆō1 to ŌĆō2 marks):** Many students quote the CRLB formula without stating the regularity assumption. The bound *only holds* when differentiation under the integral is valid.
> 2. **Mixing up the two Fisher Info forms (ŌĆō1 mark):** Use the form $I(\theta) = -E[\partial^{2}\ln p/\partial\theta^{2}]$ when you find the second derivative easier than squaring the score; the two are equivalent, but mixing them mid-derivation confuses the examiner.
> 3. **Wrong $N$-scaling (ŌĆō2 marks):** A frequent error is to write CRLB $= \sigma^{2}$ instead of $\sigma^{2}/N$ for $N$ i.i.d. samples. Always state *ŌĆ£for $N$ i.i.d. observations, $I_N = N I$ŌĆØ*.
> 4. **Conflating bias and variance:** CRLB applies to *unbiased* estimators. For biased estimators, use the modified form $\text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \tfrac{(1+db/d\theta)^{2}}{I(\theta)}$. Examiners dock 1 mark if the student silently applies the unbiased formula to a biased estimator.
> 5. **Vector-parameter CRLB ŌĆö forgetting to invert:** Always write $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$ explicitly. Many students stop at the FIM and never invert it, losing the bound entirely.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for CRLB (Module 1)**
>
> - **CRLB** = minimum achievable variance for any unbiased estimator of a deterministic parameter.
> - **Inequality (scalar):** $\text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq 1/I(\theta)$, where $I(\theta)$ is the Fisher Information.
> - **Fisher Information (two forms):**
>   * $I(\theta) = E\!\left[\bigl(\tfrac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}\bigr)^{2}\right]$
>   * $I(\theta) = -E\!\left[\tfrac{\partial^{2} \ln p}{\partial \theta^{2}}\right]$
> - **i.i.d. samples:** $I_N(\theta) = N \cdot I(\theta)$, hence CRLB scales as $1/N$.
> - **Efficient estimator:** Achieves the CRLB; equivalently, the score is linear in $(\hat{\theta}-\theta)$.
> - **Efficiency:** $e(\hat{\theta}) = \text{CRLB}/\text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \in [0,1]$.
> - **Vector CRLB:** $\mathbf{C}_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}} \geq \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ ŌĆö invert the Fisher Information Matrix.
> - **Regularity condition** must hold for the bound to be valid (differentiation under the integral).
> - **MLE** is asymptotically efficient ŌĆö variance approaches CRLB as $N \to \infty$.
> - **Sample mean of Gaussian with known variance** is the textbook example of an efficient estimator.
> - **Diagonal FIM ŌćÆ orthogonal parameters** ŌćÆ estimation of one does not affect the bound on the other.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linear Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Estimation and Detection theory, a **Linear Model** is a statistical model in which the observed data is expressed as a **linear combination of unknown parameters** corrupted by additive noise. Mathematically, the general form of a linear model is:

$$y = H\theta + w$$

where:
- $y \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 1}$ is the **observation / measurement vector**.
- $H \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times p}$ is the known **observation / regressor / measurement matrix**.
- $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times 1}$ is the unknown **parameter vector** to be estimated.
- $w \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 1}$ is the **additive noise vector**, typically assumed to be **Zero-Mean White Gaussian Noise (ZWGN)** with covariance $C_w = \sigma^{2} I$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linearity** refers to the linearity **in the parameters $\theta$**, NOT necessarily in the observation variable $x$. A model $y = \theta_1 + \theta_2 x + \theta_3 x^2 + w$ is still a **linear model** because it is linear in $\theta$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Linear Models?** They form the analytical backbone of **Wiener filtering, adaptive filters (LMS/RLS), spectral estimation, and array signal processing**. Most real-world engineering problems (channel equalization, system identification, radar/sonar beamforming) reduce to a linear model after reasonable assumptions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are baking a cake and you record two inputs: **amount of sugar (xŌéü)** and **amount of butter (xŌéé)**, along with the final **taste score (y)**. You perform $N$ experiments with different combinations.

You suspect:
> "The taste score is a weighted sum of the ingredients plus some random mess-up in the kitchen."

So you write: 
$$y_i = \theta_1 x_{1,i} + \theta_2 x_{2,i} + w_i$$

Your goal: **find the unknown weights $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$** that best explain the observed taste scores. This is exactly the **Linear Model Estimation** problem ŌĆö fitting a hyperplane through noisy data points to recover the hidden recipe.

- The **matrix $H$** is your **cookbook of experiments** (which ingredients were used in which trial).
- The **vector $w$** represents the **unpredictable noise** ŌĆö a fluctuation in oven temperature, mood of the taste-tester, etc.
- The **vector $\theta$** is the **true hidden recipe** that the chef (the estimator) wants to discover.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Linear Models

| Type | Equation | Typical Use |
|------|----------|-------------|
| **Linear Regression** | $y_i = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_i + w_i$ | Curve fitting, trend analysis |
| **Multiple Linear Regression** | $y = H\theta + w$, $H \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times p}$ | Multivariate prediction |
| **Linear Filtering / System ID** | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{p-1} h_k x[n-k] + w[n]$ | Adaptive filters, channel equalization |
| **Array Signal Model** | $x(t) = A(\theta) s(t) + n(t)$ | DOA estimation, beamforming |

> [!NOTE]
> All these are unified under the **single generic framework** $y = H\theta + w$. This unification is what makes Linear Models so powerful in KTU Estimation and Detection.

---

## 1.4 Assumptions of the Standard Linear Model (Gauss-Markov Setup)

1. **Linearity**: $E\{y\} = H\theta$
2. **Zero-Mean Noise**: $E\{w\} = 0$
3. **Homoscedastic & Uncorrelated Noise**: $C_w = E\{w w^{T}\} = \sigma^{2} I$
4. **Full Column Rank**: $\text{rank}(H) = p$ (i.e., $H^{T}H$ is invertible)
5. **$N \geq p$**: Number of observations must be **at least** the number of parameters (over-determined or exactly-determined system).
6. **$H$ is deterministic** (non-random, perfectly known).

Explicitly stated standards: the noise variance is denoted as $\sigma^{2}$ (always positive scalar under standard assumptions) and the **deterministic Cram├®r-Rao Bound (CRB)** for $\theta$ is $\text{CRB}(\theta) = \sigma^{2} (H^{T}H)^{-1}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Linear Model in Matrix Form

The set of $N$ scalar observations $\{y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_N\}$ is stacked into a vector:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1 &= h_{11}\theta_1 + h_{12}\theta_2 + \cdots + h_{1p}\theta_p + w_1 \\
y_2 &= h_{21}\theta_1 + h_{22}\theta_2 + \cdots + h_{2p}\theta_p + w_2 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
y_N &= h_{N1}\theta_1 + h_{N2}\theta_2 + \cdots + h_{Np}\theta_p + w_N
\end{aligned}
$$

Compact matrix form:

$$y = H\theta + w, \quad H \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times p}, \quad \theta \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$$

## 2.2 The Estimation Goal

We need to find an estimator $\hat{\theta} = g(y)$ such that the estimate is:
- **Unbiased**: $E\{\hat{\theta}\} = \theta$
- **Minimum Variance**: $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta})$ is minimized
- **Linear in data**: $\hat{\theta} = A y + b$ for some matrix $A$ and vector $b$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Concept ŌĆö BLUE (Best Linear Unbiased Estimator):**
> The estimator that satisfies all three properties above is called the **BLUE**, and under the Gauss-Markov conditions, it is given by the **Least Squares Estimate (LSE)**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|------------|
| 1 | $y = H\theta + w$ | Linear Model | $H$ full column rank, $w$ zero mean |
| 2 | $\hat{\theta}_{LS} = (H^{T}H)^{-1} H^{T} y$ | **Least Squares Estimator (LSE)** | $H^{T}H$ invertible |
| 3 | $H^{+} = (H^{T}H)^{-1} H^{T}$ | **Pseudo-inverse of $H$** (left-inverse when $N \geq p$) | Full column rank |
| 4 | $\hat{y} = H\hat{\theta}_{LS} = P y$ | Fitted / Predicted values | $P = H(H^{T}H)^{-1}H^{T}$ is projection matrix |
| 5 | $r = y - \hat{y} = (I - P) y$ | **Residual vector** | Orthogonal to $\hat{y}$ |
| 6 | $C_{\hat{\theta}} = \sigma^{2} (H^{T}H)^{-1}$ | **Covariance of LSE** | Under $C_w = \sigma^{2} I$ |
| 7 | $\text{var}(\hat{\theta}_{i}) = \sigma^{2} [(H^{T}H)^{-1}]_{ii}$ | Variance of $i$-th parameter | Diagonal element |
| 8 | $\text{CRB}(\theta) = \sigma^{2}(H^{T}H)^{-1}$ | **Cram├®r-Rao Bound** | Deterministic $\theta$, Gaussian $w$ |
| 9 | $J(\theta) = (y - H\theta)^{T} C_w^{-1} (y - H\theta)$ | **Weighted Least Squares cost** | For $C_w \neq \sigma^{2} I$ |
| 10 | $\hat{\theta}_{WLS} = (H^{T} C_w^{-1} H)^{-1} H^{T} C_w^{-1} y$ | **Weighted LS / MVUE / BLUE for colored noise** | $C_w$ known, $H$ full rank |
| 11 | $P = H(H^{T}H)^{-1}H^{T}$ | **Projection matrix onto $\mathcal{R}(H)$** | Idempotent: $P^{2}=P$, symmetric |
| 12 | $P_{\perp} = I - P$ | **Orthogonal projection onto $\mathcal{N}(H^{T})$** | Annihilates $H$: $P_{\perp} H = 0$ |
| 13 | $\hat{\sigma}^{2} = \dfrac{r^{T} r}{N - p}$ | **Unbiased estimate of noise variance** | Uses residual norm |
| 14 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{\theta^{T} H^{T} H \theta}{\sigma^{2}}$ | Output SNR (matched filter interpretation) | Beamforming gain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute value convention:** In all formulas above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ for absolute value / determinant should be read as $\vert A \vert = \det(A)$ when $A$ is a square matrix, but in KTU exam scripts it is usually written as $\det(H^{T}H)$.

---

## 2.4 The Cost Function (Why Least Squares?)

The **Least Squares** philosophy: choose $\hat{\theta}$ to **minimize the sum of squared errors**.

$$J(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - h_i^{T}\theta)^{2} = (y - H\theta)^{T} (y - H\theta)$$

### Why squared error?
- **Mathematical tractability**: leads to a closed-form linear solution.
- **Pythagorean / geometric**: minimizes Euclidean distance from $y$ to the column space of $H$.
- **Statistical optimality**: coincides with MLE under Gaussian noise.
- **Physical intuition**: amplifies large errors, forcing the fit to honor the worst cases.

> [!NOTE]
> In the **colored noise** case, we replace $J$ with the **Mahalanobis distance** $J(\theta) = (y - H\theta)^{T} C_w^{-1} (y - H\theta)$, which gives the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)** for non-white noise.

---

## 2.5 Geometric Interpretation (KTU Frequently Asked)

- The columns of $H$ span a $p$-dimensional **subspace** $\mathcal{R}(H) \subset \mathbb{R}^{N}$ called the **signal subspace**.
- The true noise-free signal $H\theta$ lies in $\mathcal{R}(H)$.
- The observed vector $y$ lies **outside** this subspace due to noise.
- The LSE projects $y$ **orthogonally** onto $\mathcal{R}(H)$ to obtain $\hat{y} = P y$.
- The residual $r = y - \hat{y}$ is the orthogonal component ŌĆö it is the "noise leftover".

$$\boxed{\;\text{Geometric Picture: } y = \underbrace{P y}_{\hat{y} \in \mathcal{R}(H)} + \underbrace{(I-P)y}_{r \perp \mathcal{R}(H)}\;}$$

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | How Linear Model Applies |
|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Channel Equalization** | Identify tap weights $\theta$ of a multipath channel. |
| **System Identification** | Estimate FIR filter coefficients using I/O measurements. |
| **DOA Estimation** | Estimate angles of arrival from array snapshots. |
| **Speech Enhancement** | Wiener filter coefficients from $R_{xx}$ and $r_{xd}$. |
| **Biomedical Signal Processing** | Remove baseline wander and motion artifacts. |
| **Adaptive Beamforming** | MVDR weights solved via linear inverse $R^{-1} d$. |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU Module 1, the linear model is the **gateway concept** to Wiener filtering (Module 2/3) and to adaptive filters (Module 4/5). Master the LSE derivation completely.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Least Squares Estimator (LSE) for $\hat{\theta}$

We wish to minimize:

$$J(\theta) = (y - H\theta)^{T} (y - H\theta)$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Expand the quadratic form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J(\theta) &= (y - H\theta)^{T}(y - H\theta) \\
&= y^{T} y - y^{T} H\theta - \theta^{T} H^{T} y + \theta^{T} H^{T} H \theta \\
&= y^{T} y - 2 \theta^{T} H^{T} y + \theta^{T} H^{T} H \theta
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: Since $y^{T} H \theta$ is a scalar, $y^{T} H \theta = \theta^{T} H^{T} y$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Take the gradient w.r.t. $\theta$ and set to zero:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta} &= -2 H^{T} y + 2 H^{T} H \theta = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad H^{T} H \theta &= H^{T} y
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the celebrated **Normal Equation** in linear estimation.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for $\hat{\theta}$:**

Assuming $H^{T} H$ is invertible (i.e., $H$ has full column rank):

$$
\boxed{\;\hat{\theta}_{LS} = (H^{T} H)^{-1} H^{T} y\;}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verify the second-order condition (Hessian):**

$$
\frac{\partial^{2} J(\theta)}{\partial \theta \, \partial \theta^{T}} = 2 H^{T} H \succ 0
$$

Since $H^{T} H$ is positive definite, $J$ is strictly convex and the critical point is a **unique global minimum**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: LSE is Unbiased

Given $y = H\theta + w$ with $E\{w\} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\theta}_{LS} &= (H^{T} H)^{-1} H^{T} y \\
&= (H^{T} H)^{-1} H^{T} (H\theta + w) \\
&= (H^{T} H)^{-1} H^{T} H \theta + (H^{T} H)^{-1} H^{T} w \\
&= I \cdot \theta + (H^{T} H)^{-1} H^{T} w
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking expectation:

$$
E\{\hat{\theta}_{LS}\} = \theta + (H^{T} H)^{-1} H^{T} E\{w\} = \theta + 0 = \theta \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Covariance of LSE

Starting from $\hat{\theta}_{LS} = \theta + (H^{T}H)^{-1} H^{T} w$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\hat{\theta}} &= E\{(\hat{\theta}_{LS} - \theta)(\hat{\theta}_{LS} - \theta)^{T}\} \\
&= E\{[(H^{T} H)^{-1} H^{T} w][(H^{T} H)^{-1} H^{T} w]^{T}\} \\
&= (H^{T} H)^{-1} H^{T} \, E\{w w^{T}\} \, H (H^{T} H)^{-1} \\
&= (H^{T} H)^{-1} H^{T} (\sigma^{2} I) H (H^{T} H)^{-1} \\
&= \sigma^{2} (H^{T} H)^{-1} H^{T} H (H^{T} H)^{-1} \\
&= \sigma^{2} (H^{T} H)^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Result:** The covariance matrix of the LSE is **exactly equal to the Cram├®r-Rao Bound** under the linear-Gaussian model. Therefore, LSE = **MVUE = BLUE = Efficient Estimator** when $C_w = \sigma^{2} I$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cram├®r-Rao Bound for Linear Model

The likelihood under Gaussian noise:

$$p(y; \theta) = \frac{1}{(2\pi\sigma^{2})^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\|y - H\theta\|^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

Log-likelihood:

$$\ln p(y; \theta) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^{2}) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} (y - H\theta)^{T} (y - H\theta)$$

**Score function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta} &= \frac{1}{\sigma^{2}} H^{T} (y - H\theta) \\
\Rightarrow \quad E\!\left[\frac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}\right] &= 0 \quad (\text{unbiasedness of score})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Fisher Information Matrix:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(\theta) &= -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^{2} \ln p}{\partial \theta \, \partial \theta^{T}}\right] \\
&= -E\!\left[-\frac{1}{\sigma^{2}} H^{T} H\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{\sigma^{2}} H^{T} H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cram├®r-Rao Bound:**

$$
\boxed{\;C_{\hat{\theta}} \succeq F^{-1}(\theta) = \sigma^{2} (H^{T} H)^{-1}\;}
$$

Since the LSE achieves this bound with equality, it is an **efficient estimator**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Weighted Least Squares (Colored Noise Case)

When $C_w \neq \sigma^{2} I$, we minimize the **Mahalanobis distance**:

$$J_W(\theta) = (y - H\theta)^{T} C_w^{-1} (y - H\theta)$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Take gradient and equate to zero:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial J_W}{\partial \theta} &= -2 H^{T} C_w^{-1} y + 2 H^{T} C_w^{-1} H \theta = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad H^{T} C_w^{-1} H \theta &= H^{T} C_w^{-1} y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Solve for $\hat{\theta}_{WLS}$:**

$$
\boxed{\;\hat{\theta}_{WLS} = (H^{T} C_w^{-1} H)^{-1} H^{T} C_w^{-1} y\;}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Covariance:**

$$
C_{\hat{\theta}}^{WLS} = (H^{T} C_w^{-1} H)^{-1}
$$

> This is the **Gauss-Markov BLUE** for arbitrary noise covariance. It coincides with the **Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE)** under Gaussian noise.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Gauss-Markov Theorem (Statement + Proof Sketch)

**Theorem:** Under the assumptions $E\{w\} = 0$ and $C_w = \sigma^{2} I$, the LSE $\hat{\theta}_{LS} = (H^{T}H)^{-1} H^{T} y$ is the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator**, i.e., it has minimum variance among all linear unbiased estimators.

**Proof Sketch:**

1. Any linear unbiased estimator has the form $\tilde{\theta} = A y$ with $A H = I$ (unbiasedness constraint).
2. Its covariance is $C_{\tilde{\theta}} = A C_w A^{T} = \sigma^{2} A A^{T}$.
3. Construct any other estimator: $\tilde{\theta} = (H^{T}H)^{-1} H^{T} y + B y = \hat{\theta}_{LS} + B y$.
4. Unbiasedness: $B H = 0$, i.e., rows of $B$ are orthogonal to columns of $H$.
5. The covariance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\tilde{\theta}} &= \sigma^{2} \big[(H^{T}H)^{-1} H^{T} + B\big] \big[H (H^{T}H)^{-1} + B^{T}\big] \\
&= \sigma^{2} (H^{T}H)^{-1} + \sigma^{2} B B^{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $B B^{T} \succeq 0$, we have $C_{\tilde{\theta}} \succeq C_{\hat{\theta}_{LS}}$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Estimators

| Estimator | Formula | Assumes | Achieves |
|-----------|---------|---------|----------|
| LSE | $(H^{T}H)^{-1} H^{T} y$ | $E\{w\}=0$ | BLUE if $C_w = \sigma^{2} I$ |
| WLS / BLUE | $(H^{T} C_w^{-1} H)^{-1} H^{T} C_w^{-1} y$ | $E\{w\}=0$, $C_w$ known | BLUE for general $C_w$ |
| MLE (Gaussian) | Same as WLS | $w \sim \mathcal{N}(0, C_w)$ | MVUE + Efficient |
| MMSE | $R_{\theta y} R_{yy}^{-1} y$ | Bayesian, prior on $\theta$ | Minimum MSE (not unbiased) |
| Recursive LS (RLS) | $\hat{\theta}[n] = \hat{\theta}[n-1] + K[n] e[n]$ | Sequential data | Adaptive tracking |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU Module 1, the emphasis is on **LSE and WLS / BLUE**. The recursive versions (RLS) and MMSE estimators typically appear in later modules on adaptive filtering and Wiener filtering respectively.

---

## 3.8 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Given observations $y = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 4 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix}$ and regressor matrix $H = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$ with $w \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, find the LSE $\hat{\theta}$ and its covariance. Assume $\sigma^{2} = 1$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $H^{T} H$:**

$$
H^{T} H = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 6 \\ 6 & 14 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute its inverse:**

$$
\det(H^{T} H) = 3 \cdot 14 - 6 \cdot 6 = 42 - 36 = 6
$$

$$
(H^{T} H)^{-1} = \frac{1}{6} \begin{bmatrix} 14 & -6 \\ -6 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7/3 & -1 \\ -1 & 1/2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $H^{T} y$:**

$$
H^{T} y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 4 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 16 \\ 34 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $\hat{\theta}$:**

$$
\hat{\theta} = (H^{T}H)^{-1} H^{T} y = \begin{bmatrix} 7/3 & -1 \\ -1 & 1/2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 16 \\ 34 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 112/3 - 34 \\ -16 + 17 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10/3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Covariance:**

$$
C_{\hat{\theta}} = \sigma^{2} (H^{T}H)^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 7/3 & -1 \\ -1 & 1/2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Residual and noise variance estimate:**

$$
\hat{y} = H\hat{\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 10/3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 13/3 \\ 16/3 \\ 19/3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
r = y - \hat{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 - 13/3 \\ 4 - 16/3 \\ 7 - 19/3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2/3 \\ -4/3 \\ 2/3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\|r\|^{2} = \frac{4 + 16 + 4}{9} = \frac{24}{9} = \frac{8}{3}
$$

$$
\hat{\sigma}^{2} = \frac{\|r\|^{2}}{N - p} = \frac{8/3}{3 - 2} = \frac{8}{3} \approx 2.667
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** With $N = 3$ and $p = 2$, we have only 1 degree of freedom, so the variance estimate is sensitive. KTU numerical problems typically use $N \geq 5$ for stable results.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Linear Model Signal Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Unknown Parameters theta] --> B[Regressor Matrix H]
    B --> C[Linear Combiner: H times theta]
    D[Noise w ~ N mu, sigma^2 I] --> C
    C --> E[Observation Vector y]
    E --> F[Estimator g of y]
    F --> G[Estimate theta hat]
    G --> H{Unbiased?}
    H -- Yes --> I[BLUE / MVUE]
    H -- No --> J[Biased Estimator]
    I --> K[Compute Covariance: C theta hat]
    K --> L[Compare with CRB: sigma^2 HTH inverse]
```

## 4.2 Geometric Projection Picture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Observation Space R^N] --> B[Column Space of H: Signal Subspace, dim p]
    A --> C[Null Space of H^T: Noise Subspace, dim N-p]
    B --> D[True H theta: hidden recipe]
    B --> E[Estimate H theta hat: projection of y]
    A --> F[Observation y: noisy data]
    F --> G[Orthogonal Projection P]
    G --> E
    F --> H[Residual r = y minus H theta hat]
    H --> I[Estimate of noise: w hat]
    I --> C
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: LSE Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: y in R^N] --> B[Multiply by H transpose: H^T y in R^p]
    B --> C[Multiply by H^T H inverse: estimate]
    C --> D[Output: theta hat in R^p]
    E[H in R^Nxp] --> B
    E --> F[Form H^T H in R^pxp]
    F --> G[Invert: H^T H inverse]
    G --> C
    H[Noise Covariance C_w] --> I{Colored Noise?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Use Weighted LS: H^T C_w inverse H]
    I -- No --> K[Use Standard LS]
    J --> D
    K --> D
```

## 4.4 Estimation Hierarchy (Master Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Linear Model: y = H theta + w] --> B{Noise Assumption}
    B -- C_w = sigma^2 I --> C[Standard LSE]
    B -- C_w known, non-diagonal --> D[Weighted LS / BLUE]
    B -- Gaussian --> E[MLE = WLS = MVUE]
    B -- Bayesian prior on theta --> F[MMSE / Wiener Filter]
    C --> G[theta hat LS = HTH inv HT y]
    D --> H[theta hat WLS = HTCw invH inv HTCw invely]
    E --> I[Same as WLS]
    F --> J[theta hat MMSE = R thy R yy inv y]
    G --> K[Cov = sigma^2 HTH inv]
    H --> L[Cov = HTCw invH inv]
    I --> L
    G --> M[Achieves CRB: Yes]
    H --> M
    I --> M
    J --> N[MMSE: not unbiased, but min MSE]
```

## 4.5 Decision Flow for Choosing an Estimator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Have Observations y and Model H] --> B{Is Noise White?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is Noise Variance Known?}
    B -- No --> D[Use Weighted LS / BLUE with C_w]
    C -- Yes --> E[Use Standard LSE formula]
    C -- No --> F[Estimate sigma^2 from residuals]
    D --> G[Compute H^T C_w inverse H]
    E --> H[Compute theta hat LS]
    F --> H
    G --> I[Compute theta hat WLS]
    H --> J[Verify Unbiased: E theta hat = theta]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute Covariance C theta hat]
    K --> L[Compare with CRB]
    L --> M{Achieves CRB?}
    M -- Yes --> N[OPTIMAL: Efficient BLUE]
    M -- No --> O[Sub-optimal: search for better estimator]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "show that LSE is the BLUE", the diagram above (Decision Flow) provides a perfect visual aid. Combine it with the algebraic proof from Section 3.6 for full marks.
