topics.title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Maximum Likelihood Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** is a fundamental parametric method of point estimation in statistical inference and signal processing, wherein the unknown parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ of a probability distribution is estimated by maximizing the **likelihood function** $L(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ ŌĆö equivalently, the joint probability density function (PDF) of the observed random samples, evaluated as a function of the parameter vector for fixed data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Emphasis):**
> *Maximum Likelihood Estimation is a method of estimating the parameters of a statistical model by finding the parameter values that maximize the probability (likelihood) of observing the given set of data.*

Mathematically, given $N$ i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed) observations $\mathbf{x} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ drawn from a distribution with PDF $f(x_i \mid \boldsymbol{\theta})$, the **MLE estimate** is:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \arg\max_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \prod_{i=1}^{N} f(x_i \mid \boldsymbol{\theta})$$

where $L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} f(x_i \mid \boldsymbol{\theta})$ is the likelihood function, and $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{MLE}}$ denotes the **Maximum Likelihood Estimator** (a random variable when expressed as a function of data).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö "The Detective and the Witnesses":**
> Imagine you are a detective investigating a crime. You have **N eyewitnesses**, each giving slightly different accounts of the suspect's height. You cannot ask the suspect directly, but you want the best estimate of his true height. Each witness's testimony has a *probability distribution* of being accurate.
>
> - **MLE thinking:** "Which suspect height would make the observed testimonies (data) **most probable to have occurred**?"
> - You compute, for every possible height $h$, "How likely is it that all these witnesses would report exactly what they reported, if the suspect were really $h$ tall?" Then you pick the $h$ that scores the highest.
> - This is the essence of MLE: **find the parameter that best explains the data we have already observed**.

## 1.3 Why MLE is Central to Estimation Theory

| Property | Significance in Detection & Estimation |
|---|---|
| **Asymptotic Efficiency** | Achieves Cram├®r-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) as $N \to \infty$ |
| **Consistency** | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} \xrightarrow{P} \theta$ as $N \to \infty$ |
| **Invariance** | If $\hat{\theta}$ is MLE of $\theta$, then $g(\hat{\theta})$ is MLE of $g(\theta)$ |
| **Sufficiency** | MLE is a function of the sufficient statistic |
| **Asymptotic Normality** | $\sqrt{N}(\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} - \theta) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, I^{-1}(\theta))$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently test MLE alongside CRLB. Always compute the Fisher Information and verify the CRLB achievability when solving MLE problems.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Sample size:** $N \geq 1$ (number of observations)
- **Logarithm base:** Natural logarithm $\ln(\cdot)$ (universal in MLE derivations)
- **Bias of MLE:** Generally **unbiased** for large $N$, but may be biased for finite $N$
- **Efficiency metric:** Achieves **100% relative efficiency** asymptotically
- **Confidence interval scaling:** $\sigma_{\hat{\theta}} \sim \mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{N})$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Step-by-Step Construction of MLE

The MLE procedure can be decomposed into a rigorous sequence of operational steps:

1. **Step 1 ŌĆö Model Assumption:** Specify the parametric family $f(x \mid \theta)$ governing the observations.
2. **Step 2 ŌĆö Likelihood Formation:** Construct $L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} f(x_i \mid \theta)$ assuming independence.
3. **Step 3 ŌĆö Log-Likelihood Transformation:** Take $\ell(\theta) = \ln L(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ln f(x_i \mid \theta)$, justified by the monotonicity of $\ln(\cdot)$.
4. **Step 4 ŌĆö Differentiation:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = 0$ (first-order necessary condition).
5. **Step 5 ŌĆö Second-Order Check:** Verify $\dfrac{\partial^2 \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta^2} < 0$ at the critical point (sufficient condition for maximum).
6. **Step 6 ŌĆö Solve and Report:** The solution $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}}$ is the maximum likelihood estimator.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Justification (Why) |
|---|---|
| Likelihood Product | Joint PDF under independence assumption: $f(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta) = \prod_i f(x_i \mid \theta)$ |
| Log-Transformation | Converts products to sums (easier differentiation); preserves argmax since $\ln$ is monotonic |
| First Derivative Zero | Necessary condition for an extremum (Fermat's theorem for interior maxima) |
| Second Derivative Negative | Sufficient condition to confirm a maximum (not a minimum or saddle point) |

## 2.3 Fisher Information ŌĆö The Engine Behind MLE Optimality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fisher Information $I(\theta)$** quantifies the amount of information that an observable random variable $X$ carries about the unknown parameter $\theta$ on which its distribution depends.

For a single observation:
$$I(\theta) = -E\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln f(X \mid \theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right] = E\left[\left(\frac{\partial \ln f(X \mid \theta)}{\partial \theta}\right)^2\right]$$

For $N$ i.i.d. observations:
$$I_N(\theta) = N \cdot I(\theta)$$

The **Cram├®r-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)** states that the variance of *any* unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}$ satisfies:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{I_N(\theta)} = \frac{1}{N \cdot I(\theta)}$$

An estimator that achieves this bound is called an **efficient estimator**, and MLE is asymptotically efficient.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} f(x_i \mid \theta)$ | Likelihood function | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ln f(x_i \mid \theta)$ | Log-likelihood function | Nits (log-units) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\partial \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = 0$ | MLE stationary equation | Scalar/Vector equation |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\partial^2 \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\bigg\vert_{\hat{\theta}} < 0$ | Maximum verification | Second-order check |
| 5 | $I(\theta) = -E\!\left[\dfrac{\partial^2 \ln f(X \mid \theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$ | Fisher Information (single obs.) | Per observation |
| 6 | $I_N(\theta) = N \cdot I(\theta)$ | Fisher Information ($N$ i.i.d. obs.) | Total info |
| 7 | $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \dfrac{1}{I_N(\theta)}$ | Cram├®r-Rao Lower Bound | Lower bound on variance |
| 8 | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} \xrightarrow{P} \theta$ | Consistency (as $N \to \infty$) | Convergence in probability |
| 9 | $\sqrt{N}\bigl(\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} - \theta\bigr) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}\!\bigl(0, I^{-1}(\theta)\bigr)$ | Asymptotic normality | Distribution form |
| 10 | $g(\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}}) = \widehat{g(\theta)}_{\text{MLE}}$ | Invariance property | Functional invariance |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

MLE is the **workhorse estimator** across modern engineering disciplines:

- **Radar & Sonar Detection:** Estimating target range, velocity, and SNR from noisy returns.
- **Digital Communications:** Channel estimation, symbol synchronization, fading parameter estimation.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG/EEG feature extraction, seizure detection model fitting.
- **Machine Learning:** Logistic regression weights, Gaussian Mixture Models (EM algorithm).
- **Speech Processing:** Linear Predictive Coding (LPC) coefficients ŌĆö historically the most celebrated application of MLE in DSP.
- **Image Processing:** Estimating noise parameters (variance of Gaussian noise) for Wiener filtering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Derivation ŌĆö MLE for Gaussian Mean (Known Variance)

**Problem Setup:** Let $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N$ be i.i.d. samples from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ where $\sigma^2$ is known and $\mu$ is unknown. Find $\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the PDF:**
$$f(x_i \mid \mu) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Form the Likelihood:**
$$L(\mu) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify the constant prefactor:**
$$L(\mu) = \left(\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\right)^{\!N} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2\right)$$

The factor $\left(\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\right)^{\!N}$ is independent of $\mu$, so maximizing $L(\mu)$ is equivalent to maximizing $\ln L(\mu)$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Take the Log-Likelihood:**
$$\ell(\mu) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Differentiate with respect to $\mu$ and set to zero:**
$$\frac{\partial \ell(\mu)}{\partial \mu} = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \cdot 2\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)(-1) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu) = 0$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i - N\mu = 0$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Solve for $\mu$:**
$$\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = \bar{x}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Second-Order Verification:**
$$\frac{\partial^2 \ell(\mu)}{\partial \mu^2} = -\frac{N}{\sigma^2} < 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Maximum confirmed)}$$

**Conclusion:** The MLE of the mean of a Gaussian distribution is the **sample mean**, which matches our intuition.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison of MLE Across Common Distributions

| Distribution | PDF $f(x \mid \theta)$ | Log-Likelihood $\ell(\theta)$ | MLE $\hat{\theta}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gaussian (mean) | $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\bigl(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\bigr)$ | $-\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum(x_i-\mu)^2$ | $\hat{\mu} = \bar{x}$ |
| Gaussian (variance) | $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\bigl(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\bigr)$ | $-\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi) - N\ln\sigma - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum(x_i-\mu)^2$ | $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2$ |
| Exponential | $\lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ | $N\ln\lambda - \lambda\sum x_i$ | $\hat{\lambda} = \dfrac{N}{\sum x_i} = \dfrac{1}{\bar{x}}$ |
| Bernoulli | $\theta^x (1-\theta)^{1-x}$ | $\sum x_i \ln\theta + \sum(1-x_i)\ln(1-\theta)$ | $\hat{\theta} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{N} = \bar{x}$ |
| Poisson | $\frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^x}{x!}$ | $-N\lambda + \ln\lambda \cdot \sum x_i$ | $\hat{\lambda} = \bar{x}$ |

## 3.3 Worked Derivation ŌĆö MLE for Exponential Distribution

**Setup:** $x_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ with PDF $f(x_i \mid \lambda) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x_i}$ for $x_i \geq 0$. Find $\hat{\lambda}_{\text{MLE}}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Form Likelihood:**
$$L(\lambda) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \lambda e^{-\lambda x_i} = \lambda^N \exp\!\left(-\lambda\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i\right)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Log-Likelihood:**
$$\ell(\lambda) = N\ln\lambda - \lambda\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö First Derivative:**
$$\frac{\partial \ell(\lambda)}{\partial \lambda} = \frac{N}{\lambda} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve:**
$$\hat{\lambda}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{N}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i} = \frac{1}{\bar{x}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Second Derivative Verification:**
$$\frac{\partial^2 \ell(\lambda)}{\partial \lambda^2} = -\frac{N}{\lambda^2} < 0 \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.4 Fisher Information & CRLB ŌĆö Worked Example (Gaussian Mean)

**For one observation:**
$$\ln f(x \mid \mu) = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

**First derivative:**
$$\frac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \mu} = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma^2}$$

**Second derivative:**
$$\frac{\partial^2 \ln f}{\partial \mu^2} = -\frac{1}{\sigma^2}$$

**Fisher Information:**
$$I(\mu) = -E\!\left[-\frac{1}{\sigma^2}\right] = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}$$

**For $N$ i.i.d. observations:**
$$I_N(\mu) = \frac{N}{\sigma^2}$$

**CRLB:**
$$\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}) \geq \frac{\sigma^2}{N}$$

Since $\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \bar{x}$ has variance $\sigma^2/N$ exactly, **MLE achieves the CRLB** ŌĆö it is an **efficient estimator** for this problem.

## 3.5 Asymptotic Normality Result ŌĆö The Central Limit Theorem Bridge

By the asymptotic property of MLE, as $N \to \infty$:
$$\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\theta, \frac{1}{N \cdot I(\theta)}\right) \quad \text{(approximately)}$$

This Gaussian approximation enables the construction of asymptotic confidence intervals:
$$\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{N \cdot I(\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}})}}$$

where $z_{\alpha/2}$ is the standard normal quantile (e.g., $z_{0.025} = 1.96$ for 95% CI).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MLE Procedural Flowchart

The following Mermaid block diagrams the step-by-step operational pipeline of Maximum Likelihood Estimation, from raw data acquisition to final estimator characterization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Observe Data x1 x2 ... xN] --> B[Step 1: Specify Parametric PDF f of x given theta]
    B --> C[Step 2: Form Likelihood L of theta equals Product of f of xi given theta]
    C --> D[Step 3: Take Natural Log to Obtain Log Likelihood ell of theta]
    D --> E[Step 4: Differentiate ell of theta with respect to theta]
    E --> F[Step 5: Set Derivative Equal to Zero Solve Equation]
    F --> G{Solution Exists and Unique?}
    G -- No --> H[Apply Numerical Optimization Newton Raphson or Gradient Ascent]
    G -- Yes --> I[Step 6: Verify Second Derivative Negative Maximum]
    I --> J{Maximum Confirmed?}
    J -- No --> K[Check Boundary or Saddle Points]
    J -- Yes --> L[Output: MLE Estimate theta hat MLE]
    L --> M[Compute Fisher Information I of theta]
    M --> N[Compute CRLB equals 1 over N times I of theta]
    N --> O[Construct Asymptotic Confidence Interval]
    O --> P[End: Report Estimator Properties Consistency Efficiency Normality]
```

## 4.2 Log-Likelihood Surface ŌĆö Conceptual Block Diagram

For a 1-D parameter $\theta$, the log-likelihood $\ell(\theta)$ typically forms a concave (downward-opening) curve around the true parameter value. The block diagram below maps this conceptual geometry:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Parameter Axis theta] --> B[Region 1: theta much less than theta_true]
    B --> C[Region 2: theta less than theta_true Rising Slope]
    C --> D[Region 3: Peak at theta_hat_MLE Maximum Likelihood]
    D --> E[Region 4: theta greater than theta_true Falling Slope]
    E --> F[Region 5: theta much greater than theta_true Tails]
    D --> G[Key Property: Concavity Negative Second Derivative]
    D --> H[Asymptotic Property: Approximate Parabola Near Peak]
    H --> I[Inverse Curvature equals 1 over I_N of theta equals Variance of MLE]
```

## 4.3 MLE vs. Other Estimators ŌĆö Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Parametric_Estimation_Family[PARAMETRIC ESTIMATION FAMILY]
        direction TB
        A1[MLE Maximum Likelihood Estimation] --> A2[Achieves CRLB Asymptotically]
        A1 --> A3[Requires Known PDF Form]
        A1 --> A4[Strong Asymptotic Guarantees]

        B1[Method of Moments MME] --> B2[Matches Theoretical and Sample Moments]
        B1 --> B3[May Not Achieve CRLB]
        B1 --> B4[Simpler Computationally]

        C1[Bayesian Estimation MAP and MMSE] --> C2[Incorporates Prior Distribution]
        C1 --> C3[Requires Prior Knowledge]
        C1 --> C4[MAP Approximates MLE for Flat Priors]

        D1[Least Squares LS] --> D2[Equivalent to MLE under Gaussian Noise]
        D1 --> D3[Special Case for Linear Models]
    end

    A1 -.MAP limit flat prior.-> C2
    A1 -.Gaussian noise.-> D2
    B1 -.Low order moments.-> A1
```

## 4.4 Convergence Behavior of MLE ŌĆö Asymptotic Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Number of Observations N] --> B{N Small finite N}
    A --> C{N Large as N approaches infinity}

    B --> D[MLE may be Biased]
    B --> E[Distribution Not Exactly Gaussian]
    B --> F[Variance May Exceed CRLB]

    C --> G[Consistency: theta_hat_MLE converges to theta_true]
    C --> H[Asymptotic Normality: Gaussian Distribution]
    C --> I[Asymptotic Efficiency: Achieves CRLB]
    C --> J[Sufficiency: MLE is Sufficient Statistic Function]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define the Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE). Mention two important properties of MLE.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE)** of an unknown parameter $\theta$ based on $N$ i.i.d. observations $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N$ is the value $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}}$ that maximizes the likelihood function $L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} f(x_i \mid \theta)$, or equivalently its natural logarithm $\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ln f(x_i \mid \theta)$.

$$\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_{\theta} L(\theta)$$

**Two important properties:**
1. **Asymptotic Efficiency:** As $N \to \infty$, the MLE achieves the Cram├®r-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB), i.e., $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}}) \to 1/[N \cdot I(\theta)]$.
2. **Consistency:** The MLE converges in probability to the true parameter value, i.e., $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} \xrightarrow{P} \theta$ as $N \to \infty$.
3. *(Bonus)* **Invariance:** If $\hat{\theta}$ is the MLE of $\theta$, then $g(\hat{\theta})$ is the MLE of $g(\theta)$ for any function $g$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2. What is the Likelihood Function? How is it different from the Probability Density Function (PDF)?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Likelihood Function** $L(\theta)$ is defined as the joint PDF of the observed data, viewed as a function of the unknown parameter(s) $\theta$ (with the data held fixed):

$$L(\theta) = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N \mid \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} f(x_i \mid \theta) \quad \text{(under independence)}$$

**Key Differences from PDF:**

| Aspect | PDF $f(x \mid \theta)$ | Likelihood $L(\theta)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Variable of interest | $x$ (the data) | $\theta$ (the parameter) |
| What is held fixed | $\theta$ | $x$ (the observed data) |
| Normalization | Integrates to **1** over $x$ | **Not** required to integrate to 1 over $\theta$ |
| Interpretation | Probability density of data | Relative plausibility of parameter values |
| Use | Forward problem (predict data) | Inverse problem (infer parameter) |

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Module Internal Choice Pattern]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *MLE Derivation + CRLB Computation*

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Let $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N$ be i.i.d. random samples drawn from a Rayleigh distribution with PDF:
$$f(x \mid \sigma) = \frac{x}{\sigma^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad x \geq 0, \; \sigma > 0$$
Derive the Maximum Likelihood Estimator $\hat{\sigma}_{\text{MLE}}$ of the parameter $\sigma$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the Rayleigh distribution above, compute the Fisher Information $I(\sigma)$ for a single observation, and hence determine the Cram├®r-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) for $\hat{\sigma}$. Comment on whether the MLE achieves the CRLB.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of MLE for Rayleigh Distribution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Form the Likelihood Function:**
$$L(\sigma) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{x_i}{\sigma^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x_i^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Simplify the Product:**
$$L(\sigma) = \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{N} x_i}{\sigma^{2N}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2\right)$$

Since $\prod x_i$ does not depend on $\sigma$, the optimization is over the remaining terms.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Take the Log-Likelihood:**
$$\ell(\sigma) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ln x_i - 2N\ln\sigma - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Differentiate with respect to $\sigma$:**
$$\frac{\partial \ell(\sigma)}{\partial \sigma} = -\frac{2N}{\sigma} + \frac{1}{\sigma^3}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Set to Zero and Solve:**
$$-\frac{2N}{\sigma} + \frac{1}{\sigma^3}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2 = 0$$

$$\frac{1}{\sigma^3}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2 = \frac{2N}{\sigma}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2 = 2N\sigma^2$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\sigma}_{\text{MLE}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2}}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Second-Order Verification:**
$$\frac{\partial^2 \ell(\sigma)}{\partial \sigma^2} = \frac{2N}{\sigma^2} - \frac{3}{\sigma^4}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2$$

Substituting $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{2N}\sum x_i^2$:
$$\frac{\partial^2 \ell}{\partial \sigma^2}\bigg\vert_{\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{2N}{\sigma^2} - \frac{3 \cdot 2N\sigma^2}{\sigma^4} = \frac{2N}{\sigma^2} - \frac{6N}{\sigma^2} = -\frac{4N}{\sigma^2} < 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**(b) Fisher Information and CRLB [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Log-PDF of a Single Observation:**
$$\ln f(x \mid \sigma) = \ln x - 2\ln\sigma - \frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö First Derivative:**
$$\frac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \sigma} = -\frac{2}{\sigma} + \frac{x^2}{\sigma^3}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Second Derivative:**
$$\frac{\partial^2 \ln f}{\partial \sigma^2} = \frac{2}{\sigma^2} - \frac{3x^2}{\sigma^4}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute Fisher Information (Single Observation):**
$$I(\sigma) = -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln f}{\partial \sigma^2}\right] = -\left[\frac{2}{\sigma^2} - \frac{3E[X^2]}{\sigma^4}\right]$$

For Rayleigh distribution, $E[X^2] = 2\sigma^2$:
$$I(\sigma) = -\left[\frac{2}{\sigma^2} - \frac{3 \cdot 2\sigma^2}{\sigma^4}\right] = -\left[\frac{2}{\sigma^2} - \frac{6}{\sigma^2}\right] = \frac{4}{\sigma^2}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö CRLB for $N$ i.i.d. Observations:**
$$\text{Var}(\hat{\sigma}) \geq \frac{1}{N \cdot I(\sigma)} = \frac{1}{N \cdot \frac{4}{\sigma^2}} = \frac{\sigma^2}{4N}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Verify CRLB Achievement:**
The variance of $\hat{\sigma}_{\text{MLE}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2N}\sum x_i^2}$ can be computed as $\text{Var}(\hat{\sigma}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{4N}\bigl(1 - \frac{\pi}{4}\bigr)$, which is **less than** the CRLB? *No ŌĆö actually* the MLE for Rayleigh $\sigma$ is **biased**, and the CRLB applies to unbiased estimators only.

**Conclusion:** The MLE for the Rayleigh scale parameter is **biased** for finite $N$ but becomes **asymptotically unbiased and efficient**, achieving the CRLB in the limit $N \to \infty$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *MLE for Bernoulli + Invariance Property*

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Let $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli trials with parameter $p$ (i.e., $\Pr(X = 1) = p$, $\Pr(X = 0) = 1-p$). Derive the MLE $\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}}$ and show that it is an unbiased estimator.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the invariance property of MLE, find the MLE of the odds ratio $\psi = \dfrac{p}{1-p}$. Also compute the CRLB for $\hat{p}$ and show that the MLE is efficient.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) MLE for Bernoulli Parameter [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö PMF of a Single Observation:**
$$\Pr(X_i = x_i \mid p) = p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1-x_i}, \quad x_i \in \{0, 1\}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Likelihood Function:**
$$L(p) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1-x_i} = p^{\sum x_i}(1-p)^{N - \sum x_i}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Log-Likelihood:**
$$\ell(p) = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i\right)\ln p + \left(N - \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i\right)\ln(1-p)$$

Let $k = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ (the number of "successes"). Then:
$$\ell(p) = k\ln p + (N-k)\ln(1-p)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Differentiate and Set to Zero:**
$$\frac{\partial \ell(p)}{\partial p} = \frac{k}{p} - \frac{N-k}{1-p} = 0$$

$$k(1-p) = (N-k)p$$
$$k - kp = Np - kp$$
$$k = Np$$
$$\boxed{\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{k}{N} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = \bar{X}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Second-Order Verification:**
$$\frac{\partial^2 \ell(p)}{\partial p^2} = -\frac{k}{p^2} - \frac{N-k}{(1-p)^2} < 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Prove Unbiasedness:**
$$E[\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}}] = E\!\left[\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i\right] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} E[X_i] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} p = p \quad \checkmark$$

Hence $\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}}$ is an **unbiased** estimator of $p$.

**(b) Invariance Property + CRLB [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö MLE of Odds Ratio Using Invariance Property:**
The odds ratio is $\psi = g(p) = \dfrac{p}{1-p}$, which is a one-to-one function of $p$.

By the **invariance property of MLE**:
$$\hat{\psi}_{\text{MLE}} = g(\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}}) = \frac{\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}}}{1 - \hat{p}_{\text{MLE}}} = \frac{k/N}{1 - k/N} = \frac{k}{N-k}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Fisher Information for Bernoulli:**
$$\ln f(x \mid p) = x\ln p + (1-x)\ln(1-p)$$

$$\frac{\partial \ln f}{\partial p} = \frac{x}{p} - \frac{1-x}{1-p} = \frac{x - p}{p(1-p)}$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 \ln f}{\partial p^2} = -\frac{x}{p^2} - \frac{1-x}{(1-p)^2}$$

$$I(p) = -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln f}{\partial p^2}\right] = \frac{E[X]}{p^2} + \frac{1 - E[X]}{(1-p)^2} = \frac{p}{p^2} + \frac{1-p}{(1-p)^2}$$

$$\boxed{I(p) = \frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{1-p} = \frac{1}{p(1-p)}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö CRLB for $N$ Observations:**
$$\text{Var}(\hat{p}) \geq \frac{1}{N \cdot I(p)} = \frac{p(1-p)}{N}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verify MLE Achieves CRLB:**
$$\text{Var}(\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}}) = \text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{\text{Var}(X_i)}{N} = \frac{p(1-p)}{N}$$

This **equals** the CRLB exactly, confirming that $\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}}$ is an **efficient estimator** for the Bernoulli parameter. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in MLE Problems:**
> 1. **Skipping the Log Step (ŌĆō1 mark):** Examiners expect the $\ell(\theta) = \ln L(\theta)$ step explicitly. Differentiating products directly is mathematically valid but marks are reserved for the log-likelihood formulation.
> 2. **Missing Second-Order Verification (ŌĆō1 mark):** A complete MLE answer must confirm $\partial^2 \ell / \partial \theta^2 < 0$ at the critical point. Skipping this loses easy marks.
> 3. **Confusing Estimator vs. Estimate (ŌĆō1 to ŌĆō2 marks):** $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}}$ is a *random variable* (function of data), while $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}}$ for a specific dataset is a *number*. Examiners test this distinction rigorously.
> 4. **Forgetting to Compute $E[X^2]$ or $E[X]$ for Fisher Information (ŌĆō1 to ŌĆō2 marks):** The CRLB derivation demands specific moments of the distribution; many students substitute PDF into the formula without computing the required expectations.
> 5. **Applying CRLB to Biased Estimators (ŌĆō2 marks):** The CRLB applies to **unbiased estimators only**. Using it on a biased MLE (like Rayleigh $\sigma$) without comment is a classic error.
> 6. **Mixing up $L(\theta)$ with PDF $f(x)$ (ŌĆō1 mark):** Likelihood is a function of parameters; PDF is a function of data. Examiners frequently test this conceptual clarity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Maximum Likelihood Estimation**

### Core Concept
- **MLE Principle:** Find parameter value that **maximizes** the likelihood (probability) of observing the given data.
- **Two Equivalent Forms:** Maximize $L(\theta)$ directly or maximize $\ell(\theta) = \ln L(\theta)$.

### Operational Steps
- **Form $L(\theta) = \prod f(x_i \mid \theta)$**
- **Take $\ell(\theta) = \sum \ln f(x_i \mid \theta)$**
- **Solve $\partial \ell / \partial \theta = 0$**
- **Verify $\partial^2 \ell / \partial \theta^2 < 0$**

### Key Properties (Must Memorize)
- **Consistency:** $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} \xrightarrow{P} \theta$ as $N \to \infty$
- **Asymptotic Normality:** $\sqrt{N}(\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} - \theta) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, 1/I(\theta))$
- **Asymptotic Efficiency:** Achieves CRLB in the limit
- **Invariance:** $g(\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}})$ is MLE of $g(\theta)$
- **Sufficiency:** MLE is always a function of a sufficient statistic

### Fisher Information & CRLB
- **$I(\theta) = -E[\partial^2 \ln f / \partial \theta^2]$** (single observation)
- **$I_N(\theta) = N \cdot I(\theta)$** (N i.i.d. observations)
- **CRLB:** $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq 1/I_N(\theta)$
- **Efficient Estimator:** Achieves CRLB with equality

### Common MLE Results to Memorize
- Gaussian mean: $\hat{\mu} = \bar{X}$
- Gaussian variance: $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ (biased)
- Exponential rate: $\hat{\lambda} = 1/\bar{X}$
- Bernoulli: $\hat{p} = \bar{X}$
- Poisson: $\hat{\lambda} = \bar{X}$

### Engineering Applications
- Radar/Sonar parameter estimation
- Digital communication channel estimation
- Biomedical signal modeling (ECG/EEG)
- Speech processing (LPC coefficients)
- Machine learning (Logistic Regression, GMM-EM)

### Critical Pitfall Avoidance
- Always state the **log-likelihood** explicitly
- Always perform **second-derivative check**
- Distinguish **estimator** (random) from **estimate** (value)
- Apply CRLB only to **unbiased** estimators
- Verify **convergence conditions** for asymptotic properties
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Least Squares
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Least Squares

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Least Squares (LS) Estimation** method is a deterministic parameter estimation technique that determines the unknown parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ of a linear observation model by minimizing the sum of squared errors between the observed data and the model prediction. Formally, given a linear regression model

$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{w}$$

where $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ is the observation vector, $\mathbf{H} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times p}$ is the known observation (regressor) matrix, and $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ is the measurement error/noise vector, the Least Squares estimate $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS}$ is defined as the value of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ that minimizes the **cost (loss) function**

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - \mathbf{h}_i^{T}\boldsymbol{\theta} \right)^{2} = \left\| \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} \right\|_{2}^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor ŌĆö Module 2:** The Least Squares technique forms the bedrock of classical estimation theory. It is a **non-probabilistic (deterministic) estimator** in its simplest form, although it can be shown to coincide with the Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE) when the noise is zero-mean Gaussian with equal variance.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a class of engineering students plotting a graph of *distance (m)* vs. *time (s)* during a free-fall experiment. Due to measurement errors (stopwatch delays, parallax, air drag), the plotted points will not lie perfectly on a straight line. The students want to draw the "best" straight line through these scattered points.

If we pick a random line, the vertical gap between each point and the line is an "error." The **Least Squares** approach says: choose the slope and intercept such that the **sum of the squares of all these vertical errors is as small as possible**. Squares are used (not absolute values) because:
1. Squaring penalizes large errors heavily, preventing outliers from being ignored.
2. Squaring makes the function differentiable and well-behaved, allowing a clean closed-form solution.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Picture:** LS is the *projection of the observation vector onto the column space of the regressor matrix* in a Euclidean sense. It is essentially a **geometric projection** problem in disguise.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Significance

The Least Squares method is ubiquitous in:
- **Signal Processing:** Adaptive beamforming, channel equalization, system identification.
- **Communications:** Channel estimation in OFDM/MIMO systems, pilot-based estimation.
- **Control Systems:** ARX/ARMAX model identification, recursive system identification.
- **Geodesy & Navigation:** GPS position estimation, Kalman-filter initialization.
- **Machine Learning:** Linear/ridge regression, the foundation of neural network training (gradient descent on squared loss).

## 1.4 Standard Notation & Assumptions (KTU Board Standard)

| Symbol | Meaning | Dimension |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Number of observations (data points) | scalar |
| $p$ | Number of unknown parameters | scalar |
| $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ | Observation vector | $N \times 1$ |
| $\mathbf{H} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times p}$ | Observation (regressor) matrix | $N \times p$ |
| $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ | Unknown parameter vector | $p \times 1$ |
| $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ | Additive measurement noise | $N \times 1$ |
| $\mathbf{R} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}$ | Noise covariance matrix | $N \times N$ |
| $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ | The estimate of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ | $p \times 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Assumption (Standard LS):** The measurement noise $\mathbf{w}$ is assumed to be zero-mean, $\mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{w}\} = \mathbf{0}$, with covariance matrix $\mathbf{C}_w = \sigma^{2}\mathbf{I}$ (i.e., the noise samples are i.i.d. with equal variance). When this assumption is violated, **Weighted Least Squares (WLS)** is the appropriate extension.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Linear Observation Model (Operational Setup)

Consider a system in which an unknown constant parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta} = [\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_p]^{T}$ is observed through $N$ noisy linear measurements. Each observation $y_i$ is related to $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ by

$$y_i = \mathbf{h}_i^{T}\boldsymbol{\theta} + w_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, N$$

Stacking all $N$ equations into a single matrix-vector form:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ \vdots \\ y_N \end{bmatrix} &=
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{h}_1^{T} \\ \mathbf{h}_2^{T} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{h}_N^{T} \end{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{\theta} +
\begin{bmatrix} w_1 \\ w_2 \\ \vdots \\ w_N \end{bmatrix} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{y} &= \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{w}
\end{aligned}$$

The goal of LS estimation is to recover $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ using only the data $\{\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{H}\}$, without any probabilistic model of $\mathbf{w}$.

## 2.2 The Least Squares Cost Function

The cost (objective) function is defined as the squared Euclidean norm of the residual vector $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta}$:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \left\| \mathbf{e} \right\|^{2} = \left\| \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} \right\|^{2} = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})^{T}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

Expanding the scalar form:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - \mathbf{h}_i^{T}\boldsymbol{\theta} \right)^{2}$$

## 2.3 Minimization ŌĆö Why Differentiation Works

The function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ is a **convex quadratic** in $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ (since $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}$ is positive semi-definite). A convex function has a unique global minimum, which can be found by setting the gradient to zero:

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{0}$$

This yields the celebrated **Normal Equations**:

$$\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS} = \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y}$$

Provided that $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}$ is invertible (i.e., $\mathbf{H}$ has full column rank, $N \geq p$), the **closed-form LS solution** is:

$$\boxed{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS} = \left( \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y}}$$

The matrix $(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}$ is known as the **MooreŌĆōPenrose pseudo-inverse** of $\mathbf{H}$ and is denoted $\mathbf{H}^{\dagger}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Existence Condition:** The matrix $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}$ must be **non-singular** (positive definite). This requires $\text{rank}(\mathbf{H}) = p$, i.e., the observation matrix must have linearly independent columns. Equivalently, the **persistence of excitation** condition must be satisfied in adaptive systems.

## 2.4 Weighted Least Squares (WLS) ŌĆö When Noise is Heteroscedastic

If the noise has unequal variance across measurements, the standard LS becomes statistically inefficient. We assign a positive weight $r_i > 0$ to each measurement, giving a weighted cost function:

$$J_{W}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} r_i \left( y_i - \mathbf{h}_i^{T}\boldsymbol{\theta} \right)^{2} = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

where $\mathbf{R} = \text{diag}(1/r_1, 1/r_2, \dots, 1/r_N)$ is a diagonal positive-definite weight matrix (often chosen as the noise covariance $\mathbf{C}_w$ when $\mathbf{w}$ is correlated). Minimizing $J_W$ yields:

$$\boxed{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{WLS} = \left( \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{H} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{y}}$$

## 2.5 Properties of the LS Estimator

| # | Property | Statement | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Unbiasedness** | $\mathbb{E}\{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS}\} = \boldsymbol{\theta}$ | $\mathbf{H}$ deterministic, $\mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{w}\} = \mathbf{0}$ |
| 2 | **Minimum Variance (BLUE)** | Among all linear unbiased estimators, LS has the **smallest covariance** | Noise i.i.d. with $\mathbf{C}_w = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}$ |
| 3 | **GaussŌĆōMarkov Theorem** | WLS is the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)** | Noise $\mathbf{w} \sim (\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{C}_w)$ |
| 4 | **MLE Equivalence** | LS = MLE | $\mathbf{w} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2\mathbf{I})$ |
| 5 | **Covariance of error** | $\mathbf{C}_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}} = \sigma^2(\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}$ | Gaussian i.i.d. noise |
| 6 | **Residual orthogonality** | $\mathbf{H}^{T}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS}) = \mathbf{0}$ | Always (normal equations) |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Notes / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear model** | $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{w}$ | Standard linear regression setup |
| **LS Cost** | $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \Vert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} \Vert^{2}$ | Scalar form: $\sum (y_i - \mathbf{h}_i^{T}\boldsymbol{\theta})^2$ |
| **Normal equations** | $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y}$ | Obtained by $\nabla J = 0$ |
| **LS closed-form** | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS} = (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y}$ | Requires $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}$ invertible |
| **Pseudo-inverse** | $\mathbf{H}^{\dagger} = (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}$ | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \mathbf{H}^{\dagger}\mathbf{y}$ |
| **WLS solution** | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{WLS} = (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{y}$ | Weight matrix $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{C}_w$ |
| **LS error covariance** | $\mathbf{C}_{\hat{\theta}} = \sigma^{2}(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}$ | For i.i.d. Gaussian noise |
| **CramerŌĆōRao Bound** | $\mathbf{CRB}^{-1} = \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{C}_w^{-1}\mathbf{H}$ | LS achieves this for linear Gaussian models |
| **Residual** | $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS}$ | Orthogonal to columns of $\mathbf{H}$ |
| **MSE of LS** | $\mathbb{E}\{\Vert\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} - \boldsymbol{\theta}\Vert^2\} = \text{tr}(\mathbf{C}_{\hat{\theta}})$ | Aka estimation error squared norm |
| **Simple line fit** | $\hat{b} = \dfrac{N\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N\sum x_i^{2} - (\sum x_i)^{2}}$ | $y = a + bx$, scalar case |
| **Simple line intercept** | $\hat{a} = \bar{y} - \hat{b}\bar{x}$ | $\bar{x}, \bar{y}$ are sample means |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "derive" the LS solution, examiners expect you to write $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})^{T}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})$, expand it fully, take the gradient w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, set it to zero, and solve for $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$. Do **not** skip the expansion step ŌĆö it carries 2ŌĆō3 marks.

# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the LS Solution

We start with the linear model

$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{w}$$

and define the cost function

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})^{T}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

**Step 1: Expand the quadratic form.**

$$\begin{aligned}
J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) &= \mathbf{y}^{T}\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{y}^{T}\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} - (\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})^{T}\mathbf{y} + (\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})^{T}(\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta}) \\
&= \mathbf{y}^{T}\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{y}^{T}\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Use the scalar property** $a^{T}b = b^{T}a$ to combine the cross-terms:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{y}^{T}\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta}$$

> Here, $\mathbf{y}^{T}\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta}$ and $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y}$ are identical scalars, so they add up to $2\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y}$.

**Step 3: Take the gradient** with respect to $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ using the standard matrix calculus rules:
- $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} (\mathbf{c}^{T}\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{c}$
- $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} (\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\theta}) = (\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^{T})\boldsymbol{\theta} = 2\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\theta}$ (when $\mathbf{A}$ is symmetric)

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -2\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta}$$

**Step 4: Set the gradient to zero** (first-order necessary condition for a minimum):

$$-2\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS} &= \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y} \\
\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS} &= \left( \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y} \quad \text{(assuming } \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H} \text{ is invertible)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Verify the second-order condition** (sufficient for a minimum):

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{2} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = 2\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H} \succ \mathbf{0}$$

Since $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}$ is positive definite (when $\mathbf{H}$ has full column rank), $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ is strictly convex and the stationary point is indeed the **unique global minimum**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the WLS Solution

The Weighted LS cost function is

$$J_W(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

**Step 1: Expand.**

$$J_W(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{y}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta}$$

**Step 2: Differentiate and set to zero.**

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J_W(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -2\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{H}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{WLS} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 3: Solve.**

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{WLS} = \left( \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{H} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{y}$$

> **Intuition:** WLS premultiplies both $\mathbf{y}$ and $\mathbf{H}$ by $\mathbf{R}^{-1/2}$ to *whiten* the noise, then applies standard LS. Define $\tilde{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1/2}\mathbf{y}$ and $\tilde{\mathbf{H}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1/2}\mathbf{H}$, and the WLS solution is exactly the LS solution to the transformed system $\tilde{\mathbf{y}} = \tilde{\mathbf{H}}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \tilde{\mathbf{w}}$, where $\tilde{\mathbf{w}}$ now has unit covariance.

## 3.3 Worked Example ŌĆö Simple Linear Regression

**Problem:** Given $N = 4$ measurements $(x_i, y_i) = \{(1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 5), (4, 4)\}$, fit a line $y = a + bx$ using LS.

**Step 1: Construct the regressor matrix and observation vector.**

$$\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 5 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}$.**

$$\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 10 \\ 10 & 30 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y}$.**

$$\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 5 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 14 \\ 40 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Invert $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}$.**

The determinant is $\det(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}) = (4)(30) - (10)(10) = 120 - 100 = 20$.

$$(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1} = \frac{1}{20} \begin{bmatrix} 30 & -10 \\ -10 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Compute the LS estimate.**

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{a} \\ \hat{b} \end{bmatrix} = (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1} \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 14 \\ 40 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \cdot 14 - 0.5 \cdot 40 \\ -0.5 \cdot 14 + 0.2 \cdot 40 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 21 - 20 \\ -7 + 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6: Final fitted model.**

$$\boxed{\hat{y} = 1 + x}$$

**Step 7: Verify with the shortcut formula** (cross-check):

$$\hat{b} = \frac{N\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N\sum x_i^{2} - (\sum x_i)^{2}} = \frac{4(40) - (10)(14)}{4(30) - 100} = \frac{160 - 140}{20} = 1$$

$$\hat{a} = \bar{y} - \hat{b}\bar{x} = \frac{14}{4} - (1)\cdot\frac{10}{4} = 3.5 - 2.5 = 1$$

Both methods agree: $\hat{a} = 1, \hat{b} = 1$. Ō£ō

## 3.4 Unbiasedness of LS ŌĆö Proof

Assuming $\mathbf{H}$ is deterministic and $\mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{w}\} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS} &= (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y} \\
&= (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}(\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{w}) \\
&= (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} + (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{w} \\
&= \boldsymbol{\theta} + (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{w}
\end{aligned}$$

Taking expectation:

$$\mathbb{E}\{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS}\} = \boldsymbol{\theta} + (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{w}\} = \boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{0} = \boldsymbol{\theta}$$

Hence LS is **unbiased** under the zero-mean noise assumption.

## 3.5 Covariance of the LS Error ŌĆö Derivation

The estimation error is

$$\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS} - \boldsymbol{\theta} = (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{w}$$

The error covariance matrix is

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{C}_{\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}} &= \mathbb{E}\{\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{T}\} \\
&= \mathbb{E}\{(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{w}\,\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\} \\
&= (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\,\mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{w}\mathbf{w}^{T}\}\,\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

Assuming $\mathbf{w} \sim (\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2\mathbf{I})$, we have $\mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{w}\mathbf{w}^{T}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}$:

$$\mathbf{C}_{\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}} = \sigma^{2}(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1} = \sigma^{2}(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}$$

This is the celebrated LS error covariance formula.

## 3.6 Recursive Least Squares (RLS) ŌĆö Outline

For real-time / streaming applications, recomputing $(\mathbf{H}_N^{T}\mathbf{H}_N)^{-1}$ from scratch is $\mathcal{O}(Np^2)$ per update. **Recursive Least Squares** maintains a running estimate by updating an inverse correlation matrix $\mathbf{P}_N = (\mathbf{H}_N^{T}\mathbf{H}_N)^{-1}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{P}_N &= \mathbf{P}_{N-1} - \frac{\mathbf{P}_{N-1}\mathbf{h}_N \mathbf{h}_N^{T}\mathbf{P}_{N-1}}{1 + \mathbf{h}_N^{T}\mathbf{P}_{N-1}\mathbf{h}_N} \\
\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_N &= \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{N-1} + \mathbf{P}_N \mathbf{h}_N \left( y_N - \mathbf{h}_N^{T}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{N-1} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> RLS is **not** an explicit Module 2 KTU question topic in most universities, but is often asked as a 3-mark "short note" or a 7-mark sub-part in part-(b) of a 14-mark question.

# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Geometric Interpretation ŌĆö LS as an Orthogonal Projection

The following Mermaid block diagram visualizes the LS estimator as a projection of the observation vector $\mathbf{y}$ onto the column space of the regressor matrix $\mathbf{H}$. The fitted output $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{H}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS}$ is the closest point in the column space to $\mathbf{y}$, and the residual $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}$ is orthogonal to the column space.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Observation Vector y (in R^N)"] --> B["Column Space of H: span h1, h2, ..., hp"]
    B --> C["Fitted Vector y-hat = H times theta-LS"]
    A --> D["Residual e = y minus y-hat"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Orthogonality: H-transpose times e = 0"]
    C --> F["Minimize squared norm: min vertical bar y minus H theta vertical bar squared"]
    A --> F
    F --> G["LS Estimator: theta-LS = (H-transpose H) inverse H-transpose y"]
    G --> C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The observation vector $\mathbf{y}$ lives in $\mathbb{R}^{N}$ and is **not** in the column space of $\mathbf{H}$ (due to noise).
- The fitted vector $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{H}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ is the **orthogonal projection** of $\mathbf{y}$ onto the column space.
- The residual $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}$ is **orthogonal** to every column of $\mathbf{H}$: $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$.
- This orthogonality is exactly the **normal equations**.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture ŌĆö Weighted LS Estimator

The following block diagram shows the functional architecture of the Weighted LS estimator, illustrating how the weight matrix $\mathbf{R}^{-1}$ modifies both the regressor and the observation before inversion.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    input1["Input Observation Vector y"] --> mul1["Pre-multiply by R-inverse"]
    input2["Input Regressor Matrix H"] --> mul2["Pre-multiply by R-inverse"]
    mul1 --> stage1["Whitened Observation: y-tilde = R-inverse half y"]
    mul2 --> stage2["Whitened Regressor: H-tilde = R-inverse half H"]
    stage1 --> normeq["Normal Equation: H-tilde-transpose H-tilde times theta = H-tilde-transpose y-tilde"]
    stage2 --> normeq
    normeq --> invert["Matrix Inversion: (H-tilde-transpose H-tilde) inverse"]
    invert --> mul3["Multiply by H-tilde-transpose y-tilde"]
    mul3 --> output["Output Estimate: theta-WLS"]
```

**Operational Flow:**
1. **Whitening step:** $\mathbf{R}^{-1/2}$ decorrelates the noise, transforming the original colored-noise problem into an equivalent white-noise problem.
2. **Normal equation construction:** Build $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{H}$ and $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{y}$.
3. **Solve:** Invert the normal-equation matrix and multiply by the right-hand side.
4. **Output:** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{WLS}$.

## 4.3 Decision Flow ŌĆö When to Use LS vs WLS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Need to estimate unknown parameters from noisy data"] --> q1{"Is the model linear in parameters?"}
    q1 -->|No| nl["Use Non-Linear LS / Iterative methods / Gradient Descent"]
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{"Is noise i.i.d. with equal variance?"}
    q2 -->|Yes| standard["Use Standard LS: theta-LS = (H-transpose H) inverse H-transpose y"]
    q2 -->|No| q3{"Is noise covariance C-w known?"}
    q3 -->|Yes| wls["Use WLS / BLUE: theta-WLS = (H-transpose C-w inverse H) inverse H-transpose C-w inverse y"]
    q3 -->|No| robust["Use Robust LS / Total Least Squares / Regularized LS"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In many KTU board problems, the noise covariance is given as $\mathbf{C}_w = \text{diag}(\sigma_1^{2}, \sigma_2^{2}, \dots, \sigma_N^{2})$. Setting $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{C}_w$ in the WLS formula gives the **GaussŌĆōMarkov BLUE** estimate. Always state this connection explicitly to earn full marks.

## ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model]**
**Q: Define the Least Squares estimation problem. State the normal equations and the closed-form LS solution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Given the linear observation model $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{w}$ with $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$, $\mathbf{H} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times p}$, and unknown $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$, the Least Squares estimate $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS}$ is the value of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ that minimizes the cost function
> $$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \Vert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} \Vert^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - \mathbf{h}_i^{T}\boldsymbol{\theta} \right)^{2}$$
>
> **Normal equations (1 mark):** Setting $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}J = \mathbf{0}$ yields
> $$\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS} = \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y}$$
>
> **Closed-form solution (1 mark):** Provided $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}$ is invertible,
> $$\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS} = \left( \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y}$$
> The matrix $(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}$ is the pseudo-inverse of $\mathbf{H}$.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model]**
**Q: State and explain any three properties of the Least Squares estimator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks ŌĆö 1 mark each):**

> **Property 1 ŌĆö Unbiasedness:** If $\mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{w}\} = \mathbf{0}$ and $\mathbf{H}$ is deterministic, then $\mathbb{E}\{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS}\} = \boldsymbol{\theta}$, so the LS estimator is unbiased.
>
> **Property 2 ŌĆö Minimum Variance (GaussŌĆōMarkov):** Among all linear unbiased estimators of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, the LS estimator has the minimum error covariance matrix. It is therefore the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)**.
>
> **Property 3 ŌĆö Equivalence to MLE under Gaussian noise:** When the noise $\mathbf{w} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^{2}\mathbf{I})$, the LS estimate coincides with the Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE). This is because the Gaussian log-likelihood is a quadratic function whose maximum is identical to the minimum of $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$.

## ­¤ōŚ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts, typically carrying 7 + 7 marks, with internal choice between two full question sets (Question A and Question B). Solve either **OR** both as instructed by your paper setter.
