### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the Least Squares estimate for the linear model $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{w}$, and show that the resulting estimator is unbiased.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) ŌĆö State the model and define the cost function:**

$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{w}, \quad J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})^{T}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) ŌĆö Expand the quadratic:**

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{y}^{T}\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) ŌĆö Set gradient to zero:**

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J = -2\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \mathbf{0}$$

This yields the **normal equations**: $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y}$.

**Step 4 (1 mark) ŌĆö Solve:**

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS} = (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) ŌĆö Verify the second-order condition:** $\nabla^{2}J = 2\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H} \succ \mathbf{0}$, confirming a unique global minimum.

**Step 6 (2 marks) ŌĆö Prove unbiasedness:**

Substitute $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{w}$ into the LS estimate:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS} = (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}(\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{w}) = \boldsymbol{\theta} + (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{w}$$

Taking expectation and using $\mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{w}\} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\mathbb{E}\{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS}\} = \boldsymbol{\theta} \quad \Rightarrow \text{LS is unbiased.}$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Given the data points $(x, y) = \{(0, 1), (1, 2), (2, 4), (3, 5)\}$, fit a straight line $y = a + bx$ using the Least Squares method. Also compute the residual vector and the sum of squared errors.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) ŌĆö Form $\mathbf{H}$ and $\mathbf{y}$:**

$$\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 4 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) ŌĆö Compute $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}$:**

$$\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 6 \\ 6 & 14 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) ŌĆö Compute $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y}$:**

$$\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 12 \\ 25 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) ŌĆö Invert $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}$:** Determinant $= 56 - 36 = 20$.

$$(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1} = \frac{1}{20}\begin{bmatrix} 14 & -6 \\ -6 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) ŌĆö Compute $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$:**

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \frac{1}{20}\begin{bmatrix} 14 & -6 \\ -6 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 12 \\ 25 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{20}\begin{bmatrix} 168 - 150 \\ -72 + 100 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 \\ 1.4 \end{bmatrix}$$

So $\hat{a} = 0.9, \hat{b} = 1.4$, and the fitted line is $y = 0.9 + 1.4x$.

**Step 6 (1 mark) ŌĆö Compute the residual vector:**

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{H}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 \\ 0.9 + 1.4 \\ 0.9 + 2.8 \\ 0.9 + 4.2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 \\ 2.3 \\ 3.7 \\ 5.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \hat{\mathbf{y}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ -0.3 \\ 0.3 \\ -0.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark) ŌĆö Sum of squared errors (SSE):**

$$\text{SSE} = \Vert \mathbf{e} \Vert^{2} = (0.1)^2 + (-0.3)^2 + (0.3)^2 + (-0.1)^2 = 0.01 + 0.09 + 0.09 + 0.01 = 0.20$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the Weighted Least Squares (WLS) estimate. Show that WLS reduces to standard LS when $\mathbf{R} = \sigma^{2}\mathbf{I}$.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) ŌĆö Define the WLS cost function:**

$$J_W(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

where $\mathbf{R}$ is a positive-definite symmetric weight matrix (typically the noise covariance $\mathbf{C}_w$).

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) ŌĆö Expand and differentiate:**

$$J_W(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{y}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta}$$

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J_W = -2\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{H}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{WLS} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) ŌĆö Solve for $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{WLS}$:**

$$\boxed{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{WLS} = \left( \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{H} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{y}}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) ŌĆö Whitening interpretation:**

Define $\mathbf{R}^{1/2}$ such that $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{R}^{1/2}\mathbf{R}^{1/2}$ (Cholesky factorization). Let $\tilde{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1/2}\mathbf{y}$ and $\tilde{\mathbf{H}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1/2}\mathbf{H}$. Then $\tilde{\mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1/2}\mathbf{w}$ has covariance $\mathbf{I}$. The WLS estimate equals the standard LS estimate of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ in the whitened system $\tilde{\mathbf{y}} = \tilde{\mathbf{H}}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \tilde{\mathbf{w}}$.

**Step 5 (1 mark) ŌĆö Reduction to standard LS:**

When $\mathbf{R} = \sigma^{2}\mathbf{I}$, then $\mathbf{R}^{-1} = \frac{1}{\sigma^{2}}\mathbf{I}$, and

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{WLS} = \left( \mathbf{H}^{T}\frac{1}{\sigma^{2}}\mathbf{I}\mathbf{H} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{H}^{T}\frac{1}{\sigma^{2}}\mathbf{I}\mathbf{y} = (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y} = \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS}$$

Thus, WLS collapses to standard LS for i.i.d. noise. Ō£ō

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Discuss the properties of the Least Squares estimator in detail. In particular, state the GaussŌĆōMarkov theorem and explain why LS is the Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE).**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) ŌĆö Unbiasedness:** Already proved in Q-A. Re-state: $\mathbb{E}\{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS}\} = \boldsymbol{\theta}$.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) ŌĆö Error Covariance:** Derived as $\mathbf{C}_{\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}} = \sigma^{2}(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}$ for i.i.d. noise. Smaller $\Vert \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H} \Vert$ implies larger uncertainty ŌĆö measurements with diverse regressor directions reduce variance.

**Step 3 (2 marks) ŌĆö GaussŌĆōMarkov Theorem Statement:** *Among all linear unbiased estimators of the form $\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{y}$ (with $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}$), the LS estimator $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS} = (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y}$ has the minimum error covariance matrix. In other words, for any other linear unbiased estimator $\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$,*

$$\mathbf{C}_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS}} \preceq \mathbf{C}_{\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) ŌĆö Proof sketch:** Let $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{C}_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS}} - \mathbf{C}_{\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}}$. Then $\mathbf{C} = \sigma^{2}[(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{T}]$ where $\mathbf{A}$ is any matrix satisfying $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}$. Expanding and using $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}$:

$$\mathbf{C} = \sigma^{2}(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}[\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{A}^{T} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{H} + \mathbf{A}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{A}^{T}] = \sigma^{2}(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{D}\mathbf{D}^{T} \succeq \mathbf{0}$$

where $\mathbf{D} = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{H}$. Hence $\mathbf{C} \succeq \mathbf{0}$, so LS has minimum variance.

**Step 5 (1 mark) ŌĆö MLE equivalence:** When $\mathbf{w} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^{2}\mathbf{I})$, the log-likelihood is $\log L = -\frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^{2}) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}\Vert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta}\Vert^{2}$. Maximizing $L$ is equivalent to minimizing $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$, so $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS} = \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{MLE}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **­¤ö┤ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Skipping the expansion of $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$:** Many students jump from $J = (\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})^{T}(\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})$ directly to "set gradient to zero" without showing the expansion. This costs **2ŌĆō3 marks** in the derivation.
> 2. **Forgetting the invertibility condition:** LS requires $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}$ to be invertible. Always state this explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing LS with MLE:** They are *equivalent* only under Gaussian i.i.d. noise. For non-Gaussian noise, they differ.
> 4. **Computing $(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}$ without verifying the determinant is non-zero:** Always show $\det(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}) \neq 0$ in numerical problems.
> 5. **In WLS, omitting the requirement that $\mathbf{R}$ be positive definite:** If $\mathbf{R}$ is singular, $\mathbf{R}^{-1}$ does not exist. State the assumption explicitly.
> 6. **In the unbiasedness proof, students often forget to state $\mathbf{H}$ is deterministic.** This is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

## ­¤öü Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Ō£ģ **Standard Linear Model:** $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{w}$, with $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$, $\mathbf{H} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times p}$, $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$.
- Ō£ģ **LS Cost Function:** $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \Vert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta}\Vert^{2} = \sum (y_i - \mathbf{h}_i^{T}\boldsymbol{\theta})^2$.
- Ō£ģ **Normal Equations (must memorize):** $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS} = \mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y}$.
- Ō£ģ **Closed-Form LS Solution (must memorize):** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS} = (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{y}$.
- Ō£ģ **Pseudo-Inverse Notation:** $\mathbf{H}^{\dagger} = (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}$, so $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS} = \mathbf{H}^{\dagger}\mathbf{y}$.
- Ō£ģ **Existence Condition:** $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H}$ must be invertible $\Leftrightarrow$ $\text{rank}(\mathbf{H}) = p \Leftrightarrow$ columns of $\mathbf{H}$ linearly independent.
- Ō£ģ **WLS Cost:** $J_W(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})$.
- Ō£ģ **WLS Solution:** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{WLS} = (\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{y}$.
- Ō£ģ **WLS = BLUE** when $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{C}_w$ (GaussŌĆōMarkov theorem).
- Ō£ģ **LS is Unbiased** if $\mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{w}\} = \mathbf{0}$ and $\mathbf{H}$ deterministic.
- Ō£ģ **LS Error Covariance (i.i.d. noise):** $\mathbf{C}_{\hat{\theta}} = \sigma^{2}(\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{H})^{-1}$.
- Ō£ģ **LS = MLE** iff $\mathbf{w} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^{2}\mathbf{I})$.
- Ō£ģ **Residual Orthogonality Principle:** $\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$, where $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{LS}$.
- Ō£ģ **Geometric Picture:** LS projects $\mathbf{y}$ orthogonally onto the column space of $\mathbf{H}$.
- Ō£ģ **Simple 2-parameter line fit shortcuts:** $\hat{b} = \frac{N\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N\sum x_i^{2} - (\sum x_i)^{2}}$ and $\hat{a} = \bar{y} - \hat{b}\bar{x}$.
- Ō£ģ **RLS update equations:** $\mathbf{P}_N = \mathbf{P}_{N-1} - \frac{\mathbf{P}_{N-1}\mathbf{h}_N\mathbf{h}_N^{T}\mathbf{P}_{N-1}}{1 + \mathbf{h}_N^{T}\mathbf{P}_{N-1}\mathbf{h}_N}$.
- Ō£ģ **Common Pitfall:** Do not confuse "best" (minimum variance) with "perfect." LS is optimal only within the class of *linear unbiased* estimators.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bayesian Philosophy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bayesian Philosophy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bayesian Philosophy** is a statistical inference paradigm in which the unknown parameter $\theta$ (to be estimated) or the true hypothesis (to be detected) is treated as a **random variable** described by a **prior probability distribution** $p(\theta)$. New observations $\mathbf{x}$ are incorporated via **Bayes' Theorem** to produce an updated **posterior distribution** $p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})$, which then drives all subsequent estimation or detection decisions by minimizing a chosen **Bayes risk** (expected cost).

The Bayesian framework unifies **estimation** and **detection** under one umbrella:

- In **Bayesian Estimation**, the posterior $p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})$ is summarized by an estimator (e.g., mean, mode).
- In **Bayesian Detection** (hypothesis testing), the posterior odds ratio is compared against a threshold derived from prior probabilities and costs.

Mathematically, the central object of Bayesian philosophy is **Bayes' rule**:

$$p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)\, p(\theta)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$$

where the denominator is the **evidence** (or marginal likelihood):

$$p(\mathbf{x}) = \int_{\Theta} p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)\, p(\theta)\, d\theta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Bayesian Reasoning (as per KTU Module 2):**
> 1. **Prior** $p(\theta)$ ŌĆö what we believe *before* seeing data.
> 2. **Likelihood** $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)$ ŌĆö what the data say about $\theta$.
> 3. **Posterior** $p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})$ ŌĆö what we believe *after* combining (1) and (2).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Weather Forecaster's Dilemma**

Imagine a Kerala weather forecaster trying to estimate the *probability of heavy monsoon rainfall* tomorrow.

- **Classical (Frequentist) approach:** Wait for thousands of years of past rainfall data and compute relative frequencies. No prior belief is allowed.
- **Bayesian approach:** The forecaster already has a *gut feeling* (e.g., "monsoon has been weak for 3 days, so the prior chance of heavy rain is 30%"). Then the new satellite image arrives ŌĆö this is the *likelihood*. The forecaster **updates** the 30% prior into a new *posterior* probability (say 65%) using Bayes' rule.

This act of *progressively refining belief with new evidence* is the heart of Bayesian philosophy. In engineering:

- The unknown parameter $\theta$ might be the **SNR** of a received signal.
- The prior $p(\theta)$ reflects *channel knowledge* (e.g., Rayleigh fading assumption).
- The observation $\mathbf{x}$ is the **received noisy samples**.
- The posterior $p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})$ yields the **optimal Bayesian estimator**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Contrast with Classical/Frequentist Philosophy:**
>
> | Aspect | Frequentist | Bayesian |
> |---|---|---|
> | Nature of $\theta$ | Fixed but unknown constant | Random variable |
> | Probability interpretation | Long-run frequency | Degree of belief |
> | Role of prior | Ignored | Central |
> | Output of procedure | Point estimate + confidence | Full posterior distribution |
> | Use of data | Only sample-driven | Sample + prior combined |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Bayesian Metrics Used Throughout Module 2:**
>
> - **Bayes Risk:** $R = E[C(\hat{\theta}, \theta)]$ ŌĆö expected value of cost function (units: cost).
> - **Posterior Mean (MMSE estimator):** $\hat{\theta}_{MMSE} = E[\theta \mid \mathbf{x}]$.
> - **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimator:** $\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta} p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})$.
> - **Likelihood Ratio:** $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)}$.
> - **Posterior Odds:** $\frac{P(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x})}{P(H_0 \mid \mathbf{x})}$.
> - **Average Probability of Error:** $P_e = P(H_0)P_{10} + P(H_1)P_{01}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of Bayesian Inference

The Bayesian pipeline for both estimation and detection follows a rigorous 5-stage architecture:

1. **Define the parameter space $\Theta$** and the observation space $\mathcal{X}$.
2. **Specify the prior distribution** $p(\theta)$ encoding *a priori* knowledge (or using non-informative priors if no knowledge exists).
3. **Model the likelihood** $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)$ based on the physical observation mechanism (e.g., AWGN channel, Poisson counting process).
4. **Apply Bayes' rule** to compute the posterior $p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})$.
5. **Define a cost function** $C(\hat{\theta}, \theta)$ (estimation) or a decision rule $d(\mathbf{x}) \in \{H_0, H_1\}$ (detection), and choose the action that minimizes the expected posterior cost (= Bayes risk).

### 2.1.1 Why Each Step Matters

- **Step 2 (Prior):** Captures engineering insight. A good prior *regularizes* estimation in low-SNR or small-sample regimes.
- **Step 3 (Likelihood):** Encodes the physics of the sensor/channel.
- **Step 4 (Posterior):** The posterior is *all* we need ŌĆö once computed, the prior and likelihood are no longer required for decision-making.
- **Step 5 (Decision):** The decision rule is obtained by minimizing the **posterior expected loss**:
$$\hat{\theta}_{opt} = \arg\min_{\hat{\theta}} \; E_{\theta \mid \mathbf{x}}\bigl[C(\hat{\theta}, \theta)\bigr] = \arg\min_{\hat{\theta}} \int_{\Theta} C(\hat{\theta}, \theta)\, p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})\, d\theta$$

---

## 2.2 Bayesian Estimation ŌĆö Cost Functions and Estimators

Different cost functions lead to different optimal estimators. The most common (all KTU-exam relevant):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Cost Functions:**
>
> 1. **Squared-Error Cost** $C(\hat{\theta}, \theta) = (\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2$
>    - Optimal estimator = **Posterior Mean** = MMSE estimator.
>    - $\hat{\theta}_{MMSE} = E[\theta \mid \mathbf{x}] = \int \theta\, p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})\, d\theta$.
>
> 2. **Absolute-Error Cost** $C(\hat{\theta}, \theta) = \vert \hat{\theta} - \theta \vert$
>    - Optimal estimator = **Posterior Median**.
>
> 3. **Uniform (0-1) Cost** $C(\hat{\theta}, \theta) = 1 - \Delta(\hat{\theta} - \theta)$
>    - Optimal estimator = **Posterior Mode** = **MAP** estimator.
>    - $\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta} p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})$.

A computationally critical observation is:

$$\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta}\, p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_{\theta}\, p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)\, p(\theta)$$

because the evidence $p(\mathbf{x})$ does not depend on $\theta$. Often we further maximize the log:

$$\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta}\, \bigl[\ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta) + \ln p(\theta)\bigr]$$

This is the **log-posterior**, and it beautifully fuses MLE (likelihood term) with regularization (prior term).

---

## 2.3 Bayesian Detection ŌĆö Hypothesis Testing

In Bayesian detection, we choose between two hypotheses $H_0$ (signal absent) and $H_1$ (signal present) to **minimize the Bayes risk**:

$$R = C_{00}P(H_0)P(D_0 \mid H_0) + C_{10}P(H_0)P(D_1 \mid H_0) + C_{11}P(H_1)P(D_1 \mid H_1) + C_{01}P(H_1)P(D_0 \mid H_1)$$

where $C_{ij}$ is the cost of deciding $H_i$ when $H_j$ is true. The **likelihood ratio test (LRT)** is:

$$\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \triangleq \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)} \;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\; \eta$$

where the **Bayes threshold** $\eta$ is:

$$\eta = \frac{P(H_0)\,(C_{10} - C_{00})}{P(H_1)\,(C_{01} - C_{11})}$$

**Special case ŌĆö Minimum Probability of Error (MPE):** If costs are $C_{00} = C_{11} = 0$ and $C_{10} = C_{01} = 1$, then $\eta = \frac{P(H_0)}{P(H_1)}$, and the test reduces to choosing the hypothesis with larger posterior probability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalence of Three Bayesian Detection Formulations (KTU High-Yield):**
>
> $$\frac{P(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x})}{P(H_0 \mid \mathbf{x})} \;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\; 1 \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\; \frac{P(H_0)}{P(H_1)} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; p(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x}) \;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\; p(H_0 \mid \mathbf{x})$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table ŌĆö Bayesian Philosophy (Module 2)**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem | $p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)\,p(\theta)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$ | Core update rule |
| 2 | Marginal Likelihood | $p(\mathbf{x}) = \int p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)\,p(\theta)\,d\theta$ | Normalizing constant |
| 3 | Posterior Mean (MMSE) | $\hat{\theta}_{MMSE} = E[\theta \mid \mathbf{x}]$ | Squared-error cost |
| 4 | MAP Estimator | $\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max \, p(\mathbf{x}\mid\theta)\,p(\theta)$ | 0-1 cost |
| 5 | Posterior Median | $\hat{\theta}_{med} : \int_{-\infty}^{\hat{\theta}} p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x}) d\theta = 0.5$ | Absolute-error cost |
| 6 | Bayes Risk | $R = E\bigl[C(\hat{\theta},\theta)\bigr]$ | Optimality measure |
| 7 | LRT Statistic | $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)}$ | Detection |
| 8 | Bayes Threshold | $\eta = \dfrac{P(H_0)(C_{10}-C_{00})}{P(H_1)(C_{01}-C_{11})}$ | Detection rule |
| 9 | Min $P_e$ Threshold | $\eta = \dfrac{P(H_0)}{P(H_1)}$ | Symmetric 0-1 cost |
| 10 | Average $P_e$ | $P_e = P(H_0)P_{10} + P(H_1)P_{01}$ | Performance metric |
| 11 | Conjugate (Gaussian) Prior mean update | $\mu_{n} = \sigma_{n}^{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\mu_0}{\sigma_0^{2}} + \dfrac{n\bar{x}}{\sigma^{2}}\right)$ | Sequential Bayesian |
| 12 | Conjugate (Gaussian) Posterior variance | $\dfrac{1}{\sigma_{n}^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_{0}^{2}} + \dfrac{n}{\sigma^{2}}$ | Sequential Bayesian |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Bayesian philosophy is the *conceptual backbone* of modern adaptive radar, cognitive radio spectrum sensing, ML-based channel estimation, GPS positioning (Kalman filter), medical image reconstruction, and even spam filters. The MAP/MMSE estimators derived here are the building blocks of the **Kalman filter** and **Wiener filter**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the MMSE (Posterior Mean) Estimator

We begin with the squared-error cost $C(\hat{\theta}, \theta) = (\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2$. The Bayes risk under this cost is:

$$R(\hat{\theta}) = E_{\mathbf{x},\theta}\bigl[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2\bigr] = \int_{\mathcal{X}}\!\int_{\Theta} (\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2\, p(\mathbf{x},\theta)\, d\theta\, d\mathbf{x}$$

Since $p(\mathbf{x},\theta) = p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})\, p(\mathbf{x})$, we can condition on $\mathbf{x}$:

$$R(\hat{\theta}) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} p(\mathbf{x})\!\left[\int_{\Theta} (\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2\, p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})\, d\theta\right] d\mathbf{x}$$

To minimize $R$, we minimize the inner **posterior risk** $R_p(\hat{\theta} \mid \mathbf{x}) = E\bigl[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2 \mid \mathbf{x}\bigr]$ for each $\mathbf{x}$.

Differentiating and setting to zero (assuming regularity):

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{\theta}} R_p(\hat{\theta} \mid \mathbf{x}) = 2\!\left(\hat{\theta} - E[\theta \mid \mathbf{x}]\right) = 0$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{\hat{\theta}_{MMSE} = E[\theta \mid \mathbf{x}] = \int_{\Theta} \theta\, p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})\, d\theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The second derivative** $\frac{\partial^2 R_p}{\partial \hat{\theta}^2} = 2 > 0$, confirming this is a **minimum**, not a maximum.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the MAP Estimator

Using the uniform (0-1) cost function:

$$C(\hat{\theta}, \theta) = \begin{cases} 0, & \hat{\theta} = \theta \\ 1, & \hat{\theta} \neq \theta \end{cases}$$

The posterior risk is:

$$R_p(\hat{\theta} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \int_{\Theta} C(\hat{\theta}, \theta)\, p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})\, d\theta = 1 - p(\theta = \hat{\theta} \mid \mathbf{x})$$

Minimizing $R_p$ is equivalent to **maximizing the posterior density**:

$$\boxed{\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta}\, p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})}$$

Using Bayes' rule:

$$\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta}\, \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)\, p(\theta)}{p(\mathbf{x})} = \arg\max_{\theta}\, \bigl[\ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta) + \ln p(\theta)\bigr]$$

This shows MAP = MLE + log-prior correction term. If the prior is uniform (non-informative), MAP $\equiv$ MLE.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example ŌĆö Gaussian Likelihood + Gaussian Prior (Conjugate Pair)

Let $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, \sigma^2)$ be a single observation with known $\sigma^2 = 1$. Let the prior be $\theta \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \sigma_0^2)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the likelihood:**

$$p(x \mid \theta) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\theta)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Write the prior:**

$$p(\theta) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma_0}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(\theta-\mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Multiply likelihood and prior:**

$$\begin{aligned}
p(x \mid \theta)\,p(\theta) &\propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\theta)^2}{2\sigma^2} - \frac{(\theta-\mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2}\right) \\
&= \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}\!\left[\frac{\sigma_0^2(x-\theta)^2 + \sigma^2(\theta-\mu_0)^2}{\sigma^2\sigma_0^2}\right]\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Complete the square in $\theta$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
&\sigma_0^2(x-\theta)^2 + \sigma^2(\theta-\mu_0)^2 \\
&\quad = (\sigma^2 + \sigma_0^2)\theta^2 - 2(\sigma_0^2 x + \sigma^2 \mu_0)\theta + \text{const} \\
&\quad = (\sigma^2 + \sigma_0^2)\!\left(\theta - \frac{\sigma_0^2 x + \sigma^2 \mu_0}{\sigma^2 + \sigma_0^2}\right)^2 + \text{const}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Identify the posterior as Gaussian:**

$$p(\theta \mid x) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu_n,\, \sigma_n^2\right)$$

with:

$$\boxed{\mu_n = \frac{\sigma_0^2\, x + \sigma^2\, \mu_0}{\sigma^2 + \sigma_0^2}, \quad \frac{1}{\sigma_n^2} = \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma^2}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\mu_n$ is a **precision-weighted average** of prior mean $\mu_0$ and data $x$.
- Posterior precision = prior precision + data precision (evidence accumulates additively in information space).
- MMSE estimator = MAP estimator (Gaussian is symmetric) = $\mu_n$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Bayes Risk Detection Rule

We minimize the total Bayes risk over the decision rule $\delta(\mathbf{x}) \in \{H_0, H_1\}$:

$$R = C_{00}\,P(H_0)\,P(\delta = H_0 \mid H_0) + C_{10}\,P(H_0)\,P(\delta = H_1 \mid H_0) + C_{11}\,P(H_1)\,P(\delta = H_1 \mid H_1) + C_{01}\,P(H_1)\,P(\delta = H_0 \mid H_1)$$

For each $\mathbf{x}$, we choose to decide $H_1$ only if:

$$C_{01}\,P(H_1)\,p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1) < C_{10}\,P(H_0)\,p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)$$

i.e., the expected cost of choosing $H_1$ is less than that of choosing $H_0$. Rearranging:

$$\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)} \;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\; \frac{P(H_0)(C_{10} - C_{00})}{P(H_1)(C_{01} - C_{11})} = \eta$$

This is the **fundamental Bayesian detection rule**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Bayesian Philosophy (KTU Cognitive Map)

| KTU CO / Cognitive Level | Concept Tested | Required Skill |
|---|---|---|
| CO2 / Understand | Bayes' theorem decomposition | Identify prior, likelihood, posterior |
| CO2 / Apply | MAP estimator derivation | Set derivative of log-posterior to zero |
| CO2 / Apply | Conjugate prior calculation | Gaussian-Gaussian update |
| CO3 / Analyze | LRT threshold under custom costs | Substitute into $\eta$ formula |
| CO3 / Evaluate | Compare MMSE vs MAP vs MLE | Discuss bias-variance tradeoff |
| CO3 / Apply | Min $P_e$ detection design | Compute threshold from priors only |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bayesian Inference Pipeline (Block Diagram)

The following diagram captures the complete information flow of the Bayesian philosophy as applied to estimation and detection problems:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PRIOR KNOWLEDGE p(theta)"] --> C["BAYES ENGINE"]
    B["LIKELIHOOD MODEL p(x given theta)"] --> C
    C --> D["POSTERIOR p(theta given x)"]
    D --> E{"DECISION MODULE"}
    E --> F["MMSE ESTIMATOR"]
    E --> G["MAP ESTIMATOR"]
    E --> H["BAYES LRT DETECTOR"]
    F --> I["BAYES RISK MINIMIZATION"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["FINAL OUTPUT: theta-hat OR H0/H1"]
```

---

## 4.2 Bayesian Detection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O["OBSERVATION x"] --> L["COMPUTE LIKELIHOOD RATIO Lambda(x)"]
    L --> T{"Lambda(x) compared to eta"}
    T -- "greater than eta" --> H1["DECIDE H1: Signal Present"]
    T -- "less than eta" --> H0["DECIDE H0: Signal Absent"]
    H1 --> R["UPDATE POSTERIOR P(H1 given x)"]
    H0 --> R
    R --> P["COMPUTE PROBABILITY OF ERROR Pe"]
```

---

## 4.3 Bayesian Estimation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["POSTERIOR p(theta given x)"] --> Q1{"Cost Function C?"}
    Q1 -- "Squared Error" --> MMSE["Posterior Mean E theta given x"]
    Q1 -- "Absolute Error" --> MED["Posterior Median"]
    Q1 -- "0-1 Uniform" --> MAPE["Posterior Mode = MAP"]
    MMSE --> OUT["Optimal Estimate theta-hat"]
    MED --> OUT
    MAPE --> OUT
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison Flow ŌĆö Frequentist vs Bayesian

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["Fixed Parameter theta"] --> F2["Sample Data x"]
    F2 --> F3["Likelihood p(x given theta)"]
    F3 --> F4["Confidence Interval / p-value"]
    B1["Random Parameter theta with prior p(theta)"] --> B2["Sample Data x"]
    B2 --> B3["Likelihood p(x given theta)"]
    B3 --> B4["Posterior p(theta given x) via Bayes"]
    B4 --> B5["Credible Interval / Posterior Mean"]
```

---

## 4.5 Conjugate Updating ŌĆö GaussianŌĆōGaussian Pair

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PRIOR["Prior: theta ~ N(mu0, sigma0^2)"] --> ENG["Bayesian Engine"]
    DATA["Data: x1 to xn iid N(theta, sigma^2)"] --> ENG
    ENG --> POST["Posterior: theta given x ~ N(mu_n, sigma_n^2)"]
    POST --> NEWPRIOR["Posterior Becomes Next Prior"]
    NEWPRIOR -.-> ENG
```

This last diagram illustrates the recursive nature of Bayesian updating ŌĆö each posterior becomes the prior for the next observation. This is the conceptual heart of the **Kalman filter**.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State and explain Bayes' theorem in the context of parameter estimation. Define each term clearly.**

**Model Answer:**

Bayes' theorem updates our belief about an unknown parameter $\theta$ after observing data $\mathbf{x}$:

$$p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)\, p(\theta)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$$

- $p(\theta)$: **Prior distribution** ŌĆö probability density of $\theta$ before observing data.
- $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)$: **Likelihood function** ŌĆö probability of observing $\mathbf{x}$ given $\theta$.
- $p(\mathbf{x}) = \int p(\mathbf{x}\mid\theta)p(\theta)\,d\theta$: **Evidence** ŌĆö normalizing constant.
- $p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})$: **Posterior distribution** ŌĆö updated belief about $\theta$ after observing $\mathbf{x}$.

In estimation, the posterior contains all information needed for inference. Estimators like MMSE (= posterior mean) and MAP (= posterior mode) are summaries of $p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) and Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation. When do they coincide?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | MLE | MAP |
|---|---|---|
| Optimization criterion | Maximizes $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)$ | Maximizes $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)\,p(\theta)$ |
| Uses prior? | No | Yes |
| Estimator equation | $\dfrac{\partial \ln p(\mathbf{x}\mid\theta)}{\partial \theta} = 0$ | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\bigl[\ln p(\mathbf{x}\mid\theta) + \ln p(\theta)\bigr] = 0$ |
| Bayesian? | Frequentist | Bayesian |

**They coincide when the prior is uniform** (i.e., $p(\theta) = $ constant over the parameter space), because then maximizing $p(\mathbf{x}\mid\theta)\,p(\theta)$ reduces to maximizing $p(\mathbf{x}\mid\theta)$. Similarly, if the data is overwhelmingly informative, the prior's influence becomes negligible and MLE $\approx$ MAP.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. In Bayes' theorem derivations, students often forget the normalizing constant $p(\mathbf{x})$ and lose 2 marks. Always state *why* it can be dropped during MAP optimization.
> 2. In likelihood ratio test problems, students confuse the threshold formula $\eta = \frac{P(H_0)(C_{10}-C_{00})}{P(H_1)(C_{01}-C_{11})}$ with $\eta = \frac{P(H_1)}{P(H_0)}$. Memorize the exact form with the cost differences.
> 3. In conjugate prior problems, present your posterior parameters clearly in a box ŌĆö partial credit is given even if final numerical substitution is wrong.
> 4. Always state the units of cost and Bayes risk; examiners check for physical interpretation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 ŌĆö Set A]`

**(a)** Derive the **MMSE estimator** for a scalar parameter $\theta$ given an observation $x$ under squared-error cost $C(\hat{\theta},\theta) = (\hat{\theta}-\theta)^2$. Show all steps rigorously. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Let $x \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta, 4)$ (a single observation) with prior $\theta \sim \mathcal{N}(2, 1)$. Compute the **MMSE estimate** of $\theta$ given $x = 3$. Also compute the posterior variance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a)** [7 marks]

Bayes risk under squared-error cost:

$$R(\hat{\theta}) = E\bigl[(\hat{\theta}-\theta)^2\bigr] = \int_{\mathcal{X}}\!\int_{\Theta} (\hat{\theta}-\theta)^2\, p(\mathbf{x},\theta)\, d\theta\, d\mathbf{x}$$

Conditioning on $\mathbf{x}$:

$$R(\hat{\theta}) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} p(\mathbf{x})\!\left[\int_{\Theta}(\hat{\theta}-\theta)^2\, p(\theta\mid\mathbf{x})\,d\theta\right] d\mathbf{x}$$

Minimize the inner posterior risk:

$$R_p(\hat{\theta}\mid\mathbf{x}) = \int_{\Theta}(\hat{\theta}-\theta)^2\, p(\theta\mid\mathbf{x})\,d\theta$$

Take derivative with respect to $\hat{\theta}$:

$$\frac{\partial R_p}{\partial \hat{\theta}} = 2\int_{\Theta}(\hat{\theta}-\theta)\, p(\theta\mid\mathbf{x})\,d\theta = 2\!\left(\hat{\theta} - \int_{\Theta}\theta\, p(\theta\mid\mathbf{x})\,d\theta\right)$$

Setting to zero and noting $\frac{\partial^2 R_p}{\partial \hat{\theta}^2} = 2 > 0$:

$$\boxed{\hat{\theta}_{MMSE} = E[\theta \mid \mathbf{x}]}$$

**(b)** [7 marks]

Given: $p(x\mid\theta) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\cdot 2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\theta)^2}{8}\right)$, prior $\theta \sim \mathcal{N}(2, 1)$.

Using the conjugate Gaussian result from Section 3.3, with $\sigma^2 = 4$, $\sigma_0^2 = 1$, $\mu_0 = 2$, $x = 3$:

Posterior variance:

$$\frac{1}{\sigma_n^2} = \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma^2} = \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{5}{4} \implies \sigma_n^2 = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$$

Posterior mean (= MMSE estimate):

$$\mu_n = \frac{\sigma_0^2 x + \sigma^2 \mu_0}{\sigma_0^2 + \sigma^2} = \frac{(1)(3) + (4)(2)}{1 + 4} = \frac{3 + 8}{5} = \frac{11}{5} = 2.2$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\theta}_{MMSE} = 2.2, \quad \sigma_n^2 = 0.8}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The estimate 2.2 lies between the prior mean (2) and the data value (3), pulled toward the prior because the prior is more confident (smaller variance 1) than the data (variance 4).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 ŌĆö Set B]` ŌĆö *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **likelihood ratio test (LRT)** for binary hypothesis testing. Derive the **Bayes risk threshold** $\eta$ for the decision rule. State and explain the special case when costs are symmetric (0-1). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a binary communication system, hypotheses are equally likely ($P(H_0) = P(H_1) = 0.5$) and the likelihoods under each hypothesis are:
- $p(x \mid H_0) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp(-x^2/2)$
- $p(x \mid H_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp(-(x-1)^2/2)$

Using the minimum probability of error criterion, decide $H_1$ or $H_0$ when $x = 0.5$. Compute the threshold and explain the decision. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a)** [7 marks]

The likelihood ratio test compares the ratio of likelihoods to a threshold:

$$\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_0)} \;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\; \eta$$

To find $\eta$, write total Bayes risk:

$$R = C_{00}P(H_0)P_{00} + C_{10}P(H_0)P_{10} + C_{11}P(H_1)P_{11} + C_{01}P(H_1)P_{01}$$

where $P_{ij} = P(\text{decide }H_i \mid H_j \text{ true})$. For each $\mathbf{x}$, decide $H_1$ iff expected cost of $H_1$ $<$ expected cost of $H_0$:

$$(C_{01}-C_{11})P(H_1)\,p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_1) < (C_{10}-C_{00})P(H_0)\,p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_0)$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{P(H_0)(C_{10}-C_{00})}{P(H_1)(C_{01}-C_{11})}}$$

**Symmetric 0-1 case:** $C_{00}=C_{11}=0$, $C_{10}=C_{01}=1$ ŌåÆ $\eta = \frac{P(H_0)}{P(H_1)}$. This minimizes the **average probability of error** $P_e = P(H_0)P_{10} + P(H_1)P_{01}$.

**(b)** [7 marks]

Given equal priors, $\eta = \frac{0.5}{0.5} = 1$.

Compute $\Lambda(x)$ at $x = 0.5$:

$$\Lambda(0.5) = \frac{p(0.5 \mid H_1)}{p(0.5 \mid H_0)} = \frac{\exp(-(0.5-1)^2/2)}{\exp(-(0.5)^2/2)} = \frac{\exp(-0.125)}{\exp(-0.125)} = 1$$

Wait ŌĆö let us recompute carefully:

$$\Lambda(0.5) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{(0.5-1)^2}{2} + \frac{(0.5)^2}{2}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{0.25}{2} + \frac{0.25}{2}\right) = \exp(0) = 1$$

Since $\Lambda(0.5) = 1 = \eta$, this is the **decision boundary** itself ŌĆö a tie. By convention we may declare either $H_0$ or $H_1$, but typically we choose $H_1$ when $\Lambda \geq \eta$.

$$\boxed{\text{Decision: } H_1 \text{ (with tie-breaking convention), Threshold } \eta = 1}$$

If $x = 1$ instead, $\Lambda(1) = \exp(0.5) \approx 1.65 > 1$ ŌåÆ confidently $H_1$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Bayesian Philosophy (Module 2)**

- [ ] **Bayes' theorem** = the single most important equation of this module; memorize $p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x}\mid\theta)\,p(\theta)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$.
- [ ] **Three quantities** to identify in any problem: prior, likelihood, posterior.
- [ ] **MMSE estimator** = posterior mean; derived from squared-error cost.
- [ ] **MAP estimator** = posterior mode = $\arg\max [\,\ln p(\mathbf{x}\mid\theta) + \ln p(\theta)\,]$.
- [ ] **MLE = MAP** when prior is uniform (non-informative).
- [ ] **Gaussian conjugate** prior formulas: $\mu_n = \frac{\sigma_0^2 x + \sigma^2 \mu_0}{\sigma^2+\sigma_0^2}$, $\frac{1}{\sigma_n^2} = \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2}+\frac{1}{\sigma^2}$.
- [ ] **Bayes LRT threshold** = $\eta = \frac{P(H_0)(C_{10}-C_{00})}{P(H_1)(C_{01}-C_{11})}$.
- [ ] **Minimum $P_e$ case** = $\eta = \frac{P(H_0)}{P(H_1)}$ (equal-cost, 0-1 assignment).
- [ ] **Posterior odds**, **likelihood ratio**, and **posterior probability comparisons** are all mathematically equivalent detection forms.
- [ ] **Bayes risk** = expected cost = the universal optimality criterion.
- [ ] The posterior becomes the prior for the next observation ŌĆö this is the **recursive Bayesian update** (foundation of Kalman filtering).
- [ ] **Engineering payoff:** adaptive radar, ML classifiers, cognitive radio, GPS navigation, medical imaging.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Mnemonic:** *"Prior ├Ś Likelihood ├Ę Evidence = Posterior ŌåÆ Cost Minimization ŌåÆ Decision."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimum Mean Square Error Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Minimum Mean Square Error Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Mean Square Error (MMSE) Estimation** is a Bayesian estimation criterion that seeks to find an estimator $\hat{\theta}_{MMSE}(x)$ of an unknown random parameter $\theta$ based on the observation $x$, such that the **Bayes Mean Square Error (BMSE) cost function** $B(\tilde{\theta}) = E\{(\theta - \hat{\theta}(x))^2\}$ is minimized.

The MMSE estimator is the **posterior conditional mean** of $\theta$ given the observation $x$:

$$\hat{\theta}_{MMSE}(x) = E\{\theta \mid x\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \theta \, p(\theta \mid x) \, d\theta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The MMSE estimator belongs to the class of *Bayesian estimators* and represents the optimal estimator under the squared-error loss function. It is also referred to as the **conditional mean estimator (CME)** in the KTU 2024 Estimation & Detection syllabus.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are a doctor trying to estimate a patient's unknown true body temperature $\theta$, given only a noisy thermometer reading $x$. Many factors cause the reading to differ from reality (instrument noise, ambient temperature, calibration drift). 

- A **naive** estimator would simply report the reading $\hat{\theta} = x$.
- The **MMSE estimator**, however, uses *all prior knowledge* ŌĆö your belief about how temperatures are typically distributed (the prior), and the statistical relationship between readings and actual temperatures (the likelihood) ŌĆö to compute the *best average guess* that minimizes the expected squared mistake.

In essence, the MMSE estimator is the **"center of mass"** of the posterior distribution $p(\theta \mid x)$ ŌĆö it is the single number that, if repeated over many trials, would produce the least average squared error.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constants / Metrics (Bold for emphasis):**
> - **MSE (Mean Square Error):** measured in squared units of the parameter (e.g., $\text{K}^2$, $\text{V}^2$).
> - **Posterior density $p(\theta \mid x)$:** units are inverse of $\theta$.
> - **Standard KTU Notation:** $\theta$ ŌåÆ unknown scalar parameter, $x$ ŌåÆ observation, $\hat{\theta}$ ŌåÆ estimate.

---

### Connection to KTU Course Outcomes
| KTU 2024 Course Outcome | Relevance to MMSE |
|---|---|
| **CO2:** Apply Bayesian estimation techniques | MMSE is the foundational Bayesian estimator |
| **CO3:** Derive optimal estimators under MSE criterion | Direct derivative-based derivation required |
| **CO5:** Compare estimators using orthogonality & bias | MMSE is unbiased and satisfies orthogonality principle |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Cost Function and Why "Square"?
The choice of the **quadratic (squared-error) cost function** is not arbitrary. It is preferred because:
1. It is **mathematically tractable** ŌĆö it leads to closed-form solutions.
2. It is **strictly convex** ŌĆö guarantees a unique global minimum.
3. It penalizes **large errors more heavily** than small ones, which is desirable in most engineering applications.
4. The **derivative is linear**, making optimization via calculus trivial.

The **Bayesian Mean Square Error (BMSE)** is defined as:

$$B(\hat{\theta}) = E\{(\theta - \hat{\theta}(x))^2\}$$

This expectation is taken over the **joint density** $p(\theta, x)$:

$$B(\hat{\theta}) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\theta - \hat{\theta}(x))^2 \, p(\theta, x) \, d\theta \, dx$$

---

### 2.2 Why and How ŌĆö Structured Logic Steps
- **Step 1: Apply the Tower Property.** The BMSE can be decomposed as an outer and inner expectation:
$$B(\hat{\theta}) = E_x[E_{\theta \mid x}\{ (\theta - \hat{\theta}(x))^2 \mid x \}]$$

- **Step 2: Inner minimization.** Since the outer expectation $E_x$ is non-negative, minimizing $B(\hat{\theta})$ is equivalent to minimizing the *inner conditional MSE* for every $x$:
$$M(x) = E\{(\theta - \hat{\theta}(x))^2 \mid x\}$$

- **Step 3: Differentiate w.r.t. $\hat{\theta}(x)$ and equate to zero.** Treating $\hat{\theta}(x)$ as a free variable $\eta$:
$$\frac{\partial M(x)}{\partial \eta} = -2 \, E\{\theta \mid x\} + 2\eta = 0$$

- **Step 4: Solve for $\eta$.** This yields the celebrated result:
$$\hat{\theta}_{MMSE}(x) = E\{\theta \mid x\}$$

- **Step 5: Verify it's a minimum.** The second derivative is $\frac{\partial^2 M}{\partial \eta^2} = 2 > 0$, confirming a minimum.

---

### 2.3 The Orthogonality Principle
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Orthogonality Principle (KTU Board-Favorite Theorem):** The MMSE estimator satisfies the orthogonality condition ŌĆö the estimation error $(\theta - \hat{\theta}_{MMSE}(x))$ is **orthogonal** (uncorrelated) to every measurable function $g(x)$ of the data:
$$E\{(\theta - \hat{\theta}_{MMSE}(x)) \, g(x)\} = 0$$

This is a direct consequence of the minimization and is widely used in **Wiener filtering** and **linear MMSE** derivations.

---

### 2.4 Properties of the MMSE Estimator
1. **Optimality:** No other estimator yields a smaller BMSE.
2. **Unbiasedness:** $E\{\hat{\theta}_{MMSE}\} = E\{\theta\}$.
3. **Orthogonality:** Error is orthogonal to all functions of $x$.
4. **Variance Decomposition:**
$$\text{var}(\theta) = E\{(\theta - \hat{\theta}_{MMSE})^2\} + \text{var}(\hat{\theta}_{MMSE})$$
The BMSE equals the **posterior variance**: $B_{MMSE} = E\{\text{var}(\theta \mid x)\}$.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Identity | Description | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\hat{\theta}_{MMSE}(x) = E\{\theta \mid x\}$ | Conditional mean estimator | Direct definition; short-answer |
| 2 | $B_{MMSE} = E\{(\theta - \hat{\theta}_{MMSE})^2\} = E\{\sigma_{\theta \mid x}^2\}$ | Minimum achievable BMSE | Numerical computation |
| 3 | $E\{(\theta - \hat{\theta}_{MMSE})g(x)\} = 0$ | Orthogonality principle | Proof-based problems |
| 4 | $\hat{\theta}_{MMSE} = \arg\min_{\hat{\theta}} E\{(\theta - \hat{\theta})^2\}$ | Definition via optimization | MCQ / 2-mark questions |
| 5 | $E\{(\theta - \hat{\theta}_{MMSE})\} = 0$ | Unbiasedness | Property verification |
| 6 | $\text{var}(\theta) = B_{MMSE} + \text{var}(\hat{\theta}_{MMSE})$ | Variance decomposition | Derivations |
| 7 | $\hat{\theta}_{LMMSE} = E\{\theta\} + \text{Cov}(\theta, x)\text{Var}^{-1}(x)(x - E\{x\})$ | Linear MMSE (Wiener) | Gaussian case / 14-mark problems |
| 8 | $\sigma_{\theta \mid x}^2 = \sigma_\theta^2 - \frac{\sigma_{\theta x}^2}{\sigma_x^2}$ | Posterior variance (Gaussian) | Numerical / derivation |
| 9 | $\hat{\theta}_{MMSE} = E\{\theta\} + \frac{\sigma_{\theta x}}{\sigma_x^2}(x - E\{x\})$ | MMSE for jointly Gaussian | Special case derivation |
| 10 | $p(\theta \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid \theta)p(\theta)}{p(x)}$ | Bayes' theorem (Bayesian core) | Pre-requisite for MMSE problems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** MMSE estimation is the workhorse of **Wiener filters** (audio denoising), **Kalman filters** (GPS, robotics), **channel equalization** in 5G/6G communications, **ECG/EEG signal denoising** in biomedical engineering, and **target tracking** in radar systems. For *jointly Gaussian* $\theta$ and $x$, the MMSE estimator coincides with the **linear MMSE estimator**, allowing the use of the much simpler Wiener-Hopf equation.

---

### 2.6 Special Case: Jointly Gaussian Parameters
For jointly Gaussian $(\theta, x)$, the posterior $p(\theta \mid x)$ is also Gaussian, with:

$$p(\theta \mid x) \sim \mathcal{N}\left(E\{\theta\} + \frac{\text{Cov}(\theta, x)}{\text{Var}(x)}(x - E\{x\}), \; \sigma_\theta^2 - \frac{\text{Cov}^2(\theta, x)}{\text{Var}(x)}\right)$$

Therefore, the **MMSE = Linear MMSE** for the Gaussian case:

$$\hat{\theta}_{MMSE}(x) = E\{\theta\} + \frac{\text{Cov}(\theta, x)}{\text{Var}(x)} \, (x - E\{x\})$$

This is also called the **Wiener-Hopf estimator** in scalar form.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the MMSE Estimator

**Goal:** Find $\hat{\theta}(x)$ that minimizes $B(\hat{\theta}) = E\{(\theta - \hat{\theta}(x))^2\}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the BMSE as a joint expectation:**

$$B(\hat{\theta}) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\theta - \hat{\theta}(x))^2 \, p(\theta, x) \, d\theta \, dx$$

This is a single number (a scalar) for any given estimator $\hat{\theta}(x)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Law of Total Expectation (Tower Property):**

We factor the joint density as $p(\theta, x) = p(\theta \mid x) p(x)$. Substituting:

$$B(\hat{\theta}) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\theta - \hat{\theta}(x))^2 \, p(\theta \mid x) \, d\theta \right] p(x) \, dx$$

Since $p(x) \geq 0$ everywhere, the integral $B(\hat{\theta})$ is minimized **if and only if** the bracketed inner integral is minimized at every $x$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Minimize the inner conditional expectation:**

Define the conditional MSE at observation $x$:

$$M(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\theta - \hat{\theta}(x))^2 \, p(\theta \mid x) \, d\theta = E\{(\theta - \hat{\theta}(x))^2 \mid x\}$$

For a *fixed* $x$, $\hat{\theta}(x)$ is just a scalar number $\eta$. We treat $M(\eta)$ as a function of $\eta$ and differentiate.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Differentiation with respect to the scalar $\eta$:**

Expanding the square:

$$M(\eta) = E\{(\theta - \eta)^2 \mid x\} = E\{\theta^2 \mid x\} - 2\eta \, E\{\theta \mid x\} + \eta^2$$

Differentiating with respect to $\eta$:

$$\frac{dM}{d\eta} = -2 \, E\{\theta \mid x\} + 2\eta$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Equate to zero and solve:**

Setting the derivative to zero:

$$-2 \, E\{\theta \mid x\} + 2\eta = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \eta = E\{\theta \mid x\}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Verify it is a minimum:**

$$\frac{d^2 M}{d\eta^2} = 2 > 0 \quad \text{(convex, so it's a minimum)}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\hat{\theta}_{MMSE}(x) = E\{\theta \mid x\}}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Compute the minimum BMSE:**

The minimum value of the inner integral is $\text{var}(\theta \mid x)$ (the posterior variance). Therefore:

$$B_{MMSE} = E\{ \text{var}(\theta \mid x) \} = E\{E\{\theta^2 \mid x\} - (E\{\theta \mid x\})^2\}$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Orthogonality Principle

**Claim:** $E\{(\theta - \hat{\theta}_{MMSE}) \, g(x)\} = 0$ for any real function $g(x)$.

**Proof:**

Starting with the joint expectation and applying the Tower property:

$$E\{(\theta - \hat{\theta}_{MMSE}) \, g(x)\} = E_x[ \, g(x) \, E_\theta\{(\theta - \hat{\theta}_{MMSE}) \mid x\} \,]$$

The inner conditional expectation is:

$$E\{\theta \mid x\} - \hat{\theta}_{MMSE}(x) = E\{\theta \mid x\} - E\{\theta \mid x\} = 0$$

Therefore, the full expectation is zero. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of Linear MMSE (Wiener Estimator) for Jointly Gaussian Case

**Setup:** $\theta \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_\theta, \sigma_\theta^2)$, $x \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_x, \sigma_x^2)$, with correlation coefficient $\rho$.

We *restrict* the estimator to the linear form:

$$\hat{\theta}_{LMMSE}(x) = a + b \, x$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Form the BMSE for the linear estimator:**

$$B(a, b) = E\{(\theta - a - b x)^2\}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Expand the square:**

$$B(a, b) = E\{\theta^2\} - 2a E\{\theta\} - 2b E\{\theta x\} + a^2 + 2ab E\{x\} + b^2 E\{x^2\}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Partial derivatives:**

$$\frac{\partial B}{\partial a} = -2 E\{\theta\} + 2a + 2b E\{x\} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial B}{\partial b} = -2 E\{\theta x\} + 2a E\{x\} + 2b E\{x^2\} = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve the linear system:**

From the first equation:

$$a = E\{\theta\} - b \, E\{x\}$$

Substitute into the second:

$$E\{\theta x\} - (E\{\theta\} - bE\{x\})E\{x\} - bE\{x^2\} = 0$$

$$E\{\theta x\} - E\{\theta\}E\{x\} = b \left(E\{x^2\} - (E\{x\})^2\right)$$

$$\text{Cov}(\theta, x) = b \cdot \text{Var}(x) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad b = \frac{\text{Cov}(\theta, x)}{\text{Var}(x)}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Assemble the estimator:**

$$\hat{\theta}_{LMMSE}(x) = E\{\theta\} + \frac{\text{Cov}(\theta, x)}{\text{Var}(x)} \left(x - E\{x\}\right)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Show that for jointly Gaussian, this equals the MMSE:**

For jointly Gaussian $(\theta, x)$, the conditional distribution $p(\theta \mid x)$ is Gaussian with mean given exactly by the expression above. Since the mean of a Gaussian is the MMSE estimator, we conclude:

$$\hat{\theta}_{LMMSE}(x) = \hat{\theta}_{MMSE}(x) \quad \text{(Gaussian case)}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Compute the minimum BMSE for Gaussian case:**

$$B_{LMMSE} = \text{Var}(\theta) - \frac{\text{Cov}^2(\theta, x)}{\text{Var}(x)} = \sigma_\theta^2 (1 - \rho^2)$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem:** Let $\theta \sim \mathcal{N}(2, 4)$ and $x = \theta + w$, where $w \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ is independent of $\theta$. Find the MMSE estimator of $\theta$ given $x$ and the minimum MSE.

**Solution:**

Jointly Gaussian. Using the LMMSE formula (which equals MMSE for Gaussian):

- $E\{\theta\} = 2$, $E\{x\} = E\{\theta\} + E\{w\} = 2$
- $\text{Var}(\theta) = 4$, $\text{Var}(x) = \text{Var}(\theta) + \text{Var}(w) = 4 + 1 = 5$
- $\text{Cov}(\theta, x) = E\{\theta(\theta + w)\} - E\{\theta\}E\{x\} = E\{\theta^2\} - (2)^2 = (4 + 4) - 4 = 4$

Therefore:

$$\hat{\theta}_{MMSE}(x) = 2 + \frac{4}{5}(x - 2) = 2 + \frac{4}{5}x - \frac{8}{5} = \frac{2}{5} + \frac{4}{5}x$$

$$\hat{\theta}_{MMSE}(x) = 0.4 + 0.8 \, x$$

Minimum MSE:

$$B_{MMSE} = \sigma_\theta^2 - \frac{\text{Cov}^2(\theta, x)}{\text{Var}(x)} = 4 - \frac{16}{5} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice how the estimator **shrinks** the noisy observation $x$ towards the prior mean $2$, and the minimum MSE is **smaller than both** $\sigma_\theta^2 = 4$ and $\sigma_w^2 = 1$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of MMSE Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["True Parameter ╬Ė"] -->|Generates| B["Likelihood p(x|╬Ė)"]
    C["Prior p(╬Ė)"] --> D["Bayes Theorem"]
    B --> D
    D -->|Computes| E["Posterior p(╬Ė|x)"]
    E -->|Integrate| F["╬Ė_hat MMSE = E[╬Ė|x]"]
    G["Observation x"] --> D
    F --> H["Estimate Error e = ╬Ė - ╬Ė_hat"]
    A --> H
    H -->|Squared & Averaged| I["BMSE = E[e^2]"]
    I -->|Minimized| J["Minimum BMSE = E[Var(╬Ė|x)]"]
    K["Orthogonality Principle"] -.->|Constraints| F
    L["Unbiasedness E[e]=0"] -.->|Validates| F
```

---

### 4.2 MMSE Estimation ŌĆö Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Start: Joint p(╬Ė,x)"] --> S2["Apply Tower Property"]
    S2 --> S3["Inner Minimization dM/d╬Ę=0"]
    S3 --> S4["Obtain ╬Ė_hat_MMSE = E[╬Ė|x]"]
    S4 --> S5{"Jointly Gaussian?"}
    S5 -- "Yes" --> S6["╬Ė_hat_MMSE = ╬Ė_hat_LMMSE (Wiener)"]
    S5 -- "No" --> S7["Compute E[╬Ė|x] directly from posterior"]
    S6 --> S8["Output: BMSE_min = Žā_╬Ė┬▓(1-Žü┬▓)"]
    S7 --> S9["Output: BMSE_min = E[Var(╬Ė|x)]"]
    S8 --> END["Done"]
    S9 --> END["Done"]
```

---

### 4.3 Error Decomposition Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Var(╬Ė)"] --> Q["Var(╬Ė_hat_MMSE)"]
    P --> R["BMSE = E[(╬Ė - ╬Ė_hat)^2]"]
    Q --> S["Var(╬Ė) = BMSE + Var(╬Ė_hat_MMSE)"]
    R --> S
    S --> T["Larger estimate variance ŌåÆ Smaller BMSE"]
    S --> U["Smaller estimate variance ŌåÆ Larger BMSE"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These schematics illustrate the **flow of information** in MMSE estimation: from prior and observation through Bayesian update to the optimal posterior mean, validated by the orthogonality principle and unbiasedness.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define the Minimum Mean Square Error (MMSE) estimator. State the orthogonality principle associated with it.

**Model Answer:**
The **MMSE estimator** of a random parameter $\theta$ based on observation $x$ is the estimator $\hat{\theta}(x)$ that minimizes the Bayesian mean square error $B(\hat{\theta}) = E\{(\theta - \hat{\theta}(x))^2\}$. Mathematically:

$$\hat{\theta}_{MMSE}(x) = \arg\min_{\hat{\theta}} E\{(\theta - \hat{\theta}(x))^2\} = E\{\theta \mid x\}$$

**Orthogonality Principle:** The MMSE estimation error is orthogonal to every measurable function $g(x)$ of the observation:

$$E\{(\theta - \hat{\theta}_{MMSE}(x)) \, g(x)\} = 0$$

This principle is a direct consequence of the minimization and is extensively used in deriving linear estimators like the **Wiener filter**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Show that the MMSE estimator is unbiased.

**Model Answer:**
An estimator $\hat{\theta}$ is unbiased if $E\{\hat{\theta}\} = E\{\theta\}$.

$$E\{\hat{\theta}_{MMSE}\} = E\{E\{\theta \mid x\}\} = E\{\theta\}$$

(using the Tower property of expectation: $E_x[E_\theta\{\theta \mid x\}] = E\{\theta\}$). Hence, the MMSE estimator is **unbiased**. $\blacksquare$

---

### Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]*

> **(a)** Derive the MMSE estimator of a random parameter $\theta$ based on the observation $x$. Prove that the MMSE estimator satisfies the orthogonality principle. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A parameter $\theta$ has prior distribution $\mathcal{N}(3, 4)$ and the observation model is $x = \theta + n$, where $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ is independent of $\theta$. Find the MMSE estimate of $\theta$ given $x = 5$. Compute the minimum mean square error. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to **Section 3.1** and **Section 3.2** above for the full derivation. Key steps:
- BMSE = $E\{(\theta - \hat{\theta})^2\}$
- Apply Tower property: $B = E_x[E_\theta\{(\theta-\hat\theta)^2 \mid x\}]$
- Differentiate inner w.r.t. $\hat\theta$: $-2E\{\theta \mid x\} + 2\hat\theta = 0$
- Result: $\hat\theta_{MMSE} = E\{\theta \mid x\}$
- Orthogonality: $E\{(\theta - \hat\theta_{MMSE})g(x)\} = 0$ for any function $g$.

**(b)** Jointly Gaussian case ŌåÆ use LMMSE (Wiener) formula:
- $E\{\theta\} = 3$, $\sigma_\theta^2 = 4$
- $E\{x\} = 3$, $\sigma_x^2 = \sigma_\theta^2 + \sigma_n^2 = 4 + 1 = 5$
- $\text{Cov}(\theta, x) = E\{\theta(\theta + n)\} - (3)(3) = (4 + 9) - 9 = 4$

$$\hat{\theta}_{MMSE}(x) = 3 + \frac{4}{5}(x - 3) = 3 + 0.8x - 2.4 = 0.6 + 0.8x$$

At $x = 5$:

$$\hat{\theta}_{MMSE}(5) = 0.6 + 0.8(5) = 0.6 + 4 = 4.6$$

Minimum MSE:

$$B_{MMSE} = \sigma_\theta^2 - \frac{\text{Cov}^2(\theta, x)}{\sigma_x^2} = 4 - \frac{16}{5} = 0.8$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]*

> **(a)** For jointly Gaussian random variables $\theta$ and $x$ with means $\mu_\theta$, $\mu_x$, variances $\sigma_\theta^2$, $\sigma_x^2$ and correlation $\rho$, derive the linear MMSE estimator and show that it equals the MMSE estimator. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With reference to MMSE estimation, explain the variance decomposition identity $\text{Var}(\theta) = B_{MMSE} + \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}_{MMSE})$ and discuss its significance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to **Section 3.3** above. Restrict estimator to linear form $\hat\theta = a + bx$, minimize BMSE, solve for $a, b$, obtain Wiener form:
$$\hat\theta_{LMMSE}(x) = \mu_\theta + \rho \frac{\sigma_\theta}{\sigma_x}(x - \mu_x)$$
Since $p(\theta \mid x)$ is Gaussian, its mean is this expression, so $\hat\theta_{LMMSE} = \hat\theta_{MMSE}$.

**(b)** Variance decomposition:

$$\text{Var}(\theta) = E\{(\theta - E\{\theta\})^2\} = E\{[(\theta - \hat\theta_{MMSE}) + (\hat\theta_{MMSE} - E\{\theta\})]^2\}$$

Expanding:

$$= E\{(\theta - \hat\theta_{MMSE})^2\} + 2 E\{(\theta - \hat\theta_{MMSE})(\hat\theta_{MMSE} - E\{\theta\})\} + E\{(\hat\theta_{MMSE} - E\{\theta\})^2\}$$

The cross term vanishes by the **orthogonality principle** (with $g(x) = \hat\theta_{MMSE} - E\{\theta\}$), yielding:

$$\text{Var}(\theta) = B_{MMSE} + \text{Var}(\hat\theta_{MMSE})$$

**Significance:**
- It shows that the **prior variance** decomposes into the **estimation error (BMSE)** and the **estimator's variability**.
- A *good* estimator has small BMSE and most of $\text{Var}(\theta)$ is captured in $\text{Var}(\hat\theta_{MMSE})$.
- It justifies the term "**shrinkage**" ŌĆö the MMSE estimator pulls the noisy observation toward the prior mean, controlled by $\rho^2$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing LMMSE with MMSE:** Students often write the LMMSE formula for *non-Gaussian* problems and assume it is the MMSE. **Marks lost:** up to 3 marks. Always verify Gaussianity first.
> 2. **Forgetting the constant term:** In the Wiener estimator, the offset $a = E\{\theta\} - bE\{x\}$ is frequently omitted. **Marks lost:** 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 3. **Sign error in covariance:** $\text{Cov}(\theta, x) = E\{\theta x\} - E\{\theta\}E\{x\}$ ŌĆö many students compute only $E\{\theta x\}$ and forget the product of means.
> 4. **Not stating the Tower property explicitly** in the derivation. Examiners allocate **2ŌĆō3 marks** for proper use of the Law of Total Expectation.
> 5. **Forgetting the second derivative test** to confirm a minimum ŌĆö minor but cumulative loss.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 2):**

- **Definition:** MMSE estimator is $\hat\theta_{MMSE}(x) = E\{\theta \mid x\}$ ŌĆö the posterior conditional mean.
- **Cost function:** Bayesian Mean Square Error $B(\hat\theta) = E\{(\theta - \hat\theta)^2\}$.
- **Key derivation tool:** Law of Total Expectation (Tower property).
- **Differentiation step:** $\frac{d}{d\eta} E\{(\theta - \eta)^2 \mid x\} = -2E\{\theta \mid x\} + 2\eta = 0$.
- **MMSE properties:** Unbiased, orthogonal to data functions, posterior variance = min BMSE.
- **Orthogonality principle:** $E\{(\theta - \hat\theta_{MMSE})g(x)\} = 0$ for all $g(\cdot)$.
- **Gaussian special case:** MMSE = LMMSE = Wiener estimator: $\hat\theta = \mu_\theta + \rho \frac{\sigma_\theta}{\sigma_x}(x - \mu_x)$.
- **Posterior variance (Gaussian):** $\sigma_{\theta \mid x}^2 = \sigma_\theta^2(1 - \rho^2)$.
- **Variance decomposition:** $\text{Var}(\theta) = B_{MMSE} + \text{Var}(\hat\theta_{MMSE})$.
- **Connection to MAP & ML:** MMSE minimizes MSE; MAP maximizes posterior; ML maximizes likelihood.
- **Engineering relevance:** Wiener filter, Kalman filter, channel estimation in OFDM, biomedical signal denoising.
- **Common KTU pitfall:** LMMSE formula only valid for jointly Gaussian or when estimator is *constrained* to be linear; do not generalize blindly.
- **Examiner trick:** "Show that the MMSE estimator is unbiased" ŌĆö always use the **Tower property** as the first line of the proof.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Application Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are "Application Examples" in Estimation & Detection?

In the context of **Estimation and Detection Theory**, "Application Examples" refers to the practical engineering scenarios where the abstract mathematical tools of **statistical inference** are deployed to solve real-world signal processing problems. These examples bridge the gap between theoretical derivations (likelihood functions, sufficient statistics, CRLB, Neyman-Pearson detectors) and engineering reality (radar returns, noisy communication channels, biomedical signals).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved Wording):**
> Application examples are canonical problem formulations in which observed random data is modeled as the output of a known or partially known probabilistic system, and the goal is to either **estimate** an unknown deterministic/random parameter or **decide** between competing hypotheses regarding the state of nature.

## 1.2 The Two Pillars of Statistical Inference

| Inference Type | Engineering Goal | Typical Question Answered |
|----------------|------------------|---------------------------|
| **Estimation** | Recover a continuous parameter | "What is the blood oxygen level of the patient?" |
| **Detection** | Decide between discrete hypotheses | "Is there a tumor in this MRI scan (Yes/No)?" |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Noisy Scale" Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump ŌĆö A Kitchen Scale Analogy**
> 
> Imagine you place a 1 kg reference weight on a noisy digital kitchen scale. The reading fluctuates between 0.95 kg and 1.05 kg. You want the **true weight** (the parameter ╬Ė).
> 
> - **Estimation problem**: Build an algorithm that returns the single best guess, e.g., 0.998 kg, by taking multiple readings and using MLE/MAP/Bayesian rules.
> - **Detection problem**: Decide if the object on the scale is a 1 kg weight ($H_0$) or a 1.1 kg weight ($H_1$) given a single noisy reading of 1.04 kg.
> 
> Application examples are the **engineering counterparts** of this scale ŌĆö but applied to radar echoes, communication symbols, EEG recordings, and stock-market signals.

## 1.4 Why This Topic is KTU-High-Yield

Application examples are the **favourite exam terrain** of KTU paper-setters because:

1. They test the student's ability to **map a verbal problem to a mathematical model** (the hardest skill in this course).
2. They integrate all prior modules (probability, random processes, likelihood) into a single concrete problem.
3. They are **open-ended in setup** but **deterministic in solution technique**, making them ideal for 14-mark Part B questions.

## 1.5 The Standard Application Taxonomy (KTU 2024 Convention)

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures applications into **five canonical families**:

- **Family 1 ŌĆö DC Level in White Gaussian Noise (WGN):** Estimating a constant amplitude signal buried in additive Gaussian noise.
- **Family 2 ŌĆö Signal with Unknown Parameters:** Estimating amplitude, phase, or frequency of a sinusoid in noise.
- **Family 3 ŌĆö Binary/M-ary Detection in Communication:** Detecting digital symbols (BPSK, QPSK) at the receiver.
- **Family 4 ŌĆö Radar/Sonar Target Detection:** Detecting the presence of a moving target from reflected pulses.
- **Family 5 ŌĆö System Identification & Channel Estimation:** Estimating an unknown linear filter (channel) from inputŌĆōoutput measurements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU application problem on this topic can be reduced to one of these five families. The trick is to identify the family from the problem statement, then apply the matching estimator/detector.

---

## 1.6 The Universal Modeling Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four-Step KTU Application Recipe (memorize this!)**
> 
> 1. **Observation Model:** Write the received data as $x[n] = s[n;\theta] + w[n]$, where $w[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$.
> 2. **Likelihood / pdf:** Construct $p(x;\theta)$ or $p(x\mid H_j)$.
> 3. **Choose the rule:** MLE, MAP, MMSE, LRT, or Neyman-Pearson.
> 4. **Compute the sufficient statistic and threshold**, then interpret in engineering units.

This pipeline is the **spine** of every application question you will face.

---

## 1.7 Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics Encountered

- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** ŌĆö appears in thermal noise variance.
- **Standard Temperature:** $T_0 = 290$ **K** ŌĆö reference for noise floor calculations.
- **Noise PSD:** $N_0/2$ **W/Hz** ŌĆö two-sided power spectral density of AWGN.
- **SNR threshold for BPSK at $P_e = 10^{-5}$:** Ōēł **9.6 dB** (a benchmark examiners love).
- **CRLB benchmark for DC level:** $\text{var}(\hat{A}) \geq \sigma^2/N$ ŌĆö the "floor" for unbiased estimators.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Family 1 ŌĆö DC Level Estimation in AWGN

### 2.1.1 Problem Statement (Verbatim KTU Wording)

> *"A known DC signal of amplitude $A$ is observed in additive white Gaussian noise of variance $\sigma^2$. Given $N$ independent samples $x[1], x[2], \ldots, x[N]$, obtain the MLE of $A$ and its variance."*

### 2.1.2 Observation Model

$$x[n] = A + w[n], \quad n=0,1,\ldots,N-1, \quad w[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$$

### 2.1.3 Why Each Step Works (The Intuition Behind the Math)

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Write the joint pdf:** Because samples are i.i.d. Gaussian, the joint pdf is the product of $N$ Gaussian pdfs.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Take the log:** Converts the product (hard to maximize) into a sum (easy to maximize).
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Differentiate & set to zero:** Calculus-based MLE. The derivative of a quadratic in $A$ is linear ŌåÆ closed-form solution.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Identify sufficient statistic:** The sample mean $T(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{N}\sum x[n]$ is **sufficient** for $A$ ŌĆö it captures all relevant information.

### 2.1.4 The Final MLE Result

$$\boxed{\hat{A}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]}$$

**Variance of the estimator:**

$$\text{var}(\hat{A}_{MLE}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}$$

**CRLB Comparison:**

$$\text{var}(\hat{A}_{MLE}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{N} = \text{CRLB}$$

ŌåÆ The MLE is **efficient** (achieves the CRLB) and **unbiased**, which is why it is the gold standard for KTU DC-level problems.

---

## 2.2 Family 2 ŌĆö Sinusoidal Parameter Estimation

### 2.2.1 Observation Model

$$x[n] = A\cos(2\pi f_0 n + \phi) + w[n], \quad n = 0,1,\ldots,N-1$$

Here the unknown parameters are: amplitude $A$, frequency $f_0$, and/or phase $\phi$.

### 2.2.2 Three Sub-Problems KTU Tests

| Sub-problem | Known Parameters | Unknown Parameters | Estimator |
|-------------|------------------|--------------------|-----------|
| (a) | $f_0, \phi$ | $A$ | $\hat{A} = \frac{2}{N}\sum x[n]\cos(2\pi f_0 n + \phi)$ |
| (b) | $A, f_0$ | $\phi$ | $\hat{\phi} = \angle\left[\sum x[n] e^{-j2\pi f_0 n}\right]$ (after normalization) |
| (c) | $A, \phi$ | $f_0$ | Maximize periodogram: $\hat{f}_0 = \arg\max_f \vert X(f)\vert^2$ |

### 2.2.3 CRLB for Phase Estimation (Closed-Form)

$$\text{var}(\hat{\phi}_{MLE}) \geq \frac{2\sigma^2}{NA^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** The variance shrinks as $1/N$ and $1/\text{SNR}$. Doubling the observation time reduces the variance by half ŌĆö a fundamental trade-off in radar dwell-time design.

---

## 2.3 Family 3 ŌĆö Binary Detection in Communication

### 2.3.1 The BPSK Detection Problem

Under hypothesis $H_0$ (symbol "ŌłÆ1" sent) and $H_1$ (symbol "+1" sent), after matched filtering:

$$r = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n, \quad n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$$

### 2.3.2 LRT Formulation

$$\Lambda(r) = \frac{p(r\mid H_1)}{p(r\mid H_0)} = \exp\left(\frac{2r\sqrt{E_b}}{N_0/2 \cdot 1}\right) \;\;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\;\; \eta$$

The **log-LRT** simplifies to:

$$\boxed{r \;\;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\;\; \gamma = \sqrt{E_b}\,\eta^{*}}$$

where $\eta^{*}$ is the log-threshold derived from the desired false-alarm probability.

### 2.3.3 Probability of Error for BPSK

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q(\sqrt{2\,\text{SNR}_{\text{bit}}})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol-error rate benchmark:** $P_e = 10^{-5}$ requires $\text{SNR}_{\text{bit}} \approx 9.6$ **dB**. This number appears in nearly every KTU communication-detection question.

---

## 2.4 Family 4 ŌĆö Radar Target Detection

### 2.4.1 The Cell-Averaging CFAR (CA-CFAR) Problem

A radar returns $N$ range-cell samples $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N$, each i.i.d. exponential under $H_0$ (noise-only) with pdf $p(x) = \frac{1}{\mu}e^{-x/\mu}$.

**Detection rule:**

$$\boxed{T = \frac{\vert x_0 \vert}{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i} \;\;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\;\; \alpha}$$

where $\alpha$ is the CFAR threshold chosen to maintain a constant false-alarm rate $P_{FA}$.

### 2.4.2 The CFAR Threshold Equation

$$P_{FA} = \frac{1}{(1+\alpha)^N}$$

ŌåÆ This implicit equation is solved for $\alpha$ given $P_{FA}$ and $N$, a typical KTU numerical question.

---

## 2.5 Family 5 ŌĆö System Identification / Channel Estimation

### 2.5.1 FIR Channel Model

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h[k]\,s[n-k] + w[n]$$

Stack into vector form: $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{S}\mathbf{h} + \mathbf{w}$, where $\mathbf{S}$ is the convolution matrix of the known training sequence.

### 2.5.2 LS and MMSE Channel Estimates

| Estimator | Formula | Properties |
|-----------|---------|------------|
| **Least Squares (LS)** | $\hat{\mathbf{h}}_{LS} = (\mathbf{S}^T\mathbf{S})^{-1}\mathbf{S}^T\mathbf{x}$ | No prior needed; sensitive to noise |
| **MMSE / Bayesian** | $\hat{\mathbf{h}}_{MMSE} = \mathbf{R}_h\mathbf{S}^T(\mathbf{S}\mathbf{R}_h\mathbf{S}^T + \sigma^2\mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ | Uses channel covariance $\mathbf{R}_h$; lower MSE |

---

> The following table collects **every formula** that has appeared (or can appear) in KTU university exams for this topic. Notice the use of `\vert` instead of `\|` to preserve markdown integrity.

| # | Application | Key Formula | Variables / Units | Special Note |
|---|-------------|-------------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1 | DC level MLE | $\hat{A} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]$ | $A$ in volts, $\sigma^2$ in V┬▓ | Unbiased & efficient |
| 2 | DC level variance | $\text{var}(\hat{A}) = \sigma^2/N$ | V┬▓ | Equals CRLB |
| 3 | Phase CRLB | $\text{var}(\hat{\phi}) \geq 2\sigma^2/(NA^2)$ | rad┬▓ | Valid for $A$ known |
| 4 | Frequency CRLB (linear) | $\text{var}(\hat{f}) \geq 12\sigma^2/(A^2(2\pi)^2 N(N^2-1))$ | Hz┬▓ | Increases rapidly at low SNR |
| 5 | BPSK log-LRT | $r \gtrless \gamma$ | $r$ in volts | $\gamma = \sqrt{E_b}\,\text{ln}\,\eta$ |
| 6 | BPSK error prob. | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ | Dimensionless | $E_b$ in Joules |
| 7 | Matched filter SNR | $\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = 2E_s/N_0$ | Ratio (linear) | Optimal linear filter |

| 11 | Sufficient statistic (Gaussian family) | $T(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_n x[n]$ | Linear in data | Used in Neyman-Fisher |
| 12 | GLRT for unknown amplitude | $\hat{\Lambda} = \frac{\max_\theta p(\mathbf{x};\theta,H_1)}{\max_\theta p(\mathbf{x};\theta,H_0)}$ | Likelihood ratio | Plug MLE into likelihood |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Family | Industry Use Case | Performance Metric |
|--------------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| DC level estimation | Sensor calibration, ECG baseline drift removal | Variance vs. CRLB |
| Sinusoidal estimation | Power-grid frequency monitoring, Doppler radar | Frequency accuracy in Hz |
| BPSK detection | Satellite uplink (DVB-S2), IoT (LoRa) | BER vs. $E_b/N_0$ |
| Radar CFAR | Air-traffic control, automotive radar (77 GHz) | Probability of detection $P_D$ |
| Channel estimation | 5G NR, Wi-Fi 6 OFDM pilots | MSE vs. pilot SNR |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked "give a real-world application" in viva or theory, cite the **specific standard** (e.g., DVB-S2 for BPSK, 5G NR PSS/SSS for channel estimation). Examiners reward domain specificity.

## 2.8 The EstimatorŌĆōDetector Duality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Big Idea (often tested as a 3-mark question):**
> 
> Estimation answers *"how much?"* and detection answers *"is it there?"*. Many problems start as detection and become estimation once the presence is confirmed (e.g., once a target is detected, estimate its range and velocity). The ML rule bridges both: maximize likelihood over $\theta$ ŌåÆ estimator; maximize likelihood over $H_j$ ŌåÆ detector.
