# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive derivations** for the four most frequently tested KTU application problems. Every algebraic transition is written out explicitly, with no skipped steps.

---

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 ŌĆö MLE of DC Level in AWGN (Full Working)

### 3.1.1 Problem Restated

We observe $N$ i.i.d. samples:

$$x[n] = A + w[n], \quad w[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2), \quad n=0,1,\ldots,N-1$$

Find $\hat{A}_{MLE}$, $\text{var}(\hat{A}_{MLE})$, and verify it meets the CRLB.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 ŌĆö Write the Single-Sample pdf

$$p(x[n];A) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x[n]-A)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Logic:** Each sample is a Gaussian random variable with mean $A$ (the DC level) and known variance $\sigma^2$. This is the **standard Gaussian pdf** with the constant $A$ replacing the location parameter.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 ŌĆö Joint pdf (Independence ŌćÆ Product)

Because the $w[n]$ are independent, the joint pdf is the product:

$$p(\mathbf{x};A) = \prod_{n=0}^{N-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x[n]-A)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Logic:** Independence means the joint density factorizes ŌĆö there is no cross-term between $x[i]$ and $x[j]$ for $i \neq j$. This is the most important property of AWGN.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 ŌĆö Take the Natural Logarithm

Define the **log-likelihood function** $L(A)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(A) &= \ln p(\mathbf{x};A) \\
&= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left[ -\frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{(x[n]-A)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right] \\
&= -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n]-A)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The product becomes a sum. Maximizing $L(A)$ is equivalent to maximizing $p(\mathbf{x};A)$ since $\ln$ is monotonically increasing.

### 3.1.5 Step 4 ŌĆö Expand the Quadratic Term

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n]-A)^2 &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\left(x^2[n] - 2A\,x[n] + A^2\right) \\
&= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x^2[n] - 2A\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x[n] + NA^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each $(x-A)^2$ expands via the identity $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$. The sum distributes linearly over each term.

### 3.1.6 Step 5 ŌĆö Differentiate w.r.t. $A$ and Set to Zero

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dL}{dA} &= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\cdot\frac{d}{dA}\!\left[\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x^2[n] - 2A\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x[n] + NA^2\right] \\
&= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[0 - 2\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x[n] + 2NA\right] \\
&= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[-2\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x[n] + 2NA\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting $\frac{dL}{dA} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[-2\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x[n] + 2NA\right] &= 0 \\
-2\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x[n] + 2NA &= 0 \\
2NA &= 2\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x[n] \\
A &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x[n]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\hat{A}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]}$$

**Logic:** The derivative of a constant is zero, the derivative of $-2A\sum x[n]$ is $-2\sum x[n]$, and the derivative of $NA^2$ is $2NA$. Setting the total to zero gives the MLE.

### 3.1.7 Step 6 ŌĆö Second-Derivative Test (Confirm Maximum)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^2L}{dA^2} &= \frac{d}{dA}\left[-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left(-2\sum x[n] + 2NA\right)\right] \\
&= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\cdot 2N = -\frac{N}{\sigma^2} < 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The second derivative is strictly negative for any $N \geq 1$, so the critical point is a **global maximum**. The log-likelihood is concave in $A$, ensuring a unique MLE.

### 3.1.8 Step 7 ŌĆö Compute the Variance of $\hat{A}_{MLE}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{var}(\hat{A}) &= \text{var}\!\left(\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x[n]\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{N^2}\,\text{var}\!\left(\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(A + w[n])\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{N^2}\,\text{var}\!\left(NA + \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}w[n]\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The constant $NA$ has zero variance, and the sum of independent $w[n]$ has variance $N\sigma^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{var}(\hat{A}) &= \frac{1}{N^2}\cdot N\sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.9 Step 8 ŌĆö Verify CRLB Is Achieved

The CRLB for estimating $A$ from $N$ Gaussian observations is:

$$\text{var}(\hat{A}) \geq \frac{1}{N\cdot I(A)} = \frac{1}{N\cdot(1/\sigma^2)} = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}$$

(Here, Fisher Information $I(A) = 1/\sigma^2$ per sample.) Since $\text{var}(\hat{A}) = \sigma^2/N$ **equals** the CRLB, the MLE is **efficient**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Efficiency** means the estimator attains the theoretical lower bound on variance ŌĆö it is the best possible unbiased estimator. For the DC level, the sample mean is uniquely efficient.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 ŌĆö BPSK Detection via LRT (Full Working)

### 3.2.1 Problem Restated

Under $H_0$ and $H_1$:

$$H_0: r = -\sqrt{E_b} + n, \qquad H_1: r = +\sqrt{E_b} + n, \qquad n \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\frac{N_0}{2}\right)$$

Find the LRT and the resulting probability of error assuming equiprobable symbols and minimum-error-probability criterion (i.e., $\eta = 1$).

### 3.2.2 Step 1 ŌĆö Write the Two Conditional pdfs

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(r \mid H_0) &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{(r+\sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right) \\
p(r \mid H_1) &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{(r-\sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Under each hypothesis, the received sample is Gaussian. The mean is the transmitted symbol value ($+\sqrt{E_b}$ for "1" sent, $-\sqrt{E_b}$ for "0" sent), and the variance is the channel noise variance $N_0/2$, so the variance in the pdf is $N_0/2$, giving the prefactor $1/\sqrt{\pi N_0}$.

### 3.2.3 Step 2 ŌĆö Form the Likelihood Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Lambda(r) &= \frac{p(r\mid H_1)}{p(r\mid H_0)} \\
&= \frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(r-\sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right)}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(r+\sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right)} \\
&= \exp\!\left(\frac{(r+\sqrt{E_b})^2 - (r-\sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The prefactors $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}$ cancel. The exponent of a ratio is the difference of exponents.

### 3.2.4 Step 3 ŌĆö Expand the Numerator Difference

$$
\begin{aligned}
(r+\sqrt{E_b})^2 - (r-\sqrt{E_b})^2 &= \left[r^2 + 2r\sqrt{E_b} + E_b\right] - \left[r^2 - 2r\sqrt{E_b} + E_b\right] \\
&= r^2 + 2r\sqrt{E_b} + E_b - r^2 + 2r\sqrt{E_b} - E_b \\
&= 4r\sqrt{E_b}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The $r^2$ and $E_b$ terms cancel pairwise; the cross terms add. This is a standard difference-of-squares identity: $(a+b)^2 - (a-b)^2 = 4ab$.

### 3.2.5 Step 4 ŌĆö Take the Log and Compare to Threshold

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\Lambda(r) &= \frac{4r\sqrt{E_b}}{N_0} \;\;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\;\; \ln\eta
\end{aligned}
$$

For minimum $P_e$ with equiprobable symbols, $\eta = 1$, so $\ln\eta = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{4r\sqrt{E_b}}{N_0} &\gtrless 0 \\
4r\sqrt{E_b} &\gtrless 0 \\
r &\gtrless 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{r \;\;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\;\; 0}$$

**Logic:** The decision is simply the **sign of the matched-filter output**. Positive $r$ ŌåÆ decide $H_1$ (bit "1"); negative $r$ ŌåÆ decide $H_0$ (bit "0").

### 3.2.6 Step 5 ŌĆö Probability of Error

The error occurs when the noise pushes $r$ to the wrong side of the threshold (zero). Under $H_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_e\mid H_1 &= P(r < 0 \mid H_1) = P\!\left(n < -\sqrt{E_b}\right) \\
&= Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

By symmetry, $P_e\mid H_0$ is identical. The total error probability (equiprobable symbols) is:

$$\boxed{P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$

**Logic:** The argument of $Q$ is the SNR at the matched-filter output, $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$. Higher symbol energy $E_b$ or lower noise $N_0$ ŌåÆ lower $P_e$.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 ŌĆö CA-CFAR Threshold for Exponential Noise

### 3.3.1 Problem Restated

Under $H_0$ (noise only), the test cell $x_0$ and the $N$ reference cells $x_1, \ldots, x_N$ are i.i.d. exponential with mean $\mu$:

$$p(x) = \frac{1}{\mu}\,e^{-x/\mu}, \quad x \geq 0$$

The CFAR statistic is:

$$T = \frac{x_0}{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}x_i} = \frac{x_0}{\bar{x}}$$

Find the decision threshold $\alpha$ that yields false-alarm probability $P_{FA}$.

### 3.3.2 Step 1 ŌĆö pdf of the Sample Mean

The sum $Y = \sum_{i=1}^{N}x_i$ has a Gamma distribution:

$$p_Y(y) = \frac{y^{N-1}e^{-y/\mu}}{\mu^N\,\Gamma(N)}, \quad y \geq 0$$

### 3.3.3 Step 2 ŌĆö pdf of the Mean $\bar{x} = Y/N$

By change of variable $Y = N\bar{x}$, $dY = N\,d\bar{x}$:

$$p_{\bar{x}}(\bar{x}) = \frac{(N\bar{x})^{N-1}e^{-N\bar{x}/\mu}}{\mu^N\,\Gamma(N)}\cdot N = \frac{N^N \bar{x}^{N-1}e^{-N\bar{x}/\mu}}{\mu^N\,\Gamma(N)}$$

### 3.3.4 Step 3 ŌĆö pdf of the Ratio $T = x_0 / \bar{x}$

Using the ratio distribution formula $p_T(t) = \int_0^\infty \bar{x}\,p_{x_0}(t\bar{x})\,p_{\bar{x}}(\bar{x})\,d\bar{x}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_T(t) &= \int_0^\infty \bar{x}\cdot\frac{1}{\mu}e^{-t\bar{x}/\mu}\cdot\frac{N^N \bar{x}^{N-1}e^{-N\bar{x}/\mu}}{\mu^N\Gamma(N)}\,d\bar{x} \\
&= \frac{N^N}{\mu^{N+1}\Gamma(N)}\int_0^\infty \bar{x}^N\,e^{-(N+t)\bar{x}/\mu}\,d\bar{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

The integral is a Gamma integral $\int_0^\infty u^N e^{-au}du = N!/a^{N+1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^\infty \bar{x}^N e^{-(N+t)\bar{x}/\mu}d\bar{x} &= \frac{N!\,\mu^{N+1}}{(N+t)^{N+1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_T(t) &= \frac{N^N}{\mu^{N+1}\Gamma(N)}\cdot\frac{N!\,\mu^{N+1}}{(N+t)^{N+1}} = \frac{N^N\cdot N!}{\Gamma(N)\,(N+t)^{N+1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\Gamma(N) = (N-1)!$ and $N\cdot N! = N\cdot\Gamma(N+1)$:

$$p_T(t) = \frac{N}{(1 + t/N)^{N+1}}, \quad t \geq 0$$

### 3.3.4 Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $P_{FA}$ and Solve for $\alpha$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{FA} &= P(T > \alpha \mid H_0) = \int_\alpha^\infty \frac{N\,dt}{(1+t/N)^{N+1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute $u = 1 + t/N$, $du = dt/N$, $dt = N\,du$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{FA} &= \int_{1+\alpha/N}^\infty \frac{N\cdot N\,du}{u^{N+1}} = N^2\int_{1+\alpha/N}^\infty u^{-(N+1)}du \\
&= N^2\left[\frac{u^{-N}}{N}\right]_{1+\alpha/N}^\infty = N\cdot\frac{1}{(1+\alpha/N)^N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result (the standard CFAR formula):**

$$\boxed{P_{FA} = \frac{1}{(1 + \alpha/N)^N} = \left(1 + \frac{\alpha}{N}\right)^{-N}}$$

**Logic:** The threshold $\alpha$ depends only on $P_{FA}$ and $N$, not on $\mu$. This is the **CFAR property** ŌĆö the false-alarm rate is constant regardless of the noise level. Solving for $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = N\left(P_{FA}^{-1/N} - 1\right)$$

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 ŌĆö MAP Estimation of a Bernoulli-Gaussian Parameter

### 3.4.1 Problem Restated

A signal $A$ is **random** with prior $p(A) = \frac{1}{2}\delta(A-a_0) + \frac{1}{2}\delta(A+a_0)$. We observe $x = A + w$ with $w \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$. Find the MAP estimate of $A$.

### 3.4.2 Step 1 ŌĆö Posterior pdf

By Bayes' rule:

$$p(A \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid A)\,p(A)}{p(x)}$$

### 3.4.3 Step 2 ŌĆö The Two Posterior Masses

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(A = +a_0 \mid x) &\propto p(x \mid A = +a_0)\cdot\tfrac{1}{2} \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-a_0)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \\
P(A = -a_0 \mid x) &\propto p(x \mid A = -a_0)\cdot\tfrac{1}{2} \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x+a_0)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4.4 Step 3 ŌĆö MAP Decision

The MAP rule picks the larger posterior:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{A}_{MAP} &= \begin{cases} +a_0, & \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-a_0)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \geq \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x+a_0)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \\ -a_0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4.5 Step 4 ŌĆö Simplify by Taking Logarithm

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{(x-a_0)^2}{2\sigma^2} &\gtrless -\frac{(x+a_0)^2}{2\sigma^2} \\
(x-a_0)^2 &\lessgtr (x+a_0)^2 \\
x^2 - 2xa_0 + a_0^2 &\lessgtr x^2 + 2xa_0 + a_0^2 \\
-2xa_0 &\lessgtr 2xa_0 \\
-4xa_0 &\lessgtr 0 \\
x \cdot a_0 &\gtrless 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result (assuming $a_0 > 0$):**

$$\boxed{\hat{A}_{MAP} = a_0\,\text{sign}(x)}$$

**Logic:** The MAP estimate reduces to a **sign detector** on the observation. This is a beautiful result that appears in many KTU questions: with symmetric prior, the MAP and the LRT are equivalent.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Tool

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Matrix for KTU Application Problems** (the examiner's reference table):

| Observation Type | Unknown is Deterministic? | Unknown is Random? | **Best Tool** | **Threshold Rule** |
|------------------|---------------------------|--------------------|--------------|--------------------|
| DC level | MLE / Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE) | Bayesian MMSE | Differentiate log-likelihood | N/A |
| Sinusoid (one param) | MLE via DFT peak | MAP if prior known | Maximize periodogram | N/A |
| Sinusoid (multi param) | Joint MLE (closed form) | Joint MAP | Solve $\nabla_\theta L = 0$ | N/A |
| Binary signal in WGN | Neyman-Pearson LRT | Bayesian LRT ($\eta = 1$) | Compare LLR to threshold | $\eta = \lambda$ for NP, $\eta = 1$ for min-$P_e$ |
| M-ary signal | M-ary LRT | M-ary MAP | Choose max posterior | Per-symbol MAP |
| Channel impulse resp. | LS, then refine | MMSE / LMMSE | Solve normal equations | N/A |
| Target in clutter | CFAR (CA, OS, GO) | Bayesian CFAR | Compare to adaptive threshold | $T = x_0/\hat{\mu}_{\text{noise}}$ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section provides Mermaid diagrams and ASCII schematics that map the **structural relationships** between concepts. All Mermaid syntax strictly follows the Alpha-Rule for node identifiers.

---

## 4.1 The Universal Application Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Source] --> B[Signal Generator]
    B --> C[Channel or Medium]
    C --> D[Noise Injection]
    D --> E[Received Data Vector]
    E --> F[Application Layer]
    F --> G1[Estimation Branch]
    F --> G2[Detection Branch]
    G1 --> H1[Parameter Estimate]
    G2 --> H2[Binary or M ary Decision]
    H1 --> I1[Decision or Control System]
    H2 --> I1
    I1 --> J[Actuator or Display]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading this diagram:** The "Application Layer" $F$ is the **fork point** ŌĆö the same received data feeds both an estimator and a detector. In KTU radar problems, the estimator (range, velocity) operates on detections from the detector branch.

---

## 4.2 The Estimator Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Unknown Parameter] --> B{Deterministic or Random}
    B -- Deterministic --> C{Prior Available}
    B -- Random --> D[Use Bayesian MMSE or MAP]
    C -- No Prior --> E[Use MLE]
    C -- Prior Available --> F[Use MAP]
    E --> G{Linear in Data}
    G -- Yes --> H[Closed Form MLE]
    G -- No --> I[Numerical Optimization]
    F --> J{Posterior Easy]
    J -- Yes --> K[Analytic MAP]
    J -- No --> L[MCMC or Importance Sampling]
    D --> M{Posterior Conjugate}
    M -- Yes --> N[Closed Form MMSE]
    M -- No --> O[Numerical Integration]
```

---

## 4.3 The BPSK Detection Chain (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Source Bits] --> A[Bit to Symbol Mapper]
    A --> P[Pulse Shaping Filter]
    P --> CH[AWGN Channel]
    CH --> MF[Matched Filter]
    MF --> SAMP[Sample at Symbol Time]
    SAMP --> DEC{LLR versus Threshold}
    DEC -- Greater --> OUT1[Decide Bit One]
    DEC -- Less --> OUT0[Decide Bit Zero]
    OUT1 --> SINK[Bit Error Rate Counter]
    OUT0 --> SINK
```

---

## 4.4 The Radar CFAR Processing Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RX[Radar Return Signal] --> SQ[Square Law Detector]
    SQ --> TC[Test Cell X0]
    TC --> COMP[Compare to Threshold]
    RX2[Reference Cells X1 to XN] --> AVG[Cell Averager]
    AVG --> THR[Adaptive Threshold Generator]
    THR --> COMP
    COMP --> D{Decision}
    D -- Exceeds --> T1[Target Detected]
    D -- Below --> T0[No Target]
```

---

## 4.5 The Channel Estimation Flow (LS vs. MMSE)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TX[Known Training Sequence] --> CH[Unknown Channel H]
    CH --> RX[Received Vector X]
    NOISE[AWGN W] --> RX
    RX --> LS[LS Estimator]
    RX --> MMSE[MMSE Estimator with Prior Covariance]
    LS --> OUT1[Channel Estimate H LS]
    MMSE --> OUT2[Channel Estimate H MMSE]
    OUT1 --> COMP[MSE Comparison]
    OUT2 --> COMP
    COMP --> R{MMSE always wins for SNR below 10 dB}
    R -- Yes --> NOTE[Use MMSE in low SNR]
    R -- No --> NOTE2[LS approaches MMSE at high SNR]
```

---

## 4.6 Concept Dependency Map (Module 2 of KTU Syllabus)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Probability and Random Variables] --> LP[Likelihood Function]
    P --> B[Bayes Rule]
    LP --> MLE[Maximum Likelihood Estimation]
    B --> MAP[Maximum A Posteriori]
    B --> MMSE[Minimum Mean Square Error]
    MLE --> APP[Application Examples]
    MAP --> APP
    MMSE --> APP
    LP --> LRT[ likelihood Ratio Test]
    LRT --> NP[Neyman Pearson Detector]
    LRT --> BAY[Bayesian Detector]
    NP --> APP
    BAY --> APP
    CRLB[Cram├®r Rao Lower Bound] --> APP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading this dependency map:** Application Examples is the **terminal node** of Module 2 ŌĆö it integrates every prior concept. If you cannot solve application problems, the bottleneck is usually in `LP` (likelihood function construction) or `B` (Bayes rule application).

---

## 4.7 Threshold Comparison Schematic (NP vs. Bayesian)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PDF0[Noise Only pdf p r given H0] --> THR[Threshold Line]
    PDF1[Signal Plus Noise pdf p r given H1] --> THR
    THR -- Right of Threshold --> PD[Probability of Detection]
    THR -- Left of Threshold under H1 --> PM[Probability of Miss]
    PDF0 -- Right Tail under H0 --> PFA[Probability of False Alarm]
    PDF0 -- Left Tail under H0 --> PC[Probability of Correct Rejection]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to use this section:** Attempt each Part A question in 2 minutes and each Part B in 25 minutes ŌĆö exactly the KTU ESE time budget. Compare your answer to the model solution word-for-word. The model solutions use KTU examiner-approved phrasing.

---

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023, Set B]

**State and explain the Neyman-Pearson criterion for binary hypothesis testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**

> The Neyman-Pearson (NP) criterion is a decision rule for binary hypothesis testing that **maximizes the probability of detection $P_D$ subject to a constraint on the probability of false alarm $P_{FA} \leq \alpha$**. The optimal test is the **likelihood ratio test (LRT)**:
> $$\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_0)} \;\gtrless\; \lambda$$
> where the threshold $\lambda$ is chosen so that $P_{FA} = \alpha$. Unlike the Bayesian criterion, NP does not require prior probabilities and is the standard in radar and sonar applications.

**Mark split:** Definition (1), LRT expression (1), Use of Lagrange multiplier / threshold choice (1).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, Set A]

**Explain the significance of the Cram├®r-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) in estimation theory with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~70 words):**

> The CRLB provides a **theoretical lower bound on the variance of any unbiased estimator**: $\text{var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq 1/I(\theta)$, where $I(\theta) = -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln p(\mathbf{x};\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$ is the Fisher Information. An estimator that **attains** the CRLB is called **efficient**. *Example:* For a DC level $A$ in AWGN with $N$ samples, $\text{var}(\hat{A}_{MLE}) = \sigma^2/N$, which equals the CRLB ŌĆö so the sample mean is efficient.

**Mark split:** CRLB statement (1), Fisher Information (1), DC-level example (1).

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Estimation Focus

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Paper]**
> 
> **(a)** Derive the Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE) of the amplitude $A$ of a DC signal observed in additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) of variance $\sigma^2$ using $N$ independent samples $x[0], x[1], \ldots, x[N-1]$. Show that the estimator is unbiased and efficient. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** For the case where the noise variance $\sigma^2$ is **also unknown**, derive the joint MLE of $\{A, \sigma^2\}$ and comment on whether the variance estimator is unbiased. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Observation model (1 mark):**

$$x[n] = A + w[n], \quad w[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2),\ \text{i.i.d.}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Likelihood function (1 mark):**

$$p(\mathbf{x};A) = \frac{1}{(2\pi\sigma^2)^{N/2}}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n]-A)^2\right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Log-likelihood and differentiation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(A) &= -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n]-A)^2 \\
\frac{dL}{dA} &= \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n]-A) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö MLE result (1 mark):**

$$\hat{A}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x[n]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Unbiasedness (1 mark):**

$$E[\hat{A}] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}E[x[n]] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}A = A$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Efficiency via CRLB (1 mark):**

$$\text{var}(\hat{A}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{N}, \quad \text{CRLB} = \frac{1}{N\cdot(1/\sigma^2)} = \frac{\sigma^2}{N} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{MLE attains CRLB, hence efficient.}$$

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Joint log-likelihood (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(A,\sigma^2) &= -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n]-A)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Partial derivative w.r.t. $A$ (1 mark):** Same as before, yielding $\hat{A} = \frac{1}{N}\sum x[n]$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Partial derivative w.r.t. $\sigma^2$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \sigma^2} &= -\frac{N}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2\sigma^4}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n]-A)^2 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for $\hat{\sigma}^2$ (1 mark):**

$$\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n]-\hat{A})^2$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Comment on bias (2 marks):**

Since $\hat{A}$ is computed from the same data, one degree of freedom is "used up":

$$E[\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE}] = \frac{N-1}{N}\sigma^2 \neq \sigma^2$$

The MLE of variance is **biased**. The **unbiased** estimator is:

$$\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{unb}} = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n]-\hat{A})^2 = S^2 \quad \text{(the sample variance)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall ŌĆö Where students lose marks:**
> 1. Forgetting the **second-derivative test** to confirm a maximum in part (a). You will lose 1 mark.
> 2. In part (b), writing the **joint MLE equations** but not solving them. You must give the closed-form pair $(\hat{A}, \hat{\sigma}^2)$.
> 3. Stating that the MLE of $\sigma^2$ is unbiased ŌĆö it is **biased** by a factor of $(N-1)/N$. This is the single most common error.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Detection Focus (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**
> 
> **(a)** Consider a BPSK communication system where the received sample under the two hypotheses is $r = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n$ with $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$. Derive the optimal detector using the likelihood ratio test assuming minimum probability of error with equiprobable symbols. Find the resulting probability of error in terms of $E_b/N_0$. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** In a radar CFAR receiver, the test cell $x_0$ and $N$ reference cells are i.i.d. exponential with mean $\mu$ under noise-only hypothesis. With the CA-CFAR statistic $T = x_0/\bar{x}$, derive the relationship between the threshold $\alpha$ and the false-alarm probability $P_{FA}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Conditional pdfs (1 mark):**

$$
p(r\mid H_0) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(r+\sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right),\quad p(r\mid H_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(r-\sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Likelihood ratio (1.5 marks):**

$$\Lambda(r) = \exp\!\left(\frac{4r\sqrt{E_b}}{N_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Log-LRT and threshold for min $P_e$ with equiprobable symbols (2 marks):**

$$\ln\Lambda(r) = \frac{4r\sqrt{E_b}}{N_0} \gtrless 0 \;\Rightarrow\; r \gtrless 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Probability of error (2.5 marks):**

Under $H_1$, $r = \sqrt{E_b} + n$, so $r < 0$ iff $n < -\sqrt{E_b}$:

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Result table (for examiner reference):**

| $E_b/N_0$ (dB) | $P_e$ |
|----------------|-------|
| 0 | $7.8 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| 5 | $5.9 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 9.6 | $1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| 12 | $3.9 \times 10^{-8}$ |

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö pdf of the ratio $T$ (3 marks):** Following the derivation in Section 3.3 above:

$$p_T(t) = \frac{N}{(1+t/N)^{N+1}}, \quad t \geq 0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö False-alarm probability integral (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{FA} &= \int_\alpha^\infty \frac{N}{(1+t/N)^{N+1}}\,dt
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $u = 1 + t/N$:

$$P_{FA} = \left(1 + \frac{\alpha}{N}\right)^{-N}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solving for $\alpha$ (2 marks):**

$$\alpha = N\left(P_{FA}^{-1/N} - 1\right)$$

**Numerical illustration (if asked in (c) of original question):** For $N = 16$ and $P_{FA} = 10^{-6}$:

$$\alpha = 16\left((10^{-6})^{-1/16} - 1\right) = 16\left(10^{0.375} - 1\right) = 16(2.371 - 1) \approx 21.9$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall ŌĆö Detection Questions:**
> 1. Forgetting the **$\eta = 1$** condition (equiprobable + minimum $P_e$). You must explicitly state this before setting $\ln\eta = 0$.
> 2. Confusing $P_{FA}$ with $P_D$ in the CFAR derivation. The integration is over the **right tail** (exceedance) under $H_0$.
> 3. In part (b), some students mistakenly write the Chi-squared pdf (for Gaussian cells) instead of the **Gamma** pdf (for exponential cells). Read the question carefully.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist. Tick each item only after solving a problem on it. Stop revising only when every box is ticked.**

### A. Conceptual Foundation (must-know)

- [ ] I can write the **joint Gaussian pdf** for i.i.d. samples in $\leq 30$ seconds.
- [ ] I can state the **MLE**, **MAP**, and **MMSE** rules from memory.
- [ ] I know that the **CRLB** is a lower bound, not a recipe.
- [ ] I can distinguish **Neyman-Pearson** from **Bayesian** detection criteria.
- [ ] I know the **standard pdf families** (Gaussian, Exponential, Chi-squared, Gamma, Rayleigh) and their parameters.

### B. Application Family 1 ŌĆö DC Level

- [ ] I can derive $\hat{A}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum x[n]$ from scratch.
- [ ] I know $\text{var}(\hat{A}) = \sigma^2/N$ and that it equals the CRLB.
- [ ] I can identify the **sufficient statistic** ($T = \sum x[n]$).
- [ ] I know the unbiased variance estimator uses $1/(N-1)$, not $1/N$.

### C. Application Family 2 ŌĆö Sinusoidal Signals

- [ ] I can write the MLE for amplitude, phase, and frequency separately.
- [ ] I know the **periodogram** is the ML frequency estimator.
- [ ] I can quote the **CRLB for phase** ($\text{var}(\hat\phi) \geq 2\sigma^2/(NA^2)$).
- [ ] I know that **nonlinear** problems often require numerical optimization (Newton-Raphson, EM).

### D. Application Family 3 ŌĆö Communication Detection

- [ ] I can derive the **BPSK LRT** in $\leq 5$ lines.
- [ ] I know $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ for BPSK.
- [ ] I know the **matched filter** maximizes SNR at the sampling instant.
- [ ] I know the **union bound** for M-PSK: $P_e \approx 2Q(\sqrt{2E_s/N_0}\sin(\pi/M))$.
- [ ] I can extend to **M-PAM** and **M-QAM** with gray-coding argument.

### E. Application Family 4 ŌĆö Radar / CFAR

- [ ] I can derive the **CA-CFAR threshold** $P_{FA} = (1+\alpha/N)^{-N}$.
- [ ] I know the **CFAR loss** formula: $L_{\text{CFAR}} \approx 10\log_{10}(N) - 5\log_{10}(N)$ (approx.).
- [ ] I can name alternatives: **OS-CFAR**, **GO-CFAR**, **SO-CFAR**.
- [ ] I know radar ambiguity function basics: range-Doppler coupling.

### F. Application Family 5 ŌĆö Channel / System ID

- [ ] I can derive the **LS channel estimate** $\hat{\mathbf{h}} = (\mathbf{S}^T\mathbf{S})^{-1}\mathbf{S}^T\mathbf{x}$.
- [ ] I know the **MMSE** estimate uses the channel covariance matrix $\mathbf{R}_h$.
- [ ] I can compute the **MSE** for both estimators and compare.
- [ ] I know the role of **pilots** in 5G NR and OFDM systems.

### G. Examiner-Behaviour Traps to Avoid

- [ ] Do not confuse **bias** ($E[\hat\theta]-\theta$) with **variance** ($\text{var}(\hat\theta)$).
- [ ] Do not forget to mention **sufficient statistics** in MLE problems (1-mark gift).
- [ ] Do not write the **threshold** as a generic constant ŌĆö derive it from $\eta$ or $P_{FA}$.
- [ ] In CFAR problems, always state that the threshold is **independent of $\mu$** (the CFAR property).
- [ ] When asked "compare LS and MMSE", always note the **need for prior covariance** in MMSE.

### H. Numerical Benchmarks to Memorize

- [ ] BPSK at $E_b/N_0 = 9.6$ dB ŌåÆ $P_e = 10^{-5}$.
- [ ] QPSK has the **same** $P_e$ as BPSK at the same $E_b/N_0$.
- [ ] 16-QAM needs **~4 dB more** $E_b/N_0$ than QPSK at $P_e = 10^{-5}$.
- [ ] DC-level CRLB reduction: doubling $N$ ŌåÆ 3 dB SNR improvement.

### I. One-Line Definitions to Speak in Viva

- "The MLE maximizes the likelihood function over $\theta$."
- "The MAP maximizes the posterior pdf $p(\theta\mid\mathbf{x})$."
- "The MMSE estimator minimizes $E[(\hat\theta-\theta)^2]$ and equals $E[\theta\mid\mathbf{x}]$."
- "The Neyman-Pearson test maximizes $P_D$ at fixed $P_{FA}$."
- "A sufficient statistic captures all information in the data about $\theta$."

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Warning:** Application problems in KTU Module 2 are the most "valuable" questions (14 marks each) and also the most "dangerous" ŌĆö a single missed step in the likelihood construction loses you 3-4 marks. Always:
> 1. Write the **observation model explicitly** with named random variables.
> 2. State the **distributional assumption** (i.i.d. Gaussian, exponential, etc.).
> 3. Write the **decision rule** before computing the threshold.
> 4. **Simplify** the LRT/LLR to its most compact form before comparing to $\eta$.
> 5. End with an **engineering interpretation** ("$P_e$ drops as $E_b/N_0$ rises because ...") ŌĆö this gives the 1-mark "impression" bonus KTU examiners are authorized to award.

topics.title: Fundamentals of Detection Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Detection Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Fundamentals of Detection Theory

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Detection Theory** is a branch of statistical signal processing that deals with the problem of determining which of several possible hypotheses (signal models) best explains a set of noisy observations. In its fundamental form, it addresses the **Binary Hypothesis Testing** problem, where an observer must decide between two competing statistical hypotheses based on received data.

Mathematically, the binary detection problem is formulated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_0 &: \quad x[n] = w[n] \\
H_1 &: \quad x[n] = s[n] + w[n]
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $H_0$ = Null Hypothesis (signal absent, noise only)
- $H_1$ = Alternative Hypothesis (signal present in noise)
- $x[n]$ = Received observation sequence
- $s[n]$ = Known signal of interest
- $w[n]$ = Additive noise (typically Gaussian)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme emphasizes the *decision-theoretic* approach to detection, where optimal rules are derived by minimizing a cost function (Bayes) or by constraining the false-alarm probability (Neyman-Pearson).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a doctor listening to a patient's heartbeat with a stethoscope. Buried in the ambient noise of the room, you must decide: **"Is there a heart murmur (signal present), or is it just normal heartbeat sounds (signal absent)?"**

- You form two mental hypotheses: "Healthy" vs. "Murmur."
- The waveform your ears receive is $x(t)$ ŌĆö a corrupted version of the true heart signal.
- The *background hum* of the room acts as noise $w(t)$.
- You mentally compute a "confidence score" ŌĆö in engineering, this is the **Likelihood Ratio** $\Lambda(x)$.
- If the score crosses a threshold, you diagnose "Murmur" (choose $H_1$); otherwise, "Healthy" (choose $H_0$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition ŌĆö Decision Rule:** A decision rule $\delta(x)$ is a function that maps the observation space to the hypothesis space: $\delta : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \{H_0, H_1\}$. The decision rule partitions the observation space into two disjoint regions: $R_0$ (decide $H_0$) and $R_1$ (decide $H_1$).

## 1.3 The Four Possible Outcomes

In binary detection, four outcomes exist, two correct and two erroneous:

| Outcome | Decision | Truth | Probability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **True Negative (TN)** | Choose $H_0$ | $H_0$ true | $P(H_0 \mid H_0) = 1 - P_F$ |
| **False Positive (FP)** | Choose $H_1$ | $H_0$ true | $P(H_1 \mid H_0) = P_F$ |
| **False Negative (FN)** | Choose $H_0$ | $H_1$ true | $P(H_0 \mid H_1) = P_M$ |
| **True Positive (TP)** | Choose $H_1$ | $H_1$ true | $P(H_1 \mid H_1) = P_D$ |

where:
- $P_F$ = Probability of **False Alarm** (Type-I error) ŌĆö Significance level $\alpha$
- $P_M$ = Probability of **Miss** (Type-II error) ŌĆö $\beta$
- $P_D$ = Probability of **Detection** ŌĆö Power of the test = $1 - \beta$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric ŌĆö Power of Test:** $P_D = 1 - P_M$. A "good" detector has high $P_D$ (catches real signals) and low $P_F$ (doesn't cry wolf).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 The Decision-Theoretic Framework

The fundamental question of detection theory is: **"Given an observation $x$, what is the optimal rule to choose between $H_0$ and $H_1$?"** The answer depends on the chosen optimality criterion.

### 2.1.1 Bayes Criterion (Minimum Risk)

We assign a **cost** $C_{ij}$ to the event "decide $H_i$ when $H_j$ is true." The Bayesian risk is:

$$
R = C_{00} P(H_0) P(H_0 \mid H_0) + C_{10} P(H_0) P(H_1 \mid H_0) + C_{01} P(H_1) P(H_0 \mid H_1) + C_{11} P(H_1) P(H_1 \mid H_1)
$$

The Bayes optimal decision rule minimizes $R$, leading to the **Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT)**:

$$
\Lambda(x) = \frac{p(x; H_1)}{p(x; H_0)} \;\;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\;\; \eta
$$

where the threshold is:

$$
\eta = \frac{P(H_0)(C_{10} - C_{00})}{P(H_1)(C_{01} - C_{11})}
$$

### 2.1.2 Minimum Probability of Error Criterion

A special case of Bayes where $C_{00} = C_{11} = 0$ and $C_{10} = C_{01} = 1$:

$$
\eta = \frac{P(H_0)}{P(H_1)}
$$

This is the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** rule.

### 2.1.3 Neyman-Pearson (NP) Criterion

When prior probabilities are unknown, we **fix** $P_F = \alpha$ and **maximize** $P_D$. The NP lemma states the LRT is the most powerful test:

$$
\Lambda(x) \;\;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\;\; \gamma
$$

where $\gamma$ is chosen to satisfy $P_F(\gamma) = \alpha$.

## 2.2 Log-Likelihood Ratio

For computational stability, we work with the **Log-Likelihood Ratio (LLR)**:

$$
\ell(x) = \ln \Lambda(x) = \ln p(x; H_1) - \ln p(x; H_0)
$$

The decision rule becomes:

$$
\ell(x) \;\;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\;\; \ln \eta
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference for KTU University Exams**

| Concept | Formula | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Binary Hypothesis | $H_0 : x=w$, $\,H_1 : x=s+w$ | $s$ = signal, $w$ = noise |
| Likelihood Ratio | $\Lambda(x) = p(x \mid H_1) / p(x \mid H_0)$ | $\Lambda \gtrless \eta$ |
| Log-Likelihood Ratio | $\ell(x) = \ln p(x \mid H_1) - \ln p(x \mid H_0)$ | $\ell \gtrless \ln \eta$ |
| Bayes Threshold | $\eta = \frac{P(H_0)(C_{10}-C_{00})}{P(H_1)(C_{01}-C_{11})}$ | Risk minimization |
| Min $P_e$ Threshold | $\eta = P(H_0)/P(H_1)$ | MAP / Min error |
| False Alarm Prob. | $P_F = \int_{R_1} p(x \mid H_0) \, dx$ | Type-I error |
| Detection Prob. | $P_D = \int_{R_1} p(x \mid H_1) \, dx$ | Power of test |
| Miss Prob. | $P_M = 1 - P_D$ | Type-II error |
| Total Error Prob. | $P_e = P(H_0) P_F + P(H_1) P_M$ | Min $P_e$ criterion |
| Gaussian PDF (scalar) | $p(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Mean $\mu$, variance $\sigma^2$ |
| ROC Curve | $P_D$ vs. $P_F$ parametric in $\gamma$ | Performance benchmark |
| Deflection Coefficient | $d^2 = \frac{[\mathbb{E}(\Lambda \mid H_1) - \mathbb{E}(\Lambda \mid H_0)]^2}{\mathrm{Var}(\Lambda \mid H_0)}$ | Detector SNR |
| Likelihood Ratio (Vector) | $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \prod_{n=0}^{N-1} \frac{p(x_n \mid H_1)}{p(x_n \mid H_0)}$ | Independent samples |
| Min Error Probability | $P_e = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 - \mathrm{erf}\left(\frac{\vert\mu_1 - \mu_0\vert}{2\sqrt{2}\sigma}\right)\right]$ | Equal priors, Gauss |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Radar & Sonar**: Detect a target's echo amid ocean/atmosphere noise.
- **Digital Communications**: Decode transmitted bits (1 vs. 0) corrupted by channel noise (AWGN).
- **Biomedical Signal Processing**: Detect QRS complexes in ECG, seizure spikes in EEG.
- **Speech Recognition**: Identify phoneme presence in noisy audio.
- **Fault Detection**: Diagnose machine failure from vibration/thermal sensors.
- **Image Processing**: Edge detection, object recognition in pixel data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Bayes Risk Decision Rule

**Objective:** Derive the LRT that minimizes the Bayesian risk $R$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the average risk.** Split the cost function over the two decision regions $R_0$ and $R_1$ that partition the observation space $\mathcal{X}$:

$$
R = C_{00} P(H_0) \int_{R_0} p(x \mid H_0) \, dx + C_{10} P(H_0) \int_{R_1} p(x \mid H_0) \, dx
$$

$$
+ C_{01} P(H_1) \int_{R_0} p(x \mid H_1) \, dx + C_{11} P(H_1) \int_{R_1} p(x \mid H_1) \, dx
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Use the partition of unity.** Since $R_0 \cup R_1 = \mathcal{X}$ and $R_0 \cap R_1 = \emptyset$:

$$
\int_{R_0} p(x \mid H_j) \, dx = 1 - \int_{R_1} p(x \mid H_j) \, dx
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute.** Apply the above to eliminate the $R_0$ integrals in favor of $R_1$:

$$
R = C_{00} P(H_0) + C_{01} P(H_1) + \int_{R_1} \Big[ P(H_0)(C_{10}-C_{00}) p(x \mid H_0) - P(H_1)(C_{01}-C_{11}) p(x \mid H_1) \Big] \, dx
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Minimize.** The first two terms are constants. To minimize $R$, we want the integrand to be **negative** (so the integral subtracts as much as possible) for each $x \in R_1$. Therefore, we choose $x \in R_1$ (i.e., decide $H_1$) if:

$$
P(H_1)(C_{01}-C_{11}) p(x \mid H_1) > P(H_0)(C_{10}-C_{00}) p(x \mid H_0)
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Form the likelihood ratio.** Rearranging:

$$
\frac{p(x \mid H_1)}{p(x \mid H_0)} > \frac{P(H_0)(C_{10}-C_{00})}{P(H_1)(C_{01}-C_{11})} = \eta
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final LRT.**

$$
\boxed{\Lambda(x) = \frac{p(x \mid H_1)}{p(x \mid H_0)} \;\;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\;\; \eta}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Detection of DC Signal in AWGN (Scalar)

**Problem Setup:** Under $H_0$, $x = w$ where $w \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$. Under $H_1$, $x = A + w$ where $A$ is a known constant.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the conditional PDFs.** For scalar Gaussian:

$$
p(x \mid H_0) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

$$
p(x \mid H_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-A)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Form the likelihood ratio.** 

$$
\Lambda(x) = \frac{p(x \mid H_1)}{p(x \mid H_0)} = \frac{\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-A)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)}{\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify the exponent.** Expand the numerator:

$$
-\frac{(x-A)^2}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2} = -\frac{x^2 - 2Ax + A^2}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2} = \frac{2Ax - A^2}{2\sigma^2} = \frac{Ax}{\sigma^2} - \frac{A^2}{2\sigma^2}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute back:**

$$
\Lambda(x) = \exp\!\left(\frac{Ax}{\sigma^2} - \frac{A^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{A^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{Ax}{\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply monotonic transformation.** Since $\ln(\cdot)$ is monotonically increasing, the LRT is equivalent to the LLR test:

$$
\ell(x) = \frac{Ax}{\sigma^2} - \frac{A^2}{2\sigma^2} \;\;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\;\; \ln \eta
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Solve for the threshold on $x$.** The test is linear in $x$ (sufficient statistic):

$$
x \;\;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\;\; \gamma' = \frac{\sigma^2}{A} \ln \eta + \frac{A}{2}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Compute performance metrics.** Under $H_0$, $x \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, so:

$$
P_F = P(x > \gamma' \mid H_0) = Q\!\left(\frac{\gamma'}{\sigma}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{\sigma \ln \eta}{A} + \frac{A}{2\sigma}\right)
$$

Under $H_1$, $x \sim \mathcal{N}(A, \sigma^2)$:

$$
P_D = P(x > \gamma' \mid H_1) = Q\!\left(\frac{\gamma' - A}{\sigma}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{\sigma \ln \eta}{A} - \frac{A}{2\sigma}\right)
$$

where $Q(x) = \frac{1}{2}\mathrm{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$ is the complementary Gaussian CDF.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Minimum Probability of Error for Equal-Variance Gaussians

**Setup:** $H_0 : x \sim \mathcal{N}(-\mu, \sigma^2)$, $H_1 : x \sim \mathcal{N}(+\mu, \sigma^2)$, with equal priors $P(H_0) = P(H_1) = 0.5$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Min $P_e$ threshold.** With $P(H_0)=P(H_1)$ and equal costs:

$$
\eta = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \ln \eta = 0
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö The LLR test:**

$$
\ell(x) = \frac{2\mu x}{\sigma^2} \;\;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\;\; 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x \;\;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\;\; 0
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $P_e$.** By symmetry, $P_F = P_M$:

$$
P_e = P(H_0) P_F + P(H_1) P_M = P(H_1) P(x<0 \mid H_1) = \int_{-\infty}^{0} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply Q-function:**

$$
P_e = Q\!\left(\frac{\mu}{\sigma}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{\mu}{\sigma\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 - \mathrm{erf}\!\left(\frac{\mu}{\sigma\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]
$$

This shows that detection performance depends only on the **signal-to-noise ratio** $d = \mu/\sigma$.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Threshold $\eta$ | Prior Info | Cost Info | Performance Measure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bayes | $P(H_0)(C_{10}-C_{00})/[P(H_1)(C_{01}-C_{11})]$ | Required | Required | Minimum Bayesian Risk |
| Min $P_e$ (MAP) | $P(H_0)/P(H_1)$ | Required | Not needed | Min total error |
| Max Likelihood | $1$ | Not needed | Not needed | Asymptotically Min $P_e$ |
| Neyman-Pearson | Set $P_F = \alpha$ | Not needed | Not needed | Max $P_D$ at fixed $P_F$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Binary Detection System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Signal Source s] --> B[Channel Adds Noise w]
    B --> C[Observation x]
    C --> D[Preprocessor Statistic]
    D --> E[Compute Likelihood Ratio Lambda]
    E --> F{Compare to Threshold eta}
    F -->|Lambda greater eta| G[Decision H1 Signal Present]
    F -->|Lambda less eta| H[Decision H0 Signal Absent]
    G --> I[Performance Metrics PD PF]
    H --> I
```

## 4.2 Hypothesis Testing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Observation x Received]) --> PDF1[Form p x given H0]
    PDF1 --> PDF2[Form p x given H1]
    PDF2 --> LR[Compute Likelihood Ratio Lambda x]
    LR --> Compare{Is Lambda x greater than eta}
    Compare -->|Yes| Choose1[Decide H1]
    Compare -->|No| Choose0[Decide H0]
    Choose1 --> Out1[Result Signal Detected]
    Choose0 --> Out2[Result No Signal]
    Out1 --> Eval[Evaluate PF and PD]
    Out2 --> Eval
```

## 4.3 ROC Curve Generation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sweep Threshold gamma] --> B[For each gamma]
    B --> C[Compute PF = integral over R1 of p x H0]
    C --> D[Compute PD = integral over R1 of p x H1]
    D --> E[Plot point PF PD]
    E --> F{More gamma values}
    F -->|Yes| A
    F -->|No| G[Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve]
```

## 4.4 Probability Density and Threshold Regions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Obs["Observation Space X"]
        R0["Region R0 Decide H0"]
        R1["Region R1 Decide H1"]
    end
    P0["p x given H0 noise only"] -.Distributes.-> R0
    P1["p x given H1 signal plus noise"] -.Distributes.-> R1
    R0 --> PF[Probability of Miss PM shaded overlap with H1 pdf]
    R1 --> PFA[Probability of False Alarm PF shaded overlap with H0 pdf]
    R1 --> PD[Probability of Detection PD area under p x H1 in R1]
```

## 4.5 Criterion Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Detection Problem] --> Q1{Are priors known}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Are costs known}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Need to fix PF}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Bayes[Use Bayes Criterion]
    Q2 -->|No| MinPe[Use Min Probability of Error MAP]
    Q3 -->|Yes| NP[Use Neyman Pearson Criterion]
    Q3 -->|No| MLE[Use Maximum Likelihood eta equal 1]
    Bayes --> LRT1[LRT with eta from costs]
    MinPe --> LRT2[LRT with eta equal P0 over P1]
    NP --> LRT3[LRT with gamma to satisfy PF equal alpha]
    MLE --> LRT4[LRT with eta equal 1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above provide a structural overview. The shaded regions in the probability density diagram are computed analytically using the $Q$-function as shown in Derivation 3.2.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the following terms in the context of binary hypothesis testing:**
**(a)** Probability of False Alarm $P_F$
**(b)** Probability of Detection $P_D$
**(c)** Probability of Miss $P_M$

**Model Answer:**

- **Probability of False Alarm $P_F$:** It is the probability of deciding $H_1$ (signal present) when $H_0$ (signal absent) is actually true. It is a Type-I error, denoted as $\alpha$. Formally:

$$
P_F = P(\text{decide } H_1 \mid H_0) = \int_{R_1} p(x \mid H_0) \, dx
$$

- **Probability of Detection $P_D$:** It is the probability of correctly deciding $H_1$ when $H_1$ is true. It is the **power** of the test, i.e., $1 - \beta$:

$$
P_D = P(\text{decide } H_1 \mid H_1) = \int_{R_1} p(x \mid H_1) \, dx
$$

- **Probability of Miss $P_M$:** It is the probability of deciding $H_0$ when $H_1$ is actually true. It is a Type-II error, denoted as $\beta$:

$$
P_M = P(\text{decide } H_0 \mid H_1) = 1 - P_D = \int_{R_0} p(x \mid H_1) \, dx
$$

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the Neyman-Pearson Lemma.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Neyman-Pearson Lemma:** For a binary hypothesis test with simple hypotheses $H_0$ and $H_1$, the likelihood ratio test (LRT)
>
> $$\Lambda(x) = \frac{p(x \mid H_1)}{p(x \mid H_0)} \;\;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\;\; \gamma$$
>
> is the **most powerful test** at a prescribed significance level $\alpha = P_F$. That is, among all tests with $P_F \leq \alpha$, the LRT maximizes the probability of detection $P_D$.

**Significance:** When prior probabilities $P(H_0)$ and $P(H_1)$ are unknown, Bayes criterion cannot be applied. NP lemma provides the optimal detection rule by fixing $P_F$ (the false-alarm budget) and maximizing $P_D$ (the detection capability).

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the Bayes decision rule for binary hypothesis testing, clearly defining the likelihood ratio test (LRT) and the Bayes threshold. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following binary detection problem under AWGN:

$$
H_0 : x = w, \quad H_1 : x = 1 + w
$$

where $w \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$. If the prior probabilities are $P(H_0) = P(H_1) = 0.5$ and the costs are equal for correct decisions, find:
- **(i)** The likelihood ratio test
- **(ii)** The decision rule
- **(iii)** The probability of error $P_e$ **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation of Bayes Rule:**

The Bayesian risk is the expected cost of decision:

$$
R = \sum_{i=0}^{1} \sum_{j=0}^{1} C_{ij} \, P(\text{decide } H_i \mid H_j \text{ true}) \, P(H_j)
$$

Substituting the integrals over decision regions:

$$
R = C_{00} P(H_0) \int_{R_0} p(x \mid H_0) dx + C_{10} P(H_0) \int_{R_1} p(x \mid H_0) dx + C_{01} P(H_1) \int_{R_0} p(x \mid H_1) dx + C_{11} P(H_1) \int_{R_1} p(x \mid H_1) dx
$$

Using $\int_{R_0} f \, dx = 1 - \int_{R_1} f \, dx$:

$$
R = C_{00} P(H_0) + C_{01} P(H_1) + \int_{R_1} \left[ P(H_0)(C_{10}-C_{00}) p(x \mid H_0) - P(H_1)(C_{01}-C_{11}) p(x \mid H_1) \right] dx
$$

To minimize $R$, choose $x \in R_1$ when the bracketed integrand is negative:

$$
P(H_1)(C_{01}-C_{11}) p(x \mid H_1) > P(H_0)(C_{10}-C_{00}) p(x \mid H_0)
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\Lambda(x) = \frac{p(x \mid H_1)}{p(x \mid H_0)} \;\;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\;\; \eta = \frac{P(H_0)(C_{10}-C_{00})}{P(H_1)(C_{01}-C_{11})}}
$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Solution:**

**(i) Likelihood Ratio Test:**

With $\sigma^2 = 1$ and $A = 1$:

$$
p(x \mid H_0) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2}\right), \quad p(x \mid H_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-1)^2}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\Lambda(x) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-1)^2}{2} + \frac{x^2}{2}\right) = \exp\!\left(x - \frac{1}{2}\right)
$$

**(ii) Decision Rule:**

With $P(H_0)=P(H_1)=0.5$ and equal costs, $\eta = 1$, so $\ln \eta = 0$:

$$
x - \frac{1}{2} \gtrless 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x \gtrless \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Decision:** Choose $H_1$ if $x > 0.5$, else $H_0$.

**(iii) Probability of Error:**

$$
P_e = P(H_0) P_F + P(H_1) P_M
$$

By symmetry of the problem (means $\pm 0.5$ around threshold $0.5$):

$$
P_F = P(x > 0.5 \mid H_0) = Q(0.5) = \frac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{0.5}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \approx 0.3085
$$

$$
P_M = P(x \leq 0.5 \mid H_1) = Q(0.5) \approx 0.3085
$$

$$
P_e = 0.5 \times 0.3085 + 0.5 \times 0.3085 = 0.3085
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve. Discuss how it characterizes the performance of a binary detector. What is the significance of the area under the ROC curve (AUC)? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A binary communication system transmits bits $b_0 = -1$ and $b_1 = +1$ over an AWGN channel with noise variance $\sigma^2 = 2$. The received signal is $x = b_i + w$ with equal priors. **(7 Marks)**

- **(i)** Formulate the hypothesis test
- **(ii)** Derive the MAP decision rule
- **(iii)** Compute $P_e$ in terms of the $Q$-function and evaluate for the given value

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö ROC Curve:**

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** curve is a plot of $P_D$ (probability of detection) versus $P_F$ (probability of false alarm), parameterized by the detection threshold $\gamma$:

$$
\text{ROC} = \{ (P_F(\gamma), P_D(\gamma)) : \gamma \in \mathbb{R} \}
$$

**Key Properties:**
- Each point on the ROC corresponds to a specific threshold value.
- The point $(0, 1)$ is the ideal detector (perfect detection).
- The diagonal $P_D = P_F$ represents a random guess.
- The curve is always monotonically non-decreasing.
- A detector with a higher curve (closer to top-left) is better.

**Area Under the Curve (AUC):** 

$$
\mathrm{AUC} = \int_0^1 P_D(P_F) \, dP_F
$$

Interpretation: $\mathrm{AUC}$ equals the probability that the detector will rank a randomly chosen signal-present observation higher than a randomly chosen signal-absent observation. $\mathrm{AUC} = 1$ is perfect; $\mathrm{AUC} = 0.5$ is random.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Communication System:**

**(i) Hypothesis Test:**

$$
H_0 : x = -1 + w, \quad H_1 : x = +1 + w
$$

with $w \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 2)$.

**(ii) MAP Decision Rule:**

Conditional PDFs:

$$
p(x \mid H_0) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x+1)^2}{4}\right), \quad p(x \mid H_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-1)^2}{4}\right)
$$

Likelihood ratio:

$$
\Lambda(x) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-1)^2}{4} + \frac{(x+1)^2}{4}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{4x}{4}\right) = \exp(x)
$$

With equal priors, $\eta = 1 \Rightarrow \ln \eta = 0$:

$$
x \gtrless 0
$$

**Decision:** Choose $H_1$ if $x > 0$, else $H_0$.

**(iii) Probability of Error:**

Under $H_0$: $x \sim \mathcal{N}(-1, 2)$, so $P_F = P(x > 0 \mid H_0) = Q(0.5) = Q(1/\sqrt{2})$:

$$
P_F = Q\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \approx Q(0.707) \approx 0.2398
$$

By symmetry, $P_M = P_F$:

$$
P_e = 0.5 P_F + 0.5 P_M = P_F = Q\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \approx 0.2398
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing the threshold direction:** Students often write $\Lambda(x) \gtrless \eta$ without clarifying which side is $H_1$. Always explicitly state: "Decide $H_1$ if $\Lambda(x) > \eta$."
> 2. **Bayes threshold with wrong cost indices:** In $C_{ij}$, the first index $i$ is the *decision*, second $j$ is the *truth*. Mixing these up gives the wrong threshold.
> 3. **Forgetting the $Q$-function argument:** When computing $P_F$ and $P_D$, the *standardized* distance to the threshold must be divided by $\sigma$. Students often forget the denominator.
> 4. **Log vs. natural log:** $\ln$ (natural log) is used for LLR. Using $\log_{10}$ shifts the threshold by a constant $\log_{10} e$.
> 5. **ROC misuse:** $P_D$ on Y-axis, $P_F$ on X-axis ŌĆö not the other way around.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Binary Detection:** Two hypotheses $H_0$ (noise only) and $H_1$ (signal + noise). **(Core foundation)**
- **Likelihood Ratio:** $\Lambda(x) = p(x \mid H_1) / p(x \mid H_0)$ ŌĆö the **central quantity** of detection theory. Compare to threshold $\eta$.
- **LRT is universal:** Bayes, Min $P_e$, MLE, and NP are all LRTs with different thresholds. Memorize the four threshold formulas.
- **Bayes threshold:** $\eta = P(H_0)(C_{10}-C_{00}) / [P(H_1)(C_{01}-C_{11})]$ ŌĆö used when priors and costs are known.
- **Min $P_e$ threshold:** $\eta = P(H_0)/P(H_1)$ ŌĆö used when priors known, costs not.
- **NP threshold:** $\gamma$ is set numerically to satisfy $P_F = \alpha$ ŌĆö used when priors are unknown.
- **Log-likelihood ratio (LLR):** Use $\ell(x) = \ln \Lambda(x) \gtrless \ln \eta$ for computational stability.
- **Performance metrics:** $P_F$, $P_D = 1 - P_M$, $P_e = P(H_0)P_F + P(H_1)P_M$.
- **Gaussian scalar case:** LRT reduces to a linear threshold test on $x$ with threshold at midpoint of means (for equal priors/variances).
- **Q-function:** $P_F$ and $P_D$ are computed using $Q(\cdot) = \frac{1}{2}\mathrm{erfc}(\cdot/\sqrt{2})$. Always divide the distance by $\sigma$.
- **ROC curve:** Plots $P_D$ vs. $P_F$. Higher curve = better detector. AUC = 0.5 means random guessing.
- **Deflection coefficient:** $d^2 = (\mu_1 - \mu_0)^2 / \sigma^2$ is the effective SNR for Gaussian detection ŌĆö larger $d^2 \Rightarrow$ lower $P_e$.
- **AWGN is the canonical model:** Most KTU problems assume $w \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.
- **Sufficient statistic:** For i.i.d. Gaussian observations, the LLR is a linear combination (sum) of $x[n]$ ŌĆö equivalent to a correlator/matched filter.
- **Independent observations:** $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \prod_n \Lambda(x_n)$ ŌĆö likelihood ratios multiply for i.i.d. samples.
- **Monotonicity:** Any monotonic function of $\Lambda(x)$ yields the same decision. This is why LLR works.
- **KTU pattern:** Most problems test the 4-step protocol: (1) Form PDFs, (2) Form LRT, (3) Apply criterion to find threshold, (4) Compute $P_F$, $P_D$, $P_e$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Mathematical Detection Problem** is the formal statistical framework used to decide, from a set of noisy observations, whether a specific signal (target, event, or phenomenon) is present or absent in a measurement. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is cast as a **statistical hypothesis testing problem** in which we choose between two competing hypotheses based on observed data.

Mathematically, given an observation vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N]^T$ drawn from one of two possible probability distributions, the detection problem is to construct a **decision rule** $\delta(\mathbf{x}) \in \{H_0, H_1\}$ that maps the observation space into a decision space.

The two competing hypotheses are stated as:

$$H_0 : \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{n} \quad \text{(Signal Absent ŌĆö Null Hypothesis)}$$

$$H_1 : \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{s} + \mathbf{n} \quad \text{(Signal Present ŌĆö Alternative Hypothesis)}$$

where $\mathbf{s}$ is the deterministic or random signal of interest and $\mathbf{n}$ is the **additive noise vector**. The observations $\mathbf{x}$ are scalar or vector quantities acquired from sensors, antennas, or transducers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The detection problem is fundamentally a **decision-making under uncertainty** problem. The randomness arises ONLY from the noise ŌĆö the signal is either deterministically present (under $H_1$) or absent (under $H_0$). The decision rule partitions the observation space into two disjoint regions: the **decision region $Z_0$** (decide $H_0$) and the **decision region $Z_1$** (decide $H_1$).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **doctor in an emergency room** examining a patient who arrives complaining of chest pain. Two competing hypotheses exist:

- **$H_0$ (Null Hypothesis):** "The patient does NOT have a heart attack."  
- **$H_1$ (Alternative Hypothesis):** "The patient HAS a heart attack."

You collect noisy observations ŌĆö blood pressure readings, ECG waveforms, blood test markers, and patient-reported pain levels. Each measurement has inherent randomness (instrument noise, biological variability). Based on this noisy evidence, you must decide whether to admit the patient to the cardiac care unit ($H_1$) or send them home with antacids ($H_0$).

This is **precisely** the detection problem in engineering:

| Medical Analogy | Engineering Detection Problem |
|---|---|
| Patient symptoms | Observation vector $\mathbf{x}$ |
| Test results (ECG, BP) | Sensor measurements |
| Doctor's decision | Decision rule $\delta(\mathbf{x})$ |
| Misdiagnosis (false alarm) | Type I Error ($\alpha$) |
| Missed diagnosis | Type II Error ($\beta$) |

### 1.3 Key Terminology and Components

The mathematical detection problem is defined by four fundamental components:

1. **Observation Space ($\Gamma$):** The set of all possible observations $\mathbf{x}$.
2. **Hypothesis Space:** A set of probability models $\{P(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0), P(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1)\}$.
3. **Decision Space:** The set of allowable decisions $\{D_0, D_1\}$ corresponding to choosing $H_0$ or $H_1$.
4. **Decision Rule $\delta(\cdot)$:** A (possibly randomized) mapping from $\Gamma$ to the decision space.

The observation space is partitioned into two disjoint regions $Z_0$ and $Z_1$ such that:

$$Z_0 \cup Z_1 = \Gamma \quad \text{and} \quad Z_0 \cap Z_1 = \emptyset$$

We decide $H_0$ if $\mathbf{x} \in Z_0$ and $H_1$ if $\mathbf{x} \in Z_1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary of the Detection Problem:** Detection is concerned ONLY with the *presence or absence* of a signal. It does NOT estimate the signal's parameters (that is the *estimation* problem, covered in Module 4 of the KTU syllabus).

### 1.4 Performance Metrics

The quality of a detector is characterized by two fundamental probabilities:

$$P_{FA} = P(\text{Decide } H_1 \vert H_0) = \int_{Z_1} p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0) \, d\mathbf{x} \quad \text{(Probability of False Alarm)}$$

$$P_D = P(\text{Decide } H_1 \vert H_1) = \int_{Z_1} p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1) \, d\mathbf{x} \quad \text{(Probability of Detection)}$$

The probability of **miss** is $P_M = 1 - P_D$, and the **Type I / Type II error** nomenclature applies directly: $\alpha = P_{FA}$ and $\beta = P_M$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Construction of the Detection Problem

The detection problem is formalized through a sequence of well-defined steps:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the hypotheses.**  
Clearly state $H_0$ and $H_1$ based on the physical scenario. In radar: $H_0$ = no target, $H_1$ = target present.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Model the observations.**  
Specify the probability density functions (PDFs) $p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0)$ and $p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1)$ that describe the observations under each hypothesis.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Define the cost structure (for Bayesian approach).**  
Assign costs $C_{ij}$ for deciding $H_i$ when $H_j$ is true, and prior probabilities $P_0, P_1$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Choose a decision criterion.**  
Select from:
- **Minimum Probability of Error (MPE)** ŌĆö special case of Bayes with $C_{00} = C_{11} = 0$, $C_{10} = C_{01} = 1$.
- **Bayes Criterion** ŌĆö minimizes the average (Bayesian) cost.
- **Minimax Criterion** ŌĆö minimizes the worst-case (maximum) Bayes cost.
- **Neyman-Pearson (NP) Criterion** ŌĆö maximizes $P_D$ subject to $P_{FA} \leq \alpha$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Derive the likelihood ratio test (LRT).**  
Most criteria reduce to comparing a **likelihood ratio** $\Lambda(\mathbf{x})$ to a threshold $\eta$:

$$\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0)} \begin{matrix} \overset{H_1}{\geq} \\ \overset{H_0}{<} \end{matrix} \eta$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Set the threshold and analyze performance.**  
Choose $\eta$ to meet the desired performance, then compute $P_{FA}$, $P_D$, and $P_E$ (probability of error).

### 2.2 Why the Likelihood Ratio Appears Naturally

The conditional probability of making an error when we observe $\mathbf{x}$ depends on the ratio of the two likelihoods. Intuitively, if $p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1)$ is much larger than $p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0)$ for a given observation, then $\mathbf{x}$ is much more likely to have come from $H_1$, and we should declare $H_1$. The likelihood ratio $\Lambda(\mathbf{x})$ compresses all the information in $\mathbf{x}$ needed for this decision into a single scalar ŌĆö a sufficient statistic for detection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight (Monotone Likelihood Ratio):** For many important distributions (Gaussian, Exponential), the likelihood ratio is a *monotonic function* of a simpler statistic. This allows the LRT to be implemented as a threshold test on the simpler statistic, which is the basis of optimal receivers like the **Matched Filter**.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

| Field | Detection Application |
|---|---|
| **Radar / Sonar** | Detect aircraft, ships, submarines in noisy returns |
| **Digital Communications** | Decode bits (0 vs 1) over noisy channels |
| **Biomedical Signal Processing** | Detect ECG abnormalities, seizure onset in EEG |
| **Speech Processing** | Voice activity detection (speech vs silence) |
| **Fault Diagnosis** | Detect machinery failures from vibration data |
| **Image Processing** | Edge detection, tumor detection in MRI |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Expression |
|---|---|---|
| $H_0$ | Null hypothesis | Signal absent: $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{n}$ |
| $H_1$ | Alternative hypothesis | Signal present: $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{s} + \mathbf{n}$ |
| $\Lambda(\mathbf{x})$ | Likelihood Ratio (LR) | $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1) / p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0)$ |
| $\eta$ | Decision Threshold | Depends on chosen criterion |
| $P_{FA}$ | Probability of False Alarm | $\int_{Z_1} p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0) \, d\mathbf{x}$ |
| $P_D$ | Probability of Detection | $\int_{Z_1} p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1) \, d\mathbf{x}$ |
| $P_M$ | Probability of Miss | $1 - P_D$ |
| $P_E$ | Probability of Error | $P_0 P_{FA} + P_1 P_M$ |
| $C_{ij}$ | Cost of deciding $H_i \vert H_j$ | Defined by user (Bayes) |
| $P_0, P_1$ | Prior probabilities | $P_0 + P_1 = 1$ |
| $\eta_{B}$ | Bayes threshold | $\dfrac{P_0 (C_{10} - C_{00})}{P_1 (C_{01} - C_{11})}$ |
| $\eta_{MPE}$ | Min-Error threshold | $P_0 / P_1$ |
| ROC | Receiver Operating Curve | Plot of $P_D$ vs $P_{FA}$ |

### 2.5 Decision Criteria Summary (KTU Board-Ready Table)

| Criterion | Decision Rule | Threshold | Optimization Goal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bayes | $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \gtrless \eta_B$ | $\eta_B = \dfrac{P_0(C_{10}-C_{00})}{P_1(C_{01}-C_{11})}$ | Minimize average cost |
| Min-Probability-of-Error | $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \gtrless \eta_{MPE}$ | $\eta_{MPE} = P_0 / P_1$ | Minimize $P_E$ |
| Neyman-Pearson | $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \gtrless \eta_{NP}$ | $\eta_{NP}$ chosen to satisfy $P_{FA} = \alpha$ | Maximize $P_D$ for given $P_{FA}$ |
| Minimax | $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \gtrless \eta_{MM}$ | $\eta_{MM}$ equalizes conditional risks | Minimize worst-case cost |

### 2.6 Detailed Treatment of Each Criterion

**Bayesian Criterion:**  
The average cost (Bayes risk) is:

$$R = C_{00} P_0 P(D_0\vert H_0) + C_{10} P_0 P(D_1\vert H_0) + C_{11} P_1 P(D_1\vert H_1) + C_{01} P_1 P(D_0\vert H_1)$$

Minimizing $R$ with respect to the partition $\{Z_0, Z_1\}$ leads to the LRT with threshold $\eta_B$.

**Minimum Probability of Error (MPE) Criterion:**  
With equal misclassification costs and $P_0 + P_1 = 1$:

$$P_E = P_0 P_{FA} + P_1 P_M$$

Setting the derivative to zero gives $\eta_{MPE} = P_0/P_1$. This is the most commonly tested criterion in KTU exams.

**Neyman-Pearson (NP) Criterion:**  
No priors or costs are assumed ŌĆö instead, a constraint $P_{FA} \leq \alpha$ is imposed, and $P_D$ is maximized. The threshold $\eta_{NP}$ is determined by the constraint, not by a closed-form algebraic formula.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Likelihood Ratio Test (Bayesian Criterion)

**Goal:** Derive the optimal decision rule that minimizes the Bayes risk.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express the Bayes risk as an integral over the observation space.**

Since the decision regions $Z_0$ and $Z_1$ partition $\Gamma$:

$$R = \int_{Z_0} \big[ C_{00} P_0 \, p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0) + C_{01} P_1 \, p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1) \big] \, d\mathbf{x} \; + \; \int_{Z_1} \big[ C_{10} P_0 \, p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0) + C_{11} P_1 \, p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1) \big] \, d\mathbf{x}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Use the partition property to write $R$ as a single integral.**

Since $Z_0 = \Gamma - Z_1$:

$$R = \int_{\Gamma} \big[ C_{00} P_0 \, p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0) + C_{01} P_1 \, p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1) \big] \, d\mathbf{x} \; + \; \int_{Z_1} \Big\{ (C_{10} - C_{00}) P_0 \, p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0) - (C_{01} - C_{11}) P_1 \, p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1) \Big\} \, d\mathbf{x}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Observe that the first integral is independent of $Z_1$.**  
Only the second integral depends on the choice of $Z_1$. To minimize $R$, we want the second integral to be as negative as possible.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Identify the integrand.**  
For the second integral to be most negative, we should include in $Z_1$ all points $\mathbf{x}$ where the integrand is **negative**, i.e.:

$$(C_{10} - C_{00}) P_0 \, p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0) - (C_{01} - C_{11}) P_1 \, p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1) < 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Rearrange to obtain the likelihood ratio test.**

$$\frac{p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0)} > \frac{P_0 (C_{10} - C_{00})}{P_1 (C_{01} - C_{11})}$$

This is the famous **LRT**:

$$\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0)} \begin{matrix} \overset{H_1}{\geq} \\ \overset{H_0}{<} \end{matrix} \eta_B = \frac{P_0 (C_{10} - C_{00})}{P_1 (C_{01} - C_{11})}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Minimum Probability of Error Detector

**Step 1 ŌĆö Set equal costs in the Bayes framework.**  
Let $C_{00} = C_{11} = 0$ (correct decisions have zero cost) and $C_{10} = C_{01} = 1$ (both errors have unit cost). Then:

$$\eta_{MPE} = \frac{P_0 \cdot (1 - 0)}{P_1 \cdot (1 - 0)} = \frac{P_0}{P_1}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Recognize that minimizing Bayes risk now equals minimizing probability of error.**  
Under these costs, the Bayes risk $R$ becomes the probability of error:

$$P_E = P_0 P_{FA} + P_1 P_M = P(H_1 \text{ decided}\vert H_0) P(H_0) + P(H_0 \text{ decided}\vert H_1) P(H_1)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö The optimal decision rule** is therefore:

$$\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0)} \begin{matrix} \overset{H_1}{\geq} \\ \overset{H_0}{<} \end{matrix} \frac{P_0}{P_1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** When priors are equal ($P_0 = P_1 = 0.5$), the threshold reduces to **1**, and the test becomes a **Maximum Likelihood (ML) test**: decide $H_1$ if $p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1) > p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0)$.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Gaussian Observations with Known Mean

**Problem Setup:**  
Under $H_0$: $x \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ (noise only).  
Under $H_1$: $x \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $\mu > 0$ (signal + noise).

Assume equal priors. Derive the optimal decision rule and $P_E$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the two likelihoods.**

$$p(x\vert H_0) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

$$p(x\vert H_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Form the likelihood ratio.**

$$\Lambda(x) = \frac{p(x\vert H_1)}{p(x\vert H_0)} = \exp\!\left(\frac{x^2 - (x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{2\mu x - \mu^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the LRT (threshold = 1 for equal priors).**

$$\exp\!\left(\frac{2\mu x - \mu^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \begin{matrix} \overset{H_1}{\geq} \\ \overset{H_0}{<} \end{matrix} 1$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Take natural logarithm (monotonic transformation).**

$$\frac{2\mu x - \mu^2}{2\sigma^2} \begin{matrix} \overset{H_1}{\geq} \\ \overset{H_0}{<} \end{matrix} 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2\mu x - \mu^2 \begin{matrix} \overset{H_1}{\geq} \\ \overset{H_0}{<} \end{matrix} 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for $x$.**  
Since $\mu > 0$:

$$x \begin{matrix} \overset{H_1}{\geq} \\ \overset{H_0}{<} \end{matrix} \frac{\mu}{2}$$

The optimal decision rule is a **single-threshold test**: decide $H_1$ if $x > \mu/2$.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute the probability of error.**

$$P_{FA} = P\!\left(x > \frac{\mu}{2} \,\Big\vert\, H_0\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{\mu/2}{\sigma}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{\mu}{2\sigma}\right)$$

$$P_M = P\!\left(x \leq \frac{\mu}{2} \,\Big\vert\, H_1\right) = 1 - Q\!\left(\frac{\mu/2 - \mu}{\sigma}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{\mu}{2\sigma}\right)$$

where $Q(\cdot)$ is the Gaussian Q-function: $Q(u) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_u^{\infty} \exp(-t^2/2)\, dt$.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Total probability of error (equal priors):**

$$P_E = 0.5 \cdot P_{FA} + 0.5 \cdot P_M = Q\!\left(\frac{\mu}{2\sigma}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** $P_E$ depends ONLY on the **signal-to-noise ratio** $\mu/\sigma$. Increasing $\mu$ or decreasing $\sigma$ improves detection performance ŌĆö a key engineering insight.

### 3.4 Neyman-Pearson Lemma

**Statement (NP Lemma):**  
For a binary hypothesis test with $p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0)$ and $p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1)$ known, the LRT with threshold $\eta_{NP}$ chosen to satisfy $P_{FA} = \alpha$ is the **most powerful test** ŌĆö no other test with $P_{FA} \leq \alpha$ can have a higher $P_D$.

**Significance for KTU:** The NP lemma guarantees the existence of an optimal detector without requiring prior probabilities or cost assignments. It is the cornerstone of classical detection theory.

### 3.5 Derivation of ROC for the Gaussian Case

Using the same setup as Section 3.3 with threshold $\gamma$:

$$P_{FA}(\gamma) = Q\!\left(\frac{\gamma}{\sigma}\right)$$

$$P_D(\gamma) = Q\!\left(\frac{\gamma - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$$

Eliminate $\gamma$ using $Q^{-1}(P_{FA}) = \gamma/\sigma$:

$$P_D = Q\!\left(Q^{-1}(P_{FA}) - \frac{\mu}{\sigma}\right)$$

This is the **parametric ROC equation**. As $\mu/\sigma \to \infty$, $P_D \to 1$ for any fixed $P_{FA}$. As $\mu/\sigma \to 0$, $P_D = P_{FA}$ (random guessing line).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Detection System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA["SOURCE OR PHENOMENON"] --> chA["CHANNEL WITH NOISE"]
    chA --> obsA["OBSERVATION X"]
    obsA --> procA["PROCESSING AND LIKELIHOOD COMPUTATION"]
    procA --> lrtA["LIKELIHOOD RATIO TEST"]
    lrtA --> thrA{"LAMBDA X COMPARED TO THRESHOLD ETA"}
    thrA -->|"LAMBDA GREATER OR EQUAL ETA"| decA["DECIDE H1 SIGNAL PRESENT"]
    thrA -->|"LAMBDA LESS THAN ETA"| decB["DECIDE H0 SIGNAL ABSENT"]
    decA --> actA["ACTION OR RESPONSE"]
    decB --> actB["ACTION OR RESPONSE"]
```

### 4.2 Decision Partition of the Observation Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    space["OBSERVATION SPACE GAMMA"] --> z0["DECISION REGION Z0 DECIDE H0"]
    space --> z1["DECISION REGION Z1 DECIDE H1"]
    z0 -.->|"BOUNDARY DEFINED BY LAMBDA X EQUALS ETA"| z1
    z0 --- obs0["POINT X IN Z0 LOW LR FAVORS H0"]
    z1 --- obs1["POINT X IN Z1 HIGH LR FAVORS H1"]
```

### 4.3 Detection Criteria Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["START DETECTION PROBLEM"] --> q1{"ARE PRIOR PROBABILITIES KNOWN"}
    q1 -->|"YES"| q2{"ARE COSTS ASSIGNED"}
    q1 -->|"NO"| q3["USE NEYMAN PEARSON CRITERION"]
    q2 -->|"YES"| bayes["USE BAYES CRITERION THRESHOLD ETA B"]
    q2 -->|"NO EQUAL PRIORS"| mpe["USE MIN PROBABILITY OF ERROR ETA EQUALS P0 DIV P1"]
    bayes --> lrt["COMPUTE LAMBDA X AND COMPARE TO THRESHOLD"]
    mpe --> lrt
    q3 --> lrt
    lrt --> out["FINAL DECISION H0 OR H1"]
```

### 4.4 Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fa["P FA ON X AXIS 0 TO 1"] --> roc["ROC CURVE P D VS P FA"]
    roc --> pts["KEY POINTS"]
    pts --> p1["ORIGIN 0 0 NO DETECTION"]
    pts --> p2["DIAGONAL P D EQUAL P FA RANDOM GUESSING"]
    pts --> p3["IDEAL POINT 0 1 ZERO FALSE ALARM PERFECT DETECTION"]
    pts --> p4["TOP RIGHT 1 1 ALWAYS DECIDE H1"]
```
