## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]**  
*State and explain the mathematical formulation of the binary hypothesis testing problem. Define the probability of false alarm and probability of detection.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
The binary detection problem is formulated as choosing between two hypotheses:

$$H_0: \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{n}, \qquad H_1: \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{s} + \mathbf{n}$$

A decision rule $\delta(\mathbf{x})$ partitions the observation space $\Gamma$ into two regions $Z_0$ and $Z_1$.

- **Probability of false alarm:** $P_{FA} = \int_{Z_1} p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0) \, d\mathbf{x}$ ŌĆö declaring signal when only noise is present.  
- **Probability of detection:** $P_D = \int_{Z_1} p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1) \, d\mathbf{x}$ ŌĆö correctly declaring signal when present. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]**  
*Define the likelihood ratio test (LRT). What is its significance in detection theory?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
The LRT compares the likelihood ratio $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1)/p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0)$ to a threshold $\eta$:

$$\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \begin{matrix} \overset{H_1}{\geq} \\ \overset{H_0}{<} \end{matrix} \eta$$

**Significance:** By the Neyman-Pearson lemma and Bayes criterion, the LRT is the **optimal** test ŌĆö it maximizes $P_D$ for a given $P_{FA}$ (NP) or minimizes Bayes risk. It is a sufficient statistic that compresses observation data into one scalar. (3 marks)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the likelihood ratio test for the binary hypothesis testing problem using the Bayesian criterion. Clearly state the resulting threshold.

**(b) [7 Marks)** For the hypothesis test with $x \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ under $H_0$ and $x \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ under $H_1$ with $\mu > 0$ and equal priors, derive the optimal decision rule and compute the probability of error.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Bayesian derivation (7 marks):**  
[Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation.]  
The Bayes risk is:

$$R = C_{00} P_0 P(D_0\vert H_0) + C_{10} P_0 P_{FA} + C_{11} P_1 P_D + C_{01} P_1 P_M$$

Rewriting $R$ as an integral and minimizing with respect to $Z_1$ gives:

$$\frac{p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0)} \begin{matrix} \overset{H_1}{\geq} \\ \overset{H_0}{<} \end{matrix} \eta_B = \frac{P_0 (C_{10} - C_{00})}{P_1 (C_{01} - C_{11})}$$

This is the **LRT with Bayes threshold**. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Gaussian case derivation (7 marks):**

Likelihoods:

$$p(x\vert H_0) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^2}} e^{-x^2 / (2\sigma^2)}, \quad p(x\vert H_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^2}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2 / (2\sigma^2)}$$

Likelihood ratio:

$$\Lambda(x) = \exp\!\left(\frac{2\mu x - \mu^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

With $\eta = 1$ (equal priors) and taking $\ln$:

$$x \begin{matrix} \overset{H_1}{\geq} \\ \overset{H_0}{<} \end{matrix} \frac{\mu}{2}$$

Probability of error:

$$P_E = Q\!\left(\frac{\mu}{2\sigma}\right)$$

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024 ŌĆö Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Neyman-Pearson criterion. State and prove the Neyman-Pearson lemma for binary hypothesis testing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the ROC of the detector in the Gaussian case $x \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_i, \sigma^2)$ under $H_i$, $i = 0, 1$ with $\mu_1 > \mu_0$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Neyman-Pearson criterion and lemma (7 marks):**  
The Neyman-Pearson criterion maximizes the probability of detection $P_D$ subject to the constraint that the probability of false alarm $P_{FA} \leq \alpha$. No priors or costs are required.  
**Lemma:** For any test satisfying $P_{FA} \leq \alpha$, the LRT with threshold $\eta$ chosen such that $P_{FA} = \alpha$ achieves the **maximum possible $P_D$**. This is the most powerful test at level $\alpha$.  
**Proof sketch:** Consider any other test with the same $P_{FA}$. Define the Lagrangian $J = P_D + \lambda(\alpha - P_{FA})$. The LRT maximizes $J$ pointwise in the observation space; hence no other test can achieve higher $P_D$. **(7 marks)**

**(b) ROC derivation (7 marks):**  
For threshold $\gamma$:

$$P_{FA} = Q\!\left(\frac{\gamma - \mu_0}{\sigma}\right), \quad P_D = Q\!\left(\frac{\gamma - \mu_1}{\sigma}\right)$$

Let $u = Q^{-1}(P_{FA}) = (\gamma - \mu_0)/\sigma$. Then $\gamma = \sigma u + \mu_0$ and:

$$P_D = Q\!\left(\frac{\sigma u + \mu_0 - \mu_1}{\sigma}\right) = Q\!\left(u - \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma}\right)$$

Substituting $u = Q^{-1}(P_{FA})$:

$$P_D = Q\!\left(Q^{-1}(P_{FA}) - d\right), \quad \text{where } d = \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma}$$

This is the **ROC equation**; $d$ is the **deflection coefficient** (a measure of SNR). **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $P_D$ and $P_{FA}$:** Many students mix up the conditioning hypothesis. Remember: $P_{FA}$ conditions on $H_0$ (no signal); $P_D$ conditions on $H_1$ (signal present). Marks are routinely docked for this slip.
> 2. **Forgetting to take the logarithm of the LRT:** When likelihoods are exponential (Gaussian), the LRT simplifies only AFTER taking $\ln$. Showing the unsimplified exponential form loses 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 3. **Bayes threshold sign errors:** The threshold is $P_0(C_{10}-C_{00}) / [P_1(C_{01}-C_{11})]$. Getting the numerator/denominator swapped is a frequent error.
> 4. **Threshold inversion:** Make sure you write $\Lambda \geq \eta \Rightarrow H_1$ and $\Lambda < \eta \Rightarrow H_0$. Reversing this flips the entire decision logic.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Detection = Hypothesis Testing:** The detection problem is fundamentally a binary hypothesis test between signal-present ($H_1$) and signal-absent ($H_0$).
- **Four components:** Observation space, hypothesis space, decision space, decision rule.
- **Performance metrics:** $P_{FA}$, $P_D$, $P_M = 1 - P_D$, and $P_E = P_0 P_{FA} + P_1 P_M$.
- **LRT is universal:** All major criteria (Bayes, MPE, NP) reduce to comparing $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1)/p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0)$ to a threshold $\eta$.
- **Thresholds:**
  - Bayes: $\eta_B = P_0(C_{10}-C_{00}) / [P_1(C_{01}-C_{11})]$
  - MPE: $\eta_{MPE} = P_0 / P_1$
  - NP: $\eta_{NP}$ set so that $P_{FA} = \alpha$
- **NP Lemma:** LRT is the most powerful test at any given $P_{FA}$ level ŌĆö a foundational result.
- **Gaussian case gives a simple threshold test:** Decide $H_1$ if $x > \mu/2$ (equal priors), with $P_E = Q(\mu/(2\sigma))$.
- **ROC plot:** $P_D$ vs $P_{FA}$ characterizes detector performance; the curve moves toward the ideal point $(0, 1)$ as SNR increases.
- **Deflection coefficient:** $d = (\mu_1 - \mu_0)/\sigma$ is a key SNR measure appearing in ROC equations.
- **Sufficient statistic principle:** The likelihood ratio compresses $\mathbf{x}$ into one scalar ŌĆö the optimal detector depends only on $\Lambda(\mathbf{x})$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hypothesis Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hypothesis Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the rigorous mathematical framework of **Statistical Detection Theory**, **Hypothesis Testing** is defined as a formal statistical decision-making procedure used to determine which one of two or more competing probabilistic models (called *hypotheses*) best explains a set of observed data, when the underlying true state of nature is unknown and contaminated by randomness (typically noise).

For a binary detection problem, the observation vector under additive noise is conventionally written as:

$$
H_0 : \quad \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{n}
$$
$$
H_1 : \quad \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{s} + \mathbf{n}
$$

where $\mathbf{x}$ is the received/observed signal, $\mathbf{s}$ is the known or partially known transmitted signal (the *deterministic* or *random* signal of interest), and $\mathbf{n}$ is the noise vector (commonly modeled as Additive White Gaussian Noise, **AWGN**).

- $H_0$ is the **Null Hypothesis** (signal absent ŌĆö only noise present).
- $H_1$ is the **Alternative Hypothesis** (signal present ŌĆö signal plus noise).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Hypothesis Testing in Module 3 of *Estimation and Detection* specifically focuses on binary detection (M-ary hypothesis testing), decision rules, error probabilities, the likelihood ratio test, the Bayes criterion, the Neyman-Pearson (NP) theorem, and Receiver Operating Characteristics (ROC). These form the analytical backbone of digital communication receivers, radar systems, sonar, and biomedical signal detection.

## 1.2 The Four Possible Decision Outcomes

When the receiver decides between $H_0$ and $H_1$, exactly one of the following four mutually exclusive outcomes occurs:

| True State | Decide $H_0$ | Decide $H_1$ |
|------------|--------------|--------------|
| $H_0$ true (no signal) | **Correct Rejection** | **False Alarm** (Type I Error) |
| $H_1$ true (signal) | **Miss** (Type II Error) | **Detection** (Hit) |

- **Probability of False Alarm** $\Rightarrow P_{FA} = P(H_1 \mid H_0)$ ŌĆö denoted $\alpha$.
- **Probability of Detection** $\Rightarrow P_D = P(H_1 \mid H_1)$.
- **Probability of Miss** $\Rightarrow P_M = P(H_0 \mid H_1) = 1 - P_D$ ŌĆö denoted $\beta$.
- **Probability of Correct Rejection** $\Rightarrow P_{CR} = P(H_0 \mid H_0) = 1 - P_{FA}$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Courtroom Trial:**
> Imagine you are a judge presiding over a criminal trial.
> - **$H_0$** = "The defendant is innocent" (no crime committed ŌĆö only "background noise" of life).
> - **$H_1$** = "The defendant is guilty" (a crime was committed ŌĆö "signal" present).
> - **Evidence** $\mathbf{x}$ = the testimonies, fingerprints, and CCTV footage presented in court.
> - **The Judge** = the *decision rule* that maps evidence to a verdict.
> - **Type I Error (False Alarm)** = convicting an innocent person. Society values this error highly ŌĆö it is why the standard of proof is "beyond reasonable doubt."
> - **Type II Error (Miss)** = acquitting a guilty person.
> - **ROC Curve** = the judge's characteristic trade-off between being too strict (almost never convicting ŌĆö low $P_{FA}$, low $P_D$) versus too lenient (almost always convicting ŌĆö high $P_{FA}$, high $P_D$).

Just as the judge sets a threshold of evidence, the detector sets a **decision threshold** $\gamma$ on the **likelihood ratio** to choose between hypotheses.

## 1.4 Why Hypothesis Testing is Central to Detection Engineering

Hypothesis testing is not merely an abstract statistical exercise ŌĆö it is the operational engine behind:

- **Radar target detection** (signal $\mathbf{s}$ vs noise $\mathbf{n}$).
- **Digital communication demodulation** (bit "0" vs bit "1" in AWGN channel).
- **Spectrum sensing in cognitive radio** (primary user present vs absent).
- **Biomedical abnormality detection** (tumor present vs absent in an MRI scan).
- **Fault detection in control systems** (machine healthy vs faulty).

The receiver designer's task reduces to: *given an observation $\mathbf{x}$, decide which hypothesis generated it* ŌĆö and this is exactly the hypothesis testing problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Constants / Metrics to Remember (Bolded):**
> - **Threshold $\gamma$** ŌĆö decision boundary parameter.
> - **Likelihood Ratio $\Lambda(\mathbf{x})$** ŌĆö fundamental test statistic.
> - **$P_{FA} \in [0, 1]$** and **$P_D \in [0, 1]$** ŌĆö primary detector performance metrics.
> - **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** ŌĆö in linear scale, and **$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{SNR})$**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Decision Rule ŌĆö Formal Structure

A binary hypothesis test is defined by a **decision rule** $\delta(\mathbf{x})$ that partitions the observation space $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^N$ into two disjoint regions:

- **Decision Region $\mathcal{R}_1$** ŌĆö if $\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{R}_1$, decide $H_1$.
- **Decision Region $\mathcal{R}_0$** ŌĆö if $\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{R}_0 = \mathcal{X} \setminus \mathcal{R}_1$, decide $H_0$.

The design problem: **choose the regions $\mathcal{R}_0$ and $\mathcal{R}_1$ optimally**, where "optimal" depends on the chosen criterion.

## 2.2 Decision Criteria ŌĆö Taxonomy of Tests

### A) Bayes Criterion (Minimum Risk)

The Bayes criterion minimizes the **average (Bayes) risk** $R$, which is the expected cost of making a decision. Define the costs:

- $C_{00}$ ŌĆö cost of deciding $H_0$ when $H_0$ is true.
- $C_{10}$ ŌĆö cost of deciding $H_1$ when $H_0$ is true (cost of false alarm).
- $C_{11}$ ŌĆö cost of deciding $H_1$ when $H_1$ is true.
- $C_{01}$ ŌĆö cost of deciding $H_0$ when $H_1$ is true (cost of miss).

The average risk is:

$$
R = C_{00} P(H_0) P(H_0 \mid H_0) + C_{10} P(H_0) P(H_1 \mid H_0) + C_{11} P(H_1) P(H_1 \mid H_1) + C_{01} P(H_1) P(H_0 \mid H_1)
$$

The **minimum Bayes risk decision rule** decides $H_1$ if:

$$
\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)} \;\;\gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1}\;\; \gamma_{\text{Bayes}}
$$

where the Bayes threshold is:

$$
\gamma_{\text{Bayes}} = \frac{P(H_0)(C_{10} - C_{00})}{P(H_1)(C_{01} - C_{11})}
$$

### B) Minimum Probability of Error Criterion (MPE / Min-P(e))

A special case of Bayes criterion obtained by setting $C_{00} = C_{11} = 0$ (no cost for correct decisions) and $C_{10} = C_{01} = 1$ (unit cost for errors). Then the threshold becomes:

$$
\gamma_{\text{MPE}} = \frac{P(H_0)}{P(H_1)}
$$

When **priors are equal** ($P(H_0) = P(H_1) = 0.5$):

$$
\gamma_{\text{MPE}} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{decide } H_1 \text{ if } p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1) > p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)
$$

This is the **Maximum A posteriori Probability (MAP)** rule.

### C) Maximum Likelihood (ML) Decision Rule

When priors are unknown or assumed equal, decide $H_1$ if:

$$
p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1) > p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)
$$

This is the ML rule. It is a likelihood ratio test with threshold $\gamma = 1$.

### D) Neyman-Pearson (NP) Theorem

When **priors are unknown** and assigning costs is infeasible (common in radar), the NP criterion fixes $P_{FA} = \alpha$ at a prescribed value and **maximizes $P_D$**.

The NP decision rule is again an LRT:

$$
\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \;\;\gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1}\;\; \gamma_{\text{NP}}
$$

but the threshold $\gamma_{\text{NP}}$ is chosen to satisfy the constraint $P_{FA} = \alpha$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Neyman-Pearson Lemma:** Among all tests with $P_{FA} \leq \alpha$, the likelihood ratio test $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \gtrless \gamma$ minimizes $P_M$ (equivalently, maximizes $P_D$). No other test can do better.

## 2.3 The Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT) ŌĆö Universal Framework

**Every** binary hypothesis test in the four categories above is a likelihood ratio test of the form:

$$
\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)} \;\;\gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1}\;\; \gamma
$$

The **only thing that changes** between criteria is the **threshold** $\gamma$.

### Equivalent Monotonic Transformations

It is often easier to work with the **log-likelihood ratio**:

$$
\ell(\mathbf{x}) = \ln \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1) - \ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)
$$

Since $\ln(\cdot)$ is monotonically increasing, the test becomes:

$$
\ell(\mathbf{x}) \;\;\gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1}\;\; \ln \gamma
$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Utility |
|---|-------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Likelihood Ratio | $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_1) / p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_0)$ | Sufficient statistic for detection |
| 2 | Log-Likelihood Ratio | $\ell(\mathbf{x}) = \ln \Lambda(\mathbf{x})$ | Simplifies AWGN detection (sum instead of product) |
| 3 | Bayes Threshold | $\gamma_B = [P(H_0)(C_{10}-C_{00})] / [P(H_1)(C_{01}-C_{11})]$ | Risk-minimizing decision boundary |
| 4 | MPE Threshold | $\gamma_{MPE} = P(H_0) / P(H_1)$ | Minimum $P_e$ (communication receivers) |
| 5 | ML Rule | $\gamma_{ML} = 1$ | When priors are equal/unknown |
| 6 | Probability of Error | $P_e = P(H_1) P_M + P(H_0) P_{FA}$ | Aggregate detector performance |
| 7 | $P_{FA}$ (Gaussian example) | $P_{FA} = Q\!\left(\dfrac{\gamma - \mu_0}{\sigma_0}\right)$ | Threshold design under AWGN |
| 8 | $P_D$ (Gaussian example) | $P_D = Q\!\left(\dfrac{\gamma - \mu_1}{\sigma_1}\right)$ | Detection probability computation |
| 9 | Deflection Coefficient | $d^2 = (\mu_1 - \mu_0)^2 / \sigma^2$ | SNR-equivalent measure |
| 10 | Total Probability | $P_D + P_M = 1$ | Conservation law |
| 11 | ROC Identity | $P_D = f(P_{FA})$ parametric in $\gamma$ | Detector benchmarking |

Where $Q(\cdot)$ is the Gaussian tail-probability function:

$$
Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_x^{\infty} \exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2}\right) du
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The deflection coefficient $d^2$ is the canonical SNR-equivalent figure of merit for a detector. A larger $d^2$ produces an ROC curve that bows more sharply toward the upper-left corner (ideal detector: $P_D = 1$, $P_{FA} = 0$).

## 2.5 Composite vs. Simple Hypotheses

- **Simple hypothesis** ŌĆö all parameters fully specified (e.g., $H_0: \mu = 0$, $H_1: \mu = 1$ with known $\sigma^2$).
- **Composite hypothesis** ŌĆö at least one parameter unspecified (e.g., $H_0: \mu = 0$, $H_1: \mu > 0$ ŌĆö *one-sided composite*).

For composite hypotheses, the **Generalized Likelihood Ratio Test (GLRT)** is used:

$$
\Lambda_G(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\max_{\theta_1} p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta_1, H_1)}{\max_{\theta_0} p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta_0, H_0)} \;\;\gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1}\;\; \gamma
$$

where $\theta_0, \theta_1$ are the unknown parameters under each hypothesis. The ML estimates plug into the likelihood.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Bayes Minimum-Risk Decision Rule

**Starting Point:** The expected risk over the joint distribution of hypothesis and observation is to be minimized.

**Step 1.** Write the conditional risk of deciding $H_1$ given observation $\mathbf{x}$:

$$
R(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x}) = C_{10} \, p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0) \, P(H_0) / p(\mathbf{x}) + C_{11} \, p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1) \, P(H_1) / p(\mathbf{x})
$$

Wait ŌĆö using Bayes' rule $P(H_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_i) P(H_i) / p(\mathbf{x})$, the conditional risk is correctly:

$$
R(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x}) = C_{10} P(H_0 \mid \mathbf{x}) + C_{11} P(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x})
$$

$$
R(H_0 \mid \mathbf{x}) = C_{00} P(H_0 \mid \mathbf{x}) + C_{01} P(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 2.** The optimal decision at each $\mathbf{x}$ minimizes the conditional risk. Choose $H_1$ if $R(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x}) < R(H_0 \mid \mathbf{x})$:

$$
C_{10} P(H_0 \mid \mathbf{x}) + C_{11} P(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x}) < C_{00} P(H_0 \mid \mathbf{x}) + C_{01} P(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 3.** Rearrange by collecting terms with $P(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x})$ on the left and $P(H_0 \mid \mathbf{x})$ on the right:

$$
(C_{01} - C_{11}) P(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x}) > (C_{10} - C_{00}) P(H_0 \mid \mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 4.** Substitute posterior probabilities using Bayes' rule and divide:

$$
\frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1) P(H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0) P(H_0)} > \frac{C_{10} - C_{00}}{C_{01} - C_{11}}
$$

**Step 5.** Identify the left-hand side as the likelihood ratio times prior ratio, and the right-hand side as the threshold:

$$
\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)} \;\;\gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1}\;\; \gamma_{\text{Bayes}}
$$

with

$$
\gamma_{\text{Bayes}} = \frac{P(H_0)(C_{10} - C_{00})}{P(H_1)(C_{01} - C_{11})}
$$

This derivation is **board-exam standard** ŌĆö KTU frequently asks for the Bayes rule derivation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Minimum Probability of Error (MPE) as a Bayes Special Case

**Starting Point:** Set $C_{00} = C_{11} = 0$ (correct decisions incur no cost) and $C_{10} = C_{01} = 1$ (errors cost unit penalty).

**Step 1.** Substituting into the Bayes threshold:

$$
\gamma_{\text{Bayes}} = \frac{P(H_0)(1 - 0)}{P(H_1)(1 - 0)} = \frac{P(H_0)}{P(H_1)}
$$

**Step 2.** The resulting test is:

$$
\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \;\;\gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1}\;\; \frac{P(H_0)}{P(H_1)}
$$

**Step 3.** Multiplying both sides by $p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0) P(H_1)$:

$$
p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1) P(H_1) \;\;\gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1}\;\; p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0) P(H_0)
$$

**Step 4.** Recognizing that the left and right are the joint densities $p(\mathbf{x}, H_1)$ and $p(\mathbf{x}, H_0)$:

$$
P(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x}) \;\;\gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1}\;\; P(H_0 \mid \mathbf{x})
$$

This is the **Maximum A posteriori Probability (MAP)** rule.

**Step 5.** If $P(H_0) = P(H_1) = 0.5$, the threshold becomes 1, and MAP reduces to ML:

$$
p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1) \;\;\gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1}\;\; p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Detection of a Known DC Signal in AWGN

**Problem:** Under $H_0$, $x = n$ where $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$. Under $H_1$, $x = A + n$ where $A > 0$ is a known DC level and $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$. Derive the likelihood ratio test, the threshold for $P_{FA} = \alpha$, and the resulting $P_D$.

**Step 1.** Write the two conditional densities (scalar, $N=1$ observation):

$$
p(x \mid H_0) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

$$
p(x \mid H_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x - A)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 2.** Form the likelihood ratio:

$$
\Lambda(x) = \frac{p(x \mid H_1)}{p(x \mid H_0)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-A)^2 - x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 3.** Expand the squared terms:

$$
(x - A)^2 - x^2 = x^2 - 2Ax + A^2 - x^2 = -2Ax + A^2
$$

**Step 4.** Substitute back:

$$
\Lambda(x) = \exp\!\left(\frac{2Ax - A^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{Ax}{\sigma^2} - \frac{A^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 5.** Take the natural log (monotonic) to obtain the log-likelihood ratio:

$$
\ell(x) = \ln \Lambda(x) = \frac{Ax}{\sigma^2} - \frac{A^2}{2\sigma^2}
$$

**Step 6.** The LRT decides $H_1$ if $\Lambda(x) > \gamma$, equivalently $\ell(x) > \ln \gamma$. The first term $Ax/\sigma^2$ is monotonically increasing in $x$ (since $A > 0$), so the test reduces to comparing $x$ against a threshold:

$$
x \;\;\gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1}\;\; \eta
$$

where

$$
\eta = \frac{\sigma^2}{A} \ln \gamma + \frac{A}{2}
$$

**Step 7.** Compute $P_{FA}$ ŌĆö under $H_0$, $x \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$:

$$
P_{FA} = P(x > \eta \mid H_0) = Q\!\left(\frac{\eta - 0}{\sigma}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{\eta}{\sigma}\right)
$$

**Step 8.** Invert to find $\eta$ for a prescribed $P_{FA} = \alpha$:

$$
\eta = \sigma \, Q^{-1}(\alpha)
$$

**Step 9.** Compute $P_D$ ŌĆö under $H_1$, $x \sim \mathcal{N}(A, \sigma^2)$:

$$
P_D = P(x > \eta \mid H_1) = Q\!\left(\frac{\eta - A}{\sigma}\right) = Q\!\left(Q^{-1}(\alpha) - \frac{A}{\sigma}\right)
$$

**Step 10.** Recognize the **deflection coefficient**: $d = A/\sigma$. Thus:

$$
P_D = Q\!\left(Q^{-1}(\alpha) - d\right)
$$

This is the canonical **ROC relationship** for the DC-in-AWGN detector and is the most tested result in KTU Module 3 derivations.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Vector Observation (N samples) Extension

For $N$ i.i.d. Gaussian samples $x_1, \ldots, x_N$:

**Step 1.** Joint density under $H_0$:

$$
p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x_i^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 2.** Joint density under $H_1$:

$$
p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i - A)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 3.** Log-likelihood ratio simplifies to a sum:

$$
\ell(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[\frac{2 A x_i - A^2}{2\sigma^2}\right] = \frac{A}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i - \frac{N A^2}{2\sigma^2}
$$

**Step 4.** The sufficient statistic is the **sample mean** $\bar{x} = (1/N)\sum x_i$. Decision rule:

$$
\bar{x} \;\;\gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1}\;\; \eta_N
$$

**Step 5.** Under $H_0$, $\bar{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2/N)$, so:

$$
P_{FA} = Q\!\left(\frac{\eta_N}{\sigma/\sqrt{N}}\right) = Q\!\left(\eta_N \frac{\sqrt{N}}{\sigma}\right)
$$

**Step 6.** Under $H_1$, $\bar{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(A, \sigma^2/N)$:

$$
P_D = Q\!\left(Q^{-1}(\alpha) - \frac{A \sqrt{N}}{\sigma}\right)
$$

The effective deflection grows as $\sqrt{N}$ ŌĆö integrating more samples improves detection exponentially.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Criterion | Threshold $\gamma$ | When Used | Maximizes/Minimizes |
|-----------|---------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| Bayes | $\dfrac{P(H_0)(C_{10}-C_{00})}{P(H_1)(C_{01}-C_{11})}$ | Costs & priors known | Average risk |
| MPE / MAP | $P(H_0)/P(H_1)$ | Costs equal, priors known | $P_e$ |
| ML | $1$ | Equal priors / unknown | $P_D - P_{FA}$ asymmetric |
| Neyman-Pearson | Tuned to $P_{FA}=\alpha$ | Priors/costs unknown | $P_D$ at fixed $P_{FA}$ |
| Min-Max | Worst-case over priors | Priors uncertain | Worst-case $P_e$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Binary Detection System

The complete signal-flow architecture of a hypothesis-testing receiver is shown below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1["Source H0 Signal Absent"]:::hyp --> channel["Channel plus Noise"]:::ch
    src2["Source H1 Signal Present"]:::hyp --> channel
    channel --> rx["Received Observation x"]:::obs
    rx --> lrt["Likelihood Ratio Computer Lambda of x"]:::proc
    prior["Prior Threshold Gamma"]:::param --> lrt
    lrt --> dec{"Decision: Lambda greater than Gamma"}:::dec
    dec -- Yes --> out1["Output: Decide H1 Detection"]:::outh1
    dec -- No --> out0["Output: Decide H0 No Signal"]:::outh0

    classDef hyp fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#1A1A1A,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ch fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#1A1A1A,stroke-width:2px
    classDef obs fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#8E24AA,color:#1A1A1A,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00695C,color:#1A1A1A,stroke-width:2px
    classDef param fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825,color:#1A1A1A,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dec fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outh1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1A1A1A,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outh0 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,color:#1A1A1A,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation of each block:**

- **Source H0 / Source H1**: The unknown true state of nature ŌĆö the receiver never knows which generated $\mathbf{x}$.
- **Channel + Noise**: Physical propagation medium corrupting the signal with AWGN.
- **Received Observation $\mathbf{x}$**: The evidence the detector must base its decision on.
- **Likelihood Ratio Computer**: Computes $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_1)/p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_0)$, often reduced to a sufficient statistic (e.g., sample mean).
- **Prior Threshold $\gamma$**: Set by Bayes / MAP / ML / NP criterion.
- **Decision Diamond**: Compares $\Lambda$ against $\gamma$.

## 4.2 ROC Curve ŌĆö Receiver Operating Characteristic

The ROC curve plots $P_D$ versus $P_{FA}$ as the threshold $\gamma$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ROC["ROC CURVE GEOMETRY IN UNIT SQUARE"]
        dir["Origin 0,0: Trivial No Detection"]
        ptA["Point A 0,1: Ideal Detector"]
        ptB["Point B 1,1: Always Decide H1"]
        ptC["Point C 1,0: Worst"]
        diag["Diagonal P_D equals P_FA: Random Guess"]
        curve["ROC Curve Bows Toward Upper Left: Better Detectors"]
        dir --- ptC
        ptA --- ptB
        diag -.-> curve
    end

    note["As SNR or N increases, the curve bows closer to point A 0,1"]
    ROC --- note

    classDef ideal fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:3px,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef trivial fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef curvecls fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:3px,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef notecls fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#1A1A1A,stroke-width:2px

    class ptA ideal
    class dir,ptB,ptC trivial
    class curve,diag curvecls
    class note notecls
```

**Key points on the ROC:**

- $(0,0)$ ŌĆö never decides $H_1$ (no false alarms but no detections).
- $(1,1)$ ŌĆö always decides $H_1$ (every detection but every non-signal also flagged).
- $(0,1)$ ŌĆö *ideal detector* (zero false alarms, all detections).
- **Diagonal** ŌĆö random guessing.
- **Better detectors** produce curves that bow **closer to the upper-left corner**.

## 4.3 Decision Partition Diagram ŌĆö Observation Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    space["Observation Space X Subset R^N"]:::space
    space --> r0["Region R0: Decide H0"]:::r0
    space --> r1["Region R1: Decide H1"]:::r1
    r0 ---|"Boundary"| r1

    p0["Density p of x given H0 centered at zero"]:::p0
    p1["Density p of x given H1 centered at A"]:::p1
    overlap["Overlap Area Determines Error Probabilities"]:::ovl
    p0 --- overlap
    p1 --- overlap

    classDef space fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:2px,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef r0 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef r1 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef p0 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef p1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef ovl fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:3px,color:#1A1A1A

    class space space
    class r0 r0
    class r1 r1
    class p0 p0
    class p1 p1
    class overlap ovl
```

The **overlap region** between the two conditional PDFs is the geometric source of all decision errors. Reducing this overlap (by increasing signal amplitude $A$ relative to noise $\sigma$, or by collecting more samples $N$) is the central engineering goal.

## 4.4 LRT Hierarchy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Binary Hypothesis Test"]:::start
    start --> lrt["Likelihood Ratio Test Lambda x compared to Gamma"]:::lrt
    lrt --> crit{"Selection of Criterion"}
    crit -->|"Costs and Priors Known"| bayes["Bayes Minimum Risk Criterion"]:::bayes
    crit -->|"Priors Known, Costs Equal"| map["MAP Minimum P_e Criterion"]:::map
    crit -->|"Equal or Unknown Priors"| ml["Maximum Likelihood Rule"]:::ml
    crit -->|"No Priors, Fix P_FA equals Alpha"| np["Neyman-Pearson Theorem"]:::np

    bayes --> gbayes["Gamma equals P of H0 times C10 minus C00 over P of H1 times C01 minus C11"]:::g
    map --> gmap["Gamma equals P of H0 over P of H1"]:::g
    ml --> gml["Gamma equals 1"]:::g
    np --> gnp["Gamma tuned so that P_FA equals Alpha"]:::g

    classDef start fill:#212121,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:3px
    classDef lrt fill:#1976D2,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef crit fill:#FF6F00,stroke:#E65100,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef bayes fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#1A1A1A,stroke-width:2px
    classDef map fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1A1A1A,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ml fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#1A1A1A,stroke-width:2px
    classDef np fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,color:#1A1A1A,stroke-width:2px
    classDef g fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00695C,color:#1A1A1A,stroke-width:2px

    class start start
    class lrt lrt
    class crit crit
    class bayes bayes
    class map map
    class ml ml
    class np np
    class gbayes,gmap,gml,gnp g
```

This taxonomy shows that **all** binary hypothesis testing criteria collapse to a likelihood ratio comparison; only the **threshold value** distinguishes them.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`
**Define the likelihood ratio test (LRT) for a binary hypothesis testing problem. State the Neyman-Pearson (NP) lemma.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT)** for a binary hypothesis testing problem with observations $\mathbf{x}$ is defined as:

$$
\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)} \;\;\gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1}\;\; \gamma
$$

where $\Lambda(\mathbf{x})$ is the likelihood ratio, $p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_i)$ is the conditional PDF under hypothesis $H_i$, and $\gamma$ is the decision threshold.

**Neyman-Pearson Lemma:** *Among all tests with probability of false alarm $P_{FA} \leq \alpha$, the likelihood ratio test $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \geq \gamma$ (with $\gamma$ chosen to enforce $P_{FA} = \alpha$) maximizes the probability of detection $P_D$. Equivalently, it minimizes the probability of miss $P_M = 1 - P_D$.* No other test of the same size $\alpha$ can achieve a larger $P_D$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between the MAP criterion and the ML criterion for binary hypothesis testing. When does MAP reduce to ML?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **MAP (Maximum A posteriori Probability) criterion:** Decides $H_1$ if $P(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x}) > P(H_0 \mid \mathbf{x})$, equivalently $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \gtrless \gamma_{MAP}$ with $\gamma_{MAP} = P(H_0)/P(H_1)$. It minimizes the probability of error when priors $P(H_0), P(H_1)$ are known.

- **ML (Maximum Likelihood) criterion:** Decides $H_1$ if $p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_1) > p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_0)$, equivalently $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \gtrless \gamma_{ML}$ with $\gamma_{ML} = 1$. It ignores prior information.

- **Reduction:** MAP reduces to ML when the prior probabilities are **equal**, i.e., $P(H_0) = P(H_1) = 0.5$, or when priors are unknown and the decision-maker is forced to assume equal a priori likelihood. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Module Pattern:** Each question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks. You must answer EITHER the full Q-A OR the full Q-B. Internal choice is at the question level, not at sub-part level.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Jul 2024]`

**Q-A (a) [7 Marks]** State and prove the Bayes minimum-risk decision rule for binary hypothesis testing. Hence derive the MAP and ML criteria as special cases.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Bayes Criterion Statement (1 mark):**
Among all decision rules, the Bayes rule that minimizes the average risk $R$ decides $H_1$ if

$$
\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_0)} > \frac{P(H_0)(C_{10} - C_{00})}{P(H_1)(C_{01} - C_{11})}
$$

**(ii) Proof (5 marks):**

- **Step 1:** Define average risk using costs $C_{ij}$ and conditional probabilities:

$$
R = \sum_{i=0}^{1}\sum_{j=0}^{1} C_{ij} \, P(H_i) \int_{\mathcal{R}_j} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_i) \, d\mathbf{x}
$$

- **Step 2:** Partition the observation space: $\mathcal{X} = \mathcal{R}_0 \cup \mathcal{R}_1$, $\mathcal{R}_0 \cap \mathcal{R}_1 = \emptyset$.

- **Step 3:** For a fixed observation $\mathbf{x}$, define conditional risk of deciding $H_j$:

$$
R(H_j \mid \mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=0}^{1} C_{ij} P(H_i \mid \mathbf{x})
$$

- **Step 4:** Decide $H_1$ if $R(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x}) < R(H_0 \mid \mathbf{x})$:

$$
C_{10}P(H_0 \mid \mathbf{x}) + C_{11}P(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x}) < C_{00}P(H_0 \mid \mathbf{x}) + C_{01}P(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x})
$$

- **Step 5:** Rearranging:

$$
(C_{01} - C_{11})P(H_1 \mid \mathbf{x}) > (C_{10} - C_{00})P(H_0 \mid \mathbf{x})
$$

- **Step 6:** Apply Bayes' rule $P(H_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_i)P(H_i)/p(\mathbf{x})$ and divide:

$$
\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \;\;\gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1}\;\; \frac{P(H_0)(C_{10} - C_{00})}{P(H_1)(C_{01} - C_{11})}
$$

**(iii) Special Cases (1 mark):**
- **MAP:** Set $C_{00}=C_{11}=0$, $C_{10}=C_{01}=1$ ŌåÆ $\gamma = P(H_0)/P(H_1)$.
- **ML:** Set additionally $P(H_0)=P(H_1)$ ŌåÆ $\gamma = 1$.

**[Total: 7 marks]**

---

**Q-A (b) [7 Marks]** Consider the binary hypothesis testing problem:

$$
H_0 : x = n, \quad H_1 : x = 1 + n
$$

where $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$. Design the Neyman-Pearson test for $P_{FA} = 0.1$. Compute $P_D$ and the probability of error $P_e$ assuming equal priors.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Likelihood Ratio (2 marks):**

$$
p(x \mid H_0) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2}\right), \quad p(x \mid H_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-1)^2}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\Lambda(x) = \exp\!\left(\frac{2x - 1}{2}\right)
$$

Log-LRT: $\ell(x) = x - 0.5$. Decision rule: $x \gtrless \eta$ with $\eta = \ln\gamma + 0.5$.

**(ii) NP Threshold for $P_{FA} = 0.1$ (2 marks):**

Under $H_0$, $x \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$:

$$
P_{FA} = P(x > \eta \mid H_0) = Q(\eta) = 0.1 \Rightarrow \eta = Q^{-1}(0.1) = 1.2816
$$

**(iii) Probability of Detection $P_D$ (2 marks):**

Under $H_1$, $x \sim \mathcal{N}(1, 1)$:

$$
P_D = Q(\eta - 1) = Q(1.2816 - 1) = Q(0.2816) \approx 0.3892
$$

**(iv) Probability of Error $P_e$ (1 mark):**

With equal priors $P(H_0) = P(H_1) = 0.5$:

$$
P_e = 0.5 \, P_{FA} + 0.5 \, P_M = 0.5(0.1) + 0.5(1 - 0.3892) = 0.05 + 0.3054 = 0.3554
$$

**[Total: 7 marks]**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**Q-B (a) [7 Marks]** Define the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC). Sketch and explain the ROC for the DC-detection-in-AWGN problem with $A/\sigma = 1$ and $A/\sigma = 2$ on the same axes. Comment on the effect of increasing the deflection coefficient.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Definition of ROC (2 marks):**
The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** is the locus of points $(P_{FA}, P_D)$ traced out as the decision threshold $\gamma$ varies over $(0, \infty)$. It characterizes the trade-off between detection probability and false-alarm probability for a given detector.

For DC-in-AWGN:

$$
P_D = Q\!\left(Q^{-1}(P_{FA}) - d\right), \quad d = \frac{A}{\sigma}
$$

**(ii) Sketch and Explanation (4 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    title["ROC for DC in AWGN Detector"]
    origin["Origin 0,0"]
    xmax["P_FA equals 1"]
    ymax["P_D equals 1"]
    diag["Diagonal Random Guess"]
    curve1["ROC for d equals 1 Modest SNR"]
    curve2["ROC for d equals 2 Higher SNR Closer to Ideal"]
    origin --> xmax
    origin --> ymax
    diag -.-> curve1
    diag -.-> curve2
    curve1 --> curve2
    title --- origin
    title --- xmax
    title --- ymax
```

- **$d = 1$:** ROC bows moderately above the diagonal.
- **$d = 2$:** ROC bows sharply toward upper-left corner $(0, 1)$ ŌĆö ideal detector limit.

**(iii) Effect of Increasing Deflection Coefficient (1 mark):**
Larger $d$ (i.e., higher SNR or more samples $N$, since $d \propto \sqrt{N}$) shifts the ROC curve closer to the ideal $(0,1)$ point, meaning the detector achieves higher $P_D$ at any given $P_{FA}$.

**[Total: 7 marks]**

---

**Q-B (b) [7 Marks]** $N$ independent observations $x_i$ are taken under

$$
H_0: x_i = n_i, \quad H_1: x_i = A + n_i, \quad n_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)
$$

Derive the likelihood ratio test and obtain expressions for $P_{FA}$ and $P_D$ in terms of $N, A, \sigma, \alpha$.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Joint Densities (1 mark):**

$$
p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_0) = (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-N/2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^N x_i^2\right)
$$

$$
p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_1) = (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-N/2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^N (x_i - A)^2\right)
$$

**(ii) Log-Likelihood Ratio (2 marks):**

$$
\ell(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[\sum (x_i - A)^2 - \sum x_i^2\right]
$$

Expanding:

$$
\ell(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\left[A\sum x_i - \frac{NA^2}{2}\right]
$$

**(iii) Decision Rule (1 mark):** Monotonic in $\sum x_i$, equivalently in $\bar{x}$:

$$
\bar{x} \;\;\gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1}\;\; \eta
$$

**(iv) Probability Expressions (2 marks):**

Under $H_0$: $\bar{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2/N)$:

$$
P_{FA} = Q\!\left(\eta \frac{\sqrt{N}}{\sigma}\right)
$$

For $P_{FA} = \alpha$:

$$
\eta = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}} Q^{-1}(\alpha)
$$

Under $H_1$: $\bar{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(A, \sigma^2/N)$:

$$
P_D = Q\!\left(Q^{-1}(\alpha) - \frac{A\sqrt{N}}{\sigma}\right)
$$

**(v) Interpretation (1 mark):** Effective deflection grows as $A\sqrt{N}/\sigma$, so detection performance improves as $\sqrt{N}$ with sample size ŌĆö the basis for *integration gain* in radar and digital communications.

**[Total: 7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take the natural log of the threshold** when converting LRT to log-LRT ŌĆö leading to inverted inequality signs.
> 2. **Confusing $P_D$ with $1 - P_{FA}$** ŌĆö these are NOT the same; they refer to different conditional probabilities.
> 3. **Using the wrong distribution under each hypothesis** ŌĆö remember $x \sim \mathcal{N}(A, \sigma^2)$ under $H_1$, not $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.
> 4. **Not justifying monotonic transformation** ŌĆö when you replace $\Lambda(x)$ by $x$ or by $\bar{x}$, you MUST explicitly state "since $\ln(\cdot)$ is monotonic increasing" or "since the transformation is one-to-one."
> 5. **Sign errors in Q-function arguments** ŌĆö carefully track $\eta - A$ versus $A - \eta$ in the $P_D$ expression.
> 6. **Skipping the "decide $H_1$ if ..." line** in derivations ŌĆö examiners specifically look for the final inequality direction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hypothesis Testing ŌĆö Things You MUST Remember (Board-Exam Ready):**

- **Binary hypotheses** $H_0$ (noise only) and $H_1$ (signal + noise).
- **Four outcomes**: detection, miss, false alarm, correct rejection.
- **Likelihood ratio** $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_1)/p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_0)$ is the **sufficient statistic** for detection.
- **All four criteria** (Bayes, MAP, ML, NP) reduce to an LRT; only the **threshold** differs.
- **Bayes threshold**: $\gamma_B = [P(H_0)(C_{10}-C_{00})]/[P(H_1)(C_{01}-C_{11})]$.
- **MAP threshold**: $\gamma_{MAP} = P(H_0)/P(H_1)$; **ML threshold**: $\gamma_{ML} = 1$.
- **Neyman-Pearson Lemma** ŌĆö guarantees $P_D$ maximization at fixed $P_{FA} = \alpha$.
- **Log-likelihood ratio** $\ell(\mathbf{x}) = \ln \Lambda(\mathbf{x})$ simplifies AWGN problems.
- **Gaussian tail function** $Q(x) = \int_x^\infty (1/\sqrt{2\pi}) e^{-u^2/2} du$ ŌĆö recall $Q(-x) = 1 - Q(x)$.
- **DC-in-AWGN** $P_D = Q(Q^{-1}(\alpha) - A/\sigma)$.
- **Vector (N-sample)** $P_D = Q(Q^{-1}(\alpha) - A\sqrt{N}/\sigma)$ ŌĆö integration gain $\propto \sqrt{N}$.
- **Deflection coefficient** $d = A/\sigma$ (scalar), $d_N = A\sqrt{N}/\sigma$ (N-sample).
- **ROC curve** ŌĆö locus of $(P_{FA}, P_D)$ as $\gamma$ varies; bows toward $(0,1)$ for better detectors.
- **Ideal detector**: $P_{FA} = 0$, $P_D = 1$ ŌĆö ROC passes through the upper-left corner.
- **Random guess**: $P_D = P_{FA}$ ŌĆö ROC is the diagonal $y = x$.
- **GLRT** ŌĆö for composite hypotheses, plug ML estimates of unknown parameters into the likelihood ratio.
- **Min-Max criterion** ŌĆö used when priors are uncertain; minimizes worst-case (maximum) $P_e$ over all prior choices.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classical Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classical Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the Classical Approach in Estimation Theory?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Classical Approach** to estimation refers to a collection of statistical inference methods that derive parameter estimates directly from observed data using **deterministic optimization principles** (e.g., likelihood maximization, moment matching, or least-squares minimization) **without imposing any prior probabilistic belief** on the unknown parameter. The parameter $\theta$ is treated as a **fixed but unknown deterministic constant**, and the randomness lies entirely in the observation $X$.

The classical approach stands in contrast to the **Bayesian approach** (covered in the next module), where $\theta$ itself is modelled as a random variable with a known prior distribution $p(\theta)$.

### Core Characteristics of the Classical Approach

| Property | Classical Approach | Bayesian Approach |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature of $\theta$** | Fixed, unknown constant | Random variable with prior $p(\theta)$ |
| **Information Used** | Only observed data $X$ | Data $X$ + prior $p(\theta)$ |
| **Optimality Criterion** | Variance, MSE, unbiasedness | Posterior risk, MMSE, MAP |
| **Estimator Output** | A single point estimate $\hat{\theta}(X)$ | Often a full posterior distribution |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Treasure Hunter**
>
> Imagine you are a treasure hunter trying to locate a hidden chest on a beach. You have **no map, no legend, and no local guide** (no prior information). The chest is at a fixed, unknown spot. You take measurements (metal detector readings, soil density probes, depth soundings) and you must deduce the chest's location **purely from these measurements**.
>
> The **Classical Approach** is exactly this scenario:
> - The chest's location = the unknown deterministic parameter $\theta$.
> - The noisy metal detector readings = the random observations $X$.
> - Your best guess from the readings = the estimator $\hat{\theta}(X)$.
>
> Tools like the **Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE)** correspond to asking: *"Which chest location is most likely to have produced the readings I see?"* This is the fundamental philosophical backbone of classical estimation.

---

## 1.3 The Five Pillars of the Classical Approach

The classical approach to estimation, as expected in the KTU 2024 syllabus, is built upon **five interconnected pillars**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Pillars of Classical Estimation:**
> 1. **Sufficiency** ŌĆö reducing data without losing information about $\theta$.
> 2. **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** ŌĆö finding the most plausible $\theta$.
> 3. **Cram├®r-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)** ŌĆö the theoretical floor on estimator variance.
> 4. **Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimation (MVUE)** ŌĆö the best unbiased estimator.
> 5. **Method of Moments & Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)** ŌĆö algebraic estimation tools.

These pillars are studied together because they answer complementary questions: *What is the best estimator? How good is it? Can it be improved?*

---

## 1.4 Standard Notation Used Throughout This Module

To avoid confusion, we lock down the notation from the start:

- $\theta$ ŌĆö scalar unknown deterministic parameter (extension to vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is also covered).
- $X = [X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n]$ ŌĆö random sample (i.i.d. in most cases).
- $p(x;\theta)$ or $p(x \vert \theta)$ ŌĆö probability density function (or PMF for discrete).
- $\hat{\theta}$ ŌĆö estimator (a function of the random sample $X$).
- $\hat{\theta}_{MLE}$ ŌĆö maximum likelihood estimator.
- $L(\theta) = p(X;\theta)$ ŌĆö likelihood function (joint PDF viewed as a function of $\theta$).
- $\ell(\theta) = \ln L(\theta)$ ŌĆö log-likelihood function.
- $I(\theta)$ ŌĆö Fisher Information.
- $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta})$ ŌĆö variance of the estimator.
- $\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta$ ŌĆö bias of the estimator.
- $E[\cdot]$, $\text{Var}(\cdot)$ ŌĆö expectation and variance operators.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 ŌĆö Sufficient Statistics

### 2.1.1 Concept

A statistic $T(X)$ is **sufficient** for $\theta$ if the conditional distribution of the data $X$ given $T(X)$ does **not depend on** $\theta$. Intuitively, $T(X)$ captures **all the information** about $\theta$ that is present in the sample.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Fisher-Neyman):**
> A statistic $T(X)$ is sufficient for $\theta$ if the conditional probability density $p(x \mid T(x); \theta) = p(x \mid T(x))$ is **independent of** $\theta$.

### 2.1.2 Neyman-Fisher Factorization Theorem

A practically computable criterion for sufficiency:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Factorization Theorem:**
> $T(X)$ is sufficient for $\theta$ **if and only if** the joint PDF can be factored as:
> $$p(x;\theta) = g(T(x), \theta) \cdot h(x)$$
> where $g(\cdot)$ depends on $x$ only through $T(x)$, and $h(x)$ does not depend on $\theta$.

### 2.1.3 Minimal Sufficient Statistic

A sufficient statistic $T(X)$ is **minimal sufficient** if it is a function of every other sufficient statistic. Minimal sufficient statistics are the **most data-reducing** statistics that retain all information about $\theta$.

---

## 2.2 Pillar 2 ŌĆö Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)

### 2.2.1 Concept

The MLE is the value of $\theta$ that **maximizes the probability (or probability density) of observing the actual data that was observed**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> The Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE) of $\theta$ is:
> $$\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \arg\max_{\theta \in \Theta} L(\theta) = \arg\max_{\theta \in \Theta} p(X;\theta)$$
> Equivalently, since $\ln(\cdot)$ is monotonic:
> $$\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \arg\max_{\theta \in \Theta} \ell(\theta) = \arg\max_{\theta \in \Theta} \ln p(X;\theta)$$

### 2.2.2 The Likelihood Equation

Setting the derivative of the log-likelihood to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = 0$$

This is called the **likelihood equation** or **score equation**.

### 2.2.3 Key Properties of MLE

1. **Invariance (Functional Equivariance):** If $\hat{\theta}_{MLE}$ is the MLE of $\theta$, then for any function $g(\cdot)$, the MLE of $g(\theta)$ is $g(\hat{\theta}_{MLE})$.
2. **Asymptotic Normality:** $\hat{\theta}_{MLE} \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(\theta, \frac{1}{nI(\theta)})$ as $n \to \infty$.
3. **Consistency:** $\hat{\theta}_{MLE} \to \theta$ in probability as $n \to \infty$.
4. **Asymptotic Efficiency:** Achieves the CRLB asymptotically.

---

## 2.3 Pillar 3 ŌĆö Cram├®r-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)

### 2.3.1 Concept

The CRLB is the **lowest possible variance** of any **unbiased** estimator of $\theta$. It serves as a benchmark: no unbiased estimator can outperform the CRLB.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cram├®r-Rao Inequality:**
> For any unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of $\theta$:
> $$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{I(\theta)}$$
> where $I(\theta)$ is the **Fisher Information** about $\theta$ contained in the sample.

### 2.3.2 Fisher Information

The Fisher information measures the amount of information that the observable random variable $X$ carries about $\theta$:

$$I(\theta) = -E\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln p(X;\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right] = E\left[\left(\frac{\partial \ln p(X;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right)^2\right]$$

For an i.i.d. sample of size $n$:

$$I_n(\theta) = n \cdot I_1(\theta)$$

### 2.3.3 CRLB Theorem Statement

> [!NOTE]
> **Cram├®r-Rao Theorem:**
> Assume the regularity conditions (differentiability under integral, finite Fisher information). For any unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of $\theta$:
> $$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{n I_1(\theta)} = \frac{1}{I_n(\theta)}$$

### 2.3.4 Efficiency

An estimator is called **efficient** if it achieves the CRLB, i.e., $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) = \frac{1}{I(\theta)}$.

---

## 2.4 Pillar 4 ŌĆö Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimator (MVUE)

### 2.4.1 Concept

Among all **unbiased** estimators, the **MVUE** is the one with the **smallest variance**. The CRLB gives a lower bound; if an unbiased estimator achieves it, that estimator **is** the MVUE.

### 2.4.2 Rao-Blackwell Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rao-Blackwell Theorem:**
> If $\hat{\theta}$ is any unbiased estimator of $\theta$ and $T(X)$ is a sufficient statistic for $\theta$, then the conditional expectation:
> $$\tilde{\theta} = E[\hat{\theta} \mid T(X)]$$
> is also unbiased and has variance **less than or equal to** that of $\hat{\theta}$:
> $$\text{Var}(\tilde{\theta}) \leq \text{Var}(\hat{\theta})$$
> Equality holds iff $\hat{\theta}$ is already a function of $T(X)$.

### 2.4.3 Lehmann-Scheff├® Theorem

> [!NOTE]
> **Lehmann-Scheff├® Theorem:**
> If $T(X)$ is a **complete and sufficient** statistic for $\theta$, and $\hat{\theta}$ is any unbiased estimator that is a function of $T(X)$, then $\hat{\theta}$ is the **unique MVUE** of $\theta$.

### 2.4.4 Completeness

A family of distributions $\{p(x;\theta) : \theta \in \Theta\}$ is **complete** if:
$$E_\theta[g(T(X))] = 0 \quad \forall \theta \in \Theta \implies g(T(X)) = 0 \text{ a.s.}$$

Completeness is the technical condition that guarantees the MVUE is **unique**.

---

## 2.5 Pillar 5 ŌĆö Method of Moments & BLUE

### 2.5.1 Method of Moments (MoM)

The **k-th population moment** is $\mu_k = E[X^k]$. The **k-th sample moment** is $M_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i^k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Method of Moments Estimator:**
> Set $M_k = \mu_k(\theta)$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, p$ (where $p$ is the number of unknown parameters) and solve for $\theta$. The MoM estimator is:
> $$\hat{\theta}_{MoM} = \text{solution of } M_k = \mu_k(\theta)$$

### 2.5.2 Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)

For a linear model $X = H\theta + W$ where $W$ is noise:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gauss-Markov Theorem:**
> The BLUE of $\theta$ in the linear model is:
> $$\hat{\theta}_{BLUE} = (H^T C_W^{-1} H)^{-1} H^T C_W^{-1} X$$
> with minimum variance among all linear unbiased estimators. The covariance is:
> $$C_{\hat{\theta}} = (H^T C_W^{-1} H)^{-1}$$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD ŌĆö All Classical Approach Formulas in One Table**

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Key Condition** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Likelihood Function | $L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i;\theta)$ | i.i.d. samples |
| 2 | Log-Likelihood | $\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln p(x_i;\theta)$ | monotonic transform |
| 3 | Score Function | $s(\theta) = \frac{\partial \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta}$ | set to zero for MLE |
| 4 | Fisher Information (sample) | $I_n(\theta) = -E\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$ | regularity conditions |
| 5 | Fisher Information (i.i.d.) | $I_n(\theta) = n \cdot I_1(\theta)$ | independence |
| 6 | CRLB | $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{I_n(\theta)}$ | unbiasedness |
| 7 | Factorization | $p(x;\theta) = g(T(x),\theta) \cdot h(x)$ | sufficiency condition |
| 8 | Rao-Blackwell | $\text{Var}(E[\hat{\theta}\vert T]) \leq \text{Var}(\hat{\theta})$ | $T$ sufficient, $\hat{\theta}$ unbiased |
| 9 | MLE Invariance | $\widehat{g(\theta)}_{MLE} = g(\hat{\theta}_{MLE})$ | any $g$ |
| 10 | Asymptotic MLE | $\hat{\theta}_{MLE} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\theta, \frac{1}{nI_1(\theta)}\right)$ | large $n$ |
| 11 | MoM Equation | $\frac{1}{n}\sum X_i^k = E[X^k]$ | equate moments |
| 12 | BLUE | $(H^TC_W^{-1}H)^{-1}H^TC_W^{-1}X$ | linear model |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the Classical Approach matter in engineering?**
> - **Radar/Sonar Systems:** Estimating target range, velocity, and bearing from noisy echoes ŌĆö MLE and CRLB are the standard performance benchmarks.
> - **Communications:** Synchronization, channel estimation, and signal detection all use ML detection and estimation.
> - **Biomedical Signal Processing:** Estimating heart rate from ECG, neural firing rates from EEG.
> - **Navigation (GPS/INS):** Position and velocity estimation from noisy sensor measurements.
> - **Machine Learning:** Logistic regression weights, Gaussian mixture parameters ŌĆö all trained via MLE.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Cram├®r-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)

**Setup:** Let $X$ have PDF $p(x;\theta)$ and let $\hat{\theta}$ be any unbiased estimator of $\theta$:

$$E[\hat{\theta}] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{\theta}(x) \, p(x;\theta) \, dx = \theta$$

**Step 1:** Differentiate both sides with respect to $\theta$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \int \hat{\theta}(x) \, p(x;\theta) \, dx = 1$$

**Step 2:** Bring the derivative inside the integral (regularity condition ŌĆö allows interchange of $\int$ and $\partial/\partial \theta$):

$$\int \hat{\theta}(x) \, \frac{\partial p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta} \, dx = 1$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the score-function identity. Note that:

$$\frac{\partial p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta} = p(x;\theta) \cdot \frac{\partial \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}$$

So the integral becomes:

$$\int \hat{\theta}(x) \cdot p(x;\theta) \cdot \frac{\partial \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta} \, dx = 1$$

That is:

$$E\!\left[\hat{\theta}(X) \cdot \frac{\partial \ln p(X;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right] = 1$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $E\!\left[\dfrac{\partial \ln p(X;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right] = 0$ (because the score has zero mean ŌĆö a standard property). Therefore:

$$E\!\left[(\hat{\theta}(X) - \theta) \cdot \frac{\partial \ln p(X;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right] = 1$$

**Step 5:** Apply the **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality** to the random variables $(\hat{\theta} - \theta)$ and $\dfrac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}$:

$$\left(E\!\left[(\hat{\theta} - \theta) \cdot \frac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}\right]\right)^2 \leq E\!\left[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2\right] \cdot E\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}\right)^2\right]$$

That is:

$$1^2 \leq \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \cdot I(\theta)$$

where $I(\theta) = E\!\left[\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln p(X;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right)^2\right]$ is the **Fisher Information**.

**Step 6:** Rearrange to obtain the CRLB:

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{I(\theta)}}$$

**Equality condition (Cauchy-Schwarz equality):** Achieved iff there exists a constant $k(\theta)$ such that:

$$\frac{\partial \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta} = k(\theta) \cdot (\hat{\theta}(x) - \theta)$$

This is the condition for the estimator to be **efficient**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö MLE for a Gaussian with Unknown Mean

**Setup:** Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma^2$ **known**. We wish to find $\hat{\mu}_{MLE}$.

**Step 1:** Write the joint PDF:

$$p(X;\mu) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

$$= \left(\frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}\right)^{n/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2\right)$$

**Step 2:** Take the log-likelihood:

$$\ell(\mu) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $\mu$:

$$\frac{\partial \ell(\mu)}{\partial \mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - n\mu\right)$$

**Step 4:** Set to zero:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - n\mu = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

**Step 5:** Confirm the second derivative is negative (maximum):

$$\frac{\partial^2 \ell(\mu)}{\partial \mu^2} = -\frac{n}{\sigma^2} < 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\hat{\mu}_{MLE} = \bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö CRLB for the Gaussian Mean

We now show that the sample mean **achieves** the CRLB for the Gaussian mean problem.

**Step 1:** From a single observation $X_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$:

$$\ln p(x_i;\mu) = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

**Step 2:** First derivative:

$$\frac{\partial \ln p(x_i;\mu)}{\partial \mu} = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma^2}$$

**Step 3:** Second derivative:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \ln p(x_i;\mu)}{\partial \mu^2} = -\frac{1}{\sigma^2}$$

**Step 4:** Fisher Information per sample:

$$I_1(\mu) = -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln p(x_i;\mu)}{\partial \mu^2}\right] = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}$$

**Step 5:** For $n$ i.i.d. samples:

$$I_n(\mu) = \frac{n}{\sigma^2}$$

**Step 6:** The CRLB is:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}) \geq \frac{1}{I_n(\mu)} = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

**Step 7:** The actual variance of the sample mean is:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \text{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) = \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\text{Var}(X_i) = \frac{1}{n^2} \cdot n\sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

**Conclusion:** The sample mean is an **efficient** estimator of $\mu$ for the Gaussian model with known variance.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Rao-Blackwell Improvement of an Unbiased Estimator

**Setup:** Let $X_1, \ldots, X_n \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\theta)$, with $T(X) = \sum X_i$ sufficient for $\theta$. Let $\hat{\theta} = X_1$ (an unbiased but inefficient estimator). Show that $E[X_1 \mid T]$ is the MVUE.

**Step 1:** Compute $E[X_1 \mid T = t]$:

By symmetry, conditional on $T = t$ (so $t$ ones are distributed among $n$ positions):

$$E[X_1 \mid T = t] = P(X_1 = 1 \mid T = t) = \frac{t}{n}$$

Therefore:

$$E[X_1 \mid T] = \frac{T}{n} = \bar{X}$$

**Step 2:** Verify the new estimator is unbiased:

$$E[\bar{X}] = E\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right] = \frac{1}{n} \cdot n\theta = \theta \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3:** Compare variances:

$$\text{Var}(X_1) = \theta(1-\theta)$$

$$\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{\theta(1-\theta)}{n}$$

**Conclusion:** Variance dropped by factor of $n$ ŌĆö exactly the Rao-Blackwell improvement.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 ŌĆö Method of Moments for Exponential Distribution

**Setup:** $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ with PDF $p(x;\lambda) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$, $x \geq 0$. The unknown parameter is $\lambda$.

**Step 1:** Population mean:

$$E[X] = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2:** Sample mean:

$$\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$$

**Step 3:** Equate them:

$$\frac{1}{\lambda} = \bar{X} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{1}{\bar{X}}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\hat{\lambda}_{MoM} = \frac{1}{\bar{X}}}$$

**Step 4:** (For comparison) the MLE for exponential:

The likelihood is $L(\lambda) = \lambda^n e^{-\lambda \sum x_i}$. Log-likelihood: $\ell(\lambda) = n\ln\lambda - \lambda \sum x_i$. Setting $\partial \ell / \partial \lambda = n/\lambda - \sum x_i = 0$ gives:

$$\hat{\lambda}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{\bar{X}}$$

**Insight:** For the exponential family with a single parameter, MoM and MLE **coincide**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison of Estimator Properties

| **Property** | **MLE** | **MoM** | **MVUE** | **BLUE** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unbiased** | Asymptotically | Not always | Yes (by definition) | Yes (by definition) |
| **Variance** | Achieves CRLB asymptotically | Larger | Ōēż CRLB | Minimum (linear) |
| **Computational** | Needs numerical opt. | Closed-form (usually) | Closed-form (sometimes) | Closed-form |
| **Needs prior** | No | No | No | No |
| **Requires** | Likelihood | Moments | Sufficiency + Completeness | Linear model |
| **KTU Exam Weight** | **Very High** | Medium | **High** | Medium |

flowchart LR
