# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**

> Define a **sufficient statistic**. State the **Neyman-Fisher Factorization Theorem** for identifying sufficient statistics.

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** A statistic $T(X)$ is sufficient for $\theta$ if the conditional distribution of the sample $X$ given the value of $T(X)$ does not depend on $\theta$. Formally:
> $$p(x \mid T(x); \theta) = p(x \mid T(x))$$
>
> **Factorization Theorem (2 marks):** A statistic $T(X)$ is sufficient for $\theta$ if and only if the joint PDF of the sample can be factored as:
> $$p(x;\theta) = g(T(x), \theta) \cdot h(x)$$
> where $g(\cdot, \cdot)$ depends on $x$ only through $T(x)$, and $h(x)$ is independent of $\theta$.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

> What is the **Cram├®r-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)**? Write the expression for CRLB for an unbiased estimator of a scalar parameter $\theta$ based on $n$ i.i.d. observations.

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** The CRLB is a lower bound on the variance of any unbiased estimator of a parameter. It establishes the minimum achievable variance and serves as a benchmark for estimator performance.
>
> **Formula (1.5 marks):** For an unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of a scalar parameter $\theta$:
> $$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{n \cdot I_1(\theta)} = \frac{1}{I_n(\theta)}$$
> where $I_1(\theta) = -E\!\left[\dfrac{\partial^2 \ln p(X_i;\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$ is the per-sample Fisher Information.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting regularity conditions** for CRLB ŌĆö students often quote the bound without mentioning differentiability under the integral sign.
> 2. **Sign error in the score function** ŌĆö confusing $\partial \ell / \partial \theta$ with its negative.
> 3. **Confusing Fisher information per sample vs. total** ŌĆö multiplying incorrectly by $n$.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step** that an estimator actually achieves CRLB (Cauchy-Schwarz equality condition is often ignored).
> 5. **Writing factorization as $g(\theta) \cdot h(T(x), x)$** ŌĆö students mix up the arguments. Remember: $g$ depends on $x$ **only** through $T(x)$.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Model Paper]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Cram├®r-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)** for an unbiased estimator of a scalar parameter $\theta$ based on a random sample. Clearly state the regularity conditions required.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider $n$ i.i.d. observations $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ from an exponential distribution with PDF $p(x;\lambda) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$, $x \geq 0$, where $\lambda > 0$ is the unknown parameter. Find the MLE of $\lambda$ and determine the CRLB for any unbiased estimator of $\lambda$. Verify whether the MLE is efficient.

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation of CRLB [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** State the problem. Let $X$ be a random variable with PDF $p(x;\theta)$ and $\hat{\theta}$ be any unbiased estimator of $\theta$:

$$\int \hat{\theta}(x) \, p(x;\theta) \, dx = \theta$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** State the regularity condition ŌĆö the derivative $\dfrac{\partial p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta}$ exists and $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\int p(x;\theta) \, dx = 0$ (so the integral and derivative can be exchanged).

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks):** Differentiate the unbiasedness condition with respect to $\theta$:

$$\int \hat{\theta}(x) \cdot \frac{\partial p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta} \, dx = 1$$

Using $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial \theta} = p \cdot \dfrac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}$:

$$\int \hat{\theta}(x) \cdot p(x;\theta) \cdot \frac{\partial \ln p(x;\theta)}{\partial \theta} \, dx = 1$$

**Step 4 (1.5 Marks):** Note that the score function has zero mean:

$$E\!\left[\frac{\partial \ln p(X;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right] = 0$$

So we can subtract $E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$ inside:

$$E\!\left[(\hat{\theta}(X) - \theta) \cdot \frac{\partial \ln p(X;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right] = 1$$

**Step 5 (1.5 Marks):** Apply the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality:

$$\left(E\!\left[(\hat{\theta} - \theta) \cdot \frac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}\right]\right)^2 \leq E\!\left[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2\right] \cdot E\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta}\right)^2\right]$$

$$1^2 \leq \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \cdot I(\theta)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{I(\theta)}}$$

**Step 6 (0.5 Marks):** State the equality condition (efficiency): the bound is achieved iff $\dfrac{\partial \ln p}{\partial \theta} = k(\theta)(\hat{\theta} - \theta)$ for some function $k(\theta)$.

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö MLE and CRLB for Exponential [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 (1.5 Marks):** Write the likelihood function for $n$ i.i.d. observations:

$$L(\lambda) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda e^{-\lambda x_i} = \lambda^n \exp\!\left(-\lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Log-likelihood:

$$\ell(\lambda) = n \ln \lambda - \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks):** Differentiate and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \ell(\lambda)}{\partial \lambda} = \frac{n}{\lambda} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = 0$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\lambda}_{MLE} = \frac{n}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i} = \frac{1}{\bar{X}}}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Compute Fisher Information. From the log-likelihood:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \ln p(x;\lambda)}{\partial \lambda^2} = -\frac{1}{\lambda^2}$$

So $I_1(\lambda) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$ and $I_n(\lambda) = \dfrac{n}{\lambda^2}$.

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** CRLB:

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(\hat{\lambda}) \geq \frac{\lambda^2}{n}}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Check efficiency. Compute the actual variance of $\hat{\lambda}_{MLE} = 1/\bar{X}$. Since $\sum X_i \sim \text{Gamma}(n, \lambda)$, we have:

$$E[\hat{\lambda}] = \frac{n\lambda}{n-1} \quad (n > 1)$$

The estimator is **biased** (especially for small $n$). It is asymptotically unbiased and asymptotically efficient (achieves CRLB as $n \to \infty$), but **not** efficient for finite $n$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Define **MVUE**. State and prove the **Rao-Blackwell Theorem**. How does the **Lehmann-Scheff├® Theorem** use completeness to give a unique MVUE?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with both $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ unknown. Find the MLE of $(\mu, \sigma^2)$. Compute the CRLB for $\mu$ and comment on its attainability.

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö MVUE and Theorems [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Definition of MVUE. A Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimator is an unbiased estimator of $\theta$ whose variance is less than or equal to that of every other unbiased estimator.

**Step 2 (2.5 Marks):** **Rao-Blackwell Theorem Statement and Proof.**
*Statement:* If $\hat{\theta}$ is any unbiased estimator of $\theta$ and $T(X)$ is a sufficient statistic for $\theta$, then $\tilde{\theta} = E[\hat{\theta} \mid T(X)]$ is unbiased with $\text{Var}(\tilde{\theta}) \leq \text{Var}(\hat{\theta})$.

*Proof sketch:* By the law of total expectation, $E[\tilde{\theta}] = E[E[\hat{\theta} \mid T]] = E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$. For the variance, use the law of total variance:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) = E[\text{Var}(\hat{\theta} \mid T)] + \text{Var}(E[\hat{\theta} \mid T])$$

Since $E[\text{Var}(\hat{\theta} \mid T)] \geq 0$:

$$\text{Var}(\tilde{\theta}) = \text{Var}(E[\hat{\theta} \mid T]) \leq \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks):** **Lehmann-Scheff├® Theorem.** If $T(X)$ is a **complete and sufficient** statistic, then any unbiased estimator that is a function of $T(X)$ is the **unique MVUE** of $\theta$.

*Why uniqueness?* Suppose there were two unbiased functions of $T$: $g_1(T)$ and $g_2(T)$. Then $E[g_1(T) - g_2(T)] = 0$ for all $\theta$, so by completeness $g_1(T) = g_2(T)$ almost surely.

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Practical algorithm:

1. Start with any unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}_0$.
2. Find a sufficient statistic $T(X)$ (via factorization).
3. Compute $\tilde{\theta} = E[\hat{\theta}_0 \mid T]$.
4. Verify $T$ is complete; if yes, $\tilde{\theta}$ is the **unique MVUE**.

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö Gaussian with Unknown Mean and Variance [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 (1.5 Marks):** Write the joint PDF and log-likelihood:

$$\ell(\mu, \sigma^2) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 2 (1.5 Marks):** Partial derivative w.r.t. $\mu$, set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{\mu}_{MLE} = \bar{X}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks):** Partial derivative w.r.t. $\sigma^2$, set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \sigma^2} = -\frac{n}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2\sigma^4}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2 = 0$$

Substituting $\hat{\mu} = \bar{X}$:

$$\boxed{\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Note that $\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE}$ is **biased** ($E[\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE}] = \dfrac{n-1}{n}\sigma^2$). The unbiased version is $S^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2$.

**Step 5 (1.5 Marks):** Compute CRLB for $\mu$ (treating $\sigma^2$ as known for simplicity, or jointly). The Fisher information matrix is:

$$I(\mu, \sigma^2) = \begin{bmatrix} n/\sigma^2 & 0 \\ 0 & n/(2\sigma^4) \end{bmatrix}$$

The CRLB for $\mu$ (regardless of whether $\sigma^2$ is known) is:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}) \geq \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

**Attainability:** The MLE $\hat{\mu} = \bar{X}$ is unbiased and $\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}$ ŌĆö therefore $\bar{X}$ **achieves the CRLB** and is the **MVUE** for $\mu$. Ō£ō

---

## 5.3 KTU Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST ŌĆö Classical Approach**

### **Core Definitions (Must Memorize)**
- ŌśÉ **Sufficient statistic:** Conditional distribution of data given the statistic is free of $\theta$.
- ŌśÉ **Complete statistic:** $E_\theta[g(T)] = 0$ for all $\theta$ implies $g(T) = 0$ a.s.
- ŌśÉ **Unbiased estimator:** $E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta$.
- ŌśÉ **Efficient estimator:** Achieves CRLB, i.e., $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) = 1/I(\theta)$.
- ŌśÉ **Consistent estimator:** $\hat{\theta}_n \xrightarrow{P} \theta$ as $n \to \infty$.

### **Key Theorems (Must Know Statements)**
- ŌśÉ **Neyman-Fisher Factorization Theorem** ŌĆö identifies sufficient statistics.
- ŌśÉ **Rao-Blackwell Theorem** ŌĆö improves any unbiased estimator using sufficiency.
- ŌśÉ **Lehmann-Scheff├® Theorem** ŌĆö gives unique MVUE via complete sufficiency.
- ŌśÉ **Gauss-Markov Theorem** ŌĆö BLUE for linear models.

### **Critical Formulas (Must Memorize)**
- ŌśÉ **CRLB:** $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \dfrac{1}{I_n(\theta)}$
- ŌśÉ **Fisher Information:** $I_n(\theta) = -E\!\left[\dfrac{\partial^2 \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$
- ŌśÉ **Score equation:** $\dfrac{\partial \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = 0$
- ŌśÉ **i.i.d. rule:** $I_n(\theta) = n \cdot I_1(\theta)$

### **Common Pitfalls to Avoid**
- ŌśÉ ŌØī Confusing sufficient with complete (different concepts!).
- ŌśÉ ŌØī Forgetting that the CRLB requires **unbiasedness**.
- ŌśÉ ŌØī Mixing per-sample and total Fisher Information.
- ŌśÉ ŌØī Skipping regularity conditions in CRLB derivations.
- ŌśÉ ŌØī Assuming the MLE is unbiased (often it is not for finite $n$).
- ŌśÉ ŌØī Writing the factorization theorem with reversed arguments.
- ŌśÉ ŌØī Forgetting the invariance property of MLE.

### **Standard Result Worth Remembering Cold**
- ŌśÉ For $X_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ i.i.d.: $\hat{\mu}_{MLE} = \bar{X}$ is **efficient**.
- ŌśÉ For $X_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ i.i.d.: $\hat{\lambda}_{MLE} = 1/\bar{X}$ is **biased** but **asymptotically efficient**.
- ŌśÉ For Bernoulli: MLE, MoM, and MVUE all equal $\bar{X}$ ŌĆö and it is **efficient**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neyman-Pearson Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Neyman-Pearson Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 ŌĆö Neyman-Pearson Theorem

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Neyman-Pearson Theorem (NPT):**
> For a binary hypothesis testing problem with hypotheses $H_0$ (null hypothesis) and $H_1$ (alternative hypothesis), the Neyman-Pearson Theorem states that *the likelihood ratio test (LRT) is the most powerful test* for a given level of significance $\alpha$ (probability of false alarm $P_F$). The test is most powerful in the sense that, among all tests with false alarm probability $P_F \le \alpha$, it maximizes the probability of detection $P_D$.

Mathematically, the test decides $H_1$ if:

$$\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)} \ge \gamma$$

and decides $H_0$ otherwise, where $\gamma$ is the threshold chosen so that:

$$P_F = \int_{\{\mathbf{x}:\Lambda(\mathbf{x})\ge \gamma\}} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)\, d\mathbf{x} = \alpha$$

Here $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N]^T$ is the observation vector, $p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_i)$ is the conditional probability density function (PDF) under hypothesis $H_i$, and $\Lambda(\mathbf{x})$ is the **likelihood ratio**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Airport Security Metal Detector**
> 
> Imagine a security checkpoint with two kinds of threats ŌĆö harmless items (analogous to $H_0$, the noise-only case) and weapons (analogous to $H_1$, the signal-present case). The detector is set to a sensitivity threshold $\gamma$:
> 
> - If the *evidence score* of a passenger (likelihood ratio) is high enough (Ōēź $\gamma$), the alarm rings ŌåÆ decide $H_1$ (signal detected).
> - Otherwise ŌåÆ decide $H_0$ (no signal).
> 
> The aviation authority imposes a hard limit on the **false-alarm rate** $\alpha$ (e.g., at most 2 innocent people per 1000 should be flagged). The Neyman-Pearson theorem says: *given this false-alarm constraint, the sensitivity $\gamma$ that you set is the one that catches the maximum number of real threats (highest $P_D$)*. You cannot do better than this threshold ŌĆö it is provably **optimal**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU Board Examinations

| Symbol | Meaning | KTU 2024 Notation |
|---|---|---|
| $H_0$ | Null hypothesis (noise only) | Sometimes called $H_0: \theta = \theta_0$ |
| $H_1$ | Alternative hypothesis (signal present) | $H_1: \theta = \theta_1$ |
| $\alpha$ | Size of the test (significance level) | Equals $P_F$, the false-alarm probability |
| $\beta$ | Probability of miss ($P_M = 1 - P_D$) | Type-II error |
| $P_D$ | Probability of detection | Power of the test |
| $\Lambda(\mathbf{x})$ | Likelihood ratio | Test statistic |
| $\gamma$ | Detection threshold | Lagrange multiplier from NPT |
| $N$ | Number of observations | Sample size |

### 1.4 Why NPT Matters in Estimation and Detection

> [!NOTE]
> In many engineering applications (radar, sonar, communications, biomedical signal processing, fault detection), the **prior probabilities** of $H_0$ and $H_1$ are either unknown or not stationary. Therefore the Bayesian minimum-risk criterion cannot be directly applied. The Neyman-Pearson framework circumvents this by:
> 1. Treating $P_F = \alpha$ as the only design constraint (set by the system designer, e.g., FAA radar specifications).
> 2. Maximizing $P_D$ subject to this constraint.
> 3. Producing a deterministic, data-driven optimal detector.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Binary Hypothesis Testing Problem

In detection theory, every binary detection problem reduces to choosing between two competing hypotheses based on an observation vector $\mathbf{x}$:

$$H_0: \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{n} \qquad \text{(noise only)}$$

$$H_1: \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{s} + \mathbf{n} \qquad \text{(signal + noise)}$$

where $\mathbf{s}$ is the deterministic or random signal and $\mathbf{n}$ is the noise vector.

A **decision rule** $\delta(\mathbf{x})$ partitions the observation space $\mathcal{X}$ into two regions:
- **Decision region $R_1$** ŌåÆ choose $H_1$
- **Decision region $R_0$** ŌåÆ choose $H_0$

Two error / performance metrics follow:

$$P_F = P(H_1 \text{ decided} \mid H_0 \text{ true}) = \int_{R_1} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)\, d\mathbf{x}$$

$$P_D = P(H_1 \text{ decided} \mid H_1 \text{ true}) = \int_{R_1} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)\, d\mathbf{x}$$

$$P_M = 1 - P_D = \int_{R_0} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)\, d\mathbf{x}$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Neyman-Pearson Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Idea:** The Neyman-Pearson Theorem is a *constrained optimization* problem solved using the **Calculus of Variations / Lagrange Multiplier method** in the function space of decision rules.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Formulate the optimization problem.**
Maximize $P_D$ subject to the constraint $P_F = \alpha$ (fixed).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Introduce the Lagrange multiplier $\lambda \ge 0$.**
The functional to be maximized becomes:

$$J = P_D - \lambda(P_F - \alpha)$$

$$J = \int_{R_1} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)\, d\mathbf{x} - \lambda \left[\int_{R_1} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)\, d\mathbf{x} - \alpha\right]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Combine the integrals over $R_1$.**

$$J = \int_{R_1} \left[p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1) - \lambda\, p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)\right] d\mathbf{x} + \lambda \alpha$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Choose $R_1$ to maximize $J$.**
To maximize the integral, include in $R_1$ every point where the integrand is positive, exclude every point where it is negative (or zero). The optimal region is:

$$R_1 = \left\{\mathbf{x} : p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1) - \lambda\, p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0) \ge 0\right\}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Rearrange into the likelihood ratio form.**

$$R_1 = \left\{\mathbf{x} : \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)} \ge \lambda \right\}$$

This is the **Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT)**, and the threshold is precisely the Lagrange multiplier $\gamma = \lambda$.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Select $\gamma$ to satisfy the size constraint.**
Solve the integral equation:

$$\int_{\{\mathbf{x}:\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \ge \gamma\}} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)\, d\mathbf{x} = \alpha$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)}$ | Likelihood Ratio | Core test statistic |
| 2 | Decide $H_1$ if $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \ge \gamma$ | NPT decision rule | Board exam staple |
| 3 | $P_F = \displaystyle\int_{R_1} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)\, d\mathbf{x} = \alpha$ | False alarm constraint | Threshold selection |
| 4 | $P_D = \displaystyle\int_{R_1} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)\, d\mathbf{x}$ | Detection probability | Performance measure |
| 5 | $P_M = 1 - P_D$ | Miss probability | Type-II error |
| 6 | $\log \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \ge \log \gamma$ | Log-likelihood form | Monotonic simplification |
| 7 | For i.i.d. samples: $\log\Lambda = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log\dfrac{p(x_i \mid H_1)}{p(x_i \mid H_0)}$ | Sum-form of LRT | AWGN problems |
| 8 | $\gamma = \lambda$ (Lagrange multiplier) | Threshold interpretation | Constrained optimum |
| 9 | $\mathrm{ROC}: P_D$ vs $P_F$ as $\gamma$ varies | Receiver Operating Characteristic | Detector benchmarking |
| 10 | $\mathrm{NEP} = \text{noise equiv. power}$ | Minimum detectable signal at given $P_D, P_F$ | Radar system design |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "design the NP detector," always write (i) the likelihood ratio, (ii) the decision rule, (iii) the threshold equation. This trifecta is worth full marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application | Role of NPT |
|---|---|---|
| **Digital Communications** | BPSK/QPSK demodulation in AWGN | Optimal demodulator maximizes $P_D$ at given BER |
| **Radar** | Target detection in clutter | Constant false-alarm rate (CFAR) receivers extend NPT to non-stationary noise |
| **Biomedical** | ECG arrhythmia detection | Sets specificity ($\alpha$) and maximizes sensitivity ($P_D$) |
| **Machine Learning** | Binary classification with asymmetric costs | LRT underlies logistic regression under equal covariance |
| **Quality Control** | Defect detection in manufacturing | Specified $\alpha$ (Type-I) controls producer's risk |

## 2.5 Relationship with Other Detection Criteria

| Criterion | What is Fixed | What is Optimized | Threshold Determined By |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bayes (Minimum Risk) | Prior probabilities, costs | Risk $r$ | Costs and priors |
| Minimax | No priors known; worst case | Worst-case Bayes risk | Adversarial priors |
| **Neyman-Pearson** | **$P_F = \alpha$** | **$P_D$** | **$\alpha$ alone** |
| Maximum Likelihood | ŌĆö | $p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_i)$ | $0$ (unconstrained) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the priors $P(H_0)$ and $P(H_1)$ are equal and the costs of error are equal, the NP test with $P_F = 0.5$ coincides with the **maximum likelihood** test. This is a frequently tested KTU board fact.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Neyman-Pearson Theorem

**Setup:** We have two simple hypotheses $H_0$ and $H_1$ with known PDFs $p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)$ and $p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)$. We want to find a test that maximizes $P_D$ subject to the constraint $P_F = \alpha$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the decision regions.**

We split the observation space $\mathcal{X}$ into $R_0$ and $R_1$, mutually exclusive and exhaustive. The two performance measures are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_F &= \int_{R_1} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)\, d\mathbf{x} \\
P_D &= \int_{R_1} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)\, d\mathbf{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $P_F$ is the false-alarm probability and $P_D$ is the detection probability.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Write the constrained optimization.**

We need to maximize $P_D$ subject to $P_F = \alpha$. Introduce the non-negative Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$ and form the functional:

$$J = P_D - \lambda(P_F - \alpha)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the integral expressions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \int_{R_1} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)\, d\mathbf{x} - \lambda\left[\int_{R_1} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)\, d\mathbf{x} - \alpha\right] \\
  &= \int_{R_1}\left[p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1) - \lambda\, p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)\right] d\mathbf{x} + \lambda \alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

The term $\lambda \alpha$ is a constant and does not affect the optimization over $R_1$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Choose $R_1$ to maximize the integral.**

For any point $\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X}$, the contribution to the integral is:

$$\Delta J(\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1) - \lambda\, p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)$$

- If $\Delta J(\mathbf{x}) > 0$, including $\mathbf{x}$ in $R_1$ increases $J$ ŌåÆ **include** it in $R_1$.
- If $\Delta J(\mathbf{x}) < 0$, including $\mathbf{x}$ in $R_1$ decreases $J$ ŌåÆ **exclude** it from $R_1$ (i.e., put it in $R_0$).
- If $\Delta J(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, the choice does not affect $J$ ŌåÆ ties can be broken arbitrarily.

**Step 5 ŌĆö State the optimal decision region.**

The optimal decision region $R_1^*$ is:

$$R_1^* = \left\{\mathbf{x} : p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1) - \lambda\, p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0) \ge 0\right\}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Convert to likelihood ratio form.**

Dividing both sides of the inequality by $p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0) > 0$:

$$R_1^* = \left\{\mathbf{x} : \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)} \ge \lambda\right\}$$

Define the likelihood ratio $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)}$ and the threshold $\gamma = \lambda$. The **Neyman-Pearson test** is:

$$\boxed{\delta(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{cases} H_1, & \text{if } \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \ge \gamma \\ H_0, & \text{if } \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) < \gamma \end{cases}}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Choose $\gamma$ to satisfy the constraint.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= P_F = \int_{R_1^*} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)\, d\mathbf{x} \\
       &= \int_{\{\mathbf{x}:\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \ge \gamma\}} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)\, d\mathbf{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a single equation in the unknown $\gamma$. For most textbook problems, this is solved by:
1. Working out the distribution of $\Lambda(\mathbf{x})$ under $H_0$ (often using a monotonic transform to a Gaussian, chi-square, etc.).
2. Inverting the cumulative distribution to find the threshold.

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU 2024 Style): DC Signal in AWGN

**Problem Setup:** Let $x = s + n$ where $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, $s = A > 0$ under $H_1$, and $s = 0$ under $H_0$. Find the NP detector at $P_F = \alpha$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the conditional PDFs.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x \mid H_0) &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \\
p(x \mid H_1) &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-A)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Form the likelihood ratio.**

$$\Lambda(x) = \frac{p(x \mid H_1)}{p(x \mid H_0)} = \exp\!\left(\frac{2Ax - A^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply monotonicity (exponential is monotone increasing).**

$$\Lambda(x) \ge \gamma \iff \frac{2Ax - A^2}{2\sigma^2} \ge \ln \gamma \iff x \ge \frac{\sigma^2 \ln \gamma}{A} + \frac{A}{2}$$

Define the threshold $x_T = \dfrac{\sigma^2 \ln \gamma}{A} + \dfrac{A}{2}$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Select $x_T$ using the $P_F$ constraint.**

$$P_F = P(x \ge x_T \mid H_0) = Q\!\left(\frac{x_T}{\sigma}\right) = \alpha$$

where $Q(\cdot)$ is the Gaussian tail function. Solving:

$$x_T = \sigma\, Q^{-1}(\alpha)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute $P_D$.**

$$P_D = P(x \ge x_T \mid H_1) = Q\!\left(\frac{x_T - A}{\sigma}\right) = Q\!\left(Q^{-1}(\alpha) - \frac{A}{\sigma}\right)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final decision rule.**

$$\boxed{\text{Decide } H_1 \text{ if } x \ge \sigma\, Q^{-1}(\alpha); \quad \text{otherwise decide } H_0.}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The detector reduces to a **single-sample threshold test on the observation itself** ŌĆö a beautifully simple structure that the NP theorem guarantees to be optimal.

## 3.3 Proof of the "Most Powerful" Property

To show that no other test can achieve a higher $P_D$ for the same $P_F$, consider any competing test with decision region $\tilde R_1$ giving $P_F' = \alpha$ and $P_D'$. We must show $P_D' \le P_D$.

By the construction above, $\Delta J(\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_1) - \lambda\, p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_0)$, and at the optimum $R_1^*$, $\Delta J(\mathbf{x}) \ge 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in R_1^*$ and $\Delta J(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in R_0^*$. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_D - P_D' - \lambda(P_F - P_F') &= \int_{R_1^*} p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_1)\, d\mathbf{x} - \int_{\tilde R_1} p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_1)\, d\mathbf{x} \\
&\quad - \lambda\left[\int_{R_1^*} p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_0)\, d\mathbf{x} - \int_{\tilde R_1} p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_0)\, d\mathbf{x}\right] \\
&= \int_{R_1^* \setminus \tilde R_1} \Delta J(\mathbf{x})\, d\mathbf{x} - \int_{\tilde R_1 \setminus R_1^*} \Delta J(\mathbf{x})\, d\mathbf{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

The first integral is non-negative (its integrand $\ge 0$); the second is also non-negative (subtracting an integral whose integrand $\le 0$). So:

$$P_D - P_D' - \lambda(P_F - P_F') \ge 0$$

With $P_F = P_F' = \alpha$, we obtain $P_D \ge P_D'$. Equality holds if and only if the two tests coincide almost everywhere.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This proves **uniqueness (a.e.)** of the most powerful test, which is why the NPT is the *unique* optimal solution to the constrained problem.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Distributions and Their NPT Structure

| Observation PDF $p(x \mid H_0)$ vs $p(x \mid H_1)$ | Likelihood Ratio $\Lambda(x)$ | Test Statistic $T(x)$ | Threshold Depends On |
|---|---|---|---|
| Both Gaussian, equal variance | Linear in $x$ | $x$ | $\sigma Q^{-1}(\alpha)$ |
| Both Gaussian, unequal variance | Quadratic in $x$ | $x^2$ (if same mean) | Chi-square tail |
| Exponential, different rates | $e^{(\lambda_0-\lambda_1)x}$ | $x$ | Exponential quantile |
| Poisson, different means | $e^{(\lambda_0-\lambda_1)}\,(\lambda_1/\lambda_0)^x$ | $x$ | Poisson tail |
| Bernoulli, different $p$ | $(p_1/p_0)^x\,((1-p_1)/(1-p_0))^{1-x}$ | $x$ (sum of bits) | Binomial tail |

## 3.5 Extension to $N$ IID Observations

For $N$ i.i.d. samples, the joint PDF factorizes:

$$p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_i) = \prod_{k=1}^{N} p(x_k \mid H_i)$$

The log-likelihood ratio becomes an additive sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) &= \sum_{k=1}^{N} \ln \frac{p(x_k \mid H_1)}{p(x_k \mid H_0)} \\
&= \sum_{k=1}^{N} \ln \Lambda(x_k)
\end{aligned}
$$

By the **Central Limit Theorem** (for large $N$), $\ln\Lambda(\mathbf{x})$ becomes approximately Gaussian under both hypotheses, leading to tractable closed-form expressions for $P_D$ and $P_F$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an NPT-Based Detector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Observation Vector x] --> node2[Compute p of x given H1]
    node1 --> node3[Compute p of x given H0]
    node2 --> node4[Likelihood Ratio Lambda of x]
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5{Compare Lambda of x with Threshold gamma}
    node5 -- Greater or Equal --> node6[Decide H1: Signal Detected]
    node5 -- Less Than --> node7[Decide H0: Noise Only]
    node8[Designer Sets False Alarm Level alpha] --> node9[Threshold Solver Equation]
    node9 --> node10[Threshold gamma from size constraint]
    node10 --> node5
    node6 --> node11[Compute Detection Probability PD]
    node7 --> node12[Compute Miss Probability PM]
    node11 --> node13[ROC Curve: PD vs PF]
    node12 --> node13
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow Schematic for a Single Observation (Gaussian Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    obs1[Received Sample x] --> obs2[Compute Lambda of x]
    obs2 --> obs3[Take Natural Log of Lambda]
    obs3 --> obs4{Is x Greater Than or Equal to xT}
    obs4 -- Yes --> obs5[Output: H1 Decision]
    obs4 -- No --> obs6[Output: H0 Decision]
    par1[Preset alpha] --> thr1[Threshold Solver Q inverse]
    thr1 --> obs4
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Mapping: NP Detector Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cmp1[Probability Models p of x given H0 and p of x given H1] --> cmp2[Likelihood Ratio Computer]
    cmp2 --> cmp3[Comparator with Threshold gamma]
    par1[False Alarm Level alpha from System Specs] --> par2[Threshold Equation Solver]
    par2 --> cmp3
    cmp3 --> out1[Binary Decision H0 or H1]
    out1 --> out2[Performance Metrics PD and PF]
```

## 4.4 ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) Curve Annotation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rc1[Threshold gamma Decreases] --> rc2[Likelihood Region R1 Expands]
    rc2 --> rc3[PF Increases]
    rc2 --> rc4[PD Increases]
    rc3 --> rc5[ROC Curve: PD Vertical vs PF Horizontal]
    rc4 --> rc5
    rc5 --> rc6[Area Under Curve AUC Measures Detector Quality]
    rc5 --> rc7[Diagonal Line: Random Guess Baseline]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Style]**
**Q:** State the Neyman-Pearson Theorem. Define the likelihood ratio test and explain the role of the threshold $\gamma$.

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> **Neyman-Pearson Theorem Statement:** Among all tests with false-alarm probability $P_F \le \alpha$, the test that decides $H_1$ if the likelihood ratio $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)/p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)$ exceeds a threshold $\gamma$ and $H_0$ otherwise maximizes the probability of detection $P_D$.
>
> **Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT):** A binary hypothesis test in which the decision is based on comparing the ratio of conditional PDFs to a threshold.
>
> **Role of threshold $\gamma$:** $\gamma$ is the **Lagrange multiplier** that enforces the size constraint $P_F = \alpha$. It is uniquely determined (for continuous PDFs) by solving the equation $P(\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \ge \gamma \mid H_0) = \alpha$. *(3 marks)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Style]**
**Q:** Differentiate between Bayes criterion and Neyman-Pearson criterion. Under what condition does the NP test reduce to the maximum likelihood test?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bayes | Neyman-Pearson |
|---|---|---|
| Priors | Required | Not required |
| Cost of error | Required | Not required |
| Optimization | Minimize risk | Maximize $P_D$ subject to $P_F = \alpha$ |
| Threshold | Depends on costs and priors | Depends only on $\alpha$ |

**Condition for reduction to ML test:** When $P(H_0) = P(H_1)$ (equal priors) and the costs of both types of errors are equal, the Bayes risk is minimized when $P_F + P_M$ is minimized, which is equivalent to setting $\gamma = 1$ in the NP test. This is exactly the maximum likelihood rule. *(3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper Style]**
**Q:** (a) **Derive the Neyman-Pearson Theorem** using the method of Lagrange multipliers. Clearly state the assumptions and the final decision rule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the hypothesis testing problem:
$H_0: x = n$, $\quad H_1: x = 1 + n$, where $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$.
Design the Neyman-Pearson detector for $P_F = 0.1$. Compute the corresponding $P_D$. Take $Q^{-1}(0.1) = 1.2816$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A(a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define performance metrics.** With decision regions $R_0$ and $R_1$:

$$P_F = \int_{R_1} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)\, d\mathbf{x}, \quad P_D = \int_{R_1} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)\, d\mathbf{x}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct the Lagrangian.**

$$J = P_D - \lambda(P_F - \alpha) = \int_{R_1} [p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1) - \lambda\, p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)]\, d\mathbf{x} + \lambda \alpha$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Choose $R_1$ pointwise to maximize $J$.** The integrand is maximized when the bracketed quantity is non-negative:

$$p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1) - \lambda\, p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0) \ge 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rearrange to get the LRT.**

$$R_1^* = \left\{\mathbf{x} : \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)} \ge \lambda\right\}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Decision rule.**

$$\text{Decide } H_1 \text{ if } \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \ge \gamma, \text{ where } \gamma = \lambda \text{ solves } P_F = \alpha.$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Optimality proof sketch.** For any competing test with $P_F' = \alpha$ and decision region $\tilde R_1$:

$$P_D - P_D' = \int_{R_1^* \setminus \tilde R_1} \Delta J(\mathbf{x})\, d\mathbf{x} - \int_{\tilde R_1 \setminus R_1^*} \Delta J(\mathbf{x})\, d\mathbf{x} \ge 0$$

Hence $P_D \ge P_D'$, establishing the "most powerful" property. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question A(b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Conditional PDFs.**

$$p(x \mid H_0) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-x^2/2}, \quad p(x \mid H_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-1)^2/2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Likelihood ratio.**

$$\Lambda(x) = e^{(2x - 1)/2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Log-LRT.** Since $\ln(\cdot)$ is monotone:

$$\Lambda(x) \ge \gamma \iff x \ge \frac{2\ln\gamma + 1}{2} = x_T$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Threshold from $P_F$ constraint.**

$$P_F = P(x \ge x_T \mid H_0) = Q(x_T) = 0.1 \implies x_T = Q^{-1}(0.1) = 1.2816$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Detector output.**

$$\text{Decide } H_1 \text{ if } x \ge 1.2816, \text{ else decide } H_0.$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute $P_D$.**

$$P_D = P(x \ge 1.2816 \mid H_1) = Q(x_T - 1) = Q(0.2816) \approx 0.3892$$

**Final Answer:** $P_D \approx 0.3892$, i.e., about 38.92% detection probability. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper Style]**
**Q:** (a) Explain the concept of the **likelihood ratio test (LRT)**. Show that the LRT is a monotone test, and discuss the relationship between NPT and the Bayes criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a binary detection problem with PDFs:
$p(x \mid H_0) = \frac{1}{2} e^{-x}$ for $x \ge 0$, and $p(x \mid H_1) = e^{-2x}$ for $x \ge 0$,
design the NP test for $P_F = 0.05$. Compute the threshold and the resulting $P_D$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B(a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö LRT Definition.** The likelihood ratio test compares $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)/p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)$ to a threshold $\gamma$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Monotonicity.** Since the LRT depends only on $\Lambda(\mathbf{x})$, and any monotone increasing function of $\Lambda$ is an equivalent test statistic, the LRT is a **monotone test**. The set $\{\mathbf{x}: \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \ge \gamma\}$ defines a level set of the test statistic. *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 ŌĆö NPTŌĆōBayes Relationship.** Under the Bayes criterion with priors $P(H_0), P(H_1)$ and costs $C_{ij}$:

$$\text{Decide } H_1 \text{ if } \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \ge \frac{P(H_0)(C_{10} - C_{00})}{P(H_1)(C_{01} - C_{11})}$$

This is the **same form** as the NPT with the Lagrange multiplier $\gamma$ replaced by a specific cost-prior ratio. Hence the NPT with arbitrary $\gamma$ is the most general member of the Bayes family obtained by sweeping the cost-prior ratio. *(3 marks)*

**Step 4 ŌĆö Equivalence Conditions.** NP and Bayes coincide when $P(H_0)/P(H_1) = \gamma \cdot (C_{01} - C_{11})/(C_{10} - C_{00})$ for some specific $\alpha$. *(2 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question B(b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Likelihood ratio.**

$$\Lambda(x) = \frac{e^{-2x}}{(1/2)e^{-x}} = 2 e^{-x}, \quad x \ge 0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Monotone test.** $\Lambda(x)$ is a decreasing function of $x$, so:

$$\Lambda(x) \ge \gamma \iff e^{-x} \ge \gamma/2 \iff x \le -\ln(\gamma/2) = x_T$$

The decision region for $H_1$ is $R_1 = \{0 \le x \le x_T\}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Threshold from $P_F$.**

$$P_F = \int_0^{x_T} \frac{1}{2} e^{-x}\, dx = \frac{1}{2}(1 - e^{-x_T}) = 0.05$$

Solving: $1 - e^{-x_T} = 0.1 \implies e^{-x_T} = 0.9 \implies x_T = \ln(10/9) \approx 0.1054$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $P_D$.**

$$P_D = \int_0^{x_T} 2 e^{-2x}\, dx = 1 - e^{-2x_T} = 1 - (0.9)^2 = 0.19$$

**Final Answer:** $x_T \approx 0.1054$, $P_D = 0.19$ (19%). *(7 marks)*

---

### Question C (14 Marks) ŌĆö Additional Practice

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Sample Pattern]**
**Q:** (a) Discuss the **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** curve. How is it related to the NP test? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two equiprobable hypotheses are:
$H_0: x \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$, $H_1: x \sim \mathcal{N}(1, 1)$.
Find the minimum probability of error detector and comment on its equivalence with the NP test at $\alpha = 0.5$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline for Question C:**

- **(a) ROC:** A plot of $P_D$ vs $P_F$ as the threshold $\gamma$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$. Each $\gamma$ gives one point; the curve traces the **best achievable** $(P_F, P_D)$ pairs under the NP criterion. The area under the curve quantifies detector quality.
- **(b)** With equal priors and equal error costs, the minimum $P_e$ test decides $H_1$ if $p(x \mid H_1) > p(x \mid H_0)$, i.e., $x > 0.5$. This corresponds to NP with $\alpha = 0.5$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the size constraint:** Many students write $\Lambda \ge \gamma$ and stop. *Always* explicitly state that $\gamma$ is chosen so that $P_F = \alpha$ ŌĆö examiners allocate 2ŌĆō3 marks specifically for this.
> 2. **Sign errors in the Gaussian threshold:** The formula is $x_T = \sigma Q^{-1}(\alpha)$ only for the right-tailed test. For the left tail, it is $x_T = -\sigma Q^{-1}(\alpha)$. Mixing these up costs full marks.
> 3. **Confusing $P_M$ and $P_F$:** $P_M = 1 - P_D$, not $1 - P_F$. State this distinction clearly.
> 4. **Not justifying the likelihood ratio form:** When asked to "design the NP detector," many students skip the derivation of $\Lambda(x)$ from the given PDFs. Always show the substitution.
> 5. **Missing the monotonicity argument:** A common KTU question is "show the LRT is monotone." Mention that any monotone transform of $\Lambda$ is an equivalent test statistic.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Neyman-Pearson Theorem**

- [x] **NPT** is the **most powerful test** at a fixed false-alarm rate $P_F = \alpha$.
- [x] The test statistic is the **likelihood ratio** $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)/p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)$.
- [x] The **decision rule** is: decide $H_1$ if $\Lambda \ge \gamma$, else $H_0$.
- [x] The threshold $\gamma$ is the **Lagrange multiplier** of the constrained optimization.
- [x] $\gamma$ is determined *uniquely* by the integral equation $P_F = \alpha$.
- [x] The derivation uses the **Lagrange multiplier method** on the decision region $R_1$.
- [x] For i.i.d. observations, use the **sum of log-likelihood ratios** as the test statistic.
- [x] The **monotone property** allows the use of any monotone transform of $\Lambda$.
- [x] The **ROC curve** plots $P_D$ vs $P_F$ as $\gamma$ varies.
- [x] **Equivalence with Bayes:** NP with $\gamma$ = cost-prior ratio of the Bayes criterion.
- [x] **Equivalence with ML:** NP with $P_F = 0.5$ and equal priors equals the maximum likelihood test.
- [x] **AWGN with known signal:** Detector reduces to comparing the observation $x$ with a threshold $\sigma Q^{-1}(\alpha)$.
- [x] $P_M = 1 - P_D$ (do not confuse with $P_F$).
- [x] **Practical extensions:** CFAR, generalized likelihood ratio, Wald test, Rao test.
- [x] **No priors needed** ŌĆö this is NPT's main practical advantage over Bayes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT)** is a statistical hypothesis-testing procedure used in detection theory to decide between two competing hypotheses, denoted as $H_0$ (the null hypothesis ŌĆö signal absent / noise only) and $H_1$ (the alternative hypothesis ŌĆö signal present in noise). It is the cornerstone of the **Neyman-Pearson** decision framework, providing the most powerful test for a fixed probability of false alarm.

Mathematically, the LRT is defined by forming the **likelihood ratio** $\Lambda(\mathbf{x})$ between the conditional probability density functions of the observation vector $\mathbf{x}$ under both hypotheses:

$$
\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)}
$$

The decision rule is then:

$$
\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}} \eta
$$

where $\eta$ is the **decision threshold** chosen to satisfy a prescribed false-alarm probability $P_{FA} = \alpha$, or a minimum cost criterion.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEECT527 - Module 3):** The Likelihood Ratio Test is the fundamental decision rule that unifies all other detection strategies (Bayesian, Neyman-Pearson, Min-Max). Every detector ŌĆö coherent, non-coherent, matched filter, energy detector ŌĆö ultimately reduces to comparing a likelihood ratio to a threshold.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Real-World Analogy: A Smoke Detector Decision**

Imagine you are a smoke detector installed in a kitchen. You observe a reading $x$ (smoke density) and must decide: is there actual fire ($H_1$) or is it just steam from cooking ($H_0$)?

1. **Memory of past events:** You know two probability distributions:
   - $p(x \mid H_0)$ ŌĆö readings when it's *just steam* (typically low values).
   - $p(x \mid H_1)$ ŌĆö readings when *real fire* is present (typically high values).

2. **Forming a judgment (the ratio):** For the current reading $x$, you ask: *"How many times more likely is this reading to come from a fire than from steam?"* ŌĆö this is precisely $\Lambda(x) = \frac{p(x \mid H_1)}{p(x \mid H_0)}$.

3. **Decision rule:** If the ratio is **large** (reading is *much more typical* of fire than steam), you ring the alarm ($H_1$). If it is **small** (reading is more typical of steam), you stay silent ($H_0$).

4. **Tuning the threshold $\eta$:**
   - Lower $\eta$ ŌåÆ ring the alarm more easily ŌåÆ fewer missed fires, but more false alarms (toast burning, heavy steam).
   - Higher $\eta$ ŌåÆ alarm only for unmistakable fire ŌåÆ fewer false alarms, but more missed fires (slow smoldering).

This trade-off is at the very heart of **signal detection theory**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The likelihood ratio is a *measure of evidence* in favor of $H_1$. The threshold $\eta$ is the *decision bias*. Together, they completely specify a binary detector. No other test can be uniformly better (Neyman-Pearson Lemma).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Detection Theory

The following quantities are universally used in KTU 2024 Scheme evaluations:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range / Units |
|---|---|---|
| $P_{FA}$ | Probability of False Alarm | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-2}$ (dimensionless) |
| $P_D$ | Probability of Detection | $0$ to $1$ (dimensionless) |
| $P_M$ | Probability of Miss | $1 - P_D$ |
| $\eta$ | Decision Threshold | Real positive number |
| $\gamma_d$ | Detection SNR threshold (dB) | depends on $P_{FA}$ and $P_D$ |
| $\sigma^2$ | Noise Variance | $W/\text{Hz}$ or $V^2$ |
| $E_s / N_0$ | Symbol Energy to Noise Spectral Density | dB |
| $k$ | Boltzmann's constant | $1.380 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board examinations, always quote $P_{FA} + P_D + P_M = 1 + P_{FA}$ is **false**; the correct identity is $P_D + P_M = 1$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Likelihood Ratio Test

The LRT procedure follows a **five-step algorithmic structure**:

1. **Hypothesis Formulation:** Define the observation model.
   - Under $H_0$: $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{n}$ (noise only)
   - Under $H_1$: $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{s} + \mathbf{n}$ (signal + noise)

2. **Likelihood Function Construction:** Determine the joint PDF $p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_i)$ for $i = 0, 1$, using the assumed noise/signal statistics (typically Gaussian for KTU problems).

3. **Likelihood Ratio Formation:** Compute
   $$
   \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)}
   $$

4. **Log-Likelihood Simplification (monotonic transformation):** Since $\ln(\cdot)$ is monotonically increasing, the test $\Lambda \gtrless \eta$ is equivalent to
   $$
   \ln \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}} \ln \eta
   $$
   This linearises products (from joint PDFs) into sums ŌĆö critical for analytical tractability.

5. **Threshold Setting:** Choose $\eta$ using one of three KTU-prescribed criteria:
   - **Bayesian Minimum Risk** (if prior probabilities $P_0, P_1$ and costs $C_{ij}$ are known)
   - **Neyman-Pearson** (constraint on $P_{FA} = \alpha$)
   - **Min-Max** (worst-case over priors)

---

## 2.2 The Three Threshold Selection Rules

### 2.2.1 Bayesian Minimum Risk Threshold

$$
\eta_{\text{Bayes}} = \frac{P_0 \,(C_{10} - C_{00})}{P_1 \,(C_{01} - C_{11})}
$$

where $C_{ij}$ is the cost of deciding $H_i$ when $H_j$ is true.

### 2.2.2 Neyman-Pearson Threshold (Minimum Cost Subject to Constraint)

Choose $\eta$ such that
$$
P_{FA} = \int_{\{\mathbf{x}: \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) > \eta\}} p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0) \, d\mathbf{x} = \alpha
$$

This requires solving
$$
\eta = F_{\Lambda \mid H_0}^{-1}(1 - \alpha)
$$

### 2.2.3 Min-Max Threshold

For a *least favourable prior* $P_1^*$ that maximises the Bayes risk, the threshold is the same as Bayesian but with $P_1^*$ substituted.

---

## 2.3 Performance Metrics

The performance of any LRT is characterised by two error probabilities:

**Probability of False Alarm** ($\alpha$):
$$
P_{FA} = P(\text{decide } H_1 \mid H_0) = \int_{\eta}^{\infty} p(\Lambda \mid H_0) \, d\Lambda
$$

**Probability of Detection** ($P_D$):
$$
P_D = P(\text{decide } H_1 \mid H_1) = \int_{\eta}^{\infty} p(\Lambda \mid H_1) \, d\Lambda
$$

**Probability of Miss** ($P_M$):
$$
P_M = 1 - P_D
$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x}\vert H_0)}$ | Likelihood ratio definition | Core test statistic |
| 2 | $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \gtrless \eta$ | Decision rule | Binary hypothesis test |
| 3 | $\ln\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \gtrless \ln\eta$ | Log-LRT | Simplifies products to sums |
| 4 | $T(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{s}$ | Sufficient statistic (AWGN) | Reduces $\mathbf{x}$ to scalar |
| 5 | $P_{FA} = Q\!\left(\frac{\eta - \mu_{T\vert H_0}}{\sigma_{T\vert H_0}}\right)$ | False alarm prob. (Gaussian stat.) | Threshold calculation |
| 6 | $P_D = Q\!\left(\frac{\eta - \mu_{T\vert H_1}}{\sigma_{T\vert H_1}}\right)$ | Detection prob. (Gaussian stat.) | ROC construction |
| 7 | $\eta = \sigma_n \sqrt{2\ln(1/\alpha)}$ | Threshold for energy detector | $P_{FA} = \alpha$ constraint |
| 8 | $P_D = Q\!\left(Q^{-1}(\alpha) - \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\right)$ | Detection prob. (coherent BPSK) | $E_s/N_0$ link |
| 9 | $Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_x^{\infty} e^{-t^2/2}dt$ | Gaussian Q-function | Tail probability |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{Bayes}} = \frac{P_0(C_{10}-C_{00})}{P_1(C_{01}-C_{11})}$ | Bayesian threshold | Minimum risk design |
| 11 | Neyman-Pearson: $P_{FA} = \alpha$ | Constrained optimisation | Most powerful test |
| 12 | $P_e = P_0 P_{FA} + P_1 P_M$ | Total error prob. | Binary symmetric case |
| 13 | $d^2 = \frac{(\mu_1 - \mu_0)^2}{\sigma^2}$ | Deflection coefficient | SNR of test statistic |
| 14 | ROC: $P_D = f(P_{FA}, \text{SNR})$ | Receiver Operating Characteristic | Plot for detector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The vertical bar $\vert$ in $p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)$ is rendered as `\vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown tables. Use $P(x \mid H_0)$ form consistently.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The LRT framework underpins virtually every modern engineered detection system:

- **Radar & Sonar:** Detecting aircraft, ships, submarines in noise.
- **Digital Communications:** Decoding BPSK/QPSK/16-QAM symbols transmitted through AWGN channels.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** ECG arrhythmia detection, MRI tumour segmentation.
- **Machine Learning:** The **logistic regression classifier** is essentially an LRT with a sigmoid threshold.
- **Speech & Speaker Recognition:** Voice activity detection (VAD), keyword spotting.
- **Cognitive Radio:** Spectrum sensing for licensed-user detection.

In each case, the designer:
1. Models noise statistics.
2. Defines a signal model.
3. Computes the likelihood ratio.
4. Sets a threshold based on cost, false-alarm tolerance, or Bayesian priors.

> [!TIP]
> The LRT is the **optimum detector** under the Neyman-Pearson criterion ŌĆö no other test can achieve a higher $P_D$ for the same $P_{FA}$. This optimality is the *theoretical foundation* of detection engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: LRT for AWGN Channel with Known Signal

### Problem Setup

Consider the binary hypothesis testing problem in **AWGN (Additive White Gaussian Noise)** with a known deterministic signal $s$ of energy $E_s$:

- $H_0: x[n] = w[n], \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$
- $H_1: x[n] = s[n] + w[n], \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$

where $w[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ are i.i.d. samples.

### Step 1: Write the joint likelihood under $H_0$

Since samples are i.i.d. Gaussian with mean $0$ and variance $\sigma^2$:

$$
p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0) = \prod_{n=0}^{N-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2[n]}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{(2\pi\sigma^2)^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^2[n]\right)
$$

### Step 2: Write the joint likelihood under $H_1$

Under $H_1$, the mean shifts to $s[n]$, so $x[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(s[n], \sigma^2)$:

$$
p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1) = \frac{1}{(2\pi\sigma^2)^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} (x[n] - s[n])^2\right)
$$

### Step 3: Form the likelihood ratio

$$
\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)}
$$

$$
= \frac{\dfrac{1}{(2\pi\sigma^2)^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} (x[n] - s[n])^2\right)}{\dfrac{1}{(2\pi\sigma^2)^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^2[n]\right)}
$$

### Step 4: Cancel the common prefactor

The $(2\pi\sigma^2)^{-N/2}$ terms cancel:

$$
\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum (x[n] - s[n])^2\right)}{\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum x^2[n]\right)}
$$

### Step 5: Take logarithm (monotonic transformation)

$$
\ln \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} (x[n] - s[n])^2 + \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^2[n]
$$

### Step 6: Expand the quadratic term

$$
(x[n] - s[n])^2 = x^2[n] - 2 x[n] s[n] + s^2[n]
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sum (x[n] - s[n])^2 = \sum x^2[n] - 2 \sum x[n] s[n] + \sum s^2[n]
$$

### Step 7: Substitute back

$$
\ln \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[\sum x^2[n] - 2 \sum x[n] s[n] + \sum s^2[n]\right] + \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum x^2[n]
$$

### Step 8: Cancel the $\sum x^2[n]$ terms

$$
\ln \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[- 2 \sum x[n] s[n] + \sum s^2[n]\right]
$$

### Step 9: Simplify the expression

$$
\ln \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] s[n] - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s^2[n]
$$

### Step 10: Identify the signal energy

The total signal energy is $E_s = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s^2[n]$. So:

$$
\ln \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] s[n] - \frac{E_s}{2\sigma^2}
$$

### Step 11: Apply the LRT decision rule

The test $\ln \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \gtrless \ln \eta$ becomes:

$$
\frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] s[n] - \frac{E_s}{2\sigma^2} \gtrless \ln \eta
$$

### Step 12: Rearrange to isolate the test statistic

Multiplying by $\sigma^2$ (positive, preserves inequality):

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] s[n] \gtrless \sigma^2 \ln \eta + \frac{E_s}{2}
$$

### Step 13: Define the **Matched Filter** output

Let $T(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] s[n] = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{s}$ ŌĆö this is the **sufficient statistic** (correlation detector / matched filter output):

$$
T(\mathbf{x}) \underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}} \gamma
$$

where the **modified threshold** is

$$
\gamma = \sigma^2 \ln \eta + \frac{E_s}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result (Derivation 1):** The LRT for AWGN with known signal reduces to a **correlation receiver** (or equivalently, a matched filter followed by a threshold). The LRT is implementable as a single inner product $T = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{s}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Threshold from False-Alarm Probability

### Problem

Given the test statistic $T = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{s}$ from Derivation 1, find the threshold $\gamma$ such that $P_{FA} = \alpha$ (Neyman-Pearson).

### Step 1: Find the distribution of $T$ under $H_0$

Under $H_0$, $x[n] = w[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, so

$$
T \mid H_0 = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} w[n] s[n]
$$

is a weighted sum of independent Gaussians $\Rightarrow$ Gaussian. Compute the mean:

$$
\mu_{T \mid H_0} = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum w[n] s[n]\right] = \sum s[n] \cdot \mathbb{E}[w[n]] = 0
$$

Compute the variance:

$$
\sigma_{T \mid H_0}^2 = \mathrm{Var}\!\left(\sum w[n] s[n]\right) = \sum s^2[n] \mathrm{Var}(w[n]) = \sigma^2 \sum s^2[n] = \sigma^2 E_s
$$

Thus $T \mid H_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 E_s)$.

### Step 2: Write the false-alarm probability

$$
P_{FA} = P(T > \gamma \mid H_0) = \int_{\gamma}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^2 E_s}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{t^2}{2 \sigma^2 E_s}\right) dt
$$

### Step 3: Convert to standard Gaussian form

Let $u = \frac{t}{\sigma\sqrt{E_s}}$, $du = \frac{dt}{\sigma\sqrt{E_s}}$:

$$
P_{FA} = \int_{\gamma/(\sigma\sqrt{E_s})}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2}\right) du = Q\!\left(\frac{\gamma}{\sigma\sqrt{E_s}}\right)
$$

### Step 4: Invert the Q-function to find $\gamma$

Given $P_{FA} = \alpha$:

$$
Q\!\left(\frac{\gamma}{\sigma\sqrt{E_s}}\right) = \alpha \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{\gamma}{\sigma\sqrt{E_s}} = Q^{-1}(\alpha)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\gamma = \sigma \sqrt{E_s} \, Q^{-1}(\alpha)}
$$

### Step 5: Compute $P_D$ under $H_1$

Under $H_1$, $x[n] = s[n] + w[n]$, so

$$
T \mid H_1 = \sum s^2[n] + \sum w[n] s[n]
$$

Mean: $\mu_{T \mid H_1} = E_s$
Variance: $\sigma_{T \mid H_1}^2 = \sigma^2 E_s$ (same as $H_0$, since noise variance unchanged)

$$
P_D = P(T > \gamma \mid H_1) = Q\!\left(\frac{\gamma - E_s}{\sigma\sqrt{E_s}}\right)
$$

### Step 6: Substitute $\gamma$ from Step 4

$$
P_D = Q\!\left(\frac{\sigma\sqrt{E_s} Q^{-1}(\alpha) - E_s}{\sigma\sqrt{E_s}}\right)
$$

$$
= Q\!\left(Q^{-1}(\alpha) - \frac{E_s}{\sigma\sqrt{E_s}}\right)
$$

### Step 7: Express in terms of input SNR per sample

Define per-sample SNR as $\text{SNR} = \frac{E_s}{N\sigma^2}$ (with $N$ samples and noise spectral density $N_0/2 = \sigma^2$):

$$
P_D = Q\!\left(Q^{-1}(\alpha) - \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\right)
$$

This is the famous **Albersheim equation** for the AWGN detector with known signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** Steps 4 and 7 are the most commonly tested results. Memorise $P_{FA} = Q(\gamma / \sigma\sqrt{E_s})$ and $P_D = Q(Q^{-1}(\alpha) - \sqrt{2E_s/N_0})$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Neyman-Pearson Lemma (Statement + Proof Sketch)

### Statement (Neyman-Pearson Lemma)

Let $H_0$ and $H_1$ be simple hypotheses with PDFs $p_0(\mathbf{x})$ and $p_1(\mathbf{x})$. For any test with false-alarm probability $P_{FA} = \alpha$, the LRT ŌĆö which rejects $H_0$ when $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) > \eta$ (with $\eta$ chosen so that $P_{FA} = \alpha$) ŌĆö is the **most powerful test** at level $\alpha$. That is, it maximises $P_D$ for the given $\alpha$.

### Proof Sketch (Lagrange Multipliers)

We want to maximise $P_D$ subject to $P_{FA} = \alpha$. Define the Lagrangian:

$$
\mathcal{L} = P_D - \lambda (P_{FA} - \alpha)
$$

Substitute the integral forms:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \int_{\mathcal{R}_1} p_1(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x} - \lambda \int_{\mathcal{R}_1} p_0(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x} + \lambda \alpha
$$

$$
= \int_{\mathcal{R}_1} [p_1(\mathbf{x}) - \lambda p_0(\mathbf{x})] d\mathbf{x} + \lambda \alpha
$$

To maximise $\mathcal{L}$, we choose the decision region $\mathcal{R}_1$ to include all $\mathbf{x}$ for which the integrand is positive:

$$
\mathcal{R}_1 = \{\mathbf{x} : p_1(\mathbf{x}) - \lambda p_0(\mathbf{x}) > 0\}
$$

$$
= \left\{\mathbf{x} : \frac{p_1(\mathbf{x})}{p_0(\mathbf{x})} > \lambda\right\} = \{\mathbf{x} : \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) > \eta\}
$$

with $\eta = \lambda$. The threshold $\lambda$ is selected so that the constraint $P_{FA} = \alpha$ is satisfied.

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** The Neyman-Pearson Lemma guarantees that *no other deterministic test* can outperform the LRT for a given $P_{FA}$. This makes the LRT the *gold standard* in detection engineering.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Bayesian LRT | Neyman-Pearson LRT | Min-Max LRT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Prior knowledge required** | $P_0, P_1$ | None (only $P_{FA}$ constraint) | Only set of priors |
| **Cost information** | $C_{ij}$ required | Not used | Not used |
| **Optimisation criterion** | Minimise Bayes risk | Maximise $P_D$ for given $P_{FA}$ | Minimise worst-case Bayes risk |
| **Threshold rule** | $\eta = \frac{P_0(C_{10}-C_{00})}{P_1(C_{01}-C_{11})}$ | $P_{FA} = \alpha$ | $\eta = \frac{P_0^*}{1-P_0^*}\cdot\text{cost ratio}$ |
| **When preferred** | Costs and priors known | Only $P_{FA}$ is critical | Priors uncertain |
| **KTU 2024 weight** | High (often in numericals) | Highest (most exam questions) | Moderate |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Likelihood Ratio Test Receiver

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete signal flow of an LRT-based detector, from observation vector to binary decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Observation Vector x"] --> B["Hypothesised Signal Model s"]
    A --> C["Noise PDF p(x|H0)"]
    B --> D["Joint PDF p(x|H1)"]
    C --> E["Likelihood Ratio Lambda x"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Logarithmic Stage ln Lambda"]
    F --> G["Comparator with Threshold ln eta"]
    G --> H{"Decision"}
    H -- "Greater" --> I["DECIDE H1 Signal Present"]
    H -- "Less" --> J["DECIDE H0 Signal Absent"]
    K["Threshold Generator from PFA alpha"] --> G
    L["Prior P0 P1 and Costs Cij"] --> M["Bayesian Threshold Calculator"]
    M --> K
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **observation vector** $\mathbf{x}$ is fed into two parallel PDF estimators ŌĆö one under $H_0$ (noise only) and one under $H_1$ (signal-plus-noise).
- The two PDFs form the **likelihood ratio** $\Lambda(\mathbf{x})$, which passes through a logarithm (linearising step) to produce $\ln \Lambda(\mathbf{x})$.
- The comparator checks this against the **log-threshold** $\ln \eta$.
- The threshold is derived either from a **Neyman-Pearson constraint** (using $P_{FA} = \alpha$) or from **Bayesian cost/prior information**.
- The comparator output drives the final **binary decision**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Regions in a 2-D Observation Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S0["Region R0 - Decide H0"]
        S0A["p(x|H0) dominant"]
    end
    subgraph S1["Region R1 - Decide H1"]
        S1A["p(x|H1) dominant"]
    end
    B["Decision Boundary: Lambda x = eta"] --> S0
    B --> S1
    O["Observation x"] --> B
    S0A --> O
    S1A --> O
```

The decision boundary $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \eta$ partitions the observation space into two regions:

- $\mathcal{R}_0$: where $\Lambda < \eta$ ŌåÆ we decide $H_0$ (no signal).
- $\mathcal{R}_1$: where $\Lambda > \eta$ ŌåÆ we decide $H_1$ (signal present).

The optimal placement of this boundary is given by the Neyman-Pearson Lemma.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: ROC Curve Generation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Choose Detector type LRT"] --> B["Loop over threshold eta"]
    B --> C["Compute PFA from eta under H0"]
    B --> D["Compute PD from eta under H1"]
    C --> E["Plot PD vs PFA point"]
    D --> E
    E --> F{"More eta values?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> B
    F -- "No" --> G["Connect points to form ROC curve"]
    G --> H["ROC Curve in unit square"]
    H --> I["Operating Point selected by threshold"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** is a parametric plot of $P_D$ versus $P_{FA}$ as $\eta$ varies. The LRT traces the **upper-left envelope** of the ROC ŌĆö it is the *best achievable* performance for a given detection problem.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Threshold Selection Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Threshold Selection Problem"] --> B{"Which criterion?"}
    B -- "Bayesian" --> C["Inputs: Priors P0 P1, Costs Cij"]
    C --> D["eta = P0 C10 - C00 over P1 C01 - C11"]
    D --> E["Minimise Total Bayes Risk"]
    B -- "Neyman-Pearson" --> F["Inputs: PFA constraint alpha"]
    F --> G["Solve PFA of Lambda = alpha"]
    G --> H["Maximise PD for given PFA"]
    B -- "Min-Max" --> I["Inputs: Set of priors"]
    I --> J["Find worst-case prior P1 star"]
    J --> K["Set eta as in Bayesian with P1 star"]
    K --> L["Minimise worst-case Bayes risk"]
```

This diagram consolidates the three KTU-prescribed threshold rules. Students are expected to identify which rule applies based on the problem statement.

---
