## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the Bayes criterion for multiple hypothesis testing.**

**Model Answer:**

The Bayes criterion selects the decision regions $\{R_1, \dots, R_M\}$ that minimize the **overall Bayes risk**

$$
\mathcal{R} = \sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{j=1}^{M} C_{ij}\, P(H_j) \int_{R_i} p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_j)\, d\mathbf{x},
$$

where $C_{ij}$ is the cost of deciding $H_i$ when $H_j$ is true. The optimal Bayes rule decides $H_k$ if

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{M} C_{kj}\, P(H_j)\, p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_j) \;\leq\; \sum_{j=1}^{M} C_{ij}\, P(H_j)\, p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_j) \quad \forall i.
$$

This rule **requires complete knowledge of priors and costs**, and is optimal in the sense of minimum expected cost.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Distinguish between MAP and ML decision rules. When does MAP reduce to ML?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | MAP Rule | ML Rule |
|--------|----------|---------|
| Criterion | Maximize $P(H_i \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(H_i)\, p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_i)$ | Maximize $p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_i)$ alone |
| Requires prior | Yes | No |
| Cost assumption | Symmetric 0/1 cost | Equal priors (implicit) |
| Reduces to ML when | $P(H_i) = 1/M$ for all $i$ | Always ML if priors uniform |

When priors are equal, $P(H_i)\, p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_i) = (1/M)\, p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_i)$ ŌĆö the constant $1/M$ does not affect the argmax, hence MAP $\equiv$ ML.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A ŌĆö 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the Bayes optimal decision rule for M-ary hypothesis testing. Show clearly all intermediate steps from the definition of Bayes risk to the final decision rule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-hypothesis detection problem has priors $P(H_1) = 0.4$, $P(H_2) = 0.35$, $P(H_3) = 0.25$ and observations from Gaussian distributions $\mathcal{N}(\mu_i, \sigma^2)$ with $\mu_1 = 0$, $\mu_2 = 2$, $\mu_3 = 4$, and $\sigma^2 = 1$. Determine all decision boundaries using the MAP rule. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write Bayes risk:**

$$
\mathcal{R} = \sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{j=1}^{M} C_{ij}\, P(H_j) \int_{R_i} p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_j)\, d\mathbf{x}.
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Swap summation and integration:**

$$
\mathcal{R} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \int_{R_i} \underbrace{\left[\sum_{j=1}^{M} C_{ij}\, P(H_j)\, p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_j)\right]}_{\rho_i(\mathbf{x})} d\mathbf{x}.
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Define the conditional risk** $\rho_i(\mathbf{x})$ as the integrand.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the pointwise minimization theorem** (since $\rho_i(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$):

$$
\mathbf{x} \in R_k \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \rho_k(\mathbf{x}) = \min_i \rho_i(\mathbf{x}).
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Write final Bayes rule:**

$$
\text{Decide } H_k \text{ if } \sum_{j=1}^{M} C_{kj}\, P(H_j)\, p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_j) \leq \sum_{j=1}^{M} C_{ij}\, P(H_j)\, p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_j) \;\;\forall i.
$$

This is the **Bayes-optimal M-ary decision rule**.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Set up the MAP rule:**

Decide $H_k$ if $P(H_k)\, p(x\mid H_k) \geq P(H_i)\, p(x\mid H_i)$ for all $i$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Find boundary $x_{12}$ between $H_1$ and $H_2$:**

$$
0.4 \exp(-x^2/2) = 0.35 \exp(-(x-2)^2/2).
$$

Take natural log:

$$
\ln(0.4/0.35) = -\tfrac{(x-2)^2}{2} + \tfrac{x^2}{2} = 2x - 2.
$$

So

$$
x_{12} = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\ln(0.4/0.35) = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2}(0.1335) = 1.0668.
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Find boundary $x_{23}$ between $H_2$ and $H_3$:**

$$
0.35 \exp(-(x-2)^2/2) = 0.25 \exp(-(x-4)^2/2).
$$

Take natural log:

$$
\ln(0.35/0.25) = -\tfrac{(x-4)^2}{2} + \tfrac{(x-2)^2}{2} = 2x - 6.
$$

So

$$
x_{23} = 3 + \tfrac{1}{2}\ln(0.35/0.25) = 3 + \tfrac{1}{2}(0.3365) = 3.1682.
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Find boundary $x_{13}$ between $H_1$ and $H_3$** (consistency check):

$$
\ln(0.4/0.25) = -\tfrac{(x-4)^2}{2} + \tfrac{x^2}{2} = 4x - 8.
$$

So

$$
x_{13} = 2 + \tfrac{1}{4}\ln(0.4/0.25) = 2 + \tfrac{1}{4}(0.4700) = 2.1175.
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Check consistency:** $x_{13}$ must lie **between** $x_{12}$ and $x_{23}$ for a valid partition. Here $x_{12} = 1.067 < x_{13} = 2.118 < x_{23} = 3.168$. Ō£ō

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final partition:**

$$
\text{Decide } \begin{cases} H_1, & x < 1.0668 \\ H_2, & 1.0668 \leq x < 3.1682 \\ H_3, & x \geq 3.1682 \end{cases}
$$

(where $x_{13}$ is the redundant internal check.)

---

#### **Question B ŌĆö 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**(a)** With a clear derivation, obtain the minimum probability of error criterion for M-hypothesis testing. Hence state the MAP decision rule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the binary case ($M=2$) with priors $P(H_1) = 0.7$, $P(H_2) = 0.3$, means $\mu_1 = 1$, $\mu_2 = -1$, variances $\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = 1$, find the threshold $\gamma$ of the likelihood ratio test. Compute the probability of error. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express error probability:**

$$
P_e = \sum_{j=1}^{M} P(H_j) \sum_{i\neq j} \int_{R_i} p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_j)\, d\mathbf{x}.
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Reorganize the integral:**

$$
P_e = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \int_{R_i} \underbrace{\sum_{j\neq i} P(H_j) p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_j)}_{\text{probability of error given decision } i} d\mathbf{x}.
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Note that $\sum_{j=1}^{M} P(H_j) p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_j) = p(\mathbf{x})$, so**

$$
\sum_{j\neq i} P(H_j) p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_j) = p(\mathbf{x}) - P(H_i) p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_i).
$$

Therefore

$$
P_e = \int p(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x} - \sum_{i=1}^{M} \int_{R_i} P(H_i) p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_i) d\mathbf{x} = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{M} \int_{R_i} P(H_i) p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_i) d\mathbf{x}.
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Minimize $P_e$ by maximizing the second term pointwise:**

$$
\mathbf{x} \in R_k \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; P(H_k) p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_k) \geq P(H_i) p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_i) \;\; \forall i.
$$

This is the **MAP decision rule**. $\blacksquare$

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö LRT form:** decide $H_1$ if

$$
\Lambda(x) = \frac{p(x\mid H_1)}{p(x\mid H_2)} \geq \frac{P(H_2)}{P(H_1)} = \frac{0.3}{0.7} = \gamma.
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Plug in Gaussian PDFs:**

$$
\frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp[-(x-1)^2/2]}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp[-(x+1)^2/2]} = \exp\!\left[\frac{-(x-1)^2 + (x+1)^2}{2}\right] = \exp(2x).
$$

So $\Lambda(x) = e^{2x}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Set $\Lambda(x) = \gamma$:**

$$
e^{2x} = \frac{3}{7} \;\Longrightarrow\; x_{\text{th}} = \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{3}{7}\right) = \frac{-0.8473}{2} = -0.4237.
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Probability of error:**

$$
P_e = P(H_1) P(\text{decide } H_2 \mid H_1) + P(H_2) P(\text{decide } H_1 \mid H_2).
$$

$$
P(\text{decide } H_2 \mid H_1) = P(x < -0.4237 \mid H_1) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{-0.4237 - 1}{1}\right) = \Phi(-1.4237) \approx 0.0772.
$$

$$
P(\text{decide } H_1 \mid H_2) = P(x \geq -0.4237 \mid H_2) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{-0.4237 + 1}{1}\right) = 1 - \Phi(0.5763) \approx 1 - 0.7177 = 0.2823.
$$

$$
P_e = 0.7 \times 0.0772 + 0.3 \times 0.2823 = 0.0540 + 0.0847 = \mathbf{0.1387}.
$$

So the minimum probability of error is **13.87 %**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing MAP with ML:** Students often write $p(x\mid H_k) \geq p(x\mid H_i)$ without including the prior factor $P(H_k)$. Always include **both** unless explicitly told priors are equal.
> 2. **Missing the partition condition:** When computing decision regions, you must verify that boundaries are **ordered consistently**. If $x_{12} > x_{23}$ after computation, your prior assumption is wrong.
> 3. **Forgetting symmetry of cost:** The MAP rule applies only when $C_{ij} = 1 - \delta_{ij}$. If the cost matrix is non-symmetric (e.g., $C_{12} = 5$, $C_{21} = 1$), use the **full Bayes rule** with the conditional risk.
> 4. **Sign error in LRT threshold:** The threshold is $\gamma = P(H_0)/P(H_1)$ for binary LRT, **not** $P(H_1)/P(H_0)$. Getting this inverted flips the decision rule.
> 5. **Improper handling of $M > 2$:** For M-ary, students often check only one pair of hypotheses and forget the others. Always compute **all** $\binom{M}{2}$ boundary candidates and retain only those that yield a consistent partition.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Notes ŌĆö Memorize Before Exam**

* **Multiple (M-ary) hypothesis testing** generalizes binary detection to $M$ mutually exclusive hypotheses $\{H_1, \dots, H_M\}$ with decision regions $\{R_1, \dots, R_M\}$ partitioning $\mathbb{R}^N$.

* **Bayes risk:** $\mathcal{R} = \sum_{i,j} C_{ij}\, P(H_j) \int_{R_i} p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_j)\, d\mathbf{x}$ ŌĆö minimize by pointwise minimization of conditional risks $\rho_i(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_j C_{ij} P(H_j) p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_j)$.

* **Minimum probability of error** uses the symmetric 0/1 cost matrix $C_{ij} = 1 - \delta_{ij}$, leading to the **MAP rule**: decide $H_k$ if $P(H_k) p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_k) \geq P(H_i) p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_i)$ for all $i$.

* **ML rule** is MAP with uniform priors: $P(H_j) = 1/M$. It **does not require prior knowledge**.

* **Likelihood ratio form:** Compare $\Lambda_j(\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_j) / p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_M)$ to thresholds $\eta_j = P(H_j)/P(H_M)$ (when priors are equal, $\eta_j = 1$).

* **Minmax rule** does not need priors; equalizes the conditional Bayes risk across hypotheses.

* **For Gaussian observations with equal variance**, decision boundaries are **linear**; for unequal variance, they are **quadratic**; for unequal variance *and* mean, they become **hyperquadratic** surfaces.

* **Probability of correct decision** $P_c = \sum_j P(H_j) \int_{R_j} p(\mathbf{x}\mid H_j) d\mathbf{x}$ is **maximized** by the MAP rule; $P_e = 1 - P_c$ is **minimized**.

* **Receiver Operating Characteristics (ROC) in M-ary:** Instead of one curve, an M-ary system has an **M-dimensional operating point** in the space $(P_{FA_1}, P_{FA_2}, \dots, P_{FA_M})$ constrained by the chosen decision rule.

* **Engineering applications** (always mention in long answers for context marks): digital communication (M-PSK, M-QAM, M-FSK), cognitive radio spectrum sensing, radar target classification, biomedical multi-disease diagnosis, pattern recognition.

* **Common mistakes:** forgetting priors in MAP, sign inversion in LRT threshold, ignoring the $\binom{M}{2}$ boundary consistency check, mixing up Bayes risk with probability of error.

* **Mnemonic for M-ary decision rule:** **"P-H-P-X ŌĆö Pick Highest Posterior-times-likelihood"** ŌĆö that is, MAP.

* **The Bayes optimal rule is unique**, but there can be **ties** on a set of measure zero ŌĆö these do not affect the risk.

* **When costs are uniform and priors are equal**, the Bayes rule **coincides** with the ML rule ŌĆö they are identical.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detection of Deterministic Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Detection of Deterministic Signals # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Deterministic Signal? In detection theory, a **deterministic signal** is one whose waveform shape, amplitude, phase, and time of arrival are **completely known** to the receiver in advance. The only uncertainty at the receiver is whether the signal is actually present in the noisy observation or not. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A deterministic signal $s(t)$ is a signal whose values are known functions of time for all $t$ when it is present. The receiver's task is reduced to a binary decision: **"Is the signal there, or is it just noise?"** Mathematically, we model the received waveform $x(t)$ over an observation interval $[0, T]$ as: $$x(t) = \begin{cases} s(t) + n(t), & H_1 \quad \text{(signal present)} \\ n(t), & H_0 \quad \text{(signal absent)} \end{cases}$$ Where: - $s(t)$ = known deterministic signal with energy $E = \int_0^T s^2(t)\,dt$ - $n(t)$ = Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN) with PSD $\frac{N_0}{2}$ W/Hz - $H_0$ = Null hypothesis (noise only) - $H_1$ = Alternative hypothesis (signal + noise) ## 1.2 The Detection Problem ŌĆö Plain English Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy: The Doctor's Stethoscope** > > Imagine a doctor listening to a patient's heartbeat with a stethoscope in a noisy hospital room. The doctor already knows exactly what a **healthy** heartbeat sounds like (the template, $s(t)$). Now the doctor must decide: is the sound coming from a healthy heart ($H_1$ ŌĆö signal present) or is it just background noise and silence ($H_0$ ŌĆö no signal)? > > The doctor cannot listen to the entire waveform in the time domain directly. Instead, the doctor correlates the incoming sound with the *known healthy heartbeat pattern*. If the correlation is high ŌåÆ "Signal Present" ($H_1$). If low ŌåÆ "Just Noise" ($H_0$). > > This is precisely what a **Matched Filter** does in digital communication, radar, and sonar systems! ## 1.3 Why Deterministic Signals Matter in Engineering | Application | Deterministic Signal $s(t)$ | |---|---| | **Digital Communication (BPSK)** | A known sine pulse with phase $\phi$ representing bit "1" | | **Radar / Sonar** | A known chirp pulse reflected from a target | | **Biomedical (ECG/EEG)** | A known template waveform (e.g., QRS complex) | | **Control Systems** | A known step or ramp reference input | | **Optical Communication** | A known OOK (On-Off Keying) light pulse | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Constant to Remember:** For AWGN, the **two-sided Power Spectral Density** is given as $\boxed{\frac{N_0}{2}}$ W/Hz, where $N_0$ is the noise power spectral density. This value will appear in **every** probability-of-error formula in this module. ## 1.4 Hypothesis Testing ŌĆö The Decision Framework The detection problem is fundamentally a **Binary Hypothesis Test**. The receiver must choose one of two decisions ($D_0$ or $D_1$) based on the observed vector $\mathbf{x}$: | Truth \ Decision | Decide $D_0$ (signal absent) | Decide $D_1$ (signal present) | |---|---|---| | $H_0$ true | **Correct Rejection** | False Alarm ($P_F$) | | $H_1$ true | Miss ($P_M$) | **Detection** ($P_D$) | - **Probability of False Alarm** $P_F = P(D_1 \mid H_0)$ - **Probability of Detection** $P_D = P(D_1 \mid H_1)$ - **Probability of Miss** $P_M = 1 - P_D$ # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Neyman-Pearson (NP) Detector When prior probabilities $P(H_0)$ and $P(H_1)$ are **unknown**, the **Neyman-Pearson theorem** is the KTU-mandated approach: > [!NOTE] > **Neyman-Pearson Theorem:** To maximize the probability of detection $P_D$ subject to a constraint on the probability of false alarm $P_F = \alpha$, the likelihood ratio test (LRT) compares the likelihood ratio $\Lambda(\mathbf{x})$ against a threshold $\gamma_{NP}$. $$\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)} \quad \gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1} \quad \gamma_{NP}$$ Where $\gamma_{NP}$ is chosen such that $P_F = \alpha$ is satisfied. ## 2.2 The Bayes / Minimum Risk Detector When prior probabilities and costs are known, the **Bayes criterion** minimizes the average cost (risk): $$\delta_{Bayes}: \quad \Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \quad \gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1} \quad \gamma_{Bayes} = \frac{(C_{10} - C_{00}) \cdot P(H_0)}{(C_{01} - C_{11}) \cdot P(H_1)}$$ Special case: **Minimum Probability of Error** (also called the **Maximum A Posteriori ŌĆö MAP** rule) when $C_{10} = C_{01} = 1$ and $C_{00} = C_{11} = 0$: $$\gamma_{MAP} = \frac{P(H_0)}{P(H_1)}$$ When $P(H_0) = P(H_1) = 0.5$ (equally likely), the threshold becomes unity and we get the **Maximum Likelihood (ML)** detector: $$\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \quad \gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1} \quad 1$$ ## 2.3 The Matched Filter ŌĆö Heart of Deterministic Signal Detection > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The **Matched Filter** is the **optimal linear time-invariant (LTI) filter** that maximizes the output **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** at the sampling instant $t = T$. ### Derivation Summary of Impulse Response For an input $x(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ where $n(t)$ is AWGN with PSD $\frac{N_0}{2}$: The matched filter impulse response is a **time-reversed, conjugated, and shifted** version of the known signal: $$h(t) = K \cdot s^*(T - t), \quad 0 \le t \le T$$ For real signals (which is the common KTU exam case), this simplifies to: $$h(t) = K \cdot s(T - t)$$ The output $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ is **sampled at $t = T$** to obtain the decision statistic. ## 2.4 The Correlator ŌĆö Time-Domain Equivalent > [!NOTE] > **Key Theorem:** The matched filter (continuous-time) and the correlator (which multiplies $x(t)$ by a local replica of $s(t)$ and integrates) produce **identical** decision statistics at the sampling instant. They are mathematically equivalent. $$y(T) = \int_0^T x(t) \cdot s(t) \, dt \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad y(t) = x(t) * h(t) \big\vert_{t=T}$$ This is the cornerstone of BPSK, QPSK, and most digital communication receivers. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this table ŌĆö these are the equations that appear in 90% of KTU questions on this topic!** | Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Expression | |---|---|---| | $E$ | Signal energy | $E = \int_0^T s^2(t)\,dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert S(f) \vert^2 df$ | | $N_0$ | Noise PSD (one-sided) | Two-sided PSD = $N_0/2$ | | $h(t)$ | Matched filter impulse response | $h(t) = s(T-t)$ (for real signals) | | $H(f)$ | Matched filter frequency response | $H(f) = S^*(f) e^{-j2\pi f T}$ | | $\text{SNR}_{out}$ | Output SNR at $t=T$ | $\text{SNR}_{out} = 2E/N_0$ | | $y(T)$ | Correlator output | $y(T) = \int_0^T x(t)\, s(t)\, dt$ | | $\mu$ | Mean of $y(T)$ under $H_1$ | $\mu = E$ | | $\sigma^2$ | Variance of $y(T)$ | $\sigma^2 = N_0 E / 2$ | | $P_F$ | Probability of false alarm | $P_F = Q\!\left(\frac{\gamma - 0}{\sqrt{N_0 E / 2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2\gamma^2}{N_0 E}}\right)$ | | $P_D$ | Probability of detection | $P_D = Q\!\left(\frac{\gamma - E}{\sqrt{N_0 E / 2}}\right)$ | | $P_e$ | Probability of error (BPSK) | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | | $Q(x)$ | Gaussian Q-function | $Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_x^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2} du$ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** The factor of 2 in the SNR expression $2E/N_0$ is often missed by students! It arises because the matched filter "doubles" the effective SNR compared to a simple energy detector. Memorize this with care. ## 2.6 Engineering Utility ŌĆö Where This Theory Is Used - **5G NR receivers** use matched filters for synchronization (PSS/SSS detection). - **GPS receivers** correlate incoming signals with locally generated C/A code replicas. - **Medical ultrasound** uses matched filters for echo detection. - **Spectrum analyzers** implicitly use matched filtering for narrowband tone detection. - **Wi-Fi (802.11)** preamble detection is a direct application of matched filtering. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Matched Filter Maximizes Output SNR ### Step 1: Set Up the Detection Problem Let the received signal be: $$x(t) = s(t) + n(t), \quad 0 \le t \le T$$ Where $n(t)$ is AWGN with two-sided PSD $S_n(f) = \frac{N_0}{2}$. The filter output at time $t_0$ is: $$y(t_0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f) S(f) e^{j2\pi f t_0} df$$ The output has two components: - **Signal part:** $y_s(t_0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f) S(f) e^{j2\pi f t_0} df$ - **Noise part:** $y_n(t_0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f) N(f) e^{j2\pi f t_0} df$ ### Step 2: Express the Output SNR Output signal power at $t_0$: $$s_o^2(t_0) = \left\vert \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f) S(f) e^{j2\pi f t_0} df \right\vert^2$$ Output noise power at $t_0$ (using white noise property): $$E\{y_n^2(t_0)\} = \frac{N_0}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert H(f) \vert^2 df$$ Therefore, the output SNR is: $$\text{SNR}_{out} = \frac{s_o^2(t_0)}{E\{y_n^2(t_0)\}} = \frac{\left\vert \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f) S(f) e^{j2\pi f t_0} df \right\vert^2}{\frac{N_0}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert H(f) \vert^2 df}$$ ### Step 3: Apply the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality By the **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality** for complex functions: $$\left\vert \int A(f) B(f) df \right\vert^2 \le \int \vert A(f) \vert^2 df \cdot \int \vert B(f) \vert^2 df$$ We set $A(f) = H(f)$ and $B(f) = S(f) e^{j2\pi f t_0}$: $$\left\vert \int H(f) S(f) e^{j2\pi f t_0} df \right\vert^2 \le \int \vert H(f) \vert^2 df \cdot \int \vert S(f) \vert^2 df$$ ### Step 4: Equate Equality Condition for Maximum SNR Equality in Cauchy-Schwarz holds when $A(f) = c \cdot B^*(f)$, i.e.: $$H(f) = c \cdot S^*(f) e^{-j2\pi f t_0}$$ Substituting back into the SNR expression: $$\text{SNR}_{max} = \frac{\int \vert S(f) \vert^2 df \cdot \int \vert H(f) \vert^2 df}{\frac{N_0}{2} \int \vert H(f) \vert^2 df} = \frac{2 \int \vert S(f) \vert^2 df}{N_0} = \frac{2E}{N_0}$$ Using Parseval's theorem: $E = \int \vert S(f) \vert^2 df = \int s^2(t) dt$. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Probability of Error for BPSK in AWGN For **Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK)** with signals $s_1(t) = \sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t)$ and $s_0(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t)$ where $\int \phi^2(t) dt = 1$: ### Step 1: Set Up the Observation Model $$x(t) = \pm \sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t) + n(t)$$ After matched filtering with $h(t) = \phi(T-t)$, the output at $t = T$ is: $$y = \int_0^T x(t)\phi(t) dt = \pm \sqrt{E_b} + \int_0^T n(t)\phi(t) dt$$ ### Step 2: Identify the Statistics of the Decision Statistic Let $w = \int_0^T n(t)\phi(t) dt$. Since $n(t)$ is Gaussian and the integral is linear, $w$ is also Gaussian: - **Mean:** $E\{w\} = 0$ - **Variance:** $\sigma_w^2 = \frac{N_0}{2} \int_0^T \phi^2(t) dt = \frac{N_0}{2}$ ### Step 3: Compute Conditional Densities $$p(y \mid s_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}} \exp\!\left[-\frac{(y - \sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right]$$ $$p(y \mid s_0) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}} \exp\!\left[-\frac{(y + \sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right]$$ ### Step 4: Apply the ML Decision Rule with Threshold = 0 By symmetry, the optimal threshold is $\gamma = 0$. The probability of error is: $$P_e = P(s_1)\,P(D_0 \mid s_1) + P(s_0)\,P(D_1 \mid s_0)$$ For equiprobable symbols, $P(s_0) = P(s_1) = 0.5$: $$P_e = \frac{1}{2}\,P(y < 0 \mid s_1) + \frac{1}{2}\,P(y > 0 \mid s_0)$$ ### Step 5: Evaluate Using the Q-Function $$P(y < 0 \mid s_1) = P\!\left(w < -\sqrt{E_b}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sigma_w}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$ By symmetry, $P(y > 0 \mid s_0) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$. Therefore: $$\boxed{P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)} \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.3 Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve The ROC curve plots $P_D$ versus $P_F$ as the threshold $\gamma$ is varied. For the matched filter detector: $$P_D = Q\!\left(Q^{-1}(P_F) - \sqrt{\frac{2E}{N_0}}\right)$$ The ROC depends **only** on the deflection ratio $d^2 = 2E/N_0$ ŌĆö a critical insight from Detection Theory. | SNR ($\mathbf{2E/N_0}$ in dB) | $P_F = 10^{-3}$ implies $P_D \approx$ | |---|---| | 0 dB | $\approx 0.05$ (poor) | | 6 dB | $\approx 0.5$ | | 10 dB | $\approx 0.96$ | | 13 dB | $\approx 0.998$ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always express the **deflection** $d = \sqrt{2E/N_0}$ explicitly in your answers. This single number characterizes the entire ROC curve! # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Optimal Detection System for Deterministic Signals The complete receiver chain for detecting a deterministic signal in AWGN consists of three stages: a matched filter (or correlator), a sampler, and a threshold comparator. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Received Signal
x of t] --> B[Matched Filter
h of t equals s of T minus t] B --> C[Sampler
at t equals T] C --> D[Threshold Comparator
compare y with gamma] D --> E{Decision} E -- y greater than gamma --> F[Decide H1
Signal Present] E -- y less than gamma --> G[Decide H0
Signal Absent] H[Threshold gamma
from NP or Bayes rule] --> D ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö Correlator-Based Equivalent Receiver ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Received x of t] --> B[Multiplier
x of t times s of t] B --> C[Integrator
0 to T] C --> D[Sampler at t equals T] D --> E[Threshold Comparator] E -- y exceeds gamma --> F[Output H1] E -- y below gamma --> G[Output H0] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture ŌĆö Decision Flow Under Both Hypotheses ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Source["Signal Source"] S1[H1 Branch: s of t plus n of t] --> SUM1 NOISE[AWGN n of t] --> SUM1 NOISE --> SUM2 S2[H0 Branch: n of t only] --> SUM2 end SUM1 --> MF1[Matched Filter 1] SUM2 --> MF0[Matched Filter 0] MF1 --> COMP[Comparator] MF0 --> COMP COMP --> DEC{Decide H1 or H0} DEC -- Winner H1 --> OUT1[P_D Detection Probability] DEC -- Winner H0 --> OUT0[P_F False Alarm Probability] ``` ## 4.4 Concept Map ŌĆö Detection Building Blocks ```mermaid flowchart TB CORE[Detection of Deterministic Signals] --> A1[Hypothesis Testing Framework] CORE --> A2[Matched Filter Design] CORE --> A3[Performance Analysis] A1 --> A11[Neyman Pearson Criterion] A1 --> A12[Bayes Minimum Risk] A1 --> A13[Maximum Likelihood] A2 --> A21[Time Reversed Impulse Response] A2 --> A22[Correlator Realization] A2 --> A23[Frequency Domain H of f] A3 --> A31[Probability of Detection P_D] A3 --> A32[Probability of False Alarm P_F] A3 --> A33[Receiver Operating Characteristic] A3 --> A34[Deflection d equals sqrt 2E over N0] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a deterministic signal in the context of detection theory. Give one example. **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Model Answer:** A deterministic signal is one whose waveform shape, amplitude, phase, and time of arrival are **completely known** to the receiver. The only uncertainty is whether it is present in the noisy observation. **Example:** In BPSK communication, the bit "1" is represented by a known sinusoid $s(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ over bit duration $T_b$. The receiver knows this exact waveform and must decide whether the received noisy waveform contains it (hypothesis $H_1$) or only noise (hypothesis $H_0$). --- ### Question 2: State the Neyman-Pearson theorem. **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Neyman-Pearson Theorem:** To maximize the probability of detection $P_D$ subject to a constraint on the probability of false alarm $P_F = \alpha$, the optimal decision rule is the **Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT)**: > > $$\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_1)}{p(\mathbf{x} \mid H_0)} \quad \gtrless_{H_0}^{H_1} \quad \gamma_{NP}$$ > > where $\gamma_{NP}$ is the threshold chosen such that $P_F = \alpha$. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö KTU Internal Choice Format ### Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 4]`** **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the impulse response of the matched filter for detecting a known deterministic signal $s(t)$ in AWGN. Show that the output SNR at time $t = T$ is maximized to $\frac{2E}{N_0}$. **(b)** [7 Marks] For a BPSK communication system with bit energy $E_b = 10^{-15}$ J operating in AWGN with PSD $N_0/2 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz, compute the probability of bit error at the matched filter output. #### Model Solution: **(a) Matched Filter Derivation:** (As shown in **Section 3.1** above) - Step 1: Set up SNR expression. - Step 2: Apply Cauchy-Schwarz inequality. - Step 3: Equality condition gives $H(f) = c \cdot S^*(f) e^{-j2\pi f T}$, i.e., $h(t) = c \cdot s(T-t)$. - Step 4: Maximum SNR $= \frac{2E}{N_0}$. **(b) BPSK Probability of Error Calculation:** Given: $E_b = 10^{-15}$ J, $N_0/2 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz, so $N_0 = 2 \times 10^{-20}$ W/Hz. $$\frac{2E_b}{N_0} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-15}}{2 \times 10^{-20}} = 10^{5}$$ $$\text{Argument of } Q: \quad \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}} = \sqrt{10^5} \approx 316.23$$ $$P_e = Q(316.23) \approx 0$$ **Conclusion:** At this enormous SNR (50 dB), the bit error rate is essentially zero, demonstrating the power of matched filtering. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4]`** **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the structure and operation of a correlator receiver for detecting deterministic signals. Show mathematically that the correlator output at $t = T$ is identical to the matched filter output. **(b)** [7 Marks] A radar system transmits a known pulse $s(t)$ with energy $E = 10^{-12}$ J. The received signal is corrupted by AWGN with two-sided PSD $N_0/2 = 5 \times 10^{-21}$ W/Hz. If the false alarm probability must be limited to $P_F = 10^{-4}$, determine the threshold $\gamma$ and the resulting probability of detection $P_D$. #### Model Solution: **(a) Correlator-Matched Filter Equivalence:** The correlator computes: $$y_c(T) = \int_0^T x(\tau)\, s(\tau)\, d\tau$$ The matched filter output at $t = T$ is: $$y_{MF}(T) = \int_0^T h(T-\tau)\, x(\tau)\, d\tau = \int_0^T s(\tau)\, x(\tau)\, d\tau$$ By direct comparison, $y_c(T) = y_{MF}(T)$. Hence **correlator = matched filter** in terms of decision statistic. $\blacksquare$ **(b) Radar Detection with Neyman-Pearson Criterion:** Given: $E = 10^{-12}$ J, $N_0 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz, $P_F = 10^{-4}$. **Step 1: Compute Output Noise Variance** $$\sigma^2 = \frac{N_0 E}{2} = \frac{10^{-20} \times 10^{-12}}{2} = 5 \times 10^{-33}$$ **Step 2: Find Threshold $\gamma$ from $P_F$** $$P_F = Q\!\left(\frac{\gamma}{\sigma}\right) = 10^{-4}$$ $$Q^{-1}(10^{-4}) \approx 3.719$$ $$\gamma = 3.719 \times \sigma = 3.719 \times \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-33}} \approx 3.719 \times 7.071 \times 10^{-17}$$ $$\gamma \approx 2.63 \times 10^{-16}$$ **Step 3: Compute $P_D$** $$P_D = Q\!\left(\frac{\gamma - E}{\sigma}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{2.63 \times 10^{-16} - 10^{-12}}{7.071 \times 10^{-17}}\right)$$ $$\frac{\gamma - E}{\sigma} \approx \frac{-10^{-12}}{7.071 \times 10^{-17}} \approx -14142$$ $$P_D = Q(-14142) \approx 1$$ **Conclusion:** The radar achieves near-unity detection probability at the given SNR ŌĆö the matched filter reliably detects even very weak reflected pulses. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $2E/N_0$: this is the #1 deduction source. Always show your SNR derivation. > 2. **Confusing $P_F$ and $P_D$ formulas**: $P_F$ uses a zero mean; $P_D$ uses mean $E$. Get the signs right inside the Q-function. > 3. **Not specifying real vs complex signals**: For real signals use $h(t) = s(T-t)$; for complex use $h(t) = s^*(T-t)$. > 4. **Forgetting units**: $E$ is in Joules, $N_0$ in W/Hz, so $E/N_0$ is dimensionless. The Q-function is also dimensionless. > 5. **Skipping the threshold computation**: KTU examiners *love* questions where you must solve $\gamma$ from a given $P_F$. Don't skip this step. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Memorize Before Exam Day!** - Ō£ģ Deterministic signal = completely known waveform; only presence/absence is unknown. - Ō£ģ Binary hypothesis test: $H_0: n(t)$ vs $H_1: s(t) + n(t)$. - Ō£ģ **Matched filter impulse response:** $h(t) = s(T - t)$ (real signals). - Ō£ģ **Matched filter frequency response:** $H(f) = S^*(f) e^{-j2\pi f T}$. - Ō£ģ **Maximum output SNR:** $\text{SNR}_{max} = \frac{2E}{N_0}$. - Ō£ģ **Correlator output:** $y(T) = \int_0^T x(t) s(t) dt$ ŌĆö equivalent to matched filter. - Ō£ģ **Neyman-Pearson:** LRT threshold $\gamma_{NP}$ set by $P_F = \alpha$. - Ō£ģ **Bayes threshold:** $\gamma_{Bayes} = \frac{(C_{10}-C_{00})P(H_0)}{(C_{01}-C_{11})P(H_1)}$. - Ō£ģ **BPSK probability of error:** $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$. - Ō£ģ **ROC curve:** depends only on deflection $d^2 = 2E/N_0$. - Ō£ģ Noise variance at matched filter output: $\sigma^2 = N_0 E / 2$. - Ō£ģ Threshold from $P_F$: $\gamma = \sigma \cdot Q^{-1}(P_F)$. - Ō£ģ $P_D = Q\!\left(Q^{-1}(P_F) - \sqrt{2E/N_0}\right)$ ŌĆö the master ROC equation. - Ō£ģ Use $E = \int_0^T s^2(t) dt$ or $E = \int \vert S(f) \vert^2 df$ (Parseval's theorem). > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Mantra:** Whenever you see "deterministic signal + AWGN + detection", your **first** reflex should be: *"Matched filter, $h(t) = s(T-t)$, SNR $= 2E/N_0$, $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$."* This trio will solve 80% of exam problems on this topic.

topics.title: Matched Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Matched Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **Matched Filter** is the **linear time-invariant (LTI)** filter that is *optimally* designed to maximize the **output signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** at a specific decision instant in the presence of additive **white Gaussian noise (AWGN)**. The filter's impulse response is "matched" ŌĆö i.e., tailored exactly ŌĆö to the known transmitted signal waveform $s(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A matched filter is the optimal linear detector for a known signal in AWGN. Its impulse response is the time-reversed and delayed version of the known signal:
> $$h(t) = s(T - t), \quad 0 \leq t \leq T$$
> where $T$ is the signal duration and the decision is sampled at $t = T$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy
Imagine you are standing in a **noisy stadium** filled with 50,000 screaming fans, and somewhere in the crowd, your friend whispers your name **once** at a precise moment. You want to design a single "listening device" that is **best** at catching that whisper.

- If the whisper has a specific **shape** (a soft "shhh-shhh" pattern), you don't just amplify everything equally ŌĆö you build a filter that **listens only for that exact pattern** and ignores other random noises.
- The **Matched Filter** is mathematically equivalent to: *"slide the entire known template of the whisper across the noisy recording, and at every instant measure how similar the noise is to your friend's exact whisper shape."*
- At the moment the template **aligns perfectly** with the actual whisper, the correlation value **peaks**, and the SNR is at its maximum. That peak is your **decision point**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The matched filter is a **correlation detector** in disguise. It does not "filter out" the noise per se; it **concentrates** the known signal energy at one instant while averaging the noise to zero.

### 1.3 Problem Setup
We consider the classical detection problem:
$$r(t) = s(t) + n(t), \quad 0 \leq t \leq T$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Units |
|:------:|:--------|:------|
| $r(t)$ | Received (observed) signal | V |
| $s(t)$ | Known transmitted signal (deterministic) | V |
| $n(t)$ | Zero-mean white Gaussian noise, PSD $N_0/2$ | V |
| $T$ | Observation interval duration | **seconds** |
| $E_s$ | Signal energy $\int_0^T s^2(t)\,dt$ | Joules |

The matched filter is optimal under the **Neyman-Pearson** criterion when the noise is Gaussian, and its output at $t = T$ is the **sufficient statistic** for detection.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Logic
The derivation of the matched filter follows a clean five-step logical flow:

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Define a Linear Filter Output:** Apply a causal LTI filter $h(t)$ to the received signal $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$. The output is $y(t) = y_s(t) + y_n(t)$, where $y_s(t)$ is the signal component and $y_n(t)$ is the noise component.

- **Step 2 ŌĆö Sample at Decision Instant $t_0$:** Choose a sampling instant $t_0$ (typically $t_0 = T$) at which we make the binary decision $H_1$ vs $H_0$.

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Formulate the Output SNR:** 
$$\text{SNR} = \frac{y_s^2(t_0)}{E\{y_n^2(t_0)\}}$$

- **Step 4 ŌĆö Maximize the SNR:** Apply the **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality** to the signal part while evaluating the noise variance through the filter's frequency response. The maximum occurs when $H(f) \propto S^*(f)e^{-j2\pi f t_0}$.

- **Step 5 ŌĆö Invert the Frequency Response to Time Domain:** $H(f) = S^*(f)e^{-j2\pi f t_0} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; h(t) = s(t_0 - t)$. For $t_0 = T$, we get the **canonical matched filter** $h(t) = s(T - t)$.

### 2.2 Why the Matched Filter Works
- **Concentrates Signal Energy:** The convolution integral $\int_0^T s(\tau)h(t-\tau)d\tau$ at $t = T$ evaluates to $\int_0^T s^2(\tau)d\tau = E_s$, the **full signal energy**.
- **Averages Out White Noise:** Since $n(t)$ has zero mean, integration (via the matched filter's impulse response) tends to **average** the noise contribution toward zero, while its variance grows only linearly with $N_0/2$.
- **Optimality:** No other linear filter can produce a higher SNR at the decision instant. This is provably true under the Cauchy-Schwarz bound.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | KTU Significance |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:-----------------|
| 1 | $h(t) = s(T - t)$ | Impulse response of matched filter | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ Core result |
| 2 | $H(f) = S^*(f)e^{-j2\pi f T}$ | Frequency response | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ Used in derivations |
| 3 | $y(T) = \int_0^T s^2(t)\,dt = E_s$ | Output at decision instant | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ For SNR evaluation |
| 4 | $\text{SNR}_{out} = \dfrac{E_s}{N_0/2} = \dfrac{2E_s}{N_0}$ | Max output SNR | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ Final answer of derivation |
| 5 | $P_{FA} = Q\!\left(\dfrac{V_T}{\sqrt{N_0 E_s/2}}\right)$ | False alarm probability | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ Used in performance |
| 6 | $P_D = Q\!\left(\dfrac{V_T - E_s}{\sqrt{N_0 E_s/2}}\right)$ | Detection probability | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ Used in performance |
| 7 | $h(t) \leftrightarrow S^*(f)$ | Matched Ōåö complex conjugate spectrum | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ Conceptual link |
| 8 | $S_{y_n}(f) = \dfrac{N_0}{2}\vert H(f)\vert^2$ | Output noise PSD | ŌŁÉ Foundation result |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Used at KTU:** $Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_x^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2}\,du$. Some texts use $Q(x) = \text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})/2$. The complementary error function form is also acceptable.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Digital Communications:** The matched filter is the optimal receiver for **BPSK, QPSK, OOK, PPM, and pulse-shaped modulation** (raised cosine, root-raised cosine) in AWGN channels.
- **Radar & Sonar:** Optimal detection of reflected pulses in clutter and noise.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** ECG/EEG spike detection uses matched filtering against known waveform templates.
- **Gravitational Wave Detection (LIGO):** Matched filtering against theoretical chirp templates from compact binary coalescences.
- **GNSS Receivers:** Code-acquisition stages of GPS receivers implement matched filters for the C/A code.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Impulse Response of the Matched Filter

**Given:** $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$, $0 \leq t \leq T$, where $n(t)$ is **white Gaussian noise** with PSD $S_n(f) = N_0/2$.

**Goal:** Find $h(t)$ that maximizes $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{y_s^2(t_0)}{E\{y_n^2(t_0)\}}$ at sampling instant $t_0$.

---

**Step 1 ŌĆö Filter Output Expression**

The output of the LTI filter $h(t)$ at time $t_0$ is the convolution sampled at $t_0$:

$$y(t_0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} r(\tau)\, h(t_0 - \tau)\, d\tau = \int_0^T r(\tau)\, h(t_0 - \tau)\, d\tau$$

(since $r(\tau) = 0$ outside $[0,T]$).

Substituting $r(\tau) = s(\tau) + n(\tau)$:

$$y(t_0) = \underbrace{\int_0^T s(\tau)\, h(t_0 - \tau)\, d\tau}_{y_s(t_0) \;\text{(signal part)}} + \underbrace{\int_0^T n(\tau)\, h(t_0 - \tau)\, d\tau}_{y_n(t_0) \;\text{(noise part)}}$$

The signal output is deterministic; the noise output is a **zero-mean Gaussian random variable**.

---

**Step 2 ŌĆö Signal Output Component**

$$y_s(t_0) = \int_0^T s(\tau)\, h(t_0 - \tau)\, d\tau$$

In the frequency domain, by the convolution theorem:

$$Y_s(f) = S(f) H(f) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad y_s(t_0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S(f) H(f) e^{j2\pi f t_0}\, df$$

---

**Step 3 ŌĆö Noise Output Variance**

The output noise PSD is $S_{y_n}(f) = \dfrac{N_0}{2}\vert H(f)\vert^2$. Therefore:

$$E\{y_n^2(t_0)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_{y_n}(f)\, df = \frac{N_0}{2}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert H(f)\vert^2\, df$$

This is the standard **Parseval-type** energy identity for the impulse response.

---

**Step 4 ŌĆö Construct the Output SNR**

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{y_s^2(t_0)}{E\{y_n^2(t_0)\}} = \frac{\left\vert \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S(f) H(f) e^{j2\pi f t_0}\, df \right\vert^2}{\dfrac{N_0}{2}\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert H(f)\vert^2\, df}$$

Rewrite the numerator as:

$$\left\vert \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \bigl[S(f) e^{j2\pi f t_0}\bigr] H(f)\, df \right\vert^2 = \left\vert \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) H(f)\, df \right\vert^2$$

where $X(f) \triangleq S(f) e^{j2\pi f t_0}$.

---

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality**

By the **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality** for complex-valued functions:

$$\left\vert \int X(f) H(f)\, df \right\vert^2 \leq \int \vert X(f)\vert^2\, df \cdot \int \vert H(f)\vert^2\, df$$

Therefore:

$$\text{SNR} \leq \frac{\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert S(f) e^{j2\pi f t_0}\vert^2\, df \cdot \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert H(f)\vert^2\, df}{\dfrac{N_0}{2}\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert H(f)\vert^2\, df}$$

The $\int \vert H(f)\vert^2\, df$ terms **cancel out**:

$$\text{SNR} \leq \frac{2}{N_0}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert S(f)\vert^2\, df = \frac{2E_s}{N_0}$$

(using Parseval's theorem: $\int \vert S(f)\vert^2\, df = \int s^2(t)\, dt = E_s$).

---

**Step 6 ŌĆö Condition for Equality**

Equality in Cauchy-Schwarz holds **iff** $H(f) = K \cdot X^*(f)$ for some constant $K$. Thus:

$$H(f) = K \cdot S^*(f)\, e^{-j2\pi f t_0}$$

Choosing $K = 1$ and $t_0 = T$ (a common convention for causality):

$$H(f) = S^*(f)\, e^{-j2\pi f T}$$

---

**Step 7 ŌĆö Inverse Fourier Transform to Get $h(t)$**

Using the duality property $\mathcal{F}^{-1}\{S^*(f)\} = s(-t)$:

$$h(t) = s(-(t - T)) = s(T - t)$$

**Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{\,h(t) = s(T - t), \quad 0 \leq t \leq T\,}$$

This filter is **non-causal** in general because it requires knowledge of $s(t)$ for $0 \leq t \leq T$ at time $t = 0$. To make it causal, store the entire signal in a delay line and apply $h(t)$ in real time ŌĆö this is the **stored-reference** matched filter.

---

**Step 8 ŌĆö Verify the Maximum SNR**

The output signal value at $t = T$ is:

$$y_s(T) = \int_0^T s(\tau)\, h(T - \tau)\, d\tau = \int_0^T s(\tau)\, s(\tau)\, d\tau = E_s$$

The output noise variance is:

$$E\{y_n^2(T)\} = \frac{N_0}{2}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert H(f)\vert^2\, df = \frac{N_0}{2}\int_0^T s^2(t)\, dt = \frac{N_0 E_s}{2}$$

Therefore the **maximum output SNR** is:

$$\boxed{\,\text{SNR}_{max} = \frac{E_s^2}{N_0 E_s/2} = \frac{2E_s}{N_0}\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Output for an Arbitrary Input $r(t)$

For any input $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$, the matched filter output is:

$$y(t) = \int_0^t r(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau = \int_0^t r(\tau)\, s(T - (t - \tau))\, d\tau$$

At $t = T$:

$$y(T) = \int_0^T r(\tau)\, s(\tau)\, d\tau$$

This is the **cross-correlation** of $r(t)$ with $s(t)$ evaluated at zero lag. Hence the **matched filter is mathematically equivalent to a correlator** that slides the known signal template over the received signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The matched filter and correlator receiver are **mathematically equivalent** in the sense of producing the same sufficient statistic $y(T)$, but they differ in **physical implementation**. The correlator multiplies and integrates in the time domain; the matched filter convolves in the time domain. Always state this equivalence clearly in derivation problems.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Matched Filter for Non-White (Colored) Noise

When the noise is **colored Gaussian** with PSD $S_n(f) \neq$ constant, the standard matched filter is no longer optimal. The solution is the **Whitened Matched Filter** (also called the **Generalized Matched Filter**).

**Procedure:**

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Whiten the Noise:** Find a filter $H_w(f)$ such that $\vert H_w(f)\vert^2\, S_n(f) = 1$. This "flattens" the noise spectrum to white. A common choice is $H_w(f) = 1/\sqrt{S_n(f)}$.

- **Step 2 ŌĆö Pass $r(t)$ through the whitening filter:** $r_w(t) = r(t) * h_w(t)$. The noise in $r_w(t)$ is now white.

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Apply a standard matched filter to $r_w(t)$:** The matched filter is matched to the **whitened signal** $s_w(t) = s(t) * h_w(t)$, whose spectrum is $S_w(f) = S(f) H_w(f)$.

- **Step 4 ŌĆö Combined Frequency Response:**

$$H_{GMF}(f) = H_w^*(f)\, S_w^*(f) e^{-j2\pi f T} = \frac{S^*(f)}{\sqrt{S_n(f)}}\, e^{-j2\pi f T}$$

**Equivalent Impulse Response (in time domain):**

$$h_{GMF}(t) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{S^*(f)}{\sqrt{S_n(f)}} e^{-j2\pi f T}\right\}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Intuition:** The whitened matched filter *down-weights* frequency bands where the noise is loud, and *up-weights* bands where the signal-to-noise spectral density is high.

### 3.4 Summary of Derivation Outputs

| Derivation | Resulting Formula | Use Case |
|:-----------|:-----------------|:---------|
| Optimal $h(t)$ for AWGN | $h(t) = s(T-t)$ | Standard digital comms |
| Frequency response | $H(f) = S^*(f)e^{-j2\pi f T}$ | Spectral analysis |
| Max output SNR | $2E_s/N_0$ | Performance benchmarking |
| Colored noise optimal filter | $H_{GMF}(f) = \dfrac{S^*(f)}{\sqrt{S_n(f)}} e^{-j2\pi f T}$ | Non-flat noise channels |
| Detector equivalence | $\int_0^T r(\tau)s(\tau)d\tau$ | Implementation insight |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Matched Filter Receiver Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Received Signal r of t equals s of t plus n of t] --> B[Matched Filter h of t equals s of T minus t]
    B --> C[Sampler at t equals T]
    C --> D[Threshold Comparator V sub T]
    D --> E{H0 Null Hypothesis}
    D --> F{H1 Signal Present}
    G[Known Template s of t] --> B
    H[Threshold V sub T set by Neyman Pearson] --> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The received waveform $r(t)$ enters the matched filter $h(t)$.
- The known template $s(t)$ is the **reference** used to construct $h(t)$.
- At time $t = T$, the output $y(T)$ is sampled and compared to threshold $V_T$.
- The threshold $V_T$ is set according to the **Neyman-Pearson criterion** for a desired $P_{FA}$.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Whitened Matched Filter for Colored Noise

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Received r of t with colored noise] --> B[Whitening Filter H sub w of f]
    B --> C[Whitened r sub w of t with white noise]
    C --> D[Matched Filter matched to s sub w of t]
    D --> E[Sampler at t equals T]
    E --> F[Threshold Decision]
    G[Known s of t] --> D
    H[Noise PSD S sub n of f] --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The whitening filter is designed from the noise PSD $S_n(f)$.
- The downstream matched filter is matched to the *whitened* signal $s_w(t)$.

### 4.3 Signal Flow: Matched Filter as Correlation Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input r of t] --> B[Multiplier: r of t times s of t]
    B --> C[Integrator from 0 to T]
    C --> D[Output y of T equals integral of r tau s tau d tau]
    D --> E[Compare to Threshold]
    E --> F[Decision H1 or H0]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalence Note:** This correlator receiver and the convolution-based matched filter produce **identical decision statistics**. The correlator is often preferred for **digital implementation** (sample, multiply, accumulate), while the matched filter is preferred for **analog/RF front-end** design.
