# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture Flow: From Observations to Parameter Estimates

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["True Parameter Vector theta"] --> B["Design Matrix H encoding model"]
    B --> C["Linear Combination H theta"]
    D["Noise Source w with Covariance Cw"] --> E["Additive Combination"]
    C --> E
    E --> F["Observation Vector y = H theta + w"]
    F --> G{"Choose Estimator"}
    G -->|OLS| H["W = I; minimize squared error"]
    G -->|WLS| I["W general SPD; weighted error"]
    G -->|Markov BLUE| J["W = Cw inverse; Gaussian MLE"]
    H --> K["theta hat LS = H transpose H inverse H transpose y"]
    I --> L["theta hat WLS = H transpose W H inverse H transpose W y"]
    J --> M["theta hat Markov = H transpose Cw inverse H inverse H transpose Cw inverse y"]
    K --> N["Covariance sigma squared H transpose H inverse"]
    L --> O["Covariance H transpose W H inverse"]
    M --> P["Covariance H transpose Cw inverse H inverse"]
    N --> Q["Decision / Detection Output"]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Whitening Transformation Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Original Correlated Domain"]
        Y1["y correlated observation"]
        HW1["H theta deterministic part"]
        W1["w with covariance Cw SPD"]
        Y1 --> HW1
        W1 --> Y1
    end
    S1 --> T["Whitening Transform using L inverse from Cholesky Cw = L L transpose"]
    T --> S2["Whitened Domain"]
    subgraph S2
        Y2["y tilde = L inverse y"]
        HW2["H tilde = L inverse H"]
        W2["w tilde with covariance I white"]
    end
    S2 --> R["Apply OLS in whitened domain"]
    R --> EST["theta hat Markov equivalent to MLE BLUE"]
```

## 4.3 Concept Map: Linear Model Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LM["Linear Model y = H theta + w"] --> ASSUMP["Noise Assumptions"]
    ASSUMP --> A1["sigma squared I homoscedastic uncorrelated"]
    ASSUMP --> A2["sigma squared Sigma homoscedastic correlated"]
    ASSUMP --> A3["Q general SPD heteroscedastic"]
    A1 --> EST1["OLS Estimator"]
    A2 --> EST2["GLS Generalized LS"]
    A3 --> EST3["WLS Weighted LS"]
    EST1 --> PROPS["Properties: Unbiased Minimum Variance BLUE under GM Theorem"]
    EST2 --> PROPS
    EST3 --> PROPS
    PROPS --> APPS["Applications: Beamforming Channel Estimation System ID Kalman Filter"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]
**State the GaussŌĆōMarkov theorem. What are the conditions under which OLS is BLUE?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The GaussŌĆōMarkov theorem states that, given the linear model $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{w}$ with $\mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{w}\} = \mathbf{0}$ and $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$ (with $\mathbf{H}$ of full column rank), the OLS estimator $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{LS}} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{y}$ is the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)** ŌĆö i.e., it has minimum variance among the class of all linear unbiased estimators. **Conditions:** (i) Linear model in parameters, (ii) Zero-mean noise, (iii) Homoscedastic uncorrelated noise ($\sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$), (iv) $\mathbf{H}$ has full column rank. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between the OLS estimator and the Markov estimator. When does the OLS coincide with the MLE?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **OLS:** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{LS}} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{y}$, applicable under $\mathbf{C}_w = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$.
- **Markov (BLUE):** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{M}} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{C}_w^{-1}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{C}_w^{-1}\mathbf{y}$, applicable when the noise covariance $\mathbf{C}_w$ is a known SPD matrix (correlated/heteroscedastic noise).
- **Coincidence with MLE:** Under **Gaussian noise** $\mathbf{w} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{C}_w)$, the log-likelihood is proportional to the WLS cost with $\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{C}_w^{-1}$, so MLE = Markov estimator. If additionally $\mathbf{C}_w = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$, MLE = OLS. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Comprehensive OLS + GaussŌĆōMarkov Problem

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** For the linear model $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{w}$ with $\mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{w}\} = \mathbf{0}$ and $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$, derive the OLS estimator $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{LS}}$ by minimizing $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta}\|^2$. Show all steps including the normal equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prove that the OLS estimator is **unbiased** and find its **covariance matrix**. State and explain the GaussŌĆōMarkov theorem. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of the OLS Estimator:**

**Step 1:** Define the cost function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})^T(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})$. Expand:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\theta}^T\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\theta}^T\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the gradient: $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} = -2\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta}$.

**Step 3:** Set gradient to zero (necessary condition for optimum): $\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{y}$ (Normal Equation).

**Step 4:** Verify Hessian $\frac{\partial^2 J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}^2} = 2\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H}$ is positive definite (since $\text{rank}(\mathbf{H}) = p$).

**Step 5:** Solve for $\boldsymbol{\theta}$: $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{LS}} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{y}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Unbiasedness, Covariance, and GaussŌĆōMarkov:**

**Unbiasedness:**

$$\mathbb{E}\{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{LS}}\} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T \mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{y}\} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} = \boldsymbol{\theta}$$

Hence $\text{Bias} = \mathbb{E}\{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{LS}}\} - \boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{0}$.

**Covariance:**

$$\text{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{LS}}) = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T \cdot \sigma^2\mathbf{I} \cdot \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1} = \sigma^2(\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}$$

**GaussŌĆōMarkov Theorem:** Under the assumptions: (i) linearity in $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, (ii) $\mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{w}\} = \mathbf{0}$, (iii) $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$, and (iv) $\mathbf{H}$ full column rank, the OLS estimator is the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator** ŌĆö its variance $\sigma^2(\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}$ is the smallest in the class of linear unbiased estimators (Cram├®rŌĆōRao Lower Bound is attained for each component). **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative: WLS / Markov / Cholesky Whitening

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Sample Question Bank, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the **Weighted Least Squares (WLS)** estimator for the linear model $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{w}$ where $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{C}_w$ is a known SPD matrix, by minimizing the cost function $J_W(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})^T \mathbf{C}_w^{-1} (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Cholesky decomposition $\mathbf{C}_w = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^T$, show that the WLS estimator obtained in (a) is algebraically equivalent to applying the OLS estimator to a **whitened observation model**. Comment on the practical significance of this equivalence. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of the WLS Estimator:**

**Step 1:** Define the cost function $J_W(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})^T \mathbf{C}_w^{-1} (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta})$.

**Step 2:** Since $\mathbf{C}_w$ is SPD, $\mathbf{C}_w^{-1}$ is also SPD. Expanding:

$$J_W = \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{C}_w^{-1} \mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{C}_w^{-1} \mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{C}_w^{-1} \mathbf{H} \boldsymbol{\theta}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\theta}$:

$$\frac{\partial J_W}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} = -2\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{C}_w^{-1} \mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{C}_w^{-1} \mathbf{H} \boldsymbol{\theta}$$

**Step 4:** Set to zero: $\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{C}_w^{-1} \mathbf{H} \boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{C}_w^{-1} \mathbf{y}$.

**Step 5:** Solve (Hessian $\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{C}_w^{-1} \mathbf{H}$ is SPD hence invertible):

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{WLS}} = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{C}_w^{-1} \mathbf{H})^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{C}_w^{-1} \mathbf{y}$$ **(7 marks)**

**(b) Equivalence to OLS on Whitened Data:**

**Step 1:** Apply Cholesky: $\mathbf{C}_w = \mathbf{L} \mathbf{L}^T$, so $\mathbf{C}_w^{-1} = (\mathbf{L} \mathbf{L}^T)^{-1} = \mathbf{L}^{-T} \mathbf{L}^{-1}$.

**Step 2:** Define the whitened quantities:
$\tilde{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{L}^{-1} \mathbf{y}, \quad \tilde{\mathbf{H}} = \mathbf{L}^{-1} \mathbf{H}, \quad \tilde{\mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{L}^{-1} \mathbf{w}$.

**Step 3:** Verify whitening: $\text{Cov}(\tilde{\mathbf{w}}) = \mathbf{L}^{-1} \mathbf{C}_w \mathbf{L}^{-T} = \mathbf{L}^{-1} \mathbf{L} \mathbf{L}^T \mathbf{L}^{-T} = \mathbf{I}$.

**Step 4:** Apply OLS to whitened model $\tilde{\mathbf{y}} = \tilde{\mathbf{H}}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \tilde{\mathbf{w}}$:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{OLS-whitened}} = (\tilde{\mathbf{H}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{H}})^{-1} \tilde{\mathbf{H}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{y}} = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{L}^{-T} \mathbf{L}^{-1} \mathbf{H})^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{L}^{-T} \mathbf{L}^{-1} \mathbf{y} = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{C}_w^{-1} \mathbf{H})^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{C}_w^{-1} \mathbf{y}$$

This **exactly matches** the WLS estimator from (a). **(7 marks)**

**Practical significance:** The equivalence shows that ANY correlated-noise linear estimation problem can be reduced to the standard OLS problem via a pre-whitening step. This justifies the **practical utility of OLS** even when the noise is correlated ŌĆö simply transform the data first. It also unifies OLS, WLS, and MLE under one theoretical framework.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bias computation:** Students often derive $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ but skip the $\mathbb{E}\{\cdot\}$ step to prove unbiasedness. KTU examiners **deduct 1ŌĆō2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Confusing OLS with WLS:** OLS uses $\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{I}$; WLS uses general $\mathbf{W}$. When $\mathbf{C}_w = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$, they coincide ŌĆö this special case is frequently tested.
> 3. **Assuming $\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H}$ is invertible without stating rank condition:** Always mention "$\mathbf{H}$ has full column rank, so $\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H}$ is non-singular."
> 4. **Sign errors in gradient:** $\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}}(\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\theta}) = (\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^T)\boldsymbol{\theta} = 2\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\theta}$ only when $\mathbf{A}$ is symmetric. Verify symmetry.
> 5. **Missing Cholesky justification:** The Cholesky factor $\mathbf{L}$ exists **only** for SPD matrices. Always verify the SPD property of $\mathbf{C}_w$ before applying it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Things to Remember Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- Ō£ģ **Linear Model core equation:** $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{w}$ ŌĆö write this first on every answer sheet.
- Ō£ģ **OLS estimator:** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{LS}} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{y}$ ŌĆö applicable when $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}$.
- Ō£ģ **WLS estimator:** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{WLS}} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{W}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{W}\mathbf{y}$ ŌĆö applicable for general SPD $\mathbf{W}$.
- Ō£ģ **Markov (BLUE under correlated noise):** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{M}} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{C}_w^{-1}\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{C}_w^{-1}\mathbf{y}$ with covariance $(\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{C}_w^{-1}\mathbf{H})^{-1}$.
- Ō£ģ **Covariance of OLS:** $\text{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\text{LS}}) = \sigma^2(\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}$.
- Ō£ģ **Bias:** All three estimators (OLS, WLS, Markov) are **unbiased** under zero-mean noise.
- Ō£ģ **GaussŌĆōMarkov Theorem:** OLS is BLUE under linearity, zero-mean, homoscedastic uncorrelated noise, and full-rank $\mathbf{H}$.
- Ō£ģ **MLE equivalence:** Under Gaussian noise, MLE = Markov estimator; if additionally $\mathbf{C}_w = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}$, then MLE = OLS.
- Ō£ģ **Cholesky whitening:** Always available for SPD $\mathbf{C}_w$; reduces correlated noise to white noise.
- Ō£ģ **Projection / Hat matrix:** $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T$ is symmetric and idempotent ($\mathbf{P}^2 = \mathbf{P}$).
- Ō£ģ **MSE decomposition:** $\text{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = \text{Bias}^2(\hat{\theta}) + \text{Var}(\hat{\theta})$ ŌĆö exam-favorite formula.
- Ō£ģ **Residual sum of squares:** $J_{\min} = \mathbf{y}^T(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{P})\mathbf{y}$ ŌĆö used to estimate $\sigma^2$ in practice as $\hat{\sigma}^2 = J_{\min}/(N - p)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Examples
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT527 - ESTIMATION AND DETECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application Examples

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Estimation and Detection Theory**, *Application Examples* refers to the systematic translation of abstract statistical inference machinery (hypothesis testing, parameter estimation, optimal filtering) into concrete engineering problem statements. The module consolidates the theoretical foundations covered in Modules 1ŌĆō3 by demonstrating how the **NeymanŌĆōPearson (NP) detector**, the **Bayesian estimator**, the **Wiener filter**, and the **KalmanŌĆōBucy filter** operate inside real-world systems such as digital communication receivers, pulsed radar, GPS positioning units, biomedical instrumentation, and tracking systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 4):**
> Application examples drawn from **communications, radar, sonar, speech, image, and biomedical signal processing** domains, with explicit treatment of:
> (a) **Binary detection** in Gaussian noise
> (b) **Matched filter** realisation
> (c) **Wiener filtering** for signal estimation in noise
> (d) **Kalman filtering** for dynamic state estimation
> (e) **Channel estimation and equalisation** in digital comms
> (f) **Performance metrics**: $P_D$, $P_{FA}$, $P_e$, and **Mean Square Error (MSE)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy ŌĆō "The Doctor's Stethoscope vs. The Smart Watch":**
> Imagine two diagnostic devices trying to hear a faint heartbeat inside a noisy room.
> - A *traditional stethoscope* (analog, fixed gain) is a **Wiener filter** ŌĆö it uses the *known statistical shape* of the heart signal to optimally separate it from stationary background noise.
> - A *smart watch with adaptive algorithms* (heart rate jumps when you run) is a **Kalman filter** ŌĆö it uses a *predictŌĆōcorrect cycle* to track the changing heartbeat in real time, just as a radar tracks a maneuvering aircraft.
> - A *Yes/No pregnancy test* is a *binary detector* ŌĆö based on the noisy chemical reading, it commits to one of two hypotheses ($\mathcal{H}_0$: not pregnant, $\mathcal{H}_1$: pregnant), exactly like a radar declaring "Target Present" or "No Target."

**Physical constants & standard metrics used in this module (in bold):**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann constant | $\mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \; J/K}$ |
| $T_0$ | Reference noise temperature | $\mathbf{290 \; K}$ |
| $N_0$ | Noise PSD (one-sided) | $\mathbf{k_B T_0}$ (thermal) |
| $E_b/N_0$ | Bit energy / noise spectral density | dB scale |
| $P_{FA}$ | Probability of false alarm | $\mathbf{10^{-6}}$ typical for radar |

> [!NOTE]
> The unified *signal-in-noise* model used throughout this module is:
> $$r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$$
> where $s(t)$ is the (known or parametric) signal of interest and $n(t)$ is **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$.

## 1.3 Why Application Examples Matter for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme assesses Module 4 problems as **"Design / Application"** questions mapped to **CO4 (Apply estimation/detection algorithms to real-world engineering problems)** under **Revised Bloom's Level 3 (Apply) and Level 4 (Analyse)**. Memorising formulae is insufficient ŌĆö students must demonstrate the *mapping* from the abstract likelihood ratio to the physical decision rule.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Application Frameworks

### 2.1.1 Framework A ŌĆö Binary Detection in AWGN (Communication & Radar)
- **Core idea:** Choose between $\mathcal{H}_0$ and $\mathcal{H}_1$ using the **log-likelihood ratio (LLR)**.
- **Why:** The optimal detector minimises $P_e$ (Bayes), maximises $P_D$ for a given $P_{FA}$ (NP), or maximises a utility (minimax).
- **How:** The LLR is compared to a threshold $\eta$; under AWGN with equal variances, this reduces to a **correlation (matched-filter) receiver**.

### 2.1.2 Framework B ŌĆö Matched Filter for Known Signal Detection
- **Core idea:** Pre-filter the received signal $r(t)$ with the time-reversed signal $s(T-t)$ before sampling.
- **Why:** Maximises the **output Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** at the sampling instant, achieving the **highest possible detection probability** for a given false-alarm rate.
- **How:** Output $y(T) = \int_0^T r(\tau)\,s(\tau)\,d\tau$ is the sufficient statistic.

### 2.1.3 Framework C ŌĆö Wiener Filter (Stationary Estimation)
- **Core idea:** Find the linear filter $h(t)$ that minimises $\mathrm{E}[(s(t)-\hat{s}(t))^2]$.
- **Why:** Optimal linear MMSE estimator when the *signal and noise power spectral densities* are known and the underlying processes are *wide-sense stationary*.
- **How:** Solved in the frequency domain via the **WienerŌĆōHopf equation**:
$$H_{\text{opt}}(f) = \frac{S_{ss}(f)}{S_{ss}(f) + S_{nn}(f)}$$

### 2.1.4 Framework D ŌĆö Kalman Filter (Non-stationary / Dynamic Estimation)
- **Core idea:** Recursive two-step estimator: **Predict** (using the state model) and **Update** (using the measurement).
- **Why:** Tracks *time-varying* state vectors (position, velocity, channel gains) in real time with bounded computational cost (linear in state dimension).
- **How:** The Kalman gain $K_k$ is computed online using the error covariance recursion.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for Module 4. Every KTU Part B question from this module maps to at least one row below.

| # | Application Context | Key Equation | Symbol Definitions | Output Metric |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Binary detection (AWGN, equal variance) | $\Lambda(\mathbf{r}) = \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{N_0}\int_0^T [2r(t)s(t)-s^2(t)]dt\right)$ | $\Lambda$ = likelihood ratio, $s(t)$ = known signal | $P_D, P_{FA}, P_e$ |
| 2 | Bayes threshold | $\eta = \dfrac{P(\mathcal{H}_0)(C_{10}-C_{00})}{P(\mathcal{H}_1)(C_{01}-C_{11})}$ | $C_{ij}$ = cost of deciding $\mathcal{H}_i$ when $\mathcal{H}_j$ true | Optimal Bayesian decision rule |
| 3 | NP threshold | $P_{FA} = Q\!\left(\dfrac{\gamma - \mu_0}{\sigma_0}\right) = \alpha$ | $\gamma$ = threshold, $\mu_0, \sigma_0$ = mean/std under $\mathcal{H}_0$ | $P_D = Q(Q^{-1}(\alpha) - \sqrt{d^2})$ |
| 4 | Matched-filter output SNR | $\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{2E_s}{N_0}$ | $E_s = \int_0^T s^2(t)dt$ = signal energy | Detection performance ceiling |
| 5 | BPSK bit error rate | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | $E_b$ = bit energy, $N_0$ = noise PSD | Communication link budget |
| 6 | Wiener filter (frequency domain) | $H_{\text{opt}}(f) = \dfrac{S_{ss}(f)}{S_{ss}(f) + S_{nn}(f)}$ | $S_{ss}, S_{nn}$ = PSDs of signal and noise | MMSE estimate of stationary $s(t)$ |
| 7 | Wiener filter (time domain) | $R_{ss}(t) = \int_0^T h(\tau) R_{rr}(t-\tau) d\tau$ | $R_{ss}, R_{rr}$ = autocorrelations (WienerŌĆōHopf) | Optimum LMMSE coefficient |
| 8 | MMSE of Wiener filter | $\mathrm{MMSE} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \dfrac{S_{ss}(f)S_{nn}(f)}{S_{ss}(f)+S_{nn}(f)} df$ | ŌĆö | Lower bound on residual error |
| 9 | Kalman prediction step | $\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} = A\hat{x}_{k-1 \mid k-1} + Bu_{k-1}$ | $A$ = state transition, $B$ = input matrix | A priori state estimate |
| 10 | Kalman covariance prediction | $P_{k \mid k-1} = AP_{k-1 \mid k-1}A^T + Q$ | $Q$ = process noise covariance | A priori error covariance |
| 11 | Kalman update step | $\hat{x}_{k \mid k} = \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} + K_k(y_k - H\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1})$ | $K_k$ = Kalman gain, $H$ = measurement matrix | A posteriori state estimate |
| 12 | Kalman gain | $K_k = P_{k \mid k-1}H^T(HP_{k \mid k-1}H^T + R)^{-1}$ | $R$ = measurement noise covariance | Optimal weight for innovation |
| 13 | Kalman update covariance | $P_{k \mid k} = (I - K_kH)P_{k \mid k-1}$ | ŌĆö | A posteriori error covariance |
| 14 | Innovation sequence | $\nu_k = y_k - H\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1}$, $\mathrm{E}[\nu_k\nu_k^T] = S_k$ | $S_k$ = innovation covariance | Used for fault detection / adaptive tuning |
| 15 | Radar range equation (mono) | $\mathrm{SNR} = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4 k_B T_0 B F L}$ | $P_t$=transmit power, $\sigma$=RCS, $R$=range, $F$=noise figure | Detectable range $R_{\max}$ |
| 16 | CramerŌĆōRao Lower Bound (est.) | $\mathrm{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \dfrac{1}{I(\theta)}$, $I(\theta) = -\mathrm{E}\!\left[\dfrac{\partial^2 \ln p(r;\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$ | $I(\theta)$ = Fisher Information | Performance benchmark |
| 17 | MAP estimator | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{\theta} p(\theta \,\vert\, r) = \arg\max_{\theta} p(r \vert \theta)p(\theta)$ | Posterior maximisation | Bayes-optimal point estimate |
| 18 | MLE for deterministic $\theta$ | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{ML}} = \arg\max_{\theta} p(r;\theta)$ | Likelihood maximisation | Asymptotically efficient |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Domains where these formulae are deployed:**
> - **Radar / Sonar:** Rows 1, 3, 4, 15 ŌĆö target detection and ranging.
> - **5G/Wi-Fi/LTE:** Rows 5, 9ŌĆō14 ŌĆö channel estimation, equalisation, beam tracking.
> - **GPS / INS Navigation:** Rows 9ŌĆō14 ŌĆö positionŌĆōvelocity tracking.
> - **Audio / Speech Denoising:** Rows 6, 7, 8 ŌĆö Wiener filter for stationary enhancement.
> - **Medical Imaging (fMRI, CT):** Rows 1, 2 ŌĆö lesion detection; Rows 9ŌĆō14 ŌĆö physiological state tracking.
> - **Financial Engineering:** Rows 17, 18 ŌĆö volatility and parameter estimation in stochastic models.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Matched-Filter Output SNR and the Detection Statistic

**Problem Setup (KTU Type Question):**
Under $\mathcal{H}_1$, the received signal is $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$, where $s(t)$ is a known deterministic signal of energy $E_s$ and duration $T$, and $n(t)$ is **AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$**. A linear time-invariant filter with impulse response $h(t)$ processes $r(t)$ and is sampled at time $t = T$ to produce the decision statistic $y(T)$. We wish to find $h(t)$ that **maximises the output SNR** at the sampling instant.

---

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define the output SNR

The output of the filter at time $T$ is the convolution of the input with $h(t)$:
$$y(T) = \int_0^T r(\tau) h(T-\tau) d\tau$$

Decompose $y(T)$ into signal and noise parts:
$$y_s(T) = \int_0^T s(\tau) h(T-\tau) d\tau$$
$$y_n(T) = \int_0^T n(\tau) h(T-\tau) d\tau$$

The output SNR is defined as:
$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \frac{y_s^2(T)}{\mathrm{E}[y_n^2(T)]}$$

This is the ratio of the squared signal component to the variance of the noise component at the sampling instant.

---

### Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the noise variance

Since $n(t)$ is AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$, the autocorrelation is $R_{nn}(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2}\delta(\tau)$. The noise output variance is:
$$\mathrm{E}[y_n^2(T)] = \iint r_1 r_2 \, h(T-\tau_1)h(T-\tau_2)\,R_{nn}(\tau_1 - \tau_2) d\tau_1 d\tau_2$$
$$= \frac{N_0}{2} \int_0^T h^2(T-\tau) d\tau$$

Substituting the substitution $u = T-\tau$ and reversing limits:
$$= \frac{N_0}{2} \int_0^T h^2(u) du = \frac{N_0}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert H(f) \vert^2 df$$

The second equality follows from **Parseval's theorem**, which is the key tool that links time-domain energy to frequency-domain energy.

---

### Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the CauchyŌĆōSchwarz inequality

We wish to bound $y_s^2(T) = \left[\int_0^T s(\tau) h(T-\tau) d\tau\right]^2$. By **CauchyŌĆōSchwarz**:
$$\left[\int_0^T s(\tau) h(T-\tau) d\tau\right]^2 \le \int_0^T s^2(\tau) d\tau \cdot \int_0^T h^2(T-\tau) d\tau$$
$$y_s^2(T) \le E_s \cdot \int_0^T h^2(u) du$$

The inequality is **an equality if and only if** $h(T-\tau) = K \cdot s(\tau)$ for some constant $K$, i.e. the impulse response is a scaled, time-reversed copy of the signal.

---

### Step 4 ŌĆö Maximise the SNR

Substituting into the SNR expression:
$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{out}} \le \frac{E_s \int_0^T h^2(u) du}{\frac{N_0}{2} \int_0^T h^2(u) du} = \frac{2E_s}{N_0}$$

All dependence on $h(t)$ cancels! The maximum SNR is a **constant** determined solely by signal energy and noise PSD. Equality holds when:
$$h_{\text{opt}}(t) = K \cdot s(T - t)$$

This is the **matched filter** ŌĆö its impulse response is the time-reversed and delayed version of the signal. The constant $K$ is a scale factor that does not affect detection performance.

---

### Step 5 ŌĆö Conclude the form of the decision statistic

The matched-filter output at $t = T$ equals:
$$y(T) = \int_0^T r(\tau) s(\tau) d\tau$$

This is identical to the **correlation receiver** output. It is the **sufficient statistic** for the binary detection problem in AWGN with known signals.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Probability of Error for BPSK over AWGN

**Setup:** Transmit $s_1(t) = \sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t)$ under $\mathcal{H}_1$ and $s_0(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t)$ under $\mathcal{H}_0$, where $\phi(t)$ is a unit-energy basis function. The received signal is $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$, $i \in \{0,1\}$.

---

### Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the LLR

The conditional PDFs under each hypothesis are Gaussian:
$$p(r \mid \mathcal{H}_1) = C \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N_0}\int_0^T [r(\tau) - s_1(\tau)]^2 d\tau\right)$$
$$p(r \mid \mathcal{H}_0) = C \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N_0}\int_0^T [r(\tau) - s_0(\tau)]^2 d\tau\right)$$

Here $C$ is a common constant that does not depend on the hypothesis. Taking the **logarithm** of the ratio, the constant cancels and we obtain:
$$\ln \Lambda(r) = \frac{1}{N_0}\int_0^T [2r(\tau)s_1(\tau) - s_1^2(\tau) - 2r(\tau)s_0(\tau) + s_0^2(\tau)] d\tau$$

Because $s_1 = -s_0$, the terms simplify dramatically. Note that $s_1^2 = s_0^2 = E_b$. Therefore:
$$\ln \Lambda(r) = \frac{4\sqrt{E_b}}{N_0} \int_0^T r(\tau)\,\phi(\tau)\,d\tau = \frac{4\sqrt{E_b}}{N_0}\,y$$

where $y = \int_0^T r(\tau)\phi(\tau) d\tau$ is the correlator output.

---

### Step 2 ŌĆö Optimal decision rule and threshold

For equiprobable symbols and equal costs, the Bayes threshold is $\eta = 1$. This translates to the rule:
$$\text{Decide } \mathcal{H}_1 \iff y > 0$$

---

### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the probability of error

Under $\mathcal{H}_1$, the statistic $y$ is Gaussian:
$$y \mid \mathcal{H}_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(\sqrt{E_b},\, N_0/2)$$

The probability of deciding $\mathcal{H}_0$ given $\mathcal{H}_1$ is:
$$P_{e \mid \mathcal{H}_1} = P(y < 0 \mid \mathcal{H}_1) = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

By symmetry of the BPSK constellation and equal a-priori probabilities:
$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

This is the **canonical BPSK bit-error-rate formula** and is one of the most-tested results in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Kalman Filter Update ŌĆö Derivation of the Gain $K_k$

**Setup:** The dynamic system is described by:
$$x_k = A x_{k-1} + w_{k-1}, \quad w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$$
$$y_k = H x_k + v_k, \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$$

The a-priori estimate is $\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1}$ and the a-priori error covariance is $P_{k \mid k-1}$. We seek the linear update $\hat{x}_{k \mid k} = \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} + K_k \nu_k$ where $\nu_k = y_k - H\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1}$ is the innovation.

---

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define the a-posteriori error

$$\tilde{x}_{k \mid k} \equiv x_k - \hat{x}_{k \mid k} = x_k - \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} - K_k(y_k - H\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1})$$
$$= (I - K_k H)(x_k - \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1}) - K_k v_k$$
$$= (I - K_k H)\tilde{x}_{k \mid k-1} - K_k v_k$$

where $\tilde{x}_{k \mid k-1} = x_k - \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1}$ is the a-priori error. This linearisation is the key step that enables all subsequent algebraic manipulation.

---

### Step 2 ŌĆö Form the a-posteriori covariance

$$P_{k \mid k} = \mathrm{E}[\tilde{x}_{k \mid k}\tilde{x}_{k \mid k}^T]$$
$$= (I - K_k H)P_{k \mid k-1}(I - K_k H)^T + K_k R K_k^T$$

Expand the brackets:
$$= P_{k \mid k-1} - K_k H P_{k \mid k-1} - P_{k \mid k-1} H^T K_k^T + K_k (H P_{k \mid k-1} H^T + R) K_k^T$$

---

### Step 3 ŌĆö Minimise the trace (MMSE optimality)

The optimal $K_k$ minimises the **sum of diagonal elements** of $P_{k \mid k}$, i.e. $\mathrm{tr}(P_{k \mid k})$. Differentiate with respect to $K_k$ and set to zero:
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial K_k}\mathrm{tr}(P_{k \mid k}) = -2(P_{k \mid k-1}H^T) + 2K_k(H P_{k \mid k-1} H^T + R) = 0$$

Solving for $K_k$:
$$K_k = P_{k \mid k-1} H^T (H P_{k \mid k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$$

This is the **Kalman gain** ŌĆö the optimal weighting of the new measurement that minimises the posterior MSE.

---

### Step 4 ŌĆö Simplify the covariance update

Substitute $K_k$ back into $P_{k \mid k}$. The most common form used in practice is:
$$P_{k \mid k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k \mid k-1}$$

This simplification arises from the symmetry of the optimal $K_k$ expression and is algebraically equivalent to the full expression after the optimal $K_k$ has been substituted in.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Wiener vs. Kalman

| Property | Wiener Filter | Kalman Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Process stationarity | **Wide-sense stationary** (statistics time-invariant) | **Non-stationary** (Markov state evolution) |
| Domain of operation | Frequency domain (or batch time) | **Recursive** time domain |
| State model | None (autocorrelation-based) | Explicit $\mathbf{x}_k = A\mathbf{x}_{k-1} + \mathbf{w}_{k-1}$ |
| Computational cost | One-shot (FFT-based) | Per-sample, $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ for full matrix, $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ for steady-state |
| Optimality criterion | **MMSE** for linear estimators | **MMSE** given linear Gaussian model |
| When to choose | Stationary signals, known PSDs | Time-varying, real-time, tracking problems |
| KTU application | Speech enhancement, image deblurring | GPS navigation, radar tracking, control |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Binary Detection System (Radar / Communication)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["Source Hypothesis H0"] --> sigGen["Signal Generator s0 t"]
    srcB["Source Hypothesis H1"] --> sigGen
    sigGen --> chAdd["Channel Add AWGN n t"]
    chAdd --> rxAnt["Receiver Front End Filter and Amplify"]
    rxAnt --> mfBlock["Matched Filter h t equals s T minus t"]
    mfBlock --> samp["Sampler at t equals T"]
    samp --> thresh["Comparator vs Threshold gamma"]
    thresh --> decOut["Decision Declare H0 or H1"]
    thresh --> metricOut["Performance Metric PD and PFA"]
```

**Explanation of flow:**
- The **signal generator** emits one of two possible transmitted waveforms based on the unknown hypothesis.
- The **channel** corrupts the waveform with AWGN, modelling thermal noise and interference.
- The **receiver front-end** provides band-limiting and low-noise amplification.
- The **matched filter** is the key optimal linear processor that maximises output SNR.
- The **sampler** captures the sufficient statistic at the optimal instant $t = T$.
- The **comparator** applies the threshold test to produce the binary decision and feeds performance counters.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Kalman Filter Recursive Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    predState["Predict State xhat k minus 1 over k minus 1"] --> predX["xhat k over k minus 1 equals A xhat k minus 1 over k minus 1"]
    predCov["P k minus 1 over k minus 1"] --> predP["P k over k minus 1 equals A P k minus 1 A transpose plus Q"]
    predX --> gainCalc["Compute Kalman Gain K k"]
    predP --> gainCalc
    gainCalcKg["K k equals P k over k minus 1 H transpose times inverse of H P k over k minus 1 H transpose plus R"] --> updState
    predX --> innov["Innovation nu k equals y k minus H xhat k over k minus 1"]
    sense["Sensor Measurement y k"] --> innov
    innov --> updState["Update xhat k over k equals xhat k over k minus 1 plus K k times nu k"]
    predP --> updCov["Update P k over k equals I minus K k H times P k over k minus 1"]
    updState --> outEst["Output State Estimate xhat k over k"]
    updCov --> delayOut["Delay for Next Iteration"]
    delayOut --> predState
    delayOut --> predCov
```

**Explanation of flow:**
- The Kalman filter is a **two-step recursion**: predict, then update.
- The **predict step** propagates the previous estimate and its uncertainty through the linear dynamical model.
- The **update step** blends the new measurement with the prediction using the **Kalman gain** $K_k$.
- The loop is **closed** by feeding the updated estimate and covariance back into the predictor for the next time step.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Wiener Filter Realisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xIn["Input r t equals s t plus n t"] --> fftBlock["FFT Computation"]
    fftBlock --> sss["S ss f Signal PSD"]
    fftBlock --> nnn["S nn f Noise PSD"]
    sss --> divider["Compute H opt f equals S ss divided by S ss plus S nn"]
    nnn --> divider
    divider --> filt["Apply Filter H opt f in Frequency Domain"]
    filt --> ifftBlock["IFFT to Recover Time Domain Estimate"]
    ifftBlock --> sEst["Output s hat t MMSE Estimate"]
```

**Explanation of flow:**
- The **PSD computation** can be performed using periodogram averaging (Welch's method) for non-parametric estimation.
- The **Wiener filter response** is the ratio of signal PSD to total PSD, suppressing frequencies where noise dominates.
- The **IFFT** returns the time-domain MMSE estimate.

## 4.4 Hierarchical Map of Module 4 Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["Module 4 Application Examples"]

    root --> detNode["Binary Detection Applications"]
    root --> estNode["Estimation Applications"]
    root --> filtNode["Filtering Applications"]

    detNode --> radDet["Radar Target Detection"]
    detNode --> comDet["Digital Communication BPSK and QPSK"]

```

**Explanation of mapping:**
- The three primary branches ŌĆö **detection, estimation, filtering** ŌĆö correspond to the three classical inference tasks.
- Each leaf is a concrete engineering use-case cited in the KTU syllabus.
- The hierarchical map helps students *categorise* any new problem by its inference type before selecting the appropriate algorithm.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Bayes and NeymanŌĆōPearson thresholds:** Students often use $P(\mathcal{H}_0)/P(\mathcal{H}_1)$ when the question requires fixing $P_{FA}$. Read the question stem *very carefully*.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in BPSK:** The $2E_b/N_0$ inside the $Q(\cdot)$ argument is often written as $E_b/N_0$ ŌĆö an instant 3-mark deduction.
> 3. **Mixing Wiener and Kalman domains:** A stationary process **cannot** be tracked with the time-varying Kalman recursion. The two are not interchangeable.
> 4. **Sign errors in Kalman gain transpose:** $K_k = P_{k\mid k-1}H^T(\cdots)^{-1}$ is the *only* correct form. Swapping $H$ and $H^T$ will fail the derivation mark.
> 5. **Skipping the noise variance computation in matched-filter SNR derivations:** Step 2 in Section 3.1 is the *most-skipped* step and carries up to 4 marks in ESE.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
*Define the matched filter. Derive its output SNR in AWGN for a known signal of energy $E_s$.*

**Model Answer:**

A **matched filter** is the linear time-invariant filter whose impulse response is a time-reversed and delayed replica of the known input signal: $h(t) = K\,s(T - t)$, where $K$ is a constant and $T$ is the signal duration. It maximises the output signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) at the sampling instant $t = T$ in the presence of AWGN.

**Derivation (concise):**
At the filter output, the signal component is $y_s(T) = \int_0^T s(\tau)h(T-\tau)d\tau$ and the noise variance is $\mathrm{E}[y_n^2(T)] = \frac{N_0}{2}\int_0^T h^2(u)du$.

Applying **CauchyŌĆōSchwarz** inequality:
$$y_s^2(T) \le E_s \int_0^T h^2(u)du$$

Substituting into the SNR definition:
$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \frac{y_s^2(T)}{\mathrm{E}[y_n^2(T)]} \le \frac{2E_s}{N_0}$$

Equality holds iff $h(t) = K\,s(T-t)$. Hence, **maximum output SNR = $\frac{2E_s}{N_0}$**. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for derivation.)

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*State and explain the WienerŌĆōHopf equation for the optimum linear MMSE filter.*

**Model Answer:**

The **WienerŌĆōHopf equation** gives the impulse response $h_{\text{opt}}(t)$ of the linear filter that minimises the mean-square error $\mathrm{E}[(s(t) - \hat{s}(t))^2]$ between the desired signal $s(t)$ and its estimate $\hat{s}(t)$ when both $s(t)$ and the noise $n(t)$ are jointly wide-sense stationary random processes.

**Equation:**
$$R_{ss}(\tau) = \int_0^T h_{\text{opt}}(u) R_{rr}(\tau - u) du, \quad 0 \le \tau \le T$$

where $R_{ss}(\tau) = \mathrm{E}[s(t)s(t+\tau)]$ is the autocorrelation of the desired signal and $R_{rr}(\tau) = \mathrm{E}[r(t)r(t+\tau)]$ is the autocorrelation of the received signal (signal + noise).

**Equivalent frequency-domain form:**
$$H_{\text{opt}}(f) = \frac{S_{ss}(f)}{S_{ss}(f) + S_{nn}(f)}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The Wiener filter attenuates frequency components where noise dominates ($S_{nn} \gg S_{ss}$) and passes components where signal dominates, achieving the minimum mean-square error. (3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for interpretation, 1 for equivalent frequency-domain form.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** Consider a binary hypothesis testing problem in AWGN:
$$\mathcal{H}_0: r(t) = n(t)$$
$$\mathcal{H}_1: r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$$

where $s(t) = A$ for $0 \le t \le T$ and $n(t)$ is AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$. Derive the optimal NeymanŌĆōPearson detector and express $P_D$ in terms of $P_{FA}$ and SNR. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a Kalman filter to track the position of a 1-D moving object with constant velocity $v$, modelled as:
$$x_{k} = \begin{bmatrix}1 & T_s \\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix} x_{k-1} + w_{k-1}, \quad y_k = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0\end{bmatrix} x_k + v_k$$
with $Q = \sigma_w^2 I$, $R = \sigma_v^2$, and $T_s = 1$ s. Derive the Kalman gain $K_k$ and write the steady-state update equations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Likelihood ratio**

The LLR after the matched filter (correlator with $s(t)$) is:
$$\ln \Lambda(r) = \frac{2}{N_0}\int_0^T r(\tau) s(\tau)d\tau - \frac{E_s}{N_0} = \frac{2A}{N_0}\int_0^T r(\tau)d\tau - \frac{A^2 T}{N_0}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Sufficient statistic**

$$y = \int_0^T r(\tau) d\tau$$

The optimal NP test is $y \gtrless \gamma$ where $\gamma$ is set by $P_{FA}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Distribution under $\mathcal{H}_0$**

$$y \mid \mathcal{H}_0 \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{N_0 T}{2}\right)$$

Setting $P_{FA} = Q\!\left(\dfrac{\gamma}{\sqrt{N_0 T/2}}\right) = \alpha$ gives:
$$\gamma = \sqrt{\frac{N_0 T}{2}}\,Q^{-1}(\alpha)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Distribution under $\mathcal{H}_1$**

$$y \mid \mathcal{H}_1 \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(AT, \frac{N_0 T}{2}\right)$$

Mean signal level is $AT = A^2T \cdot 1$. The output SNR is:
$$\mathrm{SNR} = \frac{(AT)^2}{N_0 T/2} = \frac{2A^2 T}{N_0} = \frac{2E_s}{N_0}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Detection probability**

$$P_D = Q\!\left(\frac{\gamma - AT}{\sqrt{N_0 T/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(Q^{-1}(\alpha) - \sqrt{\frac{2A^2T}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(Q^{-1}(\alpha) - \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\right)$$

**(7 marks: 1 LLR, 2 statistic, 2 distributions, 2 final $P_D$)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö State matrices**

With $T_s = 1$:
$$A = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}, \quad H = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0\end{bmatrix}, \quad B = 0, \quad Q = \sigma_w^2 I_2, \quad R = \sigma_v^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Predict step**

$$\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = A\hat{x}_{k-1\mid k-1}$$
$$P_{k\mid k-1} = A P_{k-1\mid k-1} A^T + Q$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Innovation covariance**

$$S_k = H P_{k\mid k-1} H^T + R = [1 \;\; 0] P_{k\mid k-1} \begin{bmatrix}1 \\ 0\end{bmatrix} + \sigma_v^2 = p_{11}^{(p)} + \sigma_v^2$$

where $p_{11}^{(p)}$ is the $(1,1)$ element of $P_{k\mid k-1}$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Kalman gain**

$$K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H^T S_k^{-1} = \frac{1}{p_{11}^{(p)} + \sigma_v^2}\begin{bmatrix}p_{11}^{(p)} \\ p_{21}^{(p)}\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Update step**

$$\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k (y_k - H\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})$$
$$P_{k\mid k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k\mid k-1}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Steady-state**

As $k \to \infty$, $P_k \to P_\infty$ satisfies the **Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation (DARE)**:
$$P_\infty = A P_\infty A^T + Q - A P_\infty H^T (H P_\infty H^T + R)^{-1} H P_\infty A^T$$

This equation can be solved numerically to obtain the steady-state Kalman gain $K_\infty$.

**(7 marks: 1 matrices, 1 predict, 1 innovation, 2 gain, 1 update, 1 DARE)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the bit error probability of a BPSK system over an AWGN channel with $E_b$ as bit energy and $N_0/2$ as the two-sided noise PSD. Show that $P_e$ depends only on $E_b/N_0$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A speech signal $s(t)$ corrupted by stationary noise $n(t)$ has PSDs $S_{ss}(f)$ and $S_{nn}(f)$ respectively. Design the **Wiener filter** to obtain the MMSE estimate $\hat{s}(t)$ and compute the minimum achievable MSE. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Signal model**

Under $\mathcal{H}_0$: $r(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t) + n(t)$
Under $\mathcal{H}_1$: $r(t) = +\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t) + n(t)$

where $\int_0^T \phi^2(t)dt = 1$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Correlator output**

$$y = \int_0^T r(\tau)\phi(\tau)d\tau$$

Under $\mathcal{H}_1$: $y \sim \mathcal{N}(\sqrt{E_b}, N_0/2)$
Under $\mathcal{H}_0$: $y \sim \mathcal{N}(-\sqrt{E_b}, N_0/2)$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Decision rule (equal priors, equal costs)**

Decide $\mathcal{H}_1$ if $y > 0$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Probability of error**

$$P_{e \mid \mathcal{H}_1} = P(y < 0 \mid \mathcal{H}_1) = Q\!\left(\frac{0 - \sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

By symmetry:
$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Dependence on $E_b/N_0$ only**

The argument of $Q$ is purely a function of the ratio $E_b/N_0$. Hence, $P_e$ is **uniquely determined by $E_b/N_0$**, independent of the bit rate or modulation waveform shape.

**(7 marks: 2 signal model, 1 correlator, 1 decision rule, 2 error computation, 1 interpretation)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Wiener filter in frequency domain**

The optimum frequency response is:
$$H_{\text{opt}}(f) = \frac{S_{ss}(f)}{S_{ss}(f) + S_{nn}(f)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Interpretation**

- If $S_{ss}(f) \gg S_{nn}(f)$ (signal-dominant frequency): $H_{\text{opt}}(f) \to 1$ (pass through).
- If $S_{ss}(f) \ll S_{nn}(f)$ (noise-dominant frequency): $H_{\text{opt}}(f) \to 0$ (suppress).
- This is the optimal **signal-to-noise ratio maximising** linear filter under the MMSE criterion.

**Step 3 ŌĆö MMSE computation**

The error signal is $e(t) = s(t) - \hat{s}(t)$. The MMSE equals the integrated PSD of the error:
$$\mathrm{MMSE} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_{ee}(f) df$$

But $S_{ee}(f) = S_{ss}(f)\left[1 - H_{\text{opt}}(f)\right] = \dfrac{S_{ss}(f) S_{nn}(f)}{S_{ss}(f) + S_{nn}(f)}$

Therefore:
$$\mathrm{MMSE} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{S_{ss}(f)\,S_{nn}(f)}{S_{ss}(f) + S_{nn}(f)} df$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Special case: white noise with PSD $\eta$**

If $S_{nn}(f) = \eta$ (constant) and $S_{ss}(f)$ is arbitrary:
$$\mathrm{MMSE} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{S_{ss}(f)\,\eta}{S_{ss}(f) + \eta} df$$

As $\eta \to 0$ (no noise), MMSE $\to 0$, recovering the original signal perfectly ŌĆö a useful sanity check.

**(7 marks: 2 filter derivation, 1 interpretation, 2 MMSE expression, 2 special-case verification)**

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 4: Application Examples**

- **Binary detection in AWGN** is solved by a **matched filter** (correlation receiver) followed by a threshold comparator.
- **Matched filter impulse response:** $h(t) = K\,s(T - t)$. **Output SNR = $\frac{2E_s}{N_0}$** (independent of $s(t)$'s shape).
- **Likelihood ratio test** is the unifying framework: $\Lambda(r) \gtrless \eta$ where $\eta$ depends on the chosen criterion (Bayes, MP, NP, minimax).
- **NeymanŌĆōPearson formula** for known signals in AWGN:
$$P_D = Q\!\left(Q^{-1}(P_{FA}) - \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\right)$$
- **BPSK bit error rate** in AWGN:
$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$
- **Wiener filter** (stationary MMSE):
$$H_{\text{opt}}(f) = \frac{S_{ss}(f)}{S_{ss}(f) + S_{nn}(f)}$$
- **WienerŌĆōHopf equation** (time domain): $R_{ss}(\tau) = \int_0^T h(u) R_{rr}(\tau - u) du$.
- **Kalman filter** operates as a **predictŌĆōupdate recursion** on time-varying state vectors under linear Gaussian models.
- **Kalman gain:** $K_k = P_{k\mid k-1}H^T(HP_{k\mid k-1}H^T + R)^{-1}$.
- **Innovation sequence** $\nu_k = y_k - H\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1}$ is **white** in an optimally tuned Kalman filter (a key diagnostic property).
- **Cram├®rŌĆōRao Lower Bound** sets a hard lower limit on estimator variance; **MLE is asymptotically efficient** and achieves CRLB as $N \to \infty$.
- **MAP estimator** = ML estimator $\times$ prior weighting; reduces to ML when prior is uniform.
- **Practical applications tested in KTU 2024:** radar target detection, BPSK/QPSK communication links, GPS position tracking, Wiener-based speech denoising, Kalman-based missile/aircraft tracking.
- **Key distinguishing factor ŌĆö Wiener vs. Kalman:**
  - Wiener ŌåÆ **stationary** signals, one-shot filtering, frequency-domain solution.
  - Kalman ŌåÆ **non-stationary** state evolution, recursive time-domain solution.
- **Common KTU exam traps:** Forgetting the factor of 2 in $E_b/N_0$, sign errors in Kalman gain, misuse of $H$ vs. $H^T$, mixing Wiener and Kalman, ignoring $P_{FA}$ specification.
- **Standard assumptions recap:** AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$, known signal $s(t)$ for matched filter, linear-Gaussian for Kalman, WSS for Wiener.
